topics.title: Introduction to Blockchain Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Blockchain Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blockchain** is a *decentralized, distributed, immutable* digital ledger implemented as a *chronologically ordered chain of cryptographically linked data blocks*, maintained collectively by a *peer-to-peer (P2P) network* of nodes through a *consensus mechanism*, without requiring a trusted central authority.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a blockchain is a **Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** in which records (transactions) are:
1. Stored in structures called **blocks**.
2. Linked sequentially using **cryptographic hash pointers**.
3. Replicated across **every participating node** in the network.
4. Validated by **consensus algorithms** (e.g., Proof of Work, Proof of Stake).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Block:** A *block* is a container data structure that holds a batch of validated transactions, a timestamp, a reference to the previous block (hash pointer), and its own cryptographic hash.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Distributed Ledger:** A *distributed ledger* is a consensus of replicated, shared, and synchronized digital data spread geographically across multiple nodes, with no central administrator.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Notice Board"

Imagine a **village notice board** in a small town. Any villager can:
- Pin a notice (transaction) on the board.
- Every new notice is **glued below** the previous one — you cannot insert a notice in the middle or remove an old one without everyone noticing.
- Every villager keeps **a personal copy** of the entire board at home.
- Before a new notice is pinned, **the majority of villagers must agree** it is valid.

Now replace:
- *Villager* → **Node**
- *Notice* → **Transaction**
- *Notice board* → **Distributed Ledger**
- *Glue (cryptographic link)* → **Hash Pointer**
- *Majority agreement* → **Consensus Mechanism**

That is essentially what a blockchain is — a tamper-evident, collectively maintained ledger.

---

## 1.3 Why Blockchain? — The Core Problem It Solves

Traditional systems (banks, governments) suffer from the **"trusted third-party problem"**:
- Single point of failure.
- High transaction cost (intermediaries).
- Risk of data tampering.
- Lack of transparency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blockchain's value proposition:** Trust is **engineered mathematically and algorithmically** rather than enforced by a central institution — the philosophical foundation of the so-called *"trustless" protocol*.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Term | Meaning | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Height** | The length of the chain (number of blocks from genesis) | Bitcoin ~880,000+ blocks (as of 2024) |
| **Block Time** | Average time to mine/produce a new block | Bitcoin: **~10 minutes**, Ethereum: **~12 seconds** |
| **Block Size** | Maximum data capacity per block | Bitcoin: **1 MB** (post-SegWit ~4 MB) |
| **Genesis Block** | The very first block in any blockchain (Block #0) | Hard-coded; has no previous hash |
| **Nonce** | Number used once; variable iterated in PoW mining | 32-bit integer in Bitcoin |
| **Hash Function** | SHA-256 in Bitcoin; Keccak-256 in Ethereum | Cryptographic, collision-resistant |

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Properties of Blockchain (KTU Board Favourites)

1. **Decentralization** — No single authority controls the network.
2. **Immutability** — Once written, data is practically unalterable.
3. **Transparency** — All participants can verify the ledger (in public chains).
4. **Anonymity / Pseudonymity** — Users identified by public-key addresses.
5. **Auditability** — Every transaction is traceable to its origin.
6. **Consensus-driven** — All nodes agree on the canonical chain state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Block

Each block in a blockchain (e.g., Bitcoin) typically contains the following fields:

- **Block Header** (metadata, ~80 bytes)
  - `Version` — protocol version number (4 bytes).
  - `Previous Block Hash` — 256-bit hash of the parent block.
  - `Merkle Root` — 256-bit root of the Merkle tree of all transactions in the block.
  - `Timestamp` — Unix epoch time of block creation (4 bytes).
  - `Difficulty Target (nBits)**` — encoded difficulty of the PoW puzzle.
  - `Nonce` — 32-bit counter incremented by miners.

- **Block Body**
  - **Transaction Counter** — number of transactions.
  - **Transactions** — the actual data payload, organized in a Merkle tree.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Merkle Root** is a single hash that uniquely and compactly represents *all* transactions in the block. If even one byte of any transaction changes, the Merkle Root changes entirely (avalanche effect of cryptographic hash functions).

---

## 2.2 How the Chain is "Glued" — Hash Pointers Explained

A **hash pointer** is a pointer (reference) that *also* contains the cryptographic hash of the data it points to. In a blockchain:

$$H_i = \text{Hash}(\text{Block}_i)$$

The block at height $i+1$ stores $H_i$ as its `Previous Block Hash`. If an attacker modifies $\text{Block}_i$, then $H_i$ changes, which invalidates every subsequent block — making tampering computationally infeasible across the whole chain.

---

## 2.3 Cryptographic Hashing — The Engine of Immutability

A **cryptographic hash function** $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ must satisfy:

1. **Deterministic** — Same input always yields same output.
2. **Quick computation** — $H(x)$ is computable in polynomial time.
3. **Pre-image resistance** — Given $y$, infeasible to find $x$ s.t. $H(x) = y$.
4. **Second pre-image resistance** — Given $x_1$, infeasible to find $x_2 \neq x_1$ with $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$.
5. **Collision resistance** — Infeasible to find any $x_1 \neq x_2$ with $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$.
6. **Avalanche effect** — Tiny input change drastically alters output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard used in Bitcoin:** SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm 256-bit, by NSA, FIPS 180-4). Ethereum uses **Keccak-256** (the original NIST SHA-3 candidate).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every formula/relationship you must memorize for Module 1. Pay special attention to notation — the `pipe` symbol is replaced with `\vert` to comply with markdown rendering rules.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cryptographic Hash | $H(x) = \text{SHA-256}(x)$ | Maps arbitrary input to 256-bit output | bits |
| Block Hash | $H_i = H(\text{Header}_i)$ | Hash of block header at height $i$ | bits |
| Hash Pointer Link | $H_{i-1} \subset \text{Header}_i$ | Previous block hash embedded in current block | bits |
| Merkle Root | $M = H(H(T_1 \,\vert\vert\, T_2) \,\vert\vert\, H(T_3 \,\vert\vert\, T_4))$ | Pairwise hashing of transactions | bits |
| Mining Target | $H(\text{Header}) \;\le\; \text{Target}$ | PoW condition (Bitcoin) | 256-bit integer |
| Difficulty | $D = \frac{D_1 \cdot T_{\text{desired}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$ | Retargeted every 2016 blocks | dimensionless |
| Block Reward | $R_{n+1} = \frac{R_n}{2}$ | Bitcoin halving every 210,000 blocks | BTC |
| Genesis Block | $H_{\text{prev}} = \text{0x000...000}$ | Hard-coded null previous hash | 256-bit zero |

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Supply Chain:** Walmart uses Hyperledger to trace food provenance in **~2.2 seconds** (vs. days in legacy systems).
- **Finance:** Ripple (XRP) settles cross-border payments in **3–5 seconds** vs. SWIFT's 2–5 days.
- **Healthcare:** Patient records stored on blockchain give tamper-proof audit trails.
- **Governance:** Estonia's e-Residency uses hash-linked registries for legal records.

---

## 2.6 Types of Blockchain Networks (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Access | Consensus Speed | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Public (Permissionless)** | Open to all | Slower (PoW/PoS) | Bitcoin, Ethereum |
| **Private (Permissioned)** | Invite-only | Fast | Hyperledger Fabric |
| **Consortium** | Pre-selected group of orgs | Moderate | R3 Corda, Quorum |
| **Hybrid** | Mix of public + private | Variable | Dragonchain |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Genesis Hash and Chain Recursion

We will mathematically formalize how a chain of $n$ blocks is recursively defined.

### Step 1: Define the genesis block

The genesis block, denoted $B_0$, has no predecessor. Its previous-block-hash field is conventionally the all-zero 256-bit string.

$$B_0 = \{\text{Header}_0,\ \text{Transactions}_0\}, \quad \text{Header}_0.\text{PrevHash} = 0^{256}$$

### Step 2: Compute the genesis block's own hash

The block hash is computed as a cryptographic hash of the block header only (Bitcoin convention). The transactions are summarized via the Merkle Root, which is itself part of the header.

$$H_0 = H(\text{Header}_0) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_0))$$

Bitcoin uses **double SHA-256** for added safety against length-extension attacks.

### Step 3: Inductive step — building block $i+1$

Assume we have computed the valid hash $H_i$ of block $B_i$. To construct the next block $B_{i+1}$:

- Collect a set of valid unconfirmed transactions $T_{i+1} = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k\}$.
- Build the Merkle Root $M_{i+1}$ from these transactions.
- Construct the header:
$$\text{Header}_{i+1} = \{\text{Version},\ H_i,\ M_{i+1},\ \text{Timestamp}_{i+1},\ \text{Difficulty}_{i+1},\ \text{Nonce}_{i+1}\}$$

- For PoW chains, find a nonce such that:
$$H(\text{Header}_{i+1}) \;\le\; \text{Target}_{i+1}$$

### Step 4: Recursive chain definition

Putting it all together, the entire chain is defined as:

$$\begin{aligned}
B_0 &: \text{Header}_0.\text{PrevHash} = 0^{256} \\
B_{i+1} &: \text{Header}_{i+1}.\text{PrevHash} = H_i,\quad i = 0, 1, 2, \dots
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the **canonical chain** is the sequence:

$$\mathcal{C} = \{B_0, B_1, B_2, \dots, B_{n-1}\}$$

with the recursive invariant $H_i = H(\text{Header}_i)$ and $\text{Header}_{i+1}.\text{PrevHash} = H_i$ holding for every $i \ge 0$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Proof-of-Work Difficulty Target

Bitcoin adjusts the mining difficulty every **2016 blocks** (approximately every 2 weeks) so that the average block time remains **600 seconds** (10 minutes).

Let:
- $T_{\text{actual}}$ = total time taken to mine the last 2016 blocks (in seconds).
- $T_{\text{desired}}$ = desired time for 2016 blocks $= 2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600$ seconds.
- $D_{\text{old}}$ = previous difficulty.
- $D_{\text{new}}$ = new difficulty.

The retargeting formula is:

$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{desired}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Clamping rule** (to prevent extreme swings):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } D_{\text{new}} &> 4 \cdot D_{\text{old}} \Rightarrow D_{\text{new}} = 4 \cdot D_{\text{old}} \\
\text{If } D_{\text{new}} &< \frac{D_{\text{old}}}{4} \Rightarrow D_{\text{new}} = \frac{D_{\text{old}}}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Suppose the last 2016 blocks took $T_{\text{actual}} = 1{,}814{,}400$ seconds (1.5× the desired time, meaning blocks were slow). Then:

$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{1{,}209{,}600}{1{,}814{,}400} = D_{\text{old}} \times 0.6667$$

So difficulty **decreases by ~33.3%**, making it easier to find a valid hash.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Hash Functions (Board Favourite)

| Property | SHA-256 | Keccak-256 | MD5 | RIPEMD-160 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output size | 256 bits | 256 bits | 128 bits | 160 bits |
| Block size | 512 bits | 1088 bits (rate) | 512 bits | 512 bits |
| Collision resistant | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No (broken)** | Weak |
| Used in | Bitcoin | Ethereum | Legacy (insecure) | Bitcoin addresses |
| Speed | Moderate | Fast | Fast | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **MD5 was broken in 2004** (Wang et al.). Never use it in any cryptographic application. KTU examiners frequently ask "Why not MD5?" — the answer is its broken collision resistance.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Merkle Root Derivation (4-Transaction Example)

Consider 4 transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$. The Merkle Root is computed bottom-up:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{12} &= H(H(T_1) \,\vert\vert\, H(T_2)) \\
H_{34} &= H(H(T_3) \,\vert\vert\, H(T_4)) \\
M     &= H(H_{12} \,\vert\vert\, H_{34})
\end{aligned}$$

If $T_2$ is altered by even one bit, $H(T_2)$ changes (avalanche effect), $H_{12}$ changes, and consequently $M$ changes — propagating the tamper evidence all the way to the block hash.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — A Single Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HDR["BLOCK HEADER"]
        A1["Version - 4 bytes"]
        A2["Previous Block Hash - 256 bits"]
        A3["Merkle Root - 256 bits"]
        A4["Timestamp - 4 bytes"]
        A5["Difficulty Target nBits - 4 bytes"]
        A6["Nonce - 4 bytes"]
    end

    subgraph BOD["BLOCK BODY"]
        B1["Transaction Counter"]
        B2["Transaction T1"]
        B3["Transaction T2"]
        B4["Transaction T3"]
        B5["Transaction TN"]
    end

    HDR --> BOD
    B2 --> MR["Merkle Root Computation"]
    B3 --> MR
    B4 --> MR
    B5 --> MR
    MR --> A3
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** The header (top portion) holds metadata, while the body (bottom portion) contains the transactions. The Merkle Root is a single 256-bit fingerprint computed from *all* transactions in the body.

---

## 4.2 Blockchain Linkage Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G["Block 0 - Genesis Block - PrevHash = 0x0000...0000"]
    B1["Block 1 - PrevHash = Hash of Block 0"]
    B2["Block 2 - PrevHash = Hash of Block 1"]
    B3["Block 3 - PrevHash = Hash of Block 2"]
    B4["Block N - PrevHash = Hash of Block N-1"]

    G -- hash pointer --> B1
    B1 -- hash pointer --> B2
    B2 -- hash pointer --> B3
    B3 -- "..." --> B4
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** Each block stores the hash of the block that came before it. Any retro-active modification invalidates every subsequent block — the basis of immutability.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block-Level Architecture of a Blockchain Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USR["USER LAYER"]
        U1["User A - Public Key PK_A"]
        U2["User B - Public Key PK_B"]
        U3["User C - Public Key PK_C"]
    end

    subgraph TX["TRANSACTION POOL - MEMPOOL"]
        TX1["Unconfirmed Transactions"]
    end

    subgraph NOD["P2P NODE NETWORK"]
        N1["Full Node 1"]
        N2["Full Node 2"]
        N3["Full Node 3"]
        N4["Full Node N"]
    end

    subgraph CON["CONSENSUS LAYER"]
        C1["Proof of Work"]
        C2["Proof of Stake"]
        C3["Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance"]
    end

    subgraph CHN["DISTRIBUTED LEDGER - BLOCKCHAIN"]
        BL1["Block 0 Genesis"]
        BL2["Block 1"]
        BL3["Block 2"]
        BLN["Block N"]
    end

    U1 --> TX1
    U2 --> TX1
    U3 --> TX1
    TX1 --> N1
    TX1 --> N2
    TX1 --> N3
    TX1 --> N4
    N1 --> C1
    N2 --> C2
    N3 --> C3
    C1 --> BL1
    BL1 --> BL2
    BL2 --> BL3
    BL3 --> BLN
    C2 --> BLN
    C3 --> BLN
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** Users broadcast signed transactions into the mempool. Network nodes collect, validate, and propose new blocks. A consensus algorithm selects the next valid block, which is appended to the replicated distributed ledger across all nodes.

---

## 4.4 Tampering Cascade — Why Immutability Holds

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Attacker changes TX_2 in Block 2"]
    B["Merkle Root of Block 2 changes"]
    C["Hash of Block 2 changes"]
    D["Block 3 PrevHash no longer matches Block 2 hash"]
    E["Block 3 becomes invalid"]
    F["Block 4, 5 ... N all become invalid"]
    G["Attacker must re-mine Block 2 AND every subsequent block"]
    H["Computationally infeasible - 51 percent attack territory"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
```

> **Diagram interpretation:** A single transaction modification triggers a cascade that invalidates the entire downstream chain. Re-mining is exponentially expensive, which is what gives blockchain its tamper-evidence property.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define blockchain. List any four key features of blockchain technology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Blockchain is a decentralized, distributed, immutable digital ledger that records transactions across many computers in a peer-to-peer network such that the records cannot be retroactively altered without the consensus of the network.

**Four key features (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Decentralization** — No central authority; control is distributed.
2. **Immutability** — Once data is added, it cannot be changed without invalidating the chain.
3. **Transparency** — All participants can view the ledger (in public chains).
4. **Consensus-driven** — New blocks are validated through agreement algorithms like PoW or PoS.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a cryptographic hash function? Mention two properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **cryptographic hash function** $H$ is a mathematical algorithm that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size bit string (digest), and is designed to be one-way and collision-resistant. (1½ marks)

**Properties (1½ marks — ¾ mark each):**
1. **Pre-image resistance:** Given $H(x) = y$, it is computationally infeasible to find $x$.
2. **Avalanche effect:** A tiny change in input (e.g., one bit) drastically changes the output hash.

*Other acceptable properties:* Deterministic, Collision resistance, Second pre-image resistance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the anatomy of a block in a blockchain with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Merkle Root** in transaction verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of **Proof of Work (PoW)** consensus mechanism with a suitable example. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

A block is the fundamental unit of a blockchain. It consists of two main parts: the **block header** and the **block body**. (1 mark)

**Block Header fields (2 marks):**
- `Version` — protocol version.
- `Previous Block Hash` — 256-bit hash of the parent block.
- `Merkle Root` — 256-bit hash summarizing all transactions in the block.
- `Timestamp` — Unix time of block creation.
- `Difficulty Target (nBits)` — current mining difficulty.
- `Nonce` — counter incremented to find a valid PoW hash.

**Block Body (1 mark):**
- Transaction Counter + list of transactions.

**Role of Merkle Root (3 marks):**
- Computed by pairwise hashing of transactions in a binary tree structure.
- Provides a **single fingerprint** for the entire set of transactions.
- Enables **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)**: a light client can verify a transaction is included in a block by checking only $O(\log n)$ hashes instead of the full transaction set.
- Any change in any transaction cascades up to a different Merkle Root, hence a different block hash — guaranteeing tamper detection.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Working of Proof of Work (4 marks):**
- Proposed by Cynthia Dwork and Moni Naor (1993); popularized by Bitcoin (Satoshi Nakamoto, 2008).
- Miners compete to find a `nonce` such that:
$$H(\text{Header}) \;\le\; \text{Target}$$
- `Target` is a 256-bit number; smaller target ⇒ higher difficulty.
- Average computational work: a miner tries $\approx 2^{D}$ nonces where $D$ is the number of leading zero bits required.
- First miner to find a valid nonce broadcasts the block; other nodes verify in $O(1)$ time and accept it upon consensus.

**Example (1 mark):** In Bitcoin, the difficulty is adjusted every 2016 blocks to maintain a 10-minute block time.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High security against Sybil attacks.
- Proven in production for 15+ years on Bitcoin.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Energy-intensive** — Bitcoin's network consumes ~150 TWh/year (more than Argentina).
- **Throughput bottleneck** — ~7 transactions/second vs. Visa's ~65,000 tps.
- Vulnerable to **51% attack** if a single entity controls majority hash power.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Compare and contrast the three main types of blockchain networks: **public, private, and consortium**. Give at least two real-world examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)**. With the help of a diagram, describe how a transaction is added to a blockchain. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

| Feature | Public | Private | Consortium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access | Open | Restricted | Pre-selected orgs |
| Consensus | PoW / PoS | Voting/Raft | PBFT variants |
| Speed | Slow | Fast | Moderate |
| Example 1 | Bitcoin | Hyperledger Fabric | R3 Corda |
| Example 2 | Ethereum | Quorum (private mode) | Energy Web Chain |

**Public blockchain (1.5 marks):** Permissionless, anyone can read, write, and validate. Examples: **Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin**.

**Private blockchain (1.5 marks):** Permissioned, controlled by a single organization. Examples: **Hyperledger Fabric (IBM), Corda (R3)**.

**Consortium blockchain (1.5 marks):** Semi-decentralized, governed by a group of organizations. Examples: **Quorum (JPMorgan-led consortium), Energy Web Foundation**.

**Verdict (1 mark):** The choice depends on the trust model, performance requirements, and regulatory environment.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT) (2 marks):**
- DLT is a decentralized database replicated across multiple nodes, with no central administrator.
- Updates are validated through consensus and recorded as an immutable chain of blocks (in blockchain-type DLTs).
- Distinguishing feature: **shared, synchronized, and replicated** across all participants.

**Transaction lifecycle (with diagram) — 5 marks:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User signs TX with private key"] --> B["Broadcast to P2P network"]
    B --> C["Nodes validate TX in mempool"]
    C --> D["Miner selects TXs into a candidate block"]
    D --> E["PoW PoS puzzle solved"]
    E --> F["New block broadcast to all nodes"]
    F --> G["Nodes verify and append to chain"]
    G --> H["TX confirmed after 6 blocks in Bitcoin"]
```

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** The sender signs the transaction with their private key (digital signature).
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** The signed transaction is broadcast to the P2P network.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Validators/nodes check the digital signature, sufficient balance, and absence of double-spend.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Valid transactions sit in the mempool until a miner/proposer includes them in a candidate block.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** The block is added to the chain upon consensus; the transaction is now confirmed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls that cost students 2–4 marks:**
> 1. **Writing "blockchain is unhackable."** It is **tamper-evident** under honest-majority assumption, not unhackable. Use precise language.
> 2. **Confusing block hash with block height.** Hash = 256-bit output of SHA-256; Height = ordinal position from genesis.
> 3. **Forgetting that Merkle Root is in the HEADER, not the body.** Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Writing MD5 as a "secure hash."** MD5 is **broken** since 2004 — never use it in modern systems.
> 5. **In Proof of Work, not stating the actual condition** $\bigl(H(\text{Header}) \le \text{Target}\bigr)$. Generic statements like "miners solve puzzles" fetch only partial marks.
> 6. **Mixing up Bitcoin's SHA-256 with Ethereum's Keccak-256.** They are different algorithms.
> 7. **Not drawing a block diagram when asked.** A diagram without proper labels is a 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Blockchain = distributed + decentralized + immutable ledger** of cryptographically linked blocks.
- [x] **Block anatomy:** Header (Version, PrevHash, MerkleRoot, Timestamp, nBits, Nonce) + Body (Transactions).
- [x] **Genesis block** has `PrevHash = 0x0000...0000` (256-bit zero).
- [x] **Hash pointer** = cryptographic link between consecutive blocks; tampering breaks the chain.
- [x] **SHA-256** is used in Bitcoin; **Keccak-256** in Ethereum.
- [x] **Cryptographic hash properties:** Deterministic, Pre-image resistant, Collision resistant, Avalanche effect.
- [x] **Merkle Root** = single hash summarizing all transactions; enables SPV (light client verification).
- [x] **Proof of Work:** Find `nonce` such that $H(\text{Header}) \le \text{Target}$. Energy-intensive but secure.
- [x] **Difficulty retarget:** every 2016 blocks, formula $D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{desired}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$, clamped to 4× range.
- [x] **Block time:** Bitcoin ~10 min, Ethereum ~12 s.
- [x] **Types of blockchain:** Public (Bitcoin), Private (Hyperledger), Consortium (R3 Corda), Hybrid.
- [x] **DLT ≠ Blockchain:** DLT is the broader category; blockchain is one specific DLT implementation.
- [x] **MD5 is broken** (2004); RIPEMD-160 is used in Bitcoin address generation.
- [x] **Immutability is probabilistic** under 51% attack assumption; the cost of attack grows with chain length.
- [x] **Bitcoin block reward halves** every 210,000 blocks → eventual cap of 21 million BTC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blockchain** is a **decentralized, distributed, append-only digital ledger** technology that records transactions across a peer-to-peer (P2P) network of computers in a **chronologically ordered, cryptographically secured, and immutable** sequence of data structures called **blocks**, which are linked together using **cryptographic hash functions** and validated through a **consensus mechanism** agreed upon by network participants (nodes) without requiring a central trusted authority.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the definition must explicitly cover **four mandatory pillars**:

| Pillar | Mandatory Keyword |
| :--- | :--- |
| Structure | Distributed ledger of **blocks** |
| Linkage | Cryptographic **hash** linking |
| Validation | **Consensus** mechanism |
| Trust Model | **Decentralized** P2P network |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 Scheme treats "Blockchain Definition" as the foundational concept that introduces the *paradigm shift* from centralized trust (banks, governments) to **trustless consensus** (mathematics + cryptography).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **public community notice board** in a village:

1. Anyone can **add a new notice**, but the notice must be signed and verified by a majority of the villagers (consensus).
2. Once a notice is pinned, it **cannot be removed or altered** — it is permanent (immutable).
3. Every villager has an **identical copy** of the entire board at home (distributed ledger).
4. The notices are **stapled in a chronological chain** — each new notice references the unique fingerprint (hash) of the previous one.

> **A blockchain works exactly like this notice board** — except it is digital, mathematical, and enforced by code instead of villagers.

**Another powerful analogy — The "Google Docs" Model:**
- A Google Doc shared with 1000 people is like a blockchain.
- Everyone can see updates (transparency).
- No single person can secretly modify it (no central control).
- Every change is tracked and timestamped (auditability).
- However, blockchain goes further: it is **cryptographically sealed**, **peer-validated**, and **economically incentivized**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology — Bold Standard Metrics

- **Block Size (Bitcoin):** ~**1 MB** (SegWit: up to ~4 MB)
- **Block Time (Bitcoin):** ~**10 minutes**
- **Block Time (Ethereum):** ~**12–15 seconds**
- **Hash Algorithm (Bitcoin):** **SHA-256** (256-bit output)
- **Hash Algorithm (Ethereum):** **Keccak-256** (Ethash)
- **Consensus (Bitcoin):** **Proof of Work (PoW)**
- **Consensus (Ethereum 2.0):** **Proof of Stake (PoS)**

> [!NOTE]
> These **standard metrics** are examinable in KTU exams whenever a "compare" or "examples" question is asked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why & How" — Operational Decomposition

A blockchain is **not** just a database. It is a **system** that combines four independent engineering fields:

- **Cryptography** → ensures tamper-evidence (hashes, digital signatures)
- **Distributed Systems** → ensures fault-tolerance and replication
- **Game Theory / Economics** → ensures honest participation (incentives, PoW, PoS)
- **Data Structures** → ensures ordering and integrity (Merkle trees, linked lists)

### The Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **A user initiates a transaction** (e.g., "Alice sends 1 BTC to Bob"). This is broadcast to the P2P network.
2. **Nodes validate the transaction** by checking digital signatures, double-spend, and account balances using established cryptographic rules.
3. **Validated transactions are grouped into a candidate block** by a special node (miner in PoW, validator in PoS).
4. **Consensus mechanism is executed** — miners compete (PoW) or are pseudo-randomly selected (PoS) to propose the next block.
5. **The new block's header contains the cryptographic hash of the previous block's header**, creating an unbreakable cryptographic chain.
6. **Once accepted, the block is appended to every node's local copy of the ledger** — and the transaction is considered finalized.

## 2.2 The Core Hash-Chain Equation (KTU High-Yield)

The mathematical heart of a blockchain is the **recursive hash linkage**. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to be able to write this relationship:

$$
H_n = \text{Hash}\bigl(H_{n-1} \;\Vert\; \text{MerkleRoot}_n \;\Vert\; \text{Timestamp}_n \;\Vert\; \text{Nonce}_n\bigr)
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $H_n$ | Hash of the **current** block header (Block $n$) |
| $H_{n-1}$ | Hash of the **previous** block header (Block $n-1$) |
| $\Vert$ | Concatenation operator (bitwise joining) |
| $\text{MerkleRoot}_n$ | Root hash of the Merkle tree containing all transactions in block $n$ |
| $\text{Timestamp}_n$ | Unix time at block creation |
| $\text{Nonce}_n$ | Number used once — varies to satisfy the difficulty target (PoW) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does tampering break the chain?** If an attacker modifies **even a single bit** in Block $n-1$, then $H_{n-1}$ changes completely (avalanche effect of hash functions), which causes $H_n$ to become invalid, which causes $H_{n+1}$ to become invalid, and so on. The **entire downstream chain is invalidated**.

## 2.3 Five Defining Properties of Blockchain (KTU Board Favourite)

| # | Property | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Decentralization** | Removes single point of failure; censorship-resistant |
| 2 | **Immutability** | Once written, history cannot be rewritten without consensus |
| 3 | **Transparency** | All participants can verify the ledger (public chains) |
| 4 | **Traceability** | Every coin/unit can be tracked to its origin (provenance) |
| 5 | **Trustlessness** | Parties transact without needing to trust each other — trust is in the math |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application of Blockchain Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Finance** | Cryptocurrencies, cross-border remittance, CBDCs |
| **Supply Chain** | Provenance tracking (pharma, food, diamonds) |
| **Healthcare** | Tamper-proof patient records |
| **Governance** | Land registry, e-voting, identity (DID) |
| **IoT** | Device-to-device micropayments and authentication |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Block Hash Derivation

## 3.1 From Data Structure to Hash Chain — Full Logical Derivation

The KTU 2024 Scheme often asks: *"Explain with a diagram how a block is linked to its predecessor."* Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step logic** expected in a model answer.

### Step 1 — A Transaction is Born

A transaction $\text{Tx}_i$ is a signed data structure:
$$
\text{Tx}_i = \{\text{Sender}, \text{Recipient}, \text{Amount}, \text{Signature}_{\text{Sender}}, \text{Timestamp}\}
$$

The **digital signature** is generated using elliptic-curve cryptography (Bitcoin uses **secp256k1**):
$$
\text{Signature} = \text{ECDSA.Sign}(\text{PrivateKey}_A,\; \text{Hash}(\text{Tx}_i))
$$

### Step 2 — Transactions are Hashed into a Merkle Tree

All transactions in a block are pairwise hashed up to a single **Merkle Root**:

$$
H_{AB} = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Tx}_A \;\Vert\; \text{Tx}_B)\bigr)
$$

$$
H_{CD} = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Tx}_C \;\Vert\; \text{Tx}_D)\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{MerkleRoot} = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(H_{AB} \;\Vert\; H_{CD})\bigr)
$$

> **Logic:** This logarithmic structure allows **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)** — a light client can prove a transaction is included in a block using only $\log_2(n)$ hashes.

### Step 3 — Block Header Construction

The block header for Block $n$ is assembled as:

$$
\text{Header}_n = \{\text{Version}, \;\text{MerkleRoot}_n,\; \text{Timestamp}_n,\; \text{DifficultyTarget}_n,\; \text{Nonce}_n,\; H_{n-1}\}
$$

### Step 4 — Hash the Header to Get $H_n$

$$
H_n = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_n)\bigr)
$$

> **Note:** Bitcoin uses **double SHA-256** (SHA-256 of SHA-256) as an extra safety layer against length-extension attacks.

### Step 5 — Link to Previous Block

The block $n$ stores $H_{n-1}$ (the hash of the previous block's header) inside its own header. Hence:

$$
H_{n-1} \;\subset\; \text{Header}_n
$$

$$
H_n = f(H_{n-1},\; \text{MerkleRoot}_n,\; \text{Timestamp}_n,\; \text{Nonce}_n)
$$

### Step 6 — Avalanche Effect Propagation

If an attacker tampers with $\text{Tx}_B$ in Block $n-1$:

$$
H_{AB} \rightarrow H'_{AB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{MerkleRoot}_{n-1} \rightarrow \text{MerkleRoot}'_{n-1}
$$

$$
H_{n-1} \rightarrow H'_{n-1}
$$

Now in Block $n$, the stored $H_{n-1}$ no longer matches the recomputed $H'_{n-1}$. The chain **breaks**, and all honest nodes reject Block $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is the *cryptographic anchor* that makes blockchain "**immutable in practice**" — to rewrite history, an attacker must out-compute the **entire honest network combined**, which is computationally infeasible for established chains (the famous **51% attack** threshold).

## 3.2 Quick Numerical Illustration (Block 0 → Genesis)

| Block # | Stored Previous Hash $H_{n-1}$ | Computed Current Hash $H_n$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 (Genesis) | $0000\ldots0000$ (all zeros) | $000000000019d6689c085ae165831e93$ |
| 1 | $000000000019d6689c085ae165831e93$ | $00000000839a8e6886ab5951d76f4117$ |
| 2 | $00000000839a8e6886ab5951d76f4117$ | $000000006a625f06636b8bb6ac7b960a$ |

> The Bitcoin Genesis Hash above is the **actual** SHA-256 of the Genesis Block header — verifiable on any public block explorer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Blockchain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blk0["BLOCK 0 GENESIS"]:::genesis
    blk1["BLOCK 1"]:::blk
    blk2["BLOCK 2"]:::blk
    blk3["BLOCK 3"]:::blk
    userA["USER A"]:::user
    userB["USER B"]:::user
    net["P2P NETWORK OF NODES"]:::net

    userA -->|broadcasts tx| net
    userB -->|broadcasts tx| net
    net --> blk1
    net --> blk2
    net --> blk3
    blk0 -->|hash H0 stored in B1| blk1
    blk1 -->|hash H1 stored in B2| blk2
    blk2 -->|hash H2 stored in B3| blk3

    classDef genesis fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef blk fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Structure of a Single Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hdr["BLOCK HEADER"]:::hdr
    ver["Version: 4 bytes"]:::field
    prev["Previous Block Hash H n-1: 32 bytes"]:::field
    mrkl["Merkle Root: 32 bytes"]:::field
    ts["Timestamp: 4 bytes"]:::field
    diff["Difficulty Target: 4 bytes"]:::field
    nonce["Nonce: 4 bytes"]:::field
    txbody["BLOCK BODY TRANSACTIONS"]:::body
    tx1["Tx1"]:::tx
    tx2["Tx2"]:::tx
    tx3["Tx3"]:::tx
    txN["TxN"]:::tx

    hdr --> ver
    hdr --> prev
    hdr --> mrkl
    hdr --> ts
    hdr --> diff
    hdr --> nonce
    txbody --> tx1
    txbody --> tx2
    txbody --> tx3
    txbody --> txN
    tx1 -.->|SHA256 pair| mrkl
    tx2 -.->|SHA256 pair| mrkl
    tx3 -.->|SHA256 pair| mrkl
    txN -.->|SHA256 pair| mrkl

    classDef hdr fill:#577590,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef field fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef body fill:#f3722c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef tx fill:#f9844a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — How a Transaction Becomes Part of the Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User signs transaction with private key"]:::step
    B["Broadcast to P2P network of nodes"]:::step
    C["Nodes validate signature and double-spend"]:::step
    D["Valid tx enters the mempool"]:::step
    E["Miner selects tx and forms candidate block"]:::step
    F["Consensus: PoW puzzle or PoS election"]:::step
    G["Block linked to chain via prev hash"]:::step
    H["Block broadcast and accepted by network"]:::step
    I["Transaction finalized immutable"]:::final

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I

    classDef step fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU Valuation):** Always **label the arrows** (e.g., "stores H(n-1)") and **number the blocks** in the chain diagram. Examiners award marks for clarity, not artistic skill.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define blockchain. List any four key characteristics of blockchain technology.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Blockchain is a decentralized, distributed digital ledger that records transactions in **chronologically linked blocks**, secured by **cryptographic hashes** and validated by a **consensus mechanism** among peer-to-peer network nodes, eliminating the need for a central authority.

**Four Key Characteristics (1.5 marks — any 4):**
1. **Decentralization** — No single point of control.
2. **Immutability** — Records cannot be altered once confirmed.
3. **Transparency** — All participants can view the ledger.
4. **Traceability** — Every transaction is traceable to its origin.
5. **Consensus-based validation** — Network agreement required for new blocks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*Differentiate between a public blockchain and a private blockchain. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Public Blockchain | Private Blockchain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Access** | Permissionless (anyone can join) | Permissioned (invitation only) |
| **Consensus** | PoW / PoS with thousands of nodes | Voting among few pre-approved nodes |
| **Example** | **Bitcoin**, Ethereum | **Hyperledger Fabric**, Ripple (XRP Ledger) |

(1 mark for table + 1 mark for examples + 1 mark for clarity)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a block in a blockchain. How are blocks cryptographically linked?*

**Model Answer:**

**Block Structure (3.5 marks):**
A block is composed of two main parts: a **Block Header** and a **Block Body** (transaction list).

The **Block Header** contains six fields:
1. **Version** — protocol version (4 bytes)
2. **Previous Block Hash** — hash of the previous block's header (32 bytes)
3. **Merkle Root** — root hash of all transactions in the block (32 bytes)
4. **Timestamp** — Unix time of block creation (4 bytes)
5. **Difficulty Target** — current mining difficulty (4 bytes)
6. **Nonce** — counter incremented during mining (4 bytes)

The **Block Body** contains the **list of transactions** included in the block, organized into a **Merkle Tree** structure.

**Cryptographic Linking (3.5 marks):**
Each block stores the **SHA-256 hash of the previous block's header** in its own header field. The recursive relationship is:

$$
H_n = \text{Hash}(H_{n-1} \;\Vert\; \text{MerkleRoot}_n \;\Vert\; \text{Timestamp}_n \;\Vert\; \text{Nonce}_n)
$$

If any data in Block $n-1$ is tampered, the avalanche effect of SHA-256 causes $H_{n-1}$ to change completely, which invalidates $H_n$, and consequently the entire downstream chain. This is what makes blockchain **tamper-evident** and **practically immutable**.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award 2 marks for the diagram (must be labelled with all header fields) and 1.5 marks each for the linkage explanation and equation.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Explain Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT). Compare blockchain with a traditional centralized database across any five parameters.*

**Model Answer:**

**Distributed Ledger Technology (3.5 marks):**
DLT is a **digital system for recording transactions** of assets in which the ledger is **replicated, shared, and synchronized** across multiple nodes in a network, with no central administrator. Blockchain is the most popular **type of DLT**, but other variants exist (e.g., Hashgraph, Holochain, Tempo).

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Centralized Database | Blockchain (DLT) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Control** | Single administrator | Distributed among all nodes |
| **Trust** | Requires trusted central party | Trustless — trust is in math/code |
| **Transparency** | Opaque to public | Public & verifiable (for public chains) |
| **Immutability** | Admin can alter/delete records | Practically immutable once confirmed |
| **Performance (TPS)** | **High** (thousands TPS) | **Low** (Bitcoin: ~7 TPS, Ethereum: ~30 TPS) |
| **Failure Mode** | Single point of failure | Fault-tolerant by design |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
*Explain the five key features of blockchain that distinguish it from a traditional database. Illustrate with real-world use cases.*

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1.4 marks per feature):**

| # | Feature | Explanation | Real-World Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Decentralization** | No single authority controls the network | Bitcoin — no central bank required |
| 2 | **Immutability** | Records cannot be altered retroactively | Land registry in **Georgia (country)** |
| 3 | **Transparency** | All nodes hold identical ledger copy | **Ethereum** public blockchain explorers |
| 4 | **Traceability** | Every transaction has a verifiable history | Walmart's food supply chain tracking |
| 5 | **Trustlessness** | Participants need not trust each other | DeFi protocols on Ethereum |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Discuss the role of cryptographic hash functions in blockchain. List any three properties of SHA-256 that make it suitable for blockchain.*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Hash Functions in Blockchain (4 marks):**
1. **Block linking** — Each block stores the hash of the previous block header, creating an immutable chain.
2. **Transaction integrity** — Hashing of transaction data via Merkle trees ensures any tampering is detected.
3. **Mining/PoW** — Miners search for a nonce such that the block hash is below a difficulty target.
4. **Address generation** — Public keys are hashed to produce wallet addresses.

**Three Properties of SHA-256 (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Deterministic** — Same input always produces the same 256-bit output.
2. **Avalanche Effect** — A 1-bit change in input changes ~50% of output bits.
3. **Pre-image Resistance** — Infeasible to reverse-engineer the input from the hash output.
4. *(Bonus)* **Collision Resistance** — Infeasible to find two inputs that hash to the same output.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Blockchain Definition" Questions:**
> 1. **Writing only "a digital ledger"** — KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for missing the keywords *decentralized*, *cryptographic*, *consensus*.
> 2. **Confusing "block" with "blockchain"** — A *block* is a single data structure; *blockchain* is the **chain of cryptographically linked blocks**. Marks lost if this distinction is blurred.
> 3. **Drawing a diagram without labels** — A block diagram without labelled fields (Version, Nonce, Merkle Root, etc.) fetches **partial marks only**. Always label the arrow linking $H_{n-1}$.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention consensus** — Consensus is one of the **four mandatory pillars** in the KTU definition. Omission costs ~**0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Writing SHA-1 or MD5 as the hash** — Bitcoin uses **SHA-256** (double hashing). Mentioning weak hash functions shows outdated knowledge and may lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Blockchain Definition (Module 1)**

- **Blockchain = Distributed Ledger + Cryptographic Chain + Consensus + Decentralization** (the four mandatory pillars).
- **Block** = Header (Version, PrevHash, MerkleRoot, Timestamp, Difficulty, Nonce) + Body (Transactions).
- **Hash Linkage Equation:** $H_n = \text{Hash}(H_{n-1} \;\Vert\; \text{MerkleRoot}_n \;\Vert\; \text{Timestamp}_n \;\Vert\; \text{Nonce}_n)$.
- **Hash Function Used:** **SHA-256** (Bitcoin), **Keccak-256** (Ethereum).
- **Merkle Root** = root of the binary hash tree of all transactions in the block.
- **Avalanche Effect** = small input change → drastically different hash → entire chain invalidated.
- **Consensus Examples:** **PoW** (Bitcoin), **PoS** (Ethereum 2.0), **PBFT** (Hyperledger).
- **Genesis Block** = Block #0 with previous hash = all zeros; Bitcoin's actual genesis hash is `000000000019d6689c085ae165831e93`.
- **Three Types of Blockchain:** Public (Bitcoin), Private (Hyperledger), Consortium (R3 Corda).
- **Five Key Features:** Decentralization, Immutability, Transparency, Traceability, Trustlessness.
- **Block Time:** Bitcoin ~**10 min**, Ethereum ~**12–15 sec**.
- **Always use these keywords in KTU answers:** *decentralized*, *cryptographic hash*, *consensus*, *immutable*, *peer-to-peer*, *distributed ledger*.
- **For diagrams:** Number the blocks, label the hash arrow, and show both header and body.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
