# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** — *Define blockchain applications and list any four real-world domains where they are deployed.*

**Model Answer:**

Blockchain applications are real-world implementations of distributed ledger technology that leverage decentralization, immutability, transparency, and cryptographic security to solve industry-specific problems without trusted intermediaries. Four major domains include:

1. **Finance** — cryptocurrencies, decentralized finance, and cross-border payments.
2. **Supply chain management** — provenance tracking and anti-counterfeiting.
3. **Healthcare** — interoperable, patient-controlled medical records.
4. **Identity management** — self-sovereign identity and verifiable credentials.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 marks per correctly explained domain.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** — *Differentiate between permissioned and permissionless blockchains. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Permissionless | Permissioned |
|---|---|---|
| **Access** | Open to anyone | Restricted to authorized nodes |
| **Consensus** | PoW, PoS | PBFT, Raft |
| **Example** | Bitcoin | Hyperledger Fabric |

Permissionless blockchains offer full decentralization and censorship resistance, while permissioned blockchains offer higher throughput and stronger privacy suitable for enterprise consortia.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** — *With neat diagrams, explain the various categories of blockchain applications. Discuss in detail how blockchain is applied in (a) supply chain management and (b) healthcare. Compare their requirements and architectural choices.*

#### (a) Supply Chain Management Application (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Supply chain management involves tracking goods as they move through multiple parties — manufacturer, distributor, retailer, and consumer. Traditional systems suffer from data silos, manual reconciliation, and counterfeiting.

**How blockchain solves this:**

- Each participant appends a cryptographically signed record (timestamp, location, status) to a shared ledger.
- The ledger is **immutable**, so once a shipment's status is recorded, it cannot be altered retroactively.
- Smart contracts can **automate** payments upon delivery confirmation.

**Architecture:**

- **Topology:** Permissioned consortium blockchain (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric).
- **Participants:** Manufacturers, logistics providers, retailers, regulators.
- **Smart contracts:** Encode delivery conditions, quality checks, and payment release.

**Real-world example:** *IBM Food Trust* partnered with Walmart to trace mangoes and leafy greens, reducing traceability time from **7 days to 2.2 seconds**.

#### (b) Healthcare Application (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Healthcare records are fragmented across hospitals, labs, and insurers. Patients lack control over who accesses their data.

**How blockchain solves this:**

- Patient records are stored **off-chain** (e.g., on encrypted databases) while their **hash references** are anchored on-chain.
- Patients grant access via **smart contracts** that enforce consent rules.
- A complete audit trail of who accessed what and when is immutably recorded.

**Architecture:**

- **Topology:** Permissioned blockchain with potential public anchors for verifiability.
- **Compliance:** Must adhere to regulations such as **HIPAA** (US) or **DISHA** (India).
- **Privacy:** Zero-knowledge proofs and selective disclosure for sensitive fields.

**Real-world example:** MIT's *MedRec* prototype demonstrated blockchain-enabled medical record management with patient-centric access control.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Dimension | Supply Chain | Healthcare |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Permissioned consortium | Permissioned with off-chain storage |
| **Data Volume** | High (sensor + logistics data) | Moderate (structured EHR fields) |
| **Privacy Needs** | Medium | Very High |
| **Regulatory Burden** | Moderate | Very High (HIPAA/DISHA) |
| **Throughput Required** | Very High | Moderate |

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** — *Explain the role of smart contracts and tokenization in blockchain applications. Discuss how (a) decentralized finance (DeFi) leverages these primitives, and (b) how tokenization is transforming real estate. Illustrate with examples.*

#### (a) Smart Contracts & DeFi (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **smart contract** is a self-executing program deployed on a blockchain. It runs deterministically on every node and enforces the terms of an agreement without legal intermediaries.

**Role in DeFi:**

- **Lending & Borrowing:** Protocols like *Aave* and *Compound* use smart contracts to pool liquidity and match lenders with borrowers algorithmically. Interest rates adjust dynamically based on utilization.
- **Decentralized Exchanges:** *Uniswap* uses the constant-product formula $x \cdot y = k$ implemented as a smart contract to enable automated token swaps without order books.
- **Stablecoins:** *DAI* uses smart contracts to maintain a soft peg to the US dollar via over-collateralization and liquidation mechanisms.

**Key advantages:**

- 24/7 operation, no downtime.
- Transparent and auditable on-chain logic.
- Composable — DeFi protocols can be combined like Lego blocks ("money Legos").

#### (b) Tokenization & Real Estate (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Tokenization** converts the rights to a real-world asset into a blockchain-based digital token. Each token represents a fractional share of ownership.

**Role in Real Estate:**

- A property worth ₹10 crores can be divided into 100,000 tokens of ₹1,000 each, lowering the entry barrier.
- Tokens can be traded on secondary markets 24/7, eliminating the need for traditional intermediaries like brokers or escrow agents.
- Smart contracts automate **rent distribution** proportional to token holdings.
- Compliance features (e.g., KYC/AML for accredited investors) can be enforced at the smart-contract level.

**Real-world example:** *Propy* is a blockchain-based real estate platform that has facilitated cross-border property transactions using on-chain title transfers.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Dimension | DeFi | Real Estate Tokenization |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset Type** | Native crypto / synthetic | Physical property rights |
| **Regulation** | Evolving, mostly unregulated | Highly regulated (RERA, SEC) |
| **Topology** | Permissionless | Often hybrid / permissioned |
| **Liquidity** | Very high | Improving rapidly |
| **Examples** | Uniswap, Aave, Compound | Propy, RealT, Brickblock |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Blockchain Applications Questions:**
>
> 1. **Vague definitions** — Writing "blockchain is a chain of blocks" without mentioning **decentralization, immutability, and cryptographic security** costs marks.
> 2. **No architecture depth** — Naming an application (e.g., "Bitcoin") without explaining the *consensus mechanism*, *data model*, and *permission model* loses at least 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Missing diagrams** — KTU examiners explicitly require "neat diagrams" for 14-mark questions. Omitting the diagram forfeits up to 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing permissioned with private** — Private blockchain ≠ Permissioned blockchain. A permissioned blockchain can be either private (single organization) or consortium (multiple organizations).
> 5. **Forgetting scalability trilemma** — When comparing blockchain applications, failure to discuss the trade-off between decentralization, security, and scalability is a recurring error.
> 6. **Not linking properties to applications** — Always explicitly state which blockchain property solves which problem. Example: "Immutability ensures tamper-proof audit trails in supply chain."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Applications in Blockchain":**
>
> - **Core Properties:** Decentralization, Immutability, Transparency, Programmability, Trustlessness.
> - **Main Categories:** Financial (Cryptocurrencies, DeFi, CBDC, Remittances) and Non-Financial (Supply Chain, Healthcare, Identity, Voting, IoT, IP, Real Estate).
> - **Permissionless vs. Permissioned:** Public/open (Bitcoin, Ethereum) vs. private/consortium (Hyperledger, Corda).
> - **Consensus Mechanisms:** PoW, PoS, DPoS, PBFT — match to application requirements.
> - **Smart Contracts:** Self-executing code; backbone of DeFi and NFTs.
> - **Tokenization:** Converts real-world assets to digital tokens; enables fractional ownership.
> - **Scalability Trilemma:** Cannot simultaneously optimize decentralization, security, and scalability.
> - **Key Formulas:** Merkle Root construction, mining probability $P = H_i / H_{\text{total}}$, block reward = subsidy + fees.
> - **Real-World Examples:** IBM Food Trust, MedRec, Uniswap, Propy, KodakONE.
> - **Privacy Tools:** Zero-knowledge proofs, ring signatures, stealth addresses.
> - **Regulation Touchpoints:** HIPAA (healthcare), RERA (real estate), KYC/AML (finance).
> - **Diagrams to Master:** Application taxonomy tree, layered blockchain architecture, supply chain sequence diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decentralisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decentralisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — DECENTRALISATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *Decentralisation refers to the relocation of authority, control, and decision-making from a central authority (individual, organisation, or geographic location) to a distributed network of participating nodes. In blockchain, it is the property by which no single entity controls the ledger, the consensus, or the validation of transactions.*

**Formal Definition (KTU Board Exam Standard Answer):**

> **Decentralisation** is an architectural and governance paradigm in which the operations, data storage, validation, and consensus of a system are spread across a peer-to-peer network of independent nodes, such that the failure, malicious behaviour, or censorship by any single node (or a small cartel of nodes) does not compromise the integrity, availability, or immutability of the system as a whole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Foundational Pillars of Decentralisation (as adopted by KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Architectural Decentralisation** — *How many physical machines is the system running on?*
> 2. **Political / Administrative Decentralisation** — *How many individuals or organisations control those machines?*
> 3. **Logical Decentralisation** — *Does the system behave as a single unified monolith, or as a swarm of independent actors?*

---

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

| Analogy | Centralised | Decentralised |
|---|---|---|
| 📚 **Encyclopedia** | *Britannica* — One editorial board decides every article | **Wikipedia** — Thousands of editors co-author with no CEO of truth |
| 🏦 **Banking** | A bank ledger controlled by a single authority (RBI-controlled bank) | **Blockchain ledger** — Replicated across thousands of nodes worldwide |
| 📢 **Communication** | A TV broadcaster pushing one signal | **BitTorrent** — Every user is both a consumer and a server |
| 🗳️ **Voting** | A company CEO casting the final vote | **DAO governance** — Token holders vote via smart contracts |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Centralised = "Ask the Manager." Decentralised = "Ask the crowd, and trust the majority that follows the rules."*

**Why does Blockchain NEED Decentralisation? (KTU Favourite Line)**

- To eliminate the **single point of failure** present in centralised systems.
- To achieve **censorship resistance** — no authority can block a valid transaction.
- To establish **trustlessness** — users do not need to trust each other or a third party, only the cryptographic protocol and consensus rules.
- To enable **permissionless participation** — anyone with an internet connection can join, validate, and transact.

---

### ⚙️ Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Metric / Constant | Value / Meaning | Relevance to KTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) Threshold** | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | Minimum honest nodes to reach consensus with $f$ faulty nodes |
| **51% Attack Threshold** | Attacker must control $> 50\%$ of total network hash-rate/stake | Defines the "majority trust" boundary |
| **Nakamoto Consensus Block Time (Bitcoin)** | **~10 minutes** | Trade-off between decentralisation and throughput |
| **Block Size Limit (Bitcoin Core)** | **~1 MB** (SegWit effective: ~4 MB) | Decentralisation vs scalability trade-off |
| **Ethereum Block Time** | **~12 seconds** | Faster decentralised finality |
| **Practical Full Node Requirement** | ~600 GB disk (Bitcoin, 2024) | Decentralisation barrier due to resource cost |
| **Six Confirmations Rule (Bitcoin)** | **6 blocks × 10 min = 60 min** | Probabilistic finality against forks |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Layers of Decentralisation (Vitalik Buterin's Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Most-Favoured Theory Question:** *"Compare and contrast the three types of decentralisation with examples."*

| Type | Question it Answers | Fully Centralised Example | Fully Decentralised Example | Blockchain Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architectural** | *How many physical machines?* | One server in a data centre | Millions of nodes on BitTorrent | **Architecturally decentralised** (Bitcoin: ~15,000+ full nodes) |
| **Political** | *How many humans/entities control the machines?* | One CEO / one government | No owner, open-source community | **Politically decentralised** (no CEO of Bitcoin) |
| **Logical** | *Does the system look like one thing or many?* | One monolithic database | A swarm with no clear centre | **Logically centralised** ⚠️ (blockchain has *one* canonical chain) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** *A blockchain is **architecturally decentralised** and **politically decentralised** but **logically centralised** — there is exactly one "current state" of the ledger that all honest nodes agree on.*

---

### 2.2 Centralised vs Decentralised vs Distributed — The Definitive Comparison

| Property | **Centralised** | **Distributed** | **Decentralised** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Authorities | 1 | Many cooperating | Many independent |
| Coordination | Master-slave | Cluster / master-worker | Peer-to-peer (P2P) |
| Failure Mode | Single point of failure | Cluster survives | No single point of failure |
| Trust Model | Trust the centre | Trust the cluster | Trust the protocol |
| Data Location | One location | Replicated across cluster | Replicated across the world |
| Control | One entity | One administrative domain | No single entity |
| Example | Bank, Facebook | Google Search backend | **Bitcoin, Ethereum, IPFS** |
| Consensus | Dictated by centre | Raft / Paxos (leader-based) | **PoW, PoS, PBFT** |
| Bottleneck | The centre | The leader | Network latency / propagation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** "Distributed" and "Decentralised" are **NOT synonyms**. A *distributed* system can still have a central coordinator (e.g., Hadoop's NameNode). A *decentralised* system has no coordinator whatsoever.

---

### 2.3 Properties of a Fully Decentralised System

A decentralised system must satisfy the following **KTU High-Yield Properties**:

1. **Open Membership / Permissionless** — Anyone can join or leave the network without approval.
2. **Sybil Resistance** — No single user can fake millions of identities (solved by PoW cost or PoS stake).
3. **Consensus Without a Leader** — Agreement is reached via algorithms (Nakamoto, PBFT, etc.) not a master.
4. **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)** — The system reaches agreement even when some nodes are malicious or faulty.
5. **Censorship Resistance** — A valid transaction, once broadcast, cannot be blocked by any single party.
6. **Self-Sovereign Identity** — Users own their keys; no central KYC is required.
7. **Data Immutability** — Past records cannot be retroactively altered without majority collusion.
8. **Liveness + Safety** — *(a)* honest transactions eventually get included *(Safety)*; *(b)* the chain never forks permanently *(Liveness)*.

---

### 2.4 The Decentralisation Trilemma (Scalability Trilemma)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vitalik Buterin's Blockchain Trilemma (KTU Hot Topic):**
> *A decentralised blockchain can achieve at most **two of the three** properties strongly:*

$$
\text{Decentralisation} + \text{Security} + \text{Scalability} \;\leq\; 2 \text{ (strongly achieved)}
$$

| Trade-off Combination | Example Protocol | Sacrifices |
|---|---|---|
| Decentralised + Secure | **Bitcoin** | Scalability (low TPS) |
| Scalability + Secure | **Solana** | Decentralisation (high node cost) |
| Decentralised + Scalable | *(Research-stage sharding)* | Often security (cross-shard attacks) |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> *Use $\vert$ in math mode instead of literal `|` to avoid Markdown table breakage.*

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Variables | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **BFT Safe Consensus** | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | $n$=total nodes, $f$=faulty nodes | Min. honest nodes required to reach agreement |
| 2 | **Quorum Size (BFT)** | $q = 2f + 1$ | $q$=quorum | Decisions need $\geq 2f+1$ votes |
| 3 | **51% Attack Probability** | $P_{\text{attack}} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} e^{-\lambda} \dfrac{\lambda^k}{k!}$ | $z$=confirmations, $\lambda$=expected blocks | Prob. attacker falls behind by $z$ blocks |
| 4 | **Censorship Cost (PoW)** | $C_{\text{censor}} = h \cdot c_{\text{hash}}$ | $h$=hashes, $c_{\text{hash}}$=cost/hash | Economic cost to censor a block |
| 5 | **Nakamoto Coefficient** | $N = \min \{n_i : \sum n_i > 0.5\}$ | $n_i$=power of $i$-th entity | Min. entities to collude for 51% — higher = more decentralised |
| 6 | **Gini Coefficient (concentration)** | $G = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_i - x_j \vert}{2 n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}$ | $x_i$=resource share | $G=0$ perfect equality; $G=1$ total centralisation |
| 7 | **TPS (Throughput)** | $\text{TPS} = \dfrac{\text{Block Size (bytes)}}{\text{Block Time (s)} \times \text{Tx Size (bytes)}}$ | — | Defines scalability ceiling |
| 8 | **Six-Confirmation Rule** | $t_{\text{finality}} = 6 \times t_{\text{block}}$ | $t_{\text{block}}$=block time | Standard for probabilistic finality |
| 9 | **Sharding Security Drop** | $S_{\text{shard}} = S_{\text{total}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k}$ | $k$=shard count | Security of each shard is reduced $k$-fold |
| 10 | **Network Latency Bound** | $t_{\text{propagation}} \ll t_{\text{block}}$ | — | Forks happen if propagation $\geq$ block time |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Decentralisation |
|---|---|
| 💰 **Finance** | Cryptocurrencies (BTC, ETH), DeFi protocols, cross-border payments without SWIFT |
| 🏥 **Healthcare** | Patient records shared across hospitals without a single vulnerable EMR server |
| 🌐 **DNS** | Handshake, Unstoppable Domains — no ICANN single-point-of-censorship |
| 📦 **Supply Chain** | Provenance of goods tracked on a shared, tamper-proof ledger |
| 🗳️ **E-Governance** | Transparent, auditable voting (e.g., Estonia's e-residency pilots) |
| 🎨 **Digital Identity** | Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) — user controls their own credentials |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Byzantine Fault Tolerance Threshold ($n \geq 3f + 1$)

> **Engineering Problem (Classic KTU):**
> *Prove that in a fully decentralised consensus system, the number of total nodes $n$ must satisfy $n \geq 3f + 1$ to tolerate $f$ Byzantine (malicious) nodes and still reach agreement.*

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

**Step 1 — Define the parties.**
- Total nodes: $n$
- Honest nodes: $h = n - f$
- The system must reach agreement even if $f$ nodes behave arbitrarily (Byzantine).

**Step 2 — The two sub-quorums must intersect.**
- For safety, every honest node's quorum must contain at least one other honest node it can compare notes with.
- In BFT, a quorum is of size $q$.
- Two quorums (one held by an honest commander, one by a recipient) must overlap in at least $f+1$ honest nodes, because up to $f$ of the overlapping nodes may be Byzantine.

**Step 3 — Express the quorum intersection condition.**
- Two quorums of size $q$ in a system of $n$ nodes satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q + q &> n + f \\
2q &> n + f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the minimum quorum size.**
- The smallest integer satisfying $2q > n + f$ is $q = \lfloor \frac{n+f}{2} \rfloor + 1$.
- For safety, every honest node must also have a *majority* of the quorum honest, so $q \geq f + 1$ additional honest nodes beyond the $f$ possible faulty ones:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &\geq f + 1 \quad \text{(honest majority inside the quorum)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine and derive the bound.**
- Combining the two conditions with the worst-case, we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - f &\geq q \quad \text{(total honest must form a quorum)} \\
n - f &\geq 2f + 1 \\
\therefore \quad n &\geq 3f + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**
- If $f = 1$: $n \geq 4$ (4 nodes tolerate 1 traitor — the classic *Byzantine Generals* problem).
- If $f = 3$: $n \geq 10$ (10 nodes tolerate 3 traitors, e.g., Cosmos Hub).
- If $f = 33$: $n \geq 100$ (100-node network can tolerate 33 traitors).

> [!TIP]
> **Final formula to memorise:** $n \geq 3f + 1$ — *“Total nodes must be at least three times the number of traitors plus one.”*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Probabilistic Finality & 51% Attack Probability

> **Engineering Problem (KTU Numerical):**
> *An attacker controls fraction $q$ of the total network hash-rate. What is the probability that the attacker catches up from $z$ blocks behind?*

**Step 1 — Set up the Poisson race.**
- The honest network finds blocks at rate $\lambda_h = (1-q) \cdot \lambda$.
- The attacker finds blocks at rate $\lambda_a = q \cdot \lambda$.
- Both processes are Poisson and memoryless.

**Step 2 — Formulate the attacker's win probability.**
- The attacker starts $z$ blocks behind. The attacker wins if, before the honest network closes the gap to 0, the attacker's chain overtakes it.
- This is a *negative binomial / Poisson race* problem. The well-known closed-form (from Satoshi Nakamoto's 2008 whitepaper) is:

$$
P_{\text{attack}}(q, z) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \sum_{k=0}^{z} \left[ \dfrac{\lambda_a^{\,k}\; e^{-\lambda_a}}{k!} \right] \cdot \left[ 1 \;-\; \left( \dfrac{q}{1-q} \right)^{z-k} \right]_{+}
$$

where $[x]_+ = \max(0, x)$.

**Step 3 — Simplify for the common case $q < 0.5$.**
- For $q < 0.5$, the attacker's probability decays **exponentially** with $z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{attack}}(q, z) &\approx \left( \dfrac{q}{1-q} \right)^{z+1} \quad \text{when } q \ll 0.5 \\
\therefore \log P_{\text{attack}} &= (z+1) \log \left( \dfrac{q}{1-q} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Six-Confirmation Rule.**
- For $q = 0.10$ and $z = 6$:

$$
P_{\text{attack}}(0.10, 6) \;\approx\; \left( \dfrac{0.10}{0.90} \right)^{7} \;\approx\; 1.78 \times 10^{-7}
$$

- For $q = 0.30$ and $z = 6$:

$$
P_{\text{attack}}(0.30, 6) \;\approx\; \left( \dfrac{0.30}{0.70} \right)^{7} \;\approx\; 0.0026 \;\approx\; 0.26\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** *This is why Bitcoin uses 6 confirmations for large transactions — it makes a 10% attacker economically unviable.*

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nakamoto Coefficient (Measuring Decentralisation)

The **Nakamoto Coefficient** is the minimum number of entities whose combined power exceeds 50% of the network.

**Step 1 — Sort the entities by power.**
- Let entities be $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$ with power shares $p_1 \geq p_2 \geq \ldots \geq p_n$ (sorted in descending order).

**Step 2 — Cumulative sum until threshold.**

$$
S(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Nakamoto Coefficient.**

$$
N = \min \left\{ k \;:\; S(k) > 0.5 \right\}
$$

**Step 4 — Example (illustrative).**
- Suppose 4 mining pools have shares: $25\%,\; 24\%,\; 23\%,\; 28\%$.
- Sorted: $28\%,\; 25\%,\; 24\%,\; 23\%$.
- $S(1) = 0.28$ (not $> 0.5$).
- $S(2) = 0.53$ ($> 0.5$).
- $\therefore N = 2$ — only **2 pools** could collude to attack the network → **less decentralised**.

> [!TIP]
> **Higher $N$ = more decentralised.** Bitcoin's Nakamoto Coefficient is ~4 (4 mining pools); ideally it should be in the dozens.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Centralisation Happens, Why Decentralisation Wins

| **Why Systems Become Centralised** | **Why Decentralisation Wins Anyway** |
|---|---|
| Economies of scale (one big data centre is cheaper) | No single point of failure / DoS target |
| Easier coordination (one CEO decides fast) | Censorship resistance |
| Simpler auditing (one ledger to inspect) | Trust minimisation — no need to trust a human |
| Faster throughput (no consensus overhead) | Permissionless innovation |
| Natural monopoly effects (network effects) | Aligned incentives via token economics |
| Regulatory capture (incumbents lobby for rules that favour them) | Global, 24×7, no business hours |

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Flow of a Transaction in a Decentralised Blockchain Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User signs transaction with private key]
    B[Broadcast transaction to P2P network]
    C{Each node independently verifies signature and balance}
    D[Verified transactions enter mempool]
    E[Miner or Validator selects highest fee transactions]
    F[Construct new block of transactions]
    G[Consensus round: PoW mining or PoS voting]
    H{Block accepted by majority of nodes}
    I[Block appended to local copy of chain]
    J[Transaction confirmed]
    K[Block propagated to all peers]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> B
    H -- No --> D
```

> **Reading:** Notice that no single authority approves the transaction. The *network* collectively validates it via the consensus protocol.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning (Module 1):**
> - Do **NOT** confuse *distributed* with *decentralised* — a 2-mark cut is standard for this slip.
> - Always mention **all three types** (architectural, political, logical) when asked to "explain decentralisation" — a 1-mark cut for each missing type.
> - When drawing diagrams, **draw a fully connected P2P mesh**, not a hub-and-spoke — full 5 marks are reserved for the topology diagram.
> - State the **BFT formula $n \geq 3f + 1$** explicitly in numerical questions, even if it seems obvious.

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
*Define decentralisation. Differentiate between logical and architectural decentralisation with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Decentralisation is the distribution of authority, data, and control across a peer-to-peer network of independent nodes, eliminating the need for a central trusted authority.

**Architectural Decentralisation (1 mark):** It refers to how many physical machines constitute the system. *Example:* Bitcoin is architecturally decentralised as it runs on ~15,000+ full nodes worldwide.

**Logical Decentralisation (1 mark):** It refers to whether the system presents itself as a single coherent monolith or as a swarm. *Example:* The Bitcoin blockchain is logically centralised because all honest nodes agree on one canonical chain.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
*State the Byzantine Fault Tolerance condition and explain why $n \geq 3f + 1$ is necessary, not $n \geq 2f + 1$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The BFT condition is $n \geq 3f + 1$, where $n$ is the total number of nodes and $f$ is the number of Byzantine (malicious) nodes.

**Why not $2f + 1$ (1 mark):** In a consensus round, two quorums of size $q$ must overlap in at least $f + 1$ honest nodes to prevent a fork, which mathematically forces $n \geq 3f + 1$.

**Why $3f + 1$ is necessary (1 mark):** When only $2f + 1$ nodes exist and $f$ are Byzantine, the honest nodes are split $f$ vs $f$ (or worse), making it impossible to determine the truth. A 1-extra-node buffer is needed to break the tie, leading to $3f + 1$.

---

### 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Long Answer, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice (A or B). Sub-parts typically carry 7 marks each. Marks are awarded for: **Definition (2M) + Explanation (3M) + Diagram/Example (2M).**

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 marks] *Explain the three types of decentralisation proposed by Vitalik Buterin. With a neat diagram, illustrate how blockchain achieves architectural and political decentralisation but remains logically centralised.*

**(b)** [7 marks] *Discuss the Blockchain Trilemma with reference to decentralisation. Show, with a numerical example, why a system with 20 nodes needs at least 14 honest nodes to tolerate 3 Byzantine failures.*

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Vitalik Buterin (Ethereum co-founder) proposed that decentralisation is not a single property but a three-dimensional concept, defined across architectural, political, and logical axes.

**Architectural Decentralisation (1.5 marks):** It refers to the number of physical computers running the system. Bitcoin runs on tens of thousands of nodes across the globe — fully architecturally decentralised. A traditional bank has a few central servers — fully architecturally centralised.

**Political Decentralisation (1.5 marks):** It refers to the number of humans or organisations controlling the machines. Bitcoin has no CEO, board, or government — it is governed by an open-source developer community and node operators. A bank is politically centralised under RBI and the bank's board.

**Logical Decentralisation (1 mark):** It refers to whether the system's *interface* is a single coherent monolith or an amorphous swarm. Blockchain is *logically centralised* — there is exactly one canonical state of the ledger that all honest nodes converge upon. (For contrast, the traditional internet is logically decentralised — each website is independent.)

**Diagram (2 marks):** A **fully connected P2P mesh** showing 8–10 nodes connected to multiple peers, with each peer replicating the same ledger. *(See Section 4.1, Diagram 3 for the standard representation.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**The Blockchain Trilemma (3 marks):** Vitalik's trilemma states that a blockchain can only strongly achieve **two out of three** properties: Decentralisation, Security, Scalability.

| Choice | Sacrifices | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Decentralised + Secure | Scalability | **Bitcoin** (~7 TPS) |
| Secure + Scalable | Decentralisation | **Solana** (high node cost, fewer validators) |
| Decentralised + Scalable | Security (partial) | Early sharding designs |

The trilemma is fundamental to engineering trade-offs in blockchain design.

**Numerical BFT Example (4 marks):**

Given: total nodes $n = 20$, faulty nodes $f = 3$.

Apply the BFT condition $n \geq 3f + 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3f + 1 &= 3(3) + 1 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $n = 20 \geq 10$, the condition is **satisfied**. The system is fault-tolerant.

Now compute the **minimum honest nodes required**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\min} &= n - f = 20 - 3 = 17 \\
\text{or equivalently,} \quad h_{\min} &= 2f + 1 = 2(3) + 1 = 7 \text{ (within a quorum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a quorum of size $q = 2f + 1 = 7$, the total honest nodes in the system must be:

$$
h_{\min} = 2f + 1 = 7 \text{ honest nodes per quorum}
$$

Therefore, with $n = 20$ nodes, even if 3 are Byzantine, the remaining $20 - 3 = 17$ honest nodes are **well above** the minimum 7 honest-in-quorum requirement, so the network safely reaches consensus.

**Conclusion (0 mark, integrated above):** Decentralisation is preserved while tolerating the stated Byzantine failures.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Internal Choice) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 marks] *Compare and contrast Centralised, Distributed, and Decentralised systems. Give two real-world examples of each. Why is blockchain classified as decentralised and not merely distributed?*

**(b)** [7 marks] *An attacker controls 30% of the total hash-rate of a PoW blockchain with a 10-minute block time. Calculate the probability of a successful double-spend attack after 6 confirmations using the simplified formula $P \approx (q/(1-q))^{z+1}$. Comment on whether 6 confirmations are economically safe.*

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Centralised | Distributed | Decentralised |
|---|---|---|---|
| Authority | One | Few cooperating | Many independent |
| Failure Mode | SPOF | Cluster survives | No SPOF |
| Consensus | Dictated | Leader-based (Raft) | Leaderless (PoW, PoS) |
| Examples | Bank, Facebook | Hadoop, Google Search | Bitcoin, Ethereum |

**Examples (2 marks):**
- Centralised: Reserve Bank of India ledger, Facebook's single data centre.
- Distributed: Google's search index across many data centres.
- Decentralised: Bitcoin, IPFS, BitTorrent.

**Why Blockchain ≠ Just Distributed (1 mark):** A distributed system like Hadoop still has a NameNode (master). Blockchain is decentralised because *no node has a special role* — every full node is an equal peer that independently validates blocks.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $q = 0.30$, $z = 6$ confirmations.

**Apply the simplified double-spend formula:**

$$
P_{\text{attack}} \;\approx\; \left( \dfrac{q}{1-q} \right)^{z+1}
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{attack}} &\approx \left( \dfrac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} \right)^{6+1} \\
&= \left( \dfrac{0.30}{0.70} \right)^{7} \\
&= (0.4286)^{7} \\
&\approx 2.56 \times 10^{-3} \\
&\approx 0.256\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (3 marks):**

- The probability of a successful double-spend after 6 confirmations against a 30% attacker is **~0.26%**.
- However, this is **NOT economically safe** for very high-value transactions.
- A 30% attacker can statistically succeed 1 in every ~390 attempts — still profitable for transactions worth millions.
- **Recommendation:** For high-value transactions against a 30% attacker, use **12–60 confirmations** to drop the probability below $10^{-6}$.
- For a 10% attacker, 6 confirmations give $\approx 1.78 \times 10^{-7}$ — which is **economically safe**.

**Final Time (1 mark):**

$$
t_{\text{finality}} = 6 \times 10 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ minutes}
$$

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- ✅ **Decentralisation = distribution of power, data, and validation across P2P nodes** — no single point of failure.
- ✅ **Three types** (must memorise): **Architectural, Political, Logical** — but blockchain is *logically centralised*.
- ✅ **Distributed ≠ Decentralised** — Hadoop is distributed; Bitcoin is decentralised.
- ✅ **BFT formula (MEMORISE):** $n \geq 3f + 1$.
- ✅ **51% Attack:** Attacker needs $> 50\%$ of hash-rate/stake.
- ✅ **Six-Confirmation Rule:** $P_{\text{attack}} \approx (q/(1-q))^{z+1}$ for $q < 0.5$.
- ✅ **Nakamoto Coefficient** = minimum entities to collude for 51%; higher is better.
- ✅ **Trilemma:** Decentralisation + Security + Scalability — pick two.
- ✅ **Full Node Cost** in 2024 ≈ 600 GB disk for Bitcoin — a *decentralisation barrier*.
- ✅ **Gossip Protocol** propagates transactions/blocks; **Kademlia DHT** handles node discovery.
- ✅ **Three properties a decentralised system must have:** Open membership, Sybil resistance, Leaderless consensus.
- ✅ **Difference between *censorship resistance* and *permissionless*:** Censorship = no one can block; Permissionless = anyone can join.
- ✅ **Logical centralisation** is a *feature*, not a bug — it is what gives the ledger its single source of truth.
- ✅ **Vitalik Buterin's framework** is the most cited reference in KTU exams for decentralisation questions.
- ✅ **Nakamoto Consensus** is the original decentralised consensus (PoW + longest chain rule).
- ✅ **Probabilistic vs Absolute Finality:** Bitcoin = probabilistic (6 conf.); PBFT-based chains = absolute (single block).
- ✅ **TPS formula:** Block Size / (Block Time × Tx Size) — defines scalability ceiling.
- ✅ **Sharding reduces security per shard** by a factor of $k$ (number of shards) — a key trilemma trade-off.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Decentralisation Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECST747)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Ledger Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distributed Ledger Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A **Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** is a digital system for recording the transaction of assets (financial, physical, or digital) in which the transactions and their details are recorded simultaneously in multiple places, across a network of computers (nodes), without a central administrator or central data store. Each node in the network maintains, approves, and updates new entries in a synchronized, cryptographically secure, and immutable manner.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, DLT is the **umbrella technological concept** of which **Blockchain** is a specific implementation. While every Blockchain is a DLT, not every DLT is a Blockchain. The distinction lies in the data structure: Blockchain organizes data into **chronologically linked blocks**, whereas other DLTs may use directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), hashgraphs, or radix trees.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Village Ledger Book Analogy (KTU-favourite):**
> Imagine a village of 500 people where everyone trades goods. In a **traditional centralized system** (like a bank), there is one big ledger kept by the village chief (the central server). If the chief's book burns down or is corrupted, the entire trade history is lost.
>
> In a **Distributed Ledger**, every villager owns a *photocopy* of the same ledger book. When a trade happens (say, Anita gives 2 kg rice to Balu), this transaction is announced to the whole village. Every villager verifies the transaction and updates their own copy. Now, even if 300 villagers lose their books, the trade record survives. To tamper with a record, a malicious actor would need to alter **thousands of copies simultaneously** — practically impossible.

### Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

| Term | Symbol / Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Node | $N$ | A single participant computer in the DLT network |
| Consensus | $\mathcal{C}$ | Protocol agreement rule used to validate new entries |
| Block Time | $T_b$ (seconds) | Average time to produce a new validated block |
| Throughput | $\lambda$ (TPS) | Transactions Per Second the ledger can process |
| Latency | $\tau$ (seconds) | Time from transaction broadcast to final confirmation |
| Fork Tolerance | $f$ | Number of faulty/malicious nodes the system can survive |

### Centralized vs. Distributed vs. Decentralized

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction (frequently asked 3-mark question):**
>
> 1. **Centralized Ledger** → Single owner/administrator (e.g., a bank's MySQL database). Single point of failure.
> 2. **Distributed Ledger** → Multiple copies held across nodes, but a governing body may still exist (e.g., permissioned Hyperledger Fabric). No single point of failure.
> 3. **Decentralized Ledger** → No central authority. All nodes are equal peers (e.g., public Bitcoin/Ethereum). Trust is established purely through consensus and cryptography.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of DLT Architecture

Every DLT system, irrespective of whether it is blockchain-based or DAG-based, rests on three fundamental pillars. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate and explain these:

**Pillar 1 — Cryptographic Security**
- Each transaction is digitally signed using **asymmetric cryptography** (public-private key pairs).
- The user signs with their **private key** $sk$, and anyone can verify using the corresponding **public key** $pk$.
- Hashing (e.g., **SHA-256**, producing a 256-bit output) ensures that any tampering of historical data is immediately detectable.

**Pillar 2 — Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Network**
- Each node is a peer; no master-slave architecture exists.
- New transactions are broadcast via a **gossip protocol**, where each randomly chosen node forwards the data to a few neighbours until the entire network is saturated.

**Pillar 3 — Consensus Mechanism**
- This is the *rulebook* the network follows to agree on the next valid state.
- Common consensus algorithms include **Proof of Work (PoW)**, **Proof of Stake (PoS)**, **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)**, and **Raft**.

### 2.2 CAP Theorem (KTU-Favourite Theoretical Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 1998):**
> In any distributed data store, only **two of the following three guarantees** can be achieved simultaneously:
>
> - **C**onsistency — Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
> - **A**vailability — Every request receives a non-error response (without guarantee that it contains the most recent write).
> - **P**artition tolerance — The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

Since network partitions are **inevitable in real distributed systems**, DLTs must choose between CP and AP. Most public blockchains (e.g., Bitcoin) prioritize **AP** (eventual consistency), while permissioned DLTs often prefer **CP** (strong consistency).

### 2.3 Byzantine Generals' Problem (Foundational Theorem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lamport, Shostak, Pease (1982) — Byzantine Generals' Problem:**
> Several divisions of the Byzantine army surround a city. They can only win if they attack *simultaneously* via messengers. Some generals may be **traitors** (faulty/malicious). The loyal generals must reach agreement on a common plan *despite* the traitors.
>
> - If there are $n$ total generals and $f$ traitors, the system reaches consensus iff:
>
> $$n \geq 3f + 1$$
>
> This is the **fundamental threshold** for Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) DLTs. For $f=1$ traitor, we need at least $4$ generals; for $f=3$ traitors, at least $10$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Application / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| Hash output length (SHA-256) | $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^{256}$ | Avalanche effect guarantee |
| Digital signature verification | $\text{Verify}(pk, m, \sigma) \to \{0,1\}$ | Transaction authenticity proof |
| Byzantine fault threshold | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | Minimum honest-node requirement for BFT |
| Block reward (Bitcoin) | $R = R_0 \cdot \frac{1}{2}^{\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ | Halving schedule |
| Transaction throughput (avg) | $\lambda = \frac{1}{T_b}$ | TPS calculation for any DLT |
| Nakamoto consensus security | $\text{Safe if } \alpha > \frac{1}{2}$ | $\alpha$ = honest hashing power fraction |
| CAP Trade-off | $\text{Cap}(S) = (C \lor A) \land P$ | System design choice |

> **Important:** In the table above, I used `\geq` and `\to` LaTeX inside the markdown to prevent the pipe `|` character from breaking the KTU renderer — this is a strict formatting safeguard for KTU PDF exports.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of DLT

- **Cross-border Payments** — Ripple (XRP) and Stellar use DLT to settle international remittances in under 5 seconds.
- **Supply Chain** — IBM Food Trust (Hyperledger) tracks mangoes, coffee, and pharmaceuticals from farm to shelf.
- **Healthcare** — MedRec (MIT) stores patient records on a permissioned DLT so patients control access.
- **Digital Identity** — Self-sovereign identity (SSI) systems like Sovrin use DLT to give citizens ownership of their credentials.
- **Smart Contracts** — Ethereum's EVM uses DLT as a "world computer" to execute deterministic code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Byzantine Fault Tolerance Threshold

We are given $n$ generals, of which $f$ are traitors (faulty) and the remaining $(n - f)$ are loyal (honest). We want the minimum $n$ such that the loyal generals can still reach agreement.

**Step 1 — Define the scenario.**
The loyal generals must agree on a single battle plan (Attack or Retreat). Each general sends a message to every other general reporting what the commander ordered.

**Step 2 — Traitor's effect.**
A traitor may send conflicting messages to different generals. So the $(n - f)$ loyal generals receive $n - 1$ messages, of which up to $f$ may be lies.

**Step 3 — Information from loyal generals.**
Among the $n - 1$ senders, the loyal generals are exactly $(n - 1 - f)$ in number. The loyal general's message is correct.

**Step 4 — Majority requirement.**
The loyal general must outvote the traitors. For majority to hold:

$$n - 1 - f > f$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$n - 1 > 2f$$

$$n > 2f + 1$$

**Step 6 — Converting to strict inequality (integer requirement).**
Since $n$ and $f$ are integers, the strict inequality $n > 2f + 1$ is equivalent to:

$$n \geq 2f + 2 = 3f + 1 \quad \text{(for f ≥ 1)}$$

**Step 7 — Final condition.**

$$\boxed{n \geq 3f + 1}$$

This is the **fundamental Byzantine fault tolerance inequality** that any BFT-based DLT (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric, Cosmos Tendermint) must satisfy. For example, with $f = 3$ traitors, the network must have at least:

$$n_{\min} = 3(3) + 1 = 10 \text{ nodes}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Ledger Architectures

| Property | Centralized Ledger | Distributed Ledger (Permissioned) | Decentralized Ledger (Public) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Authority** | Single admin | Consortium / Pre-approved | No authority |
| **Trust model** | Trust the admin | Trust the consortium | Trust the code (cryptography + consensus) |
| **Throughput (TPS)** | **High (1,000–100,000)** | High (1,000–10,000) | Low (Bitcoin: ~7, Ethereum: ~30) |
| **Latency** | Milliseconds | Sub-second | Seconds to minutes (finality) |
| **Energy cost** | Low | Low | **Very high (PoW)** |
| **Immutability** | Mutable by admin | Semi-immutable | Truly immutable |
| **Examples** | Bank SQL DB | Hyperledger Fabric | Bitcoin, Ethereum |
| **Consensus** | None (admin decides) | PBFT, Raft, Istanbul BFT | PoW, PoS, etc. |
| **CAP choice** | N/A | Usually CP | Usually AP |
| **KTU exam weightage** | 1 mark | 2 marks | 7 marks |

### 3.3 Derivation: Probability of a 51% Attack

Let $\alpha$ be the fraction of total hashing power controlled by honest miners. The probability that an honest block is found first in a race is $\alpha$, and the attacker's success probability is $(1 - \alpha) = q$.

For an attacker to forge a chain of $z$ blocks, the probability is given by the **Poisson distribution** approximation used in Satoshi Nakamoto's whitepaper:

$$P(\text{success}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{q}{p} \right)^{z-k} \right]$$

where $p = \alpha$ (honest power), $q = 1 - \alpha$ (attacker power), and $\lambda = z \cdot \frac{q}{p}$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $p = \alpha$, $q = 1 - \alpha$.**

$$P(\text{success}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{1 - \alpha}{\alpha} \right)^{z-k} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.** As $z$ (the number of confirmation blocks) increases, this probability drops **exponentially**. This is why exchanges wait for **6 confirmations** before crediting a Bitcoin deposit.

**Step 3 — Threshold.** When $\alpha = 0.5$ (50% honest power), the attacker's success probability is non-zero and the system becomes insecure — hence the name "**51% attack**."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Centralized vs. Distributed vs. Decentralized Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Centralized: All clients connect to a single central server database]
    B[Distributed: Multiple interconnected replicated nodes share the ledger]
    C[Decentralized: Peer to peer network with no central authority]
    D[Client 1] --> A
    E[Client 2] --> A
    F[Client 3] --> A
    A -.->|Replicated copies| G[Node 1]
    A -.->|Replicated copies| H[Node 2]
    A -.->|Replicated copies| I[Node 3]
    G <-->|Gossip Protocol| H
    H <-->|Gossip Protocol| I
    G <-->|Direct Peer Link| I
```

### 4.2 DLT Functional Architecture Flow (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        U1[User Wallet A]
        U2[User Wallet B]
    end
    subgraph NETWORK_LAYER
        N1[Peer Node 1]
        N2[Peer Node 2]
        N3[Peer Node 3]
        N4[Peer Node N]
    end
    subgraph CONSENSUS_LAYER
        CON1[Validator Pool]
        CON2[Consensus Engine: PoW PoS PBFT]
    end
    subgraph DATA_LAYER
        D1[Cryptographic Hash Chain]
        D2[Merkle Tree of Transactions]
        D3[Immutable State Database]
    end
    U1 -->|Signed TX| N1
    U2 -->|Signed TX| N2
    N1 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    N1 --> N4
    N2 --> N3
    N2 --> N4
    N3 --> N4
    N1 --> CON1
    N2 --> CON1
    N3 --> CON1
    N4 --> CON1
    CON1 --> CON2
    CON2 -->|Valid TX set| D2
    D2 --> D1
    D1 --> D3
```

### 4.3 DLT Transaction Lifecycle Flowchart

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as User Wallet
    participant Net as P2P Network
    participant Val as Validator Node
    participant Con as Consensus Engine
    participant Led as Distributed Ledger
    User->>Net: Broadcast Signed Transaction
    Net->>Val: Gossip Propagation to Validators
    Val->>Val: Verify Digital Signature and Balance
    Val->>Con: Submit Validated TX to Consensus
    Con->>Con: Run PoW or PoS or PBFT Agreement
    Con->>Led: Append to Block and Commit to Chain
    Led-->>Net: Replicate Updated Block to All Peers
    Net-->>User: Confirm Transaction Receipt
```

### 4.4 CAP Theorem Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
graph LR
    C[Consistency] --- A[Availability]
    A --- P[Partition Tolerance]
    C --- P
    C -->|Cannot coexist fully| AP[Public Blockchains]
    A -->|Cannot coexist fully| CP[Permissioned DLT]
    P -->|Mandatory| ALL[Every Real Network has Partitions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every real DLT must choose either **CP** (Hyperledger) or **AP** (Bitcoin/Ethereum). Pure **CA** is impossible in a distributed environment because partitions *will* eventually happen.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse *Distributed Ledger* with *Blockchain*. A Blockchain **is** a DLT, but a DLT is not necessarily a Blockchain. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you misuse these terms interchangeably.
> 2. In Byzantine Fault Tolerance questions, students often write $n \geq 2f + 1$ — this is the bound for **non-Byzantine** crash faults only. The correct bound for BFT is $n \geq 3f + 1$.
> 3. For CAP theorem, many students write "we can have all three" — **strictly wrong**. Only two of the three are achievable in the presence of a network partition.
> 4. When listing advantages of DLT, do not forget to mention **immutability** and **transparency** — these are KTU-marker words.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Distributed Ledger Technology. List any four key characteristics of DLT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT) is a digital system that records asset transactions simultaneously across multiple nodes (computers) in a peer-to-peer network, eliminating the need for a central administrator. Each node maintains an identical copy of the ledger, and updates are validated through a consensus mechanism and secured using cryptographic techniques.

**Four key characteristics:**

1. **Decentralization** — No single point of control or failure; data is replicated across all participants.
2. **Immutability** — Once a transaction is recorded and confirmed by consensus, it cannot be altered or deleted without detection, as any change would invalidate the cryptographic hashes.
3. **Transparency** — All participants with permission can view the entire transaction history, ensuring auditability and trust.
4. **Consensus-driven** — Every new entry is validated by the network participants following a pre-agreed consensus algorithm (e.g., PoW, PoS, PBFT).

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four characteristics, half-mark each.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the Byzantine Generals' Problem. What is the minimum number of nodes required to tolerate 3 faulty nodes in a BFT system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Byzantine Generals' Problem**, formalized by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease in 1982, describes a situation in distributed computing where multiple participants (generals) must reach a unanimous agreement on a single course of action (attack or retreat), but some participants may be faulty or malicious (traitors) and broadcast conflicting or false information. The challenge is for the loyal participants to guarantee consensus despite the presence of traitors.

For a BFT system to reach consensus, the minimum number of nodes required is given by:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

For $f = 3$ faulty nodes:

$$n_{\min} = 3(3) + 1 = \mathbf{10 \text{ nodes}}$$

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for explanation + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for calculation.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare centralized, distributed, and decentralized ledgers with a neat comparison diagram. Explain the CAP theorem and its implications for DLT design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the various types of Distributed Ledger Technologies. Differentiate between public and permissioned DLTs with examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Centralized | Distributed | Decentralized |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control | Single authority | Multiple authorities (consortium) | No authority |
| Node count | 1 (with backups) | Limited, known | Unlimited, open |
| Trust | In authority | In consortium | In code/consensus |
| Example | Bank DB | Hyperledger | Bitcoin, Ethereum |
| Failure tolerance | Low | High | Highest |

**Centralized vs Distributed vs Decentralized Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    C[Centralized Single Hub Model]
    D[Distributed Multi Replica Model]
    DE[Decentralized P2P Mesh Model]
    C --> C1[Server at center] --> C2[Clients 1, 2, 3]
    D --> D1[Nodes 1, 2, 3, 4] --> D2[Connected to all]
    DE --> DE1[All peers equal] --> DE2[No central node]
```

**CAP Theorem (2 marks):** The CAP theorem, proposed by Eric Brewer, states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most two of the following three guarantees: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition tolerance (P)**. Since network partitions (P) are inevitable in real distributed systems, DLTs must choose between CP and AP. Public blockchains (Bitcoin, Ethereum) favor **AP** (eventual consistency), while permissioned DLTs like Hyperledger Fabric favor **CP** (strong consistency, no forks).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Types of DLTs (3 marks):**

1. **Blockchain** — Data grouped into cryptographically linked blocks. Examples: Bitcoin, Ethereum.
2. **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — Transactions linked directly as a graph, no blocks. Examples: IOTA Tangle, Nano.
3. **Hashgraph** — Uses gossip-about-gossip and virtual voting. Example: Hedera Hashgraph.
4. **Holochain** — Agent-centric; each user maintains their own chain. Example: Holo.
5. **Tempo (Radix)** — Uses a sharded ledger with logical clocks. Example: Radix.

**Public vs Permissioned DLT Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | Public DLT | Permissioned DLT |
|---|---|---|
| Access | Open to anyone | Restricted, vetted members |
| Identity | Pseudonymous | Verified KYC identity |
| Consensus | PoW, PoS (open) | PBFT, Raft (closed) |
| Throughput | Low (7–1000 TPS) | High (1000–10000 TPS) |
| Immutability | Full (cryptographic) | Semi (admin can override) |
| Examples | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric, Corda |
| Use case | Cryptocurrency, public dApps | Enterprise, supply chain, banking |
| Energy | High (PoW) | Low |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Distributed Ledger Technology in detail. Describe the role of cryptography, peer-to-peer networking, and consensus in DLT.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the advantages and limitations of DLT. Explain the Byzantine fault tolerance model with the proof of the $n \geq 3f + 1$ bound.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A DLT architecture is composed of **five inter-locking layers**, each addressing a specific engineering problem:

1. **Application Layer (1 mark)** — End-user interfaces (wallets, dApps, web portals) that initiate transactions and read ledger state.

2. **Network / Propagation Layer (1 mark)** — A peer-to-peer overlay where nodes use a **gossip protocol** to broadcast transactions and blocks. Each node has 8–125 peers on average, and the message reaches the entire network within O(log N) hops.

3. **Consensus Layer (2 marks)** — The rulebook for agreeing on the next valid state.
   - **PoW**: Miners solve a SHA-256 puzzle; first to find the nonce wins the block.
   - **PoS**: Validators stake tokens; selected proportionally to stake size.
   - **PBFT**: Pre-prepare → Prepare → Commit voting rounds among known validators.
   - This layer prevents double-spending and Sybil attacks.

4. **Data / Storage Layer (1 mark)** — Transactions are organized using a **Merkle Tree** so that any transaction's integrity can be verified in $O(\log n)$ time. Blocks are linked via previous block hashes, forming an immutable chain.

5. **Cryptography / Security Layer (2 marks)** — All transactions are signed using **ECDSA** (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm) with a private key. Hashing uses **SHA-256** or **Keccak-256**. Public-key infrastructure (PKI) ensures non-repudiation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Advantages of DLT (3 marks):**
1. **Trust minimization** — Parties transact without needing mutual trust or a central intermediary.
2. **Transparency & auditability** — All authorized participants can verify the full history.
3. **Resilience** — No single point of failure; system survives loss of many nodes.
4. **Reduced reconciliation cost** — Single shared source of truth eliminates cross-system reconciliation overhead.
5. **Programmability** — Smart contracts enable self-executing business logic.

**Limitations of DLT (2 marks):**
1. **Scalability** — Public DLTs have low TPS due to consensus overhead.
2. **Energy consumption** — PoW-based DLTs like Bitcoin consume massive electricity.
3. **Regulatory uncertainty** — Lack of global legal frameworks.
4. **Storage bloat** — Full nodes must store the entire ledger history.
5. **51% attack risk** — When one entity gains majority hashing power.

**Byzantine Fault Tolerance Proof (2 marks):**
Given $n$ generals and $f$ traitors, the loyal generals receive $n - 1$ messages, of which up to $f$ may be lies. The number of honest senders is $n - 1 - f$. For the loyal generals' majority to hold:

$$n - 1 - f > f \implies n > 2f + 1 \implies n \geq 3f + 1$$

Hence, for $f = 1$, at least 4 nodes are needed; for $f = 3$, at least 10 nodes are required.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ DLT = Decentralized, distributed, replicated digital ledger without a central administrator.
- ✅ Blockchain ⊂ DLT (Blockchain is a *type* of DLT using blocks; DLT is the broader category).
- ✅ Three pillars: **Cryptography, P2P network, Consensus**.
- ✅ CAP Theorem → only **two of three** guarantees possible; real systems must choose **CP or AP**.
- ✅ Byzantine Generals' threshold → $n \geq 3f + 1$ (commonly mistyped as $2f+1$ — avoid this trap).
- ✅ Centralized = 1 admin, Distributed = consortium, Decentralized = no authority.
- ✅ Hashing: **SHA-256** → 256-bit fixed-size output with avalanche property.
- ✅ Consensus: **PoW (high security, low TPS)**, **PoS (energy-efficient)**, **PBFT (fast, permissioned)**.
- ✅ Digital signature uses **ECDSA**; signing with private key, verification with public key.
- ✅ 51% attack possible when honest hashing power $\alpha \leq 0.5$.
- ✅ Public DLTs (Bitcoin, Ethereum) prioritize **AP**; Permissioned DLTs (Hyperledger) prioritize **CP**.
- ✅ Common KTU keywords: *immutability, transparency, decentralization, trust minimization, single source of truth, cryptographic chaining, Merkle tree, gossip protocol, fault tolerance*.
- ✅ For numerical problems: always show the formula, substitution, and final boxed answer.
- ✅ Mention at least **two real-world use cases** (e.g., supply chain, cross-border payments) for full marks on essay-type questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blockchain Variants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Blockchain Variants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Blockchain Variants — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST747)** and distributed ledger technology, **Blockchain Variants** refer to the different architectural classifications of blockchain networks that vary based on **access control**, **consensus participation**, **read/write permissions**, **decentralization degree**, and **trust assumptions**.

According to the standard taxonomy adopted in KTU Module 1, blockchain networks are broadly classified into four major variants:

1. **Public Blockchain (Permissionless)**
2. **Private Blockchain (Permissioned)**
3. **Consortium Blockchain (Federated / Semi-Private)**
4. **Hybrid Blockchain**

Additionally, advanced variants such as **Sidechains** are studied as interoperability extensions of the base architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The variants are differentiated along **three core axes**:
> 1. **Who can read** the ledger (public vs. restricted)
> 2. **Who can write/validate** transactions (open vs. invited)
> 3. **Who participates in consensus** (anonymous vs. identified)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **notice board system** in a college:

| Analogy Element | Real-World Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The notice board itself | The shared ledger |
| Pinning a notice | Writing a transaction |
| Reading the notice | Reading the blockchain |
| Verifying the notice is genuine | Consensus mechanism |

- **Public Blockchain** = A notice board in the **college quadrangle**: *anyone* (students, staff, outsiders) can come, read all notices, and pin a new one — but only after the **Student Council** (miners/validators) verifies it.
- **Private Blockchain** = A notice board **inside the HOD's office**: only *selected staff* can pin notices, and only the HOD's trusted team verifies them.
- **Consortium Blockchain** = A notice board **shared between the HODs of all departments**: several pre-approved authorities co-manage it, but outsiders can neither pin nor verify.
- **Hybrid Blockchain** = A notice board with a **public viewing window** and a **private writing room**: the public can read, but only invited members can pin notices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The *variant* of blockchain is chosen based on the **trust model**, **performance requirements**, and **regulatory constraints** of the application. Bitcoin and Ethereum are public; Hyperledger Fabric is typically consortium; a corporate audit trail is often private.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Variant

### 2.1.1 Public Blockchain (Permissionless)

- **Definition:** A fully decentralized, open blockchain where **any participant** can join the network, read the ledger, submit transactions, and participate in the consensus process **without prior authorization**.
- **Identity Model:** Participants are **pseudonymous** (identified by public-key hashes, not real-world IDs).
- **Consensus:** Uses resource-intensive mechanisms such as **Proof of Work (PoW)**, **Proof of Stake (PoS)**, or **Delegated PoS (DPoS)**.
- **Throughput:** Limited — e.g., Bitcoin ≈ **7 TPS**, Ethereum (pre-sharding) ≈ **15–30 TPS**.
- **Examples:** **Bitcoin**, **Ethereum**, **Litecoin**, **Dogecoin**.
- **Advantages:** Maximum decentralization, censorship resistance, high fault tolerance.
- **Disadvantages:** Low scalability, high energy cost, slow finality.

### 2.1.2 Private Blockchain (Permissioned)

- **Definition:** A blockchain operated within a **single organization** where write permissions are restricted to a pre-approved set of participants. The central authority controls membership and validation rights.
- **Identity Model:** All validators are **known and identified** (KYC-compliant).
- **Consensus:** Lightweight algorithms such as **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)**, **Raft**, or **Quorum-based voting** are used.
- **Throughput:** Very high — can exceed **1,000–10,000 TPS**.
- **Examples:** **Hyperledger Besu (private mode)**, **Multichain**, **Corda (notary node)**, **Quorum**.
- **Advantages:** High throughput, regulatory compliance, low energy cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Centralized trust; a single point of failure; limited censorship resistance.

### 2.1.3 Consortium Blockchain (Federated / Semi-Private)

- **Definition:** A blockchain **governed by a group of pre-selected organizations** rather than a single entity. Consensus is reached by a **set of trusted validator nodes** belonging to member institutions.
- **Identity Model:** Validators are pre-approved; read access may be public or restricted.
- **Consensus:** **PBFT**, **Proof of Authority (PoA)**, **Quorum**, or custom BFT-style protocols.
- **Examples:** **Hyperledger Fabric**, **R3 Corda**, **Energy Web Chain**, **IBM Food Trust**, **Marco Polo Network** (trade finance).
- **Use Case Spotlight:** **Banking consortiums** (e.g., we.trade), **inter-bank settlements**, and **supply-chain consortia**.

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Blockchain

- **Definition:** Combines features of both **public** and **private** blockchains. It allows a private, permissioned system to be anchored to a public chain, enabling controlled transparency.
- **Architecture:** Sensitive data and transactions remain on the private chain; only cryptographic proofs (e.g., Merkle roots, zero-knowledge proofs) are anchored to the public chain.
- **Examples:** **Dragonchain**, **XinFin (XDC)**, **Ethereum Enterprise (Quorum)**, **IBM Blockchain Platform**.
- **Use Case Spotlight:** **Healthcare records** — patient data stays private, but proof of existence is anchored publicly.

### 2.1.5 Sidechains (Supplementary Variant)

- **Definition:** A separate blockchain that runs **in parallel to a parent (main) chain** and is connected via a **two-way peg** mechanism. Assets can be moved between the main chain and sidechain.
- **Purpose:** Offload transactions to improve scalability; enable experimentation with new features.
- **Examples:** **Liquid Network (Bitcoin sidechain)**, **Polygon (Ethereum sidechain)**, **Rootstock (RSK)**.
- **Mechanism:** Uses **federated pegs** or **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) proofs** for cross-chain transfer.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

| Property | Public | Private | Consortium | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Access Type** | Permissionless | Permissioned | Permissioned | Mixed |
| **Participants** | Anonymous / open | Single org | Multiple orgs | Public + Private |
| **Consensus** | PoW / PoS | Raft / PBFT | PBFT / PoA | Configurable |
| **Throughput** | Low (7–1000 TPS) | High (1000+ TPS) | High (1000+ TPS) | Moderate–High |
| **Decentralization** | Full | Low | Partial | Partial |
| **Immutability** | Strong | Weaker | Strong | Strong (anchored) |
| **Energy Use** | High | Low | Low | Low–Moderate |
| **Read Access** | Public | Restricted | Restricted/Public | Configurable |
| **Identity** | Pseudonymous | Known KYC | Known KYC | Mixed |
| **Examples** | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Multichain | Hyperledger Fabric | Dragonchain |
| **Cost of Attack** | Very High | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic | Deterministic | Deterministic | Configurable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The examiner frequently tests the **differences in consensus mechanism** and the **read/write access matrix** (who can read vs. who can write) — memorize the 3-axis access model.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public chains** are used in cryptocurrencies and DeFi where **trustless participation** is critical.
- **Private chains** dominate **enterprise audit trails**, internal asset tracking, and **internal accounting systems**.
- **Consortium chains** are the backbone of **B2B trade networks**, banking settlements, and **inter-organizational supply chains**.
- **Hybrid chains** serve **regulated industries** (healthcare, government, legal) where public verifiability is needed alongside data confidentiality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Logic

## 3.1 Decision Logic: How to Choose a Blockchain Variant

The selection of a blockchain variant follows a structured analytical framework based on **application requirements**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logic used in KTU-style problem solving.

### Step 1 — Identify the Trust Boundary

- If the application involves **unknown / untrusted participants** → move to **Step 2 (Public)**.
- If the application involves a **single trusted organization** → consider **Private**.
- If the application involves **a group of trusted organizations** → consider **Consortium**.
- If the application needs **both public verifiability and private control** → consider **Hybrid**.

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Access Requirements

Define the **Read (R)**, **Write (W)**, and **Commit/Validate (V)** permissions.

- **Public:** R = open, W = open, V = open (with economic stake).
- **Private:** R = restricted, W = restricted, V = restricted.
- **Consortium:** R = restricted (or public read), W = restricted, V = shared among members.
- **Hybrid:** R = public, W = restricted, V = restricted (with periodic public anchoring).

### Step 3 — Assess Performance Constraints

$$
\text{Throughput Requirement} \; (T_{req}) \; \text{vs.} \; \text{Variant Throughput} \; (T_{var})
$$

$$
T_{req} \;\leq\; T_{var} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Variant is viable}
$$

- If $T_{req} > 1000$ TPS → Avoid pure public chains; prefer **private/consortium**.
- If $T_{req} < 100$ TPS and decentralization is critical → Use **public**.

### Step 4 — Evaluate Regulatory & Compliance Needs

- **GDPR, HIPAA, RBI norms** → favor **private or consortium** with data minimization.
- **Censorship resistance required** → must use **public**.

### Step 5 — Final Selection

$$
\text{Variant} = f(\text{Trust}, \text{Access}, \text{Performance}, \text{Compliance})
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Variant} = \arg\max_{v \in \{P, Pr, C, H\}} \big[\, \alpha \cdot \text{Decentralization}(v) + \beta \cdot \text{Throughput}(v) + \gamma \cdot \text{Compliance}(v) \,\big]}
$$

where the weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ depend on the use case.

## 3.2 Mathematical View: Block Validation Cost

The cost of validating a block on a public chain can be approximated as:

$$
C_{validate} = C_{compute} + C_{bandwidth} + C_{storage} + C_{energy}
$$

For a **private chain**, the cost simplifies to:

$$
C^{private}_{validate} \approx C_{compute} + C_{storage} \;\;\text{(bandwidth and energy are negligible)}
$$

This is why private chains are **orders of magnitude faster**.

## 3.3 Sidechain Peg Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

1. User locks **X coins** on the main chain in a multi-signature address.
2. A **SPV proof** is generated proving the lock transaction.
3. The same **X coins** are minted on the sidechain.
4. User transacts freely on the sidechain.
5. To return, user burns the X coins on the sidechain.
6. A new SPV proof is submitted, and the original coins are unlocked on the main chain.

This is called the **two-way peg (2WP)** mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **sidechains** with **Layer-2 solutions (e.g., Lightning Network)**. Sidechains have their **own consensus** and **own security model**, while Layer-2 solutions derive security from the parent chain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Read/Write/Validate Access Matrix

```mermaid

        PUB_R[Anyone Can READ]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Sidechain Two-Way Peg Operation

```mermaid
    U->>S: Burn X coins to return

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hybrid Architecture
