
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Public Blockchain. List two examples.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Public Blockchain** is a permissionless, fully decentralized distributed ledger where any participant can join the network, read the ledger, submit transactions, and participate in the consensus process without requiring prior authorization. Participants are pseudonymous, identified only by their public keys.

**Examples:** (1) **Bitcoin** (2) **Ethereum**.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Permissioned and Permissionless Blockchains.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Permissionless (Public) | Permissioned (Private/Consortium) |
|---|---|---|
| Access | Open to all | Restricted by an authority |
| Identity | Pseudonymous | Known, KYC-verified |
| Consensus | PoW / PoS | PBFT / Raft / PoA |
| Throughput | Low | High |
| Energy | High | Low |
| Examples | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger, Corda |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the four major variants of blockchain with their key characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Public and Consortium blockchains along any 7 parameters with a suitable use case for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Four Variants (7 Marks)

**1. Public Blockchain (Permissionless)**
- Open, decentralized, pseudonymous participants.
- Anyone can read, write, and validate.
- Uses PoW/PoS.
- **Examples:** Bitcoin, Ethereum.
- **Use Case:** Cryptocurrencies, DeFi.

**2. Private Blockchain (Permissioned)**
- Operated within a single organization.
- Write and validate permissions restricted to authorized nodes.
- Fast, scalable, low energy.
- **Examples:** Multichain, Quorum.
- **Use Case:** Internal audit, asset tracking.

**3. Consortium Blockchain (Federated)**
- Governed by a group of pre-selected organizations.
- Consensus shared among member institutions.
- Balances decentralization and performance.
- **Examples:** Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda.
- **Use Case:** Inter-bank settlement, supply chain.

**4. Hybrid Blockchain**
- Combines public and private features.
- Sensitive data private; proofs anchored publicly.
- **Examples:** Dragonchain, XinFin.
- **Use Case:** Healthcare, regulated industries.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks per variant + 0.5 marks for use case = **7 marks**.

#### Part (b) — Comparison (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Public Blockchain | Consortium Blockchain |
|---|---|---|
| **Access** | Open to all | Pre-approved members |
| **Identity** | Pseudonymous | Known (KYC) |
| **Consensus** | PoW / PoS | PBFT / PoA |
| **Throughput** | Low (7–1000 TPS) | High (1000+ TPS) |
| **Energy** | High | Low |
| **Decentralization** | Full | Partial |
| **Use Case** | Bitcoin cryptocurrency | Hyperledger Fabric for trade finance |

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per parameter × 7 = **7 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a Sidechain? Explain the two-way peg mechanism with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of Consortium and Hybrid blockchains in enterprise applications with two real-world examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Sidechain and Two-Way Peg (7 Marks)

A **sidechain** is a separate blockchain that runs in parallel to a main (parent) chain, connected via a **two-way peg (2WP)** that allows assets to be transferred between the two chains.

**Two-Way Peg Mechanism Steps:**

1. The user **locks** an asset (e.g., X BTC) on the main chain in a multi-signature address.
2. The main chain generates an **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) proof** confirming the lock.
3. The SPV proof is submitted to the sidechain.
4. The sidechain **mints** the equivalent amount of tokens (e.g., X side-BTC).
5. The user performs transactions freely on the sidechain.
6. To return, the user **burns** tokens on the sidechain; a burn proof is generated.
7. The burn proof is submitted to the main chain, which **releases** the original assets.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (2) + Steps (4) + Diagram (1) = **7 marks**.

#### Part (b) — Enterprise Applications (7 Marks)

**Consortium Blockchain in Enterprises:**
- **Example 1: IBM Food Trust (Hyperledger Fabric)** — Used by Walmart, Nestlé, and Tyson Foods to track food provenance, reducing trace-back time from 7 days to 2.2 seconds.
- **Example 2: R3 Corda in Banking** — Used by a consortium of 200+ banks (e.g., HSBC, JPMorgan) for inter-bank settlement and trade finance.

**Hybrid Blockchain in Enterprises:**
- **Example 1: Dragonchain in Healthcare** — Patient records stay private, while proof of medical events is anchored to a public chain for verifiability.
- **Example 2: XinFin (XDC) in Trade Finance** — Combines private validation among banks with public anchoring for regulatory transparency.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks per example × 4 = **6 marks** + 1 mark for overall discussion = **7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** write "Public = Bitcoin only" — examiners expect at least 2 examples.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **sidechains** with **Layer-2 solutions**; they have different security models.
> 3. **Do not** omit the **consensus mechanism** while comparing variants — it carries 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Do not** describe Consortium as "centralized" — it is **partially decentralized** (decentralized across a federation).
> 5. **Always draw a diagram** for the two-way peg — it is worth 1–2 marks separately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Public Blockchain** = Permissionless, fully decentralized, pseudonymous, uses PoW/PoS (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum).
- 🔑 **Private Blockchain** = Single organization, fully permissioned, high TPS, uses PBFT/Raft (e.g., Multichain).
- 🔑 **Consortium Blockchain** = Group of organizations, semi-decentralized, uses PoA/PBFT (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric).
- 🔑 **Hybrid Blockchain** = Public read + Private write + Public anchoring (e.g., Dragonchain).
- 🔑 **Sidechain** = Parallel chain connected via **two-way peg (2WP)** using SPV proofs.
- 🔑 **Three Access Axes** = Read (R), Write (W), Validate (V) — memorize who controls each.
- 🔑 **Consensus mapping:** Public → PoW/PoS; Private → Raft/PBFT; Consortium → PBFT/PoA.
- 🔑 **Throughput order:** Public < Hybrid < Consortium ≤ Private.
- 🔑 **Decentralization order:** Private < Consortium < Hybrid < Public.
- 🔑 **GDPR/HIPAA compliance** → prefer private/consortium; **censorship resistance** → must be public.
- 🔑 **Finality type:** Public = probabilistic; Private/Consortium = deterministic.
- 🔑 **Cost of attack** in public chain is the highest (requires 51% hash power/stake).
- 🔑 **2WP** = Lock → SPV Proof → Mint on sidechain → Use → Burn → Unlock on main.
- 🔑 **KTU favorite questions:** Comparison tables, sidechain diagrams, and use-case identification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptography in Blockchain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cryptography in Blockchain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cryptography in Blockchain** refers to the disciplined application of mathematical techniques — specifically cryptographic hash functions, public-key (asymmetric) cryptography, and digital signature schemes — to secure data integrity, authenticate identity, ensure non-repudiation, and establish trustless consensus among distributed, mutually untrusted peers in a blockchain network.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 2), this topic is positioned as the *foundational cryptographic layer* upon which all higher-level blockchain constructs (blocks, Merkle trees, transactions, wallets, consensus) are engineered. Without cryptography, a blockchain degenerates into a trivial, tamper-prone database.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Verbatim)**
> Cryptography in Blockchain is the study of the cryptographic primitives — **Hash Functions**, **Merkle Trees**, **Public-Key Cryptography (PKC)**, **Digital Signatures**, and **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** — that collectively enforce the *immutability*, *authenticity*, and *integrity* guarantees of a distributed ledger.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **transparent glass mailbox** sitting in a busy public square. Anyone can walk up and drop a sealed letter into the slot, but once the letter falls in, nobody can:
1. **Read** it (confidentiality)
2. **Swap** it with a forged one (integrity)
3. **Pretend** they didn't send it (non-repudiation)
4. **Lie** about who sent it (authenticity)

The "sealing wax" used in this analogy is **cryptography**. Each cryptographic primitive plays a precise role:

| Real-World Analogy | Cryptographic Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Tamper-evident wax seal | **Hash Function** (SHA-256) | Detect any data alteration |
| Unique handwritten signature | **Digital Signature** (ECDSA) | Prove sender identity |
| Postage stamp with public ID | **Public-Key Cryptography** | Bind identity to address |
| Tree of sealed envelopes | **Merkle Tree** | Efficient proof of inclusion |
| Glass mailboxes chained by wire | **Hash-linked Blocks** | Immutability of history |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Trustless"?** In traditional banking, you *trust* the bank to record your balance correctly. In blockchain, cryptography replaces institutional trust with **mathematical certainty** — you don't need to trust any party because the math guarantees correctness.

---

## 1.3 Standard Cryptographic Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following quantitative metrics are **routinely tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers and **must be memorized**:

- **SHA-256 Output Length:** $256$ bits $\equiv 32$ bytes $\equiv 64$ hexadecimal characters
- **Block Size of SHA-256:** $512$ bits $\equiv 64$ bytes
- **Number of Rounds in SHA-256:** $64$ rounds
- **Bitcoin Address Length:** $160$ bits (after RIPEMD-160 hashing of public key)
- **ECDSA Curve Used by Bitcoin/Ethereum:** **secp256k1**
- **Private Key Length (secp256k1):** $256$ bits
- **Public Key Length (secp256k1):** $512$ bits (uncompressed: x and y coordinates)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Hash Properties — "PIC-PAT":**
> **P**re-image resistance, **I**nverse pre-image resistance, **C**ollision resistance, **P**uzzle-friendliness, **A**v**A**lanche effect, **T**hird **T**rusted-party resistance.

---

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| KTU 2024 CO | Cognitive Level (Bloom's Revised) | Topic Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Understand, Apply | Identify and apply hash functions, Merkle trees, PKC, and digital signatures in blockchain contexts |
| **CO3** | Analyze, Evaluate | Analyze the cryptographic security of a given blockchain transaction and evaluate its resilience |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Blockchain Cryptography

The KTU 2024 syllabus decomposes blockchain cryptography into three logically sequential pillars. Mastering this hierarchy is essential for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

---

### Pillar 1 — Cryptographic Hash Functions

A **hash function** $H$ is a deterministic mathematical function that maps an input of *arbitrary length* to an output of *fixed length*:

$$H: \{0,1\}^{*} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$$

where $n$ is the fixed output length (e.g., $n = 256$ for SHA-256).

**Operational Steps of a Hash Function (SHA-256 Architecture):**

1. **Padding** — Append a '1' bit, then $k$ '0' bits, then a 64-bit big-endian representation of the original message length, such that the total length becomes a multiple of $512$ bits.
2. **Parse** — Break the padded message into $N$ blocks of $512$ bits each.
3. **Initialize Hash Value (H)** — Eight 32-bit words: $H_{0}, H_{1}, \ldots, H_{7}$ (the initial hash values are the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 primes).
4. **Compression Function** — For each $512$-bit block, run $64$ rounds of bitwise operations (AND, OR, XOR, ROTR, SHR) and modular addition.
5. **Output** — Concatenate $H_{0} \Vert H_{1} \Vert \ldots \Vert H_{7}$ to produce a $256$-bit digest.

**The "Why" Behind Each Property:**

| Property | Definition | Why It Matters in Blockchain |
|---|---|---|
| **Determinism** | Same input $\Rightarrow$ same output | Any node can re-verify a block hash and reach consensus |
| **Pre-image Resistance** | Given $h$, finding $m$ such that $H(m)=h$ is computationally infeasible | Prevents reversing a block hash to recover transaction data |
| **Second Pre-image Resistance** | Given $m_1$, finding $m_2 \neq m_1$ with $H(m_1)=H(m_2)$ is infeasible | Prevents forging a new block with the same hash |
| **Collision Resistance** | Finding any $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1)=H(m_2)$ is infeasible | Underpins transaction-ID uniqueness (no two TXs share a hash) |
| **Avalanche Effect** | Flipping 1 input bit changes ~50% of output bits | Any tampering is statistically detectable |
| **Puzzle-Friendliness** | For target $T$, finding $m$ with $H(m) < T$ requires brute force | Enables Proof-of-Work mining (Bitcoin's consensus) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — SHA-256 vs SHA-3**
> SHA-256 is a **Merkle–Damgård** construction (vulnerable to *length-extension attacks*). SHA-3 (Keccak) uses a **sponge construction**, which is immune to length-extension attacks. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know this distinction.

---

### Pillar 2 — Public-Key Cryptography (Asymmetric Cryptography)

A **public-key cryptosystem** uses a mathematically related key pair:

$$\text{Key Pair} = (k_{\text{private}},\; k_{\text{public}})$$

- The **private key** $k_{\text{private}}$ is kept secret by the owner.
- The **public key** $k_{\text{public}}$ is derived from the private key and freely distributed.
- The mathematical relationship is a **trapdoor function** — easy to compute in one direction, infeasible to invert without the trapdoor (private key).

**RSA Trapdoor Function:**

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

where $n = p \cdot q$ (product of two large primes), $e$ is the public exponent, and $d$ is the private exponent satisfying $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, with $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.

**Elliptic Curve Trapdoor Function (secp256k1):**

The curve is defined by:

$$y^{2} \equiv x^{3} + 7 \pmod{p}$$

where $p = 2^{256} - 2^{32} - 977$. A public key is the result of scalar multiplication of a base point $G$ by a private key $k$:

$$K_{\text{public}} = k_{\text{private}} \cdot G$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ECDSA and not RSA in Bitcoin/Ethereum?**
> ECDSA with a 256-bit key offers security equivalent to RSA with a 3072-bit key. Smaller keys $\Rightarrow$ smaller signatures $\Rightarrow$ smaller transactions $\Rightarrow$ more transactions per block. **ECC is 10× to 100× more efficient** for equivalent security levels.

---

### Pillar 3 — Digital Signatures

A **digital signature** binds an identity to a message using asymmetric cryptography. It provides **authenticity**, **integrity**, and **non-repudiation**.

**ECDSA Signing Algorithm (Simplified):**

1. Generate a random nonce $k$ (ephemeral, single-use).
2. Compute the point $(x_1, y_1) = k \cdot G$.
3. Compute $r = x_1 \pmod{n}$ where $n$ is the curve order.
4. Compute $s = k^{-1} \cdot (H(m) + r \cdot d_{\text{private}}) \pmod{n}$.
5. The signature is the pair $(r, s)$.

**ECDSA Verification:**

$$u_1 = H(m) \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{n}$$

$$u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{n}$$

$$R = u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot Q_{\text{public}}$$

The signature is **valid** if and only if $R_x \equiv r \pmod{n}$.

---

## 2.2 Merkle Trees — Efficient Verification of Large Data Structures

A **Merkle tree** (invented by Ralph Merkle in 1979) is a binary tree of hashes used to summarize and verify large datasets efficiently.

**Construction Steps:**

1. Take $n$ data blocks (transactions): $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$.
2. Hash each: $H_1 = \text{SHA-256}(T_1)$, $H_2 = \text{SHA-256}(T_2)$, $\ldots$, $H_n = \text{SHA-256}(T_n)$. These are the **leaf nodes**.
3. Pairwise concatenate and hash: $H_{12} = \text{SHA-256}(H_1 \Vert H_2)$, $H_{34} = \text{SHA-256}(H_3 \Vert H_4)$, etc. These are the **internal nodes**.
4. Repeat upward until a single hash remains — the **Merkle Root** $H_{\text{root}}$.

**Formal Hash Relation:**

$$H_{\text{root}} = H\left( H\left( H(T_1) \Vert H(T_2) \right) \Vert H\left( H(T_3) \Vert H(T_4) \right) \right)$$

For $n$ transactions, the Merkle root requires exactly $\lceil \log_2(n) \rceil$ levels, and a **Merkle proof** (path from leaf to root) requires only $\log_2(n)$ hashes for verification.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE. Every formula here is a potential 14-mark question stem.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Parameters | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hash Function | $H: \{0,1\}^{*} \to \{0,1\}^{n}$ | $n=256$ for SHA-256 | Deterministic, fixed output |
| 2 | SHA-256 Padding | $\text{len}(M) + 1 + k + 64 \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$ | $k \geq 0$ zero bits | Length appended as 64-bit big-endian |
| 3 | Block Hash (Blockchain) | $H_{i} = \text{SHA-256}\big(\text{SHA-256}(H_{i-1} \Vert M_i \Vert \text{nonce} \Vert \text{ts})\big)$ | $M_i$ = Merkle root of block $i$ | Double-hashing in Bitcoin |
| 4 | RSA Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $n=pq$, $e$ public | $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| 5 | RSA Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | $d$ private | Trapdoor inversion |
| 6 | ECC Public Key | $Q = d \cdot G$ | $d$ private, $G$ generator | Discrete log problem |
| 7 | ECDSA Signature | $(r, s)$ where $s = k^{-1}(H(m) + r \cdot d) \pmod{n}$ | $k$ = ephemeral nonce | **Never reuse $k$!** |
| 8 | Merkle Root (4 leaves) | $H_{R} = H(H_{12} \Vert H_{34})$ | $H_{12} = H(H_1 \Vert H_2)$ | Binary hash tree |
| 9 | Merkle Proof Length | $\log_2(n)$ hashes | $n$ = # of transactions | $O(\log n)$ verification |
| 10 | PoW Difficulty | $\text{Target} = \text{MaxTarget} / \text{Difficulty}$ | SHA-256 hash $\leq$ Target | Probabilistic mining |
| 11 | Birthday Attack Bound | $O(2^{n/2})$ for $n$-bit hash | $n = 256 \Rightarrow 2^{128}$ | Why $n=256$ is chosen |
| 12 | Secp256k1 Field Prime | $p = 2^{256} - 2^{32} - 977$ | Curve: $y^{2} = x^{3} + 7 \pmod p$ | Bitcoin & Ethereum standard |
| 13 | Bitcoin Address | $\text{Addr} = \text{RIPEMD-160}(\text{SHA-256}(Q_{\text{pub}}))$ | Prepended with 0x00, Base58Check | Yields 25-byte address |
| 14 | Hash Pointer | $H_{i} \rightarrow H_{i-1}$ | Cryptographic back-reference | Creates tamper-evident chain |
| 15 | Probabilistic Hash Collision | $P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-k^{2}/(2N)}$ | $N = 2^{n}$, $k$ samples | Birthday paradox |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SHA-256 Padding of an Arbitrary Message

This derivation is a **high-probability 14-mark KTU question**. We work through it without skipping any algebraic transition.

**Problem:** Given a message $M$ of length $L = 1000$ bits, compute the number of padding zero bits required to make the total padded message length a multiple of $512$ bits.

---

### Step 1 — Recall the SHA-256 Padding Rule

The SHA-256 padding rule states that the total length of the padded message must be a **non-negative integer multiple of $512$**. The padding consists of:
- One mandatory '1' bit
- Followed by $k$ '0' bits (where $k \geq 0$)
- Followed by a $64$-bit big-endian representation of the original message length $L$

The total padded length must satisfy:

$$L + 1 + k + 64 \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

---

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Value $L = 1000$

$$1000 + 1 + k + 64 \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

$$1065 + k \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute $1065 \pmod{512}$

$$1065 = 2 \times 512 + 41$$

$$1065 \equiv 41 \pmod{512}$$

---

### Step 4 — Solve for $k$

$$41 + k \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

$$k \equiv -41 \pmod{512}$$

$$k \equiv 512 - 41 \pmod{512}$$

$$k \equiv 471 \pmod{512}$$

Since $k \geq 0$ and must be the *smallest* non-negative solution, we take:

$$k = 471$$

---

### Step 5 — Verify the Total Padded Length

$$\text{Total} = 1000 + 1 + 471 + 64 = 1536 \text{ bits}$$

$$\frac{1536}{512} = 3 \text{ blocks} \checkmark$$

---

### Step 6 — Express the Final Padded Bitstring

$$\underbrace{M}_{1000 \text{ bits}} \Vert \underbrace{1}_{1 \text{ bit}} \Vert \underbrace{00\cdots 0}_{471 \text{ zero bits}} \Vert \underbrace{L_{64}}_{64 \text{-bit length encoding}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge Case:** If $L \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$, then $k$ would have to be $512$ to satisfy the constraint, and an **additional full 512-bit block** is appended. KTU examiners love to test this edge case.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: RSA Key Pair Generation

**Problem:** Generate a working RSA key pair for the small primes $p = 61$ and $q = 53$, choosing $e = 17$. Then encrypt the message $M = 42$ and decrypt to verify.

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Modulus $n$

$$n = p \cdot q = 61 \times 53$$

$$61 \times 53 = 61 \times 50 + 61 \times 3 = 3050 + 183 = 3233$$

$$n = 3233$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

Since $p$ and $q$ are distinct primes:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = 60 \times 52$$

$$60 \times 52 = 60 \times 50 + 60 \times 2 = 3000 + 120 = 3120$$

$$\phi(n) = 3120$$

---

### Step 3 — Verify that $e$ is Coprime with $\phi(n)$

$\gcd(17, 3120)$: Since $3120 = 17 \times 183 + 9$, then $17 = 9 \times 1 + 8$, then $9 = 8 \times 1 + 1$, then $8 = 1 \times 8 + 0$. So:

$$\gcd(17, 3120) = 1 \checkmark$$

Thus $e = 17$ is a valid public exponent.

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Private Exponent $d$ via the Extended Euclidean Algorithm

We need $d$ such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

$$17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Applying the Extended Euclidean Algorithm step by step:

$$3120 = 17 \times 183 + 9$$

$$17 = 9 \times 1 + 8$$

$$9 = 8 \times 1 + 1$$

$$8 = 1 \times 8 + 0$$

Back-substituting:

$$1 = 9 - 8 \times 1$$

$$1 = 9 - (17 - 9 \times 1) \times 1 = 9 \times 2 - 17 \times 1$$

$$1 = (3120 - 17 \times 183) \times 2 - 17 \times 1$$

$$1 = 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 366 - 17 \times 1$$

$$1 = 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 367$$

Therefore:

$$-17 \times 367 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

$$17 \times (-367) \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

$$17 \times (3120 - 367) \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

$$17 \times 2753 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

So:

$$d = 2753$$

---

### Step 5 — Encryption of $M = 42$

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} = 42^{17} \pmod{3233}$$

We compute this using **modular exponentiation by repeated squaring**:

$$42^{1} \equiv 42 \pmod{3233}$$

$$42^{2} \equiv 1764 \pmod{3233}$$

$$42^{4} \equiv 1764^{2} = 3{,}111{,}696 \pmod{3233}$$

$$3{,}111{,}696 \div 3233 \approx 962, \text{ remainder } = 3{,}111{,}696 - 962 \times 3233 = 3{,}111{,}696 - 3{,}110{,}146 = 1550$$

$$42^{4} \equiv 1550 \pmod{3233}$$

$$42^{8} \equiv 1550^{2} = 2{,}402{,}500 \pmod{3233}$$

$$2{,}402{,}500 \div 3233 \approx 743, \text{ remainder } = 2{,}402{,}500 - 743 \times 3233 = 2{,}402{,}500 - 2{,}402{,}119 = 381$$

$$42^{8} \equiv 381 \pmod{3233}$$

$$42^{16} \equiv 381^{2} = 145{,}161 \pmod{3233}$$

$$145{,}161 \div 3233 \approx 44, \text{ remainder } = 145{,}161 - 44 \times 3233 = 145{,}161 - 142{,}252 = 2909$$

$$42^{16} \equiv 2909 \pmod{3233}$$

Now combine: $17 = 16 + 1$, so:

$$42^{17} \equiv 42^{16} \times 42^{1} \equiv 2909 \times 42 \pmod{3233}$$

$$2909 \times 42 = 122{,}178$$

$$122{,}178 \div 3233 \approx 37, \text{ remainder } = 122{,}178 - 37 \times 3233 = 122{,}178 - 119{,}621 = 2557$$

$$C = 2557$$

---

### Step 6 — Decryption Verification

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 2557^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$

By Euler's theorem, since $\gcd(2557, 3233) = 1$:

$$2557^{\phi(3233)} \equiv 1 \pmod{3233}$$

Thus $M \equiv 2557^{2753} \pmod{3233} \equiv 42$ (computationally verifiable). $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** KTU examiners frequently set RSA with $p, q$ in the 50–100 range, $e = 17$ or $e = 5$, and $M < 100$. Memorize the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** — it is asked almost every semester.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cryptographic Primitives

| Dimension | SHA-256 (Hash) | RSA (PKC) | ECDSA (Signature) | Merkle Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Symmetric, keyless | Asymmetric | Asymmetric (signature) | Data structure |
| **Key Required?** | No | Yes (key pair) | Yes (key pair) | No (uses hash) |
| **Reversible?** | No (one-way) | Yes (with private key) | Verification only | No |
| **Output Size** | $256$ bits | Variable (mod $n$) | $\sim 512$ bits | $256$ bits (root) |
| **Primary Use in Blockchain** | Block hashing, PoW | Encryption (rare) | Transaction signatures | Tx summarization |
| **Security Basis** | Pre-image resistance | Integer factorization | Elliptic curve discrete log | Hash collision resistance |
| **Computational Cost** | Fast | Expensive (large keys) | Moderate | $O(n)$ to build, $O(\log n)$ to verify |
| **Quantum Vulnerability** | Partially (Grover) | Yes (Shor) | Yes (Shor) | Inherits hash vulnerability |
| **Real-World Example** | Bitcoin block hash | TLS handshakes | Bitcoin TX signing | Bitcoin block header |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Merkle Root Construction

**Problem:** Given four transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ with hashes (after a single SHA-256 round) $H_1, H_2, H_3, H_4$, write the explicit hash sequence that produces the Merkle root. Use $H_{ab}$ to denote the parent hash of leaves $a$ and $b$.

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Leaf Hashes

$$H_1 = \text{SHA-256}(T_1)$$

$$H_2 = \text{SHA-256}(T_2)$$

$$H_3 = \text{SHA-256}(T_3)$$

$$H_4 = \text{SHA-256}(T_4)$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Level-1 (Internal) Hashes

$$H_{12} = \text{SHA-256}(H_1 \Vert H_2)$$

$$H_{34} = \text{SHA-256}(H_3 \Vert H_4)$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Merkle Root (Level 2)

$$H_{\text{root}} = \text{SHA-256}(H_{12} \Vert H_{34})$$

---

### Step 4 — Total Hash Operations Required

For $n = 4$ transactions:
- Leaf hashes: $4$ operations
- Level 1: $2$ operations
- Root: $1$ operation
- **Total: $7$ operations** $= 2n - 1$ in general

---

### Step 5 — Merkle Proof Length

To prove that $T_3$ is included in the block, a **light client** needs only:
$$\{H_4, H_{12}\}$$
i.e., $\log_2(4) = 2$ sibling hashes. The client computes:
$$H_3' = \text{SHA-256}(T_3)$$
$$H_{34}' = \text{SHA-256}(H_3' \Vert H_4)$$
$$H_{\text{root}}' = \text{SHA-256}(H_{12} \Vert H_{34}')$$
and checks $H_{\text{root}}' = H_{\text{root}}$ stored in the block header. $\blacksquare$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hash Function Pipeline (SHA-256 Internal Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Message M of arbitrary length] --> B[Step 1: Padding with 1 bit, k zero bits, 64-bit length]
    B --> C[Step 2: Parse into N x 512-bit blocks]
    C --> D[Step 3: Initialize H0 to H7 from first 8 prime square roots]
    D --> E[Step 4: For each 512-bit block run 64 rounds of compression]
    E --> F[Step 5: Update H0 to H7 with modular addition]
    F --> G{All blocks processed?}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Step 6: Concatenate H0 to H7]
    H --> I[Final 256-bit hash digest]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diagram shows the **Merkle–Damgård iterative structure** of SHA-256. Each $512$-bit message block is fed sequentially into a compression function that updates the running hash state $H$. This iterative chaining is what makes SHA-256 vulnerable to *length-extension attacks* — a fact KTU 2024 expects students to know.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Merkle Tree Construction with Verification Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Transaction T1] --> H1[SHA256: H1]
    T2[Transaction T2] --> H2[SHA256: H2]
    T3[Transaction T3] --> H3[SHA256: H3]
    T4[Transaction T4] --> H4[SHA256: H4]
    H1 --> H12[SHA256: H12 = root of H1, H2]
    H2 --> H12
    H3 --> H34[SHA256: H34 = root of H3, H4]
    H4 --> H34
    H12 --> HROOT[SHA256: Merkle Root Hroot]
    H34 --> HROOT
    HROOT --> BH[Stored in Block Header]
    BH --> BC[Verified by Every Full Node]
    H3 -.-> MP[Merkle Proof for T3]
    H4 -.-> MP
    H12 -.-> MP
    MP --> LC[Sent to Light Client]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Light Client Power:** Notice the dashed arrows from $H_3$, $H_4$, and $H_{12}$ forming a **Merkle proof**. A smartphone (light client) holding only the block header can cryptographically verify that $T_3$ is included in the block using just these three sibling hashes — *without downloading the entire block*. This is the engineering elegance of Merkle trees.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Public-Key Cryptography and Digital Signature Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER
        A[User Wallet] --> B[Generate Private Key d]
        B --> C[Derive Public Key Q = d x G]
        C --> D[Hash Transaction: h = SHA256 TX]
        D --> E[Sign: r, s = ECDSA sign h with d]
    end
    E --> F[Broadcast: TX, r, s, Q]
    subgraph BLOCKCHAIN
        F --> G[Transaction Pool]
        G --> H[Included in Block]
        H --> I[Block Hash via Merkle Root]
    end
    subgraph RECEIVER
        I --> J[Receive Block]
        J --> K[Verify: R = u1 G plus u2 Q]
        K --> L{Valid signature?}
        L -- Yes --> M[Accept Transaction]
        L -- No --> N[Reject as Forged]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-to-End Flow Decoded:** The sender signs the *hash* of the transaction (not the raw transaction) for efficiency. The signature $(r, s)$ is *attached* to the transaction, not embedded. The receiver uses only the public key $Q$ (no private key) to verify — this is the magic of asymmetric cryptography.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block Hash Chaining (Immutability Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GEN[Genesis Block: Hash 0000abc] --> B1[Block 1: Hash H1 = SHA256 prev + tx + nonce + ts]
    B1 --> B2[Block 2: Hash H2 = SHA256 H1 + tx2 + nonce2 + ts2]
    B2 --> B3[Block 3: Hash H3 = SHA256 H2 + tx3 + nonce3 + ts3]
    B3 --> B4[Block n: Hash Hn = SHA256 Hn-1 + txn + noncen + tsn]
    TAMPER([Adversary tampers with Block 1]) -.-> B1B[Block 1 Modified: New Hash H1 prime]
    B1B -.-> CASC[Cascading Invalidity: H2 now points to old H1, mismatch detected]
    CASC -.-> REJ[Network Rejects the Tampered Chain]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **The Immutability Argument:** A single byte change in Block 1 propagates a *completely different* hash (avalanche effect), which invalidates *every subsequent block's* stored previous-hash pointer. To successfully tamper, an attacker would need to recompute *every* block from Block 1 onwards, faster than the honest network — this is computationally infeasible for a sufficiently long chain under Proof-of-Work.

---

## 4.5 Functional Block Diagram — Cryptographic Layer of a Blockchain Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[User Application Layer] --> WALLET[Wallet: Key Management Module]
    WALLET --> KP[Key Pair Generator: secp256k1]
    KP --> PRIV[Private Key: 256 bits, secret]
    KP --> PUB[Public Key: 512 bits, shared]
    PRIV --> SIGN[ECDSA Sign Module]
    SIGN --> SIG[Signature: r, s pair, 512 bits]
    PUB --> VER[ECDSA Verify Module]
    SIG --> TX[Transaction Builder]
    TX --> HASH[SHA-256 Hash Engine]
    HASH --> MRKL[Merkle Tree Constructor]
    MRKL --> MR[Merkle Root: 256 bits]
    MR --> BH[Block Header Assembler]
    BH --> POW[Proof-of-Work: Nonce Search Engine]
    POW --> BC[Blockchain Network Broadcast]
    BC --> NODES[Peer-to-Peer Propagation]
    VER --> VR[Verification Result: Accept or Reject]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture:** This is the **canonical architecture** of the cryptographic subsystem within a single blockchain node (e.g., Bitcoin Core, Geth). Every component maps directly to a software module a KTU 2024 graduate might implement or audit in their career.
