$$N_{i,j} = H(L_{2j} \parallel L_{2j+1}) \quad \text{(for internal node at level } i \text{)}$$

where $\parallel$ denotes bitwise string concatenation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Merkle Root as the Block Fingerprint**
> In Bitcoin's block header, the **Merkle Root** is one of the **80-byte header fields** and is computed over the hash of all transactions in the block. A change in *any single bit* of *any single transaction* propagates upward and produces a completely different Merkle Root, breaking the chain of **proof-of-work** validation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Diplomatic Passport Stamping System"

Imagine an embassy processing the visa applications of **eight travellers** (transactions) before issuing a single group permit. Instead of stamping each traveller's passport individually at the gate (which would be slow and easy to forge), the embassy does the following:

1. **First Pass — Individual Stamps:** Each traveller's passport gets a unique security stamp (the leaf hash $L_i$).
2. **Second Pass — Pairs Combined:** The stamps of every two adjacent travellers are sealed together into a single envelope (internal hash $H(L_{2j} \parallel L_{2j+1})$).
3. **Third Pass — Envelopes Combined:** Envelopes are paired and sealed again.
4. **Final Seal — Group Permit:** A single master seal (the **Merkle Root**) is affixed to the group permit.

If a customs officer later questions whether *Traveller #3* is genuinely part of the group, he doesn't need to inspect all 8 passports. He only needs the 3 sibling stamps along the path to the root. The system is **compact, hierarchical, and tamper-evident** — exactly the properties a blockchain needs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Merkle"?** The structure is named after **Ralph C. Merle** (actually **Ralph Merkle**), who patented the concept in **1979** under the title *"Secrecy, authentication, and public key systems"*, decades before Satoshi Nakamoto applied it to Bitcoin in **2008**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Properties

| Property | Standard Value / Notation | KTU Significance |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Tree arity | $k = 2$ (binary) | Most common; generalised $k$-ary Merkle trees exist (e.g., Ethereum's Patricia Trie) |
| Height of tree with $n$ leaves | $h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | Determines proof length |
| Hash function | **SHA-256** (Bitcoin), Keccak-256 (Ethereum) | Collision resistance is the security assumption |
| Merkle Proof size | $h$ hashes = $O(\log n)$ | Enables **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification)** |
| Block size in Bitcoin | Up to **~1 MB** (~2000–4000 tx/block) | Each block computes exactly **one** Merkle Root |
| Time to build tree | $O(n)$ | One pass over all transactions |
| Time to verify membership | $O(\log n)$ | The killer feature of the structure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction Algorithm — Operational Logic

Let $T = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ be the set of $n$ data blocks (transactions) in a Bitcoin block. The Merkle tree is built bottom-up as follows:

### Step 1 — Leaf Hashing
Compute the cryptographic hash of every individual data block. If $n$ is odd, the **last leaf is duplicated** to make the count even (Bitcoin convention).

$$\forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\} : \quad L_i = H(D_i)$$

### Step 2 — Iterative Pairing
Pair adjacent hashes $(L_{2j}, L_{2j+1})$ and compute their parent hash. This is repeated level-by-level until only one node remains.

$$N^{(k)}_j = H\big(N^{(k-1)}_{2j} \,\|\, N^{(k-1)}_{2j+1}\big)$$

where $k$ is the current level (level 0 = leaves, level $h$ = root).

### Step 3 — Termination
When exactly **one** node remains, it is designated the **Merkle Root** $\mathcal{R}$:

$$\mathcal{R} = H\big(N^{(h-1)}_{0} \,\|\, N^{(h-1)}_{1}\big)$$

### Why Bottom-Up? (The "How" Explained)
- **Why hash in pairs?** Hashing two children together **binds** them cryptographically; an attacker cannot change one without changing the parent, then the grandparent, and so on up to the root.
- **Why duplicate the last leaf on odd counts?** It preserves the **complete binary tree** property, ensuring every internal node has exactly two children. This avoids special-casing the tree shape in the implementation.
- **Why store only the root in the block header?** It compresses the integrity of *all* transactions into **32 bytes**, which is cheap to compare during mining and validation.

## 2.2 Merkle Proofs (Proof of Inclusion / SPV)

A **Merkle Proof** (also called an **authentication path**) is the set of $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ sibling hashes required to recompute the root from a specific leaf $D_t$. It allows a lightweight client to verify that $D_t$ is included in the block **without downloading the entire block**.

### The Verification Algorithm
Given a target transaction $D_t$, the Merkle root $\mathcal{R}_{\text{claimed}}$, and an authentication path $\mathcal{P} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_h\}$ where each $s_k$ is the sibling hash at level $k$:

1. Start with $c_0 = H(D_t)$.
2. For $k = 1$ to $h$:
   - If $c_{k-1}$ was the **left** child, compute $c_k = H(c_{k-1} \parallel s_k)$.
   - If $c_{k-1}$ was the **right** child, compute $c_k = H(s_k \parallel c_{k-1})$.
3. **Accept** if and only if $c_h = \mathcal{R}_{\text{claimed}}$.

### Why this Works — The Cryptographic Logic
Each step **reconstructs the parent hash** using the claimed sibling. If the leaf $D_t$ is *not* in the tree, the recomputed path will diverge from the genuine Merkle root with **astronomically high probability** (specifically $\leq 2^{-256}$ under SHA-256), because finding a pre-image that hashes to a desired value is computationally infeasible.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| Leaf hash | $L_i = H(D_i)$ | 32 bytes (SHA-256) |
| Internal node hash | $N = H(L_\text{left} \parallel L_\text{right})$ | 32 bytes |
| Merkle Root | $\mathcal{R} = \text{root of the tree}$ | 32 bytes; stored in block header |
| Tree height | $h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | $n$ = number of transactions |
| Proof size | $O(\log_2 n)$ hashes | Each hash = 32 bytes |
| Construction time | $O(n)$ hashes | One pass over $n$ leaves |
| Verification time | $O(\log_2 n)$ hashes | Lightweight client operation |
| Tamper-detection probability | $\leq 2^{-256}$ (under SHA-256) | Per single bit-flip |
| Bitcoin block size limit | $\approx 4{,}000{,}000$ weight units | $\approx 1$ MB / block |
| Merkle proof size for 4000 tx | $\lceil \log_2 4000 \rceil = 12$ hashes | $\approx 384$ bytes total |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall — Do not confuse Merkle Root with Block Hash!**
> - **Merkle Root** = hash of all transactions in a block (cryptographic integrity of *contents*).
> - **Block Hash** = SHA-256 of the block *header* (which *includes* the Merkle Root, the previous block hash, the nonce, etc.).
> They are **related but distinct**. Many students lose marks by treating them as synonymous.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bitcoin & cryptocurrencies:** Compact, deterministic commitment to all transactions in a block.
- **Distributed version control (Git):** Git uses a Merkle-like structure (trie of SHA-1 hashes) to verify repository integrity.
- **IPFS & distributed file systems:** Content-addressed storage via Merkle DAGs.
- **Certificate Transparency (Google):** Merkle trees prove that a specific TLS certificate is in a public log.
- **Databases & NoSQL systems:** Used for sync protocols and tamper-evident logs (e.g., certificate transparency, AWS QLDB).
- **Zero-Knowledge Proofs:** Merkle commitments are a core building block in ZK-SNARK / ZK-STARK systems (Merkle Mountain Ranges in STARKs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Merkle Tree from 4 Transactions

Let us work through a **complete, fully-evaluated** example with $n = 4$ data blocks. We use **SHA-256** but represent the output as short 8-character hex strings (the last 8 hex chars of the real 64-char digest) for readability. In an actual exam, the examiner will give you these values.

### Given Data

| Data Block | Symbolic Name | SHA-256 (last 8 hex chars) |
|------------|---------------|----------------------------|
| $D_1$ | Tx-Alice-Bob | `A1B2C3D4` |
| $D_2$ | Tx-Carol-Dave | `5E6F7081` |
| $D_3$ | Tx-Eve-Frank | `2468ACE0` |
| $D_4$ | Tx-Grace-Heidi | `FEDCBA98` |

### Step 1 — Compute the Leaf Hashes

The leaf hash is simply the SHA-256 of each transaction. We are *given* the values in the table above, so we use them directly. In KTU exam questions, these are **pre-computed for you**.

$$L_1 = H(D_1) = \texttt{A1B2C3D4}$$
$$L_2 = H(D_2) = \texttt{5E6F7081}$$
$$L_3 = H(D_3) = \texttt{2468ACE0}$$
$$L_4 = H(D_4) = \texttt{FEDCBA98}$$

> **Logic:** Each transaction is independently hashed. This is the **atomic** level of cryptographic commitment — any modification to $D_i$ will change $L_i$.

### Step 2 — Compute Level-1 (Parent) Hashes

The parent of $L_1$ and $L_2$ is $H(L_1 \parallel L_2)$. We are given the result as a synthetic hash. Let us use the values:

$$N_{0,0} = H(L_1 \parallel L_2) = H(\texttt{A1B2C3D4} \parallel \texttt{5E6F7081}) = \texttt{11223344}$$
$$N_{0,1} = H(L_3 \parallel L_4) = H(\texttt{2468ACE0} \parallel \texttt{FEDCBA98}) = \texttt{55667788}$$

> **Logic:** Concatenation of the two hex strings yields a 16-hex-character (64-bit) input. The hash function maps this to 8 hex chars again. This step **binds** two leaves together.

### Step 3 — Compute the Merkle Root (Level-2)

The single root hash is the parent of $N_{0,0}$ and $N_{0,1}$:

$$\mathcal{R} = H(N_{0,0} \parallel N_{0,1}) = H(\texttt{11223344} \parallel \texttt{55667788}) = \texttt{99AABBCC}$$

> **Logic:** This single 32-byte value **commits** to all four transactions. Changing *any* transaction changes its leaf, then its parent, then the root.

### Tree Structure Summary

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R} = \texttt{99AABBCC} = H(\,H(L_1 \parallel L_2)\, \parallel \, H(L_3 \parallel L_4)\,)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — A Merkle Proof for $D_2$

Suppose a lightweight client (e.g., a mobile phone) wants to verify that $D_2$ (Tx-Carol-Dave) is included in the block, but has only the Merkle Root $\mathcal{R} = \texttt{99AABBCC}$.

### The Authentication Path

A full node supplies the **sibling hashes** along the path from $L_2$ to the root:

- **Level 0 (leaf):** sibling of $L_2$ is $L_1 = \texttt{A1B2C3D4}$ (because $L_2$ is the right child, its sibling is the left).
- **Level 1 (parent):** sibling of $N_{0,0} = H(L_1 \parallel L_2)$ is $N_{0,1} = \texttt{55667788}$ (because $N_{0,0}$ is the left child).

So the **Merkle Proof** is the set $\mathcal{P} = \{\texttt{A1B2C3D4},\ \texttt{55667788}\}$, which is exactly $h = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ hashes.

### Step-by-Step Verification on the Client

#### Step 1 — Hash the target transaction:

$$c_0 = H(D_2) = L_2 = \texttt{5E6F7081}$$

> **Logic:** The client must hash the transaction itself to obtain the leaf value. (In practice, the client already has the transaction body, so this is a local SHA-256 operation.)

#### Step 2 — Combine $c_0$ with its sibling $s_1 = L_1$:

Since $L_2$ is the **right** child, $L_1$ goes on the **left**:

$$c_1 = H(L_1 \parallel c_0) = H(\texttt{A1B2C3D4} \parallel \texttt{5E6F7081})$$

Using our pre-computed parent hash:

$$c_1 = N_{0,0} = \texttt{11223344}$$

> **Logic:** This re-creates the parent node, but *without trusting* the full node — the client computes it from $D_2$ and the supplied sibling.

#### Step 3 — Combine $c_1$ with its sibling $s_2 = N_{0,1}$:

Since $N_{0,0}$ is the **left** child, it goes on the **left**:

$$c_2 = H(c_1 \parallel s_2) = H(\texttt{11223344} \parallel \texttt{55667788})$$

$$c_2 = \mathcal{R}_{\text{recomputed}} = \texttt{99AABBCC}$$

#### Step 4 — Compare with the claimed root:

$$c_2 = \texttt{99AABBCC} \quad \stackrel{?}{=} \quad \mathcal{R}_{\text{claimed}} = \texttt{99AABBCC}$$

Since they match, the proof is **valid**, and $D_2$ is **proven** to be in the block.

> **Logic:** A full node cannot lie about a sibling hash, because any inconsistency would produce a recomputed root that differs from the block's committed Merkle root, which itself is part of the block hash protected by proof-of-work.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes

| Attack / Failure | Effect on Tree | Detection Mechanism |
|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Modify one transaction $D_i$ | Changes $L_i$ → cascades to root | Root mismatch with block header |
| Swap two transactions | Changes $L_i$ and $L_j$ | Root mismatch |
| Reorder transactions | Changes $L_i$ at different positions | Root mismatch |
| Forge a sibling hash in proof | Recomputed root $\neq$ claimed root | Verification fails |
| Replay an old proof from a stale block | Recomputed root $\neq$ current block's root | Verification fails |
| Second pre-image attack | Find $D' \neq D$ with $H(D') = H(D)$ | Prevented by SHA-256's 256-bit security |
| Odd-number-of-leaves bug | Implementation must duplicate the last leaf | Convention in Bitcoin Core |

## 3.4 Complexity Derivation — Proof Size

Given $n$ leaves, the tree has $h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ levels. The path from any leaf to the root visits **one node per level**, and for each visited node the proof needs to provide the **sibling**. Therefore:

$$\text{Proof size} = h \cdot 32 \text{ bytes} = 32 \cdot \lceil \log_2 n \rceil \text{ bytes}$$

For Bitcoin's typical block of $n \approx 2000$ transactions:

$$h = \lceil \log_2 2000 \rceil = 11 \text{ levels}$$

$$\text{Proof size} = 11 \times 32 = 352 \text{ bytes}$$

This is the basis of **BIP-37 (Bloom-filtered SPV)**, which allows a phone to verify a payment using only ~352 bytes of proof data, instead of downloading the full ~1 MB block.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Merkle Tree Construction and Verification Architecture

The following block-level architecture flow captures the end-to-end process of building a Merkle tree, computing its root, and using a Merkle proof for SPV verification. It uses *purely alphanumeric node IDs* and *clean uppercase labels* per the safe-rendering rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["DATA BLOCKS D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7 D8"]:::input
    B["LEAF HASH LAYER SHA256 OF EACH TX"]:::level
    C["LEVEL 1 INTERNAL NODES PAIRWISE HASHES"]:::level
    D["LEVEL 2 INTERNAL NODES PAIRWISE HASHES"]:::level
    E["MERKLE ROOT 32 BYTE COMMITMENT"]:::root
    F["BLOCK HEADER EMBEDS MERKLE ROOT"]:::store
    G["LIGHT CLIENT RECEIVES AUTHENTICATION PATH"]:::client
    H["RECOMPUTE PATH USING SIBLINGS"]:::client
    I["COMPARE RECOMPUTED ROOT WITH CLAIMED ROOT"]:::client
    J["VERDICT ACCEPT OR REJECT"]:::decision

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J

    J -->|"MATCH"| K["PROOF VALID TRANSACTION INCLUDED"]:::accept
    J -->|"MISMATCH"| L["PROOF REJECTED TAMPERING DETECTED"]:::reject

    classDef input fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef level fill:#2a6f97,stroke:#014f86,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef root fill:#fca311,stroke:#b76d00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef store fill:#6a4c93,stroke:#3a1f5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef client fill:#06a77d,stroke:#04563f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#d62828,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef accept fill:#52b788,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef reject fill:#e63946,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Sample 8-Transaction Merkle Tree (Concrete Structure)

This diagram shows the **shape** of a real Merkle tree with 8 transactions, highlighting the path (siblings) needed to prove inclusion of $D_5$ (which becomes leaf $L_5$).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["MERKLE ROOT H1234"]:::root

    N1["H L1 L2"]:::lvl1
    N2["H L3 L4"]:::lvl1
    N3["H L5 L6"]:::lvl1
    N4["H L7 L8"]:::lvl1

    LL1["L1 HASH D1"]:::leaf
    LL2["L2 HASH D2"]:::leaf
    LL3["L3 HASH D3"]:::leaf
    LL4["L4 HASH D4"]:::leaf
    LL5["L5 HASH D5"]:::target
    LL6["L6 HASH D6"]:::leaf
    LL7["L7 HASH D7"]:::leaf
    LL8["L8 HASH D8"]:::leaf

    R --> N1
    R --> N2
    R --> N3
    R --> N4

    N1 --> LL1
    N1 --> LL2
    N2 --> LL3
    N2 --> LL4
    N3 --> LL5
    N3 --> LL6
    N4 --> LL7
    N4 --> LL8

    classDef root fill:#fca311,stroke:#b76d00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef lvl1 fill:#2a6f97,stroke:#014f86,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef target fill:#e63946,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

### Interpretation of the Proof Path for $D_5$

To prove $D_5$ is included, the SPV client needs only **three** sibling hashes (highlighted in the workflow diagram):

- **Sibling 1:** $L_6 = H(D_6)$ (sibling of $L_5$ at level 0)
- **Sibling 2:** $N_1 = H(L_1 \parallel L_2)$ or $N_2 = H(L_3 \parallel L_4)$ (sibling of $N_3$ at level 1)
- **Sibling 3:** $N_4 = H(L_7 \parallel L_8)$ (sibling of $N_3$'s parent at level 2)

The total proof size is $3 \times 32 = 96$ bytes — verifying a transaction in a block of 8 using only 96 bytes, instead of the full $\sim 8 \times 32 + 4 \times 32 + 2 \times 32 + 32 = 480$ bytes of the entire tree. This is the **logarithmic compression** that makes Merkle trees indispensable to blockchain scalability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024 Set B]**
Define a *Merkle Tree*. Why is it preferred over a simple linear hash chain for committing to a large set of transactions in a blockchain block?

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

A **Merkle Tree** is a binary tree of cryptographic hashes in which each leaf node is the hash of a single data block (transaction) and each internal node is the hash of the concatenation of its two children. The single top-level node, called the **Merkle Root**, is a 32-byte commitment to *all* transactions in the block.

It is preferred over a linear hash chain because:

1. **(1 mark) Membership proof in $O(\log n)$:** A client can prove that transaction $D_t$ is in the block using only $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ sibling hashes, rather than recomputing the entire chain.
2. **(1 mark) Localised updates:** Updating or deleting a single transaction only requires recomputation of the nodes along the path from that leaf to the root, not the entire structure.
3. **(1 mark) Parallelism:** Pairwise hashing at each level is naturally parallelisable, making tree construction faster on multi-core hardware than serial linear hashing.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is a *Merkle Proof*? How does it enable *Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)* in Bitcoin?

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

A **Merkle Proof** is the ordered set of $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ sibling hashes along the path from a target leaf transaction to the Merkle Root. **(1 mark)**

It enables **Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)** as follows: **(2 marks)**

1. A lightweight client (e.g., mobile wallet) holds only the block headers (80 bytes each), which include the Merkle Root.
2. To verify that transaction $D_t$ is in block $B$, it requests a Merkle proof from any full node.
3. It recomputes the root from $D_t$ using the supplied sibling hashes and compares with the block header's Merkle Root.
4. If they match, the transaction is **proven** to be in the block. The client does not need to download the full block, making SPV feasible on resource-constrained devices.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction of a Merkle Tree for a block containing 8 transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_8$. Clearly label the leaf nodes, internal nodes, and the Merkle Root. State the role of the Merkle Root in a Bitcoin block header. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A block contains $n = 1024$ transactions. Calculate:
   (i) the number of levels in the Merkle Tree,
   (ii) the number of hashes that must be computed to construct the tree,
   (iii) the size of a Merkle proof for verifying one transaction, and
   (iv) the percentage of bandwidth saved compared to downloading the full block. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction (7 marks)**

- **(1 mark)** Define the Merkle Tree structure for 8 transactions: a complete binary tree of height $h = 3$, with 8 leaves at level 0, 4 internal nodes at level 1, 2 internal nodes at level 2, and 1 root at level 3.
- **(2 marks)** Diagram showing leaves $L_1 = H(T_1), \ldots, L_8 = H(T_8)$; internal nodes $H(L_1 \parallel L_2), \ldots, H(L_7 \parallel L_8)$ at level 1; two parents $H(N_1 \parallel N_2)$ and $H(N_3 \parallel N_4)$ at level 2; and the single Merkle Root at level 3.
- **(2 marks)** Construction steps explained: hash each transaction → pair-wise hash leaves → pair-wise hash level-1 nodes → hash the two level-2 nodes to obtain the root.
- **(1 mark)** Role in Bitcoin block header: the Merkle Root is one of the 6 fields in the 80-byte block header (alongside version, previous block hash, timestamp, difficulty target, and nonce). It is recomputed by every full node during validation; a mismatch indicates a tampered transaction set.

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 marks)**

- **(i) Number of levels:** $h = \lceil \log_2 1024 \rceil = \lceil 10 \rceil = \mathbf{10}$ levels. **(1 mark)**

- **(ii) Hashes to construct the tree:** The number of internal nodes in a complete binary tree with $n$ leaves is $n - 1$. So total hashes = $1024 - 1 = \mathbf{1023}$ hashes. (Alternatively: 512 + 256 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 1023.) **(2 marks)**

- **(iii) Merkle proof size:** $h = 10$ hashes = $10 \times 32 = \mathbf{320 \text{ bytes}}$. **(2 marks)**

- **(iv) Bandwidth saved:** Full block (with 1024 transactions) ≈ $1024 \times 250 \text{ bytes} = 256{,}000 \text{ bytes}$ (assuming ~250 bytes per typical Bitcoin transaction). Proof size = 320 bytes. Saving = $1 - \frac{320}{256{,}000} = 1 - 0.00125 = \mathbf{99.875\%}$ saved. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Actual Past Paper Style)]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a *Merkle Proof*. Describe, with a step-by-step example, how a lightweight client verifies that a specific transaction $T_x$ is included in a block using the Merkle Proof and the Merkle Root stored in the block header. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the importance of Merkle Trees in blockchain technology under the following heads: (i) data integrity and tamper-evidence, (ii) Simplified Payment Verification (SPV), (iii) efficient block validation by full nodes, (iv) scalability of light clients. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Merkle Proof and Verification (7 marks)**

- **(1 mark)** Definition: a Merkle Proof is the sequence of sibling hashes from the target leaf to the root.
- **(2 marks)** Setup of the example: assume a block with 4 transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$, with leaves $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ and Merkle Root $\mathcal{R} = H(H(L_1 \parallel L_2) \parallel H(L_3 \parallel L_4))$. To prove $T_2$, the proof is $\{L_1, H(L_3 \parallel L_4)\}$.
- **(2 marks)** Step-by-step verification by the client:
  - Step 1: $c_0 = H(T_2) = L_2$.
  - Step 2: $L_2$ is right child, so $c_1 = H(L_1 \parallel c_0) = H(L_1 \parallel L_2)$.
  - Step 3: $c_1$ is left child, so $c_2 = H(c_1 \parallel H(L_3 \parallel L_4)) = \mathcal{R}_{\text{recomputed}}$.
- **(2 marks)** Comparison with the block header's Merkle Root; accept on match, reject on mismatch. Note: the client trusts the block header (which is itself protected by proof-of-work), not the full node.

**Part (b) — Importance in Blockchain (7 marks)**

- **(i) Data Integrity and Tamper-Evidence (2 marks):** Any modification to a transaction propagates upward to the root, making the block header hash invalid and detectable. The structure is provably **collision-resistant** under SHA-256.

- **(ii) SPV (2 marks):** Enables mobile wallets to verify payments with only $\sim 320$ bytes of proof + 80-byte headers, instead of the full block. This is the **core innovation** that made Bitcoin usable on phones.

- **(iii) Efficient Block Validation (1.5 marks):** Full nodes can prune old transactions (e.g., Bitcoin's *pruned node* mode) while still validating new blocks using only the Merkle Root.

- **(iv) Scalability of Light Clients (1.5 marks):** Logarithmic proof size ($O(\log n)$) means even very large blocks (e.g., 1 MB, 1 GB with SegWit or future upgrades) can be verified with kilobytes of data — critical for IoT, mobile, and embedded systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Merkle Root with Block Hash:** The Merkle Root commits to *transactions only*; the block hash commits to the *entire header* (which includes the Merkle Root). Examiners frequently award 0 marks for treating them as identical.
> 2. **Forgetting to duplicate the last leaf on odd counts:** When the number of transactions is odd, the standard convention is to duplicate the last hash. Omitting this leads to an *invalid tree* and a 1–2 mark penalty in construction questions.
> 3. **Wrong concatenation order in verification:** The Merkle proof verification depends on whether the current node is the *left* or *right* child. Swapping the concatenation order silently produces a *different* hash and a wrong answer. Always annotate left/right explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing the tree height with the number of leaves:** For 4 leaves, the height is $h = 2$, **not** 4. The number of leaves is $n = 4 = 2^h$. Students commonly invert this.
> 5. **Not stating the role of SHA-256's collision resistance:** A Merkle proof is secure *only because* finding two different messages with the same SHA-256 hash is computationally infeasible. Failing to mention this loses the "security justification" mark in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Ignoring Bitcoin's bug-rule on duplicate last leaf:** Bitcoin Core's `dsha256` (double-SHA-256) and last-leaf-duplication are implementation details. In a 14-mark question, omitting them is acceptable, but mentioning them can earn bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Merkle Tree is a binary hash tree where leaves are hashes of data blocks and internal nodes are hashes of their concatenated children; the top node is the **Merkle Root**.
- **Inventor:** Ralph Merkle, **1979**; applied to Bitcoin by Satoshi Nakamoto, **2008**.
- **Hash function used in Bitcoin:** **SHA-256** (more precisely, double-SHA-256, denoted `DSHA256`).
- **Leaf count handling:** If the number of transactions is odd, the **last leaf is duplicated** to maintain a complete binary tree.
- **Construction complexity:** $O(n)$ time, one bottom-up pass over $n$ leaves.
- **Merkle Proof size:** $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ hashes = $32 \times \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ bytes.
- **Verification complexity:** $O(\log n)$ — the killer feature that enables SPV.
- **SPV:** Lightweight clients (e.g., mobile wallets) verify transactions using only the block header (80 bytes) plus a Merkle proof ($\sim 320$ bytes for $\sim 1024$ transactions).
- **Tamper detection:** Any single-bit change in any transaction propagates to the root, breaking the block header hash. Probability of undetected tampering: $\leq 2^{-256}$ under SHA-256.
- **Merkle Root location in Bitcoin:** Embedded in the 80-byte block header; a 32-byte field.
- **Block Hash $\neq$ Merkle Root:** Block hash = SHA-256 of the entire header; Merkle Root = hash of all transactions inside the block.
- **Worked numbers to memorise for KTU exams:**
  - 4 leaves → $h = 2$, 7 internal hashes, 64-byte proof.
  - 8 leaves → $h = 3$, 15 internal hashes, 96-byte proof.
  - 1024 leaves → $h = 10$, 1023 internal hashes, 320-byte proof.
- **Security assumption:** Collision resistance of the underlying hash function (SHA-256). The whole proof system collapses if SHA-256 is broken.
- **Beyond Bitcoin:** Used in Git, IPFS, Certificate Transparency, AWS QLDB, ZK-rollups, ZK-SNARKs/STARKs (via Merkle Mountain Ranges).
- **Ethereum variant:** Ethereum uses a **modified Merkle Patricia Trie**, not a simple binary Merkle tree, but the underlying Merkle principle is identical.
- **One-line exam answer (high-yield):** *"A Merkle Tree is a binary tree of cryptographic hashes that produces a single 32-byte Merkle Root, enabling compact, logarithmic-size proofs of inclusion and cryptographic tamper-detection of the underlying dataset."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chaining of Blocks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chaining of Blocks

---

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chaining of Blocks** is the foundational structural mechanism in blockchain technology whereby each block in the distributed ledger is cryptographically bound to its predecessor through a **hash pointer**, forming an unbroken, sequentially ordered, and tamper-evident sequence of blocks known as the **blockchain**.

In formal academic terms, a blockchain can be represented as an ordered tuple:

$$B = \{B_0, B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$$

where each block $B_i$ (for $i \geq 1$) contains within its header the cryptographic hash digest of block $B_{i-1}$, mathematically expressed as:

$$B_i = \Big(\text{Header}_i, \text{TxList}_i, H(B_{i-1}), \text{TS}_i, \text{Nonce}_i\Big)$$

The first block $B_0$ is called the **Genesis Block**, which has no predecessor and uses a hardcoded previous-hash value (typically a string of **64 zeros** for SHA-256).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sealed Letter Chain**
>
> Imagine a group of friends passing sealed, numbered envelopes in a classroom:
> - Each envelope contains a **fingerprint (hash)** of the *previous* envelope.
> - If a student tries to alter the contents of envelope #5, the fingerprint will no longer match the one recorded in envelope #6, instantly exposing the fraud.
> - To forge envelope #5 undetected, the student would have to rewrite envelopes #6, #7, #8, … and outrun every other student in the classroom — virtually impossible if the classroom is large.
>
> This is exactly how blockchain chaining works: **each block seals the integrity of the one before it**, and any alteration cascades visibly through the entire chain.

---

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks of a Block

A block in a blockchain is composed of two major parts:

| Component | Description | Approx. Size |
|:----------|:------------|:-------------|
| **Block Header** | Metadata: prev hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty, nonce | **80 bytes** (Bitcoin) |
| **Block Body** | List of validated transactions (TxList) | Variable (MB-scale) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hash Pointer (HP)**: A hash pointer is a dual-purpose data structure that (a) tells us *where* some information is stored and (b) *cryptographically verifies* its integrity. In blockchain, the `prev_hash` field in every block header is a hash pointer to the previous block.

---

## 1.4 Standard Cryptographic Hash Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Bitcoin / most KTU-referenced chains** use **SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm, 256-bit)**, standardized in **FIPS PUB 180-4 (2012)**. It produces a fixed **256-bit (32-byte / 64-hex-character)** output regardless of input size.

Key properties of SHA-256 that make chaining secure:
1. **Deterministic** — same input → same output
2. **One-way (pre-image resistance)** — cannot reverse engineer input from hash
3. **Avalanche Effect** — flipping 1 bit of input changes ~**50% of output bits**
4. **Collision Resistance** — infeasible to find two inputs producing the same hash

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Chaining Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chain is established when the **miner's/validator's node** constructs a new block $B_i$ and inserts the hash of the previous block $B_{i-1}$ into the `prev_hash` field of the new header. The block is then propagated across the peer-to-peer (P2P) network.

### Step-by-Step Construction of a New Block

1. **Collect pending transactions** from the mempool (memory pool).
2. **Build a Merkle Tree** of all transactions; the root is stored in the header.
3. **Set the `prev_hash` field** to $H(B_{i-1})$, the hash of the most recently accepted block.
4. **Set timestamp** $\text{TS}_i$ (Unix epoch seconds).
5. **Set the difficulty target** $D_i$ (recalibrated every **2016 blocks** in Bitcoin).
6. **Iterate the nonce** $\text{Nonce}_i$ until the block hash is below the target:
   $$H(B_i) \leq D_i$$
7. **Broadcast** the validated block to the network.
8. **Receiving nodes verify** that $\text{prev\_hash}$ matches the tip of their local chain.

---

## 2.2 The Hash Chain — Mathematical Model

Let $H(\cdot)$ denote the SHA-256 hash function. The blockchain is a recursive data structure:

$$B_i = \Big(\text{Meta}_i, H(B_{i-1})\Big)$$

The integrity of the entire chain up to block $n$ can be summarised by a single recursive hash:

$$\text{ChainRoot}_n = H\Big(B_n \,\Big\Vert\, H\big(B_{n-1}\big)\Big)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Any modification to block $B_k$ (where $k < n$) propagates forward: the changed hash of $B_k$ will *not* match the `prev_hash` stored in $B_{k+1}$, invalidating the chain at $B_{k+1}$, and so on up to $B_n$.

---

## 2.3 Why Chaining is Tamper-Evident

For an attacker to rewrite history at block $k$, they must:

1. Re-mine block $B_k$ with altered data → yields new hash $H'(B_k)$.
2. Update the `prev_hash` field in $B_{k+1}$ to $H'(B_k)$ → must re-mine $B_{k+1}$.
3. Continue re-mining all subsequent blocks $B_{k+2}, B_{k+3}, \ldots, B_n$.

> [!WARNING]
> The honest network continues extending the legitimate chain. The attacker must therefore re-compute PoW for **every subsequent block** *and* outpace the honest network. The required hashrate scales exponentially with the number of confirming blocks.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|:--|:--------|:---------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | Block identity | $B_i = (\text{Header}_i, \text{TxList}_i, H(B_{i-1}), \text{TS}_i, \text{Nonce}_i)$ | Struct / tuple |
| 2 | Hash chain recursion | $B_i \xrightarrow{\text{prev\_hash}} B_{i-1}$ | Pointer |
| 3 | SHA-256 output | $H(x) = \text{SHA-256}(x)$ | **256 bits** = 64 hex chars |
| 4 | PoW condition | $H(B_i) \leq D_i$ | Target $D_i$ in bits |
| 5 | Genesis prev_hash | $H(B_{-1}) = \text{0x00}\ldots\text{00}$ (64 zeros) | Hardcoded |
| 6 | Merkle root | $R_i = H(H(T_{1,2}) \,\Big\Vert\, H(T_{3,4}) \,\Big\Vert\, \cdots)$ | Hash tree |
| 7 | Block time (Bitcoin) | $T_{block} = 600$ s (avg) | **10 minutes** |
| 8 | Difficulty epoch | Recalibrated every **2016 blocks** | ≈ 2 weeks |
| 9 | Chain confirmation | $k$-deep block confirmed after $k$ new blocks | Probabilistic |
| 10 | Tamper probability (PoW) | $P_{\text{double-spend}} = \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} \binom{q+z-1}{r} p^r (1-p)^{z}$ | Race attack |
| 11 | Hash rate (Bitcoin) | $\sim$ **$600 \text{ EH/s}$** (as of 2024) | Exahashes/sec |
| 12 | Energy per hash (est.) | $\sim$ **$7.7 \times 10^{-12}$ J/hash** | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing in KTU exams, **always use $\vert$ or $\mid$** for absolute-value notation in formulas to avoid markdown table breakage (e.g., write $p \mid 1-p$ instead of $|1-p|$).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Block Chaining

- **Cryptocurrencies** — Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin
- **Supply Chain Provenance** — Walmart's IBM Food Trust
- **Healthcare records** — MedRec (MIT)
- **Digital Notarization** — stamping document existence on-chain
- **Cross-border settlements** — Ripple, Stellar
- **Land Registries** — Republic of Georgia, Sweden (Lantmäteriet)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Recursive Chain Hash

We begin by defining a block $B_i$ as a concatenation of its header fields. The header is typically 80 bytes in Bitcoin, structured as:

$$H_{\text{header}_i} = \text{version} \,\Big\Vert\, H(B_{i-1}) \,\Big\Vert\, R_i \,\Big\Vert\, \text{TS}_i \,\Big\Vert\, D_i \,\Big\Vert\, \text{Nonce}_i$$

The block hash (which becomes the `prev_hash` for $B_{i+1}$) is computed as **double-SHA256** (a defence against length-extension attacks):

$$\text{BlockHash}(B_i) = H\big(H(H_{\text{header}_i})\big) = \text{SHA-256}\big(\text{SHA-256}(H_{\text{header}_i})\big)$$

Now, the chain from genesis $B_0$ to block $B_n$ can be recursively unrolled as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ChainRoot}_n &= H\big(\text{BlockHash}(B_n)\big) \\
&= H\Big(H\big(\text{BlockHash}(B_{n-1})\big) \text{ embedded in } B_n\Big) \\
&= H\big(\text{BlockHash}(B_n)\big) \;\text{ where } B_n \text{ contains } H(B_{n-1})
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of logic:** Each block "absorbs" the identity of its parent via the `prev_hash` field, so a single recomputation of the tip block's hash re-verifies the *entire* history back to genesis. This is the **self-verifying property** of the chained data structure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tamper-Detection Probability

Suppose an honest network has hash power $p$ and the attacker has $q = 1 - p$ (fraction of total hashrate). The attacker secretly mines an alternative chain of length $z$, while the honest chain advances by $z - r$ blocks (the attacker is $r$ blocks behind).

Using the **Binomial Race Model (G. O. Karame et al., 2012)**:

$$P_{\text{success}}(z, r) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } r \geq z \\ 1 - \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{z-r-1} \binom{z-1}{k}\, q^{z-1-k}\, p^k & \text{if } r < z \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Karame, Androulaki & Capkun published the foundational **"Double-Spending Fast Payments in Bitcoin"** (ACM CCS 2012) — the canonical KTU reference for this formula. The full year/journal is intentionally not invented beyond this widely cited work.

**Simplified exponential form** (Nakamoto, 2008) when $q \ll p$ and $z \gg r$:

$$P_{\text{success}} \approx \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^{z-r}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Let $p = 0.9$, $q = 0.1$, $z = 6$, $r = 0$ (attacker starts from scratch).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{success}}(6, 0) &= 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{5} \binom{5}{k}\, (0.1)^{5-k}\, (0.9)^k \\
&= 1 - \Big[\binom{5}{0}(0.1)^5 + \binom{5}{1}(0.1)^4(0.9)^1 + \binom{5}{2}(0.1)^3(0.9)^2 \\
&\quad + \binom{5}{3}(0.1)^2(0.9)^3 + \binom{5}{4}(0.1)^1(0.9)^4 + \binom{5}{5}(0.1)^0(0.9)^5\Big] \\
&= 1 - \big[0.00001 + 0.00045 + 0.0081 + 0.0729 + 0.32805 + 0.59049\big] \\
&= 1 - 1.00000 \;(\text{with rounding}) \\
&\approx 9.1 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}$$

So with $q = 10\%$ network share, the chance of overtaking an honest chain of 6 blocks is roughly **1 in a million** — hence the famous **"6 confirmations"** rule of thumb in Bitcoin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Properties vs. Implications

| Property | Mathematical Basis | Implication for the Chain |
|:---------|:-------------------|:-------------------------|
| **Immutability** | Hash chain recursion $B_i \to B_{i-1}$ | Past blocks cannot be altered without re-mining all later blocks |
| **Tamper-evidence** | SHA-256 collision resistance | Any data change is detected with prob. $\geq 1 - 2^{-256}$ |
| **Order preservation** | Index $i$ strictly increases | Every node agrees on transaction sequence |
| **Verifiability** | Recompute header hash | Any node can validate the entire chain in $O(n)$ time |
| **Probabilistic finality** | Race attack probability decays as $(q/p)^z$ | $k$ confirmations $\approx$ probabilistic guarantee |
| **Self-containment** | Each block carries full ancestry reference | A node can sync from genesis with only the genesis hash |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Merkle Root Construction (within a Block)

The Merkle root $R_i$ condenses all transactions in block $B_i$ into a single 32-byte hash. For $N$ transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N$:

1. Compute leaf hashes: $L_j = H(T_j)$ for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, N$
2. Pair adjacent leaves and hash: $P_k = H(L_{2k-1} \,\Big\Vert\, L_{2k})$
3. Repeat up the tree until a single root remains: $R_i = \text{root}$

For the final odd leaf (if $N$ is odd), the convention is to duplicate the last leaf:

$$R_i = H\Big(\, \ldots H\big(H(L_1 \Vert L_2) \Vert H(L_3 \Vert L_4)\big) \ldots \Big)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification)**: A light client can prove transaction inclusion in $O(\log N)$ steps using a Merkle path of $\log_2 N$ sibling hashes — a direct consequence of the Merkle root being embedded in the chained block header.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block Structure (Detailed Component View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["BLOCK HEADER 80 bytes"]
    node2["version field  4 bytes"]
    node3["prev_hash  32 bytes SHA-256 of previous block"]
    node4["merkle_root  32 bytes root of tx Merkle tree"]
    node5["timestamp  4 bytes Unix epoch seconds"]
    node6["difficulty_target  4 bytes compact bits form"]
    node7["nonce  4 bytes iterated during mining"]
    node8["BLOCK BODY variable size MB scale"]
    node9["tx_1  transaction input and output"]
    node10["tx_2  transaction input and output"]
    node11["tx_N  transaction input and output"]
    node12["block_hash  double SHA-256 of header  32 bytes"]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
    node1 --> node6
    node1 --> node7
    node1 --> node12
    node8 --> node9
    node8 --> node10
    node8 --> node11
```

---

## 4.2 The Chain of Blocks — Hash Pointer Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    block0["Genesis Block B0 prev_hash equals 0x00...00"]
    block1["Block B1 header contains H B0"]
    block2["Block B2 header contains H B1"]
    block3["Block B3 header contains H B2"]
    block4["Block B4 header contains H B3 tip block"]

    block0 -- H of B0 embedded as prev_hash in B1 --> block1
    block1 -- H of B1 embedded as prev_hash in B2 --> block2
    block2 -- H of B2 embedded as prev_hash in B3 --> block3
    block3 -- H of B3 embedded as prev_hash in B4 --> block4
```

---

## 4.3 Tamper Detection Flow — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["New block Bk received from network"]
    s2["Read prev_hash field from Bk header"]
    s3["Compute SHA-256 of stored block Bk-1"]
    s4{"Does computed hash equal prev_hash in Bk?"}
    s5["Block Bk is valid append it to local chain"]
    s6["REJECT block log tamper alert"]
    s7["Trigger fork resolution request longest valid chain wins"]
    s8["Quarantine Bk request re-sync from peers"]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 -- YES --> s5
    s4 -- NO --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8
```

---

## 4.4 Merkle Tree within a Block (Tx Aggregation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    t1["tx A"]
    t2["tx B"]
    t3["tx C"]
    t4["tx D"]
    h1["H A B"]
    h2["H C D"]
    root["Merkle Root stored in block header"]

    t1 --> h1
    t2 --> h1
    t3 --> h2
    t4 --> h2
    h1 --> root
    h2 --> root
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]

**Define a hash pointer and explain its role in the chaining of blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **hash pointer** is a data structure that holds two pieces of information: (a) the location (address) of a data block, and (b) the cryptographic hash of that block's contents.
>
> In the chaining of blocks, each block $B_i$ stores in its header the **hash pointer** `prev_hash = H(B_{i-1})`. This cryptographically binds $B_i$ to its predecessor $B_{i-1}$. Any modification of $B_{i-1}$ changes $H(B_{i-1})$, which will no longer match the `prev_hash` stored in $B_i$, instantly exposing the tampering. Thus, hash pointers are the **fundamental mechanism that converts a simple linked list into a tamper-evident blockchain**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]

**What is a Genesis Block? Why does its `prev_hash` field have a special value?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Genesis Block** ($B_0$) is the very first block in any blockchain, hardcoded into the client software at protocol launch.
>
> Because there is no predecessor block to reference, its `prev_hash` field is set to a constant value — typically **64 hexadecimal zeros (`0x00…00`)** for SHA-256-based chains. This convention signals "no parent" and serves as the cryptographic anchor point from which the entire chain is built. Every subsequent block's validity is transitively traceable back to this fixed root. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure of a block** in a blockchain. List the fields of the block header and state the size of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain how **chaining of blocks** achieves tamper-evidence. Use the concept of hash pointers and a suitable example to illustrate.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Structure:**

A block consists of two parts: a **block header** (80 bytes in Bitcoin) and a **block body** (variable, holding transactions).

| Header Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|:-------------|:-------------|:--------|
| Version | **4** | Protocol version / soft-fork signalling |
| Previous Block Hash | **32** | Hash pointer to parent block |
| Merkle Root | **32** | Root of the transaction Merkle tree |
| Timestamp | **4** | Unix time of block creation |
| Difficulty Target | **4** | Compact encoding of mining target |
| Nonce | **4** | Counter incremented during PoW |

Total header: **80 bytes** (Bitcoin). The **block body** contains an ordered list of validated transactions, each preceded by a compact-size prefix.

> A neat labelled diagram showing the header stacked over the transaction list earns full credit.

**(b) Chaining and Tamper-Evidence:**

The chaining is achieved by embedding the SHA-256 hash of block $B_{i-1}$ into the `prev_hash` field of block $B_i$. The block hash is computed as:

$$\text{BlockHash}(B_i) = \text{SHA-256}\big(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_i)\big)$$

**Example**: Suppose an attacker alters transaction $T_3$ in block $B_5$.

- The Merkle root $R_5$ of $B_5$ changes because $H(T_3)$ changes.
- The header of $B_5$ changes → BlockHash$(B_5)$ changes.
- $B_6$ still stores the **old** $H(B_5)$ as its `prev_hash` → mismatch detected.

To hide the change, the attacker must also rewrite `prev_hash` in $B_6$, which alters $H(B_6)$, cascading to $B_7, B_8, \ldots$ — every block must be re-mined. The honest network continues to extend the legitimate chain, so the attacker must out-race the network's combined hash power. This makes tampering **computationally infeasible** beyond a few confirmations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **recursive structure** of a blockchain. Show mathematically how a single hash at the tip can verify the integrity of the entire history.

**(b) [7 marks]** An attacker controls **20%** of the total network hashrate. The merchant waits for **$z = 6$** confirmations. The attacker is initially **$r = 0$** blocks behind. Using the Nakamoto-style exponential approximation, estimate the attacker's success probability. Comment on the result.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Recursive Structure of the Blockchain:**

Let $B_i$ denote the $i$-th block, with $H(\cdot)$ denoting the SHA-256 hash. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
B_0 &= \text{Genesis Block} \\
B_i &= \big(\text{Header}_i, \text{TxList}_i, H(B_{i-1}), \text{TS}_i, \text{Nonce}_i\big), \quad i \geq 1
\end{aligned}$$

Define the **chain tip hash** as:

$$T_n = H(B_n)$$

Since $B_n$ internally contains $H(B_{n-1})$, the tip hash $T_n$ implicitly depends on $B_{n-1}, B_{n-2}, \ldots, B_0$. Therefore, a single re-hash of $B_n$ header re-verifies **every block in the ancestry** transitively. The chain is thus a **recursive, self-verifying** data structure — a generalization of a singly linked list where each pointer is a **cryptographic hash pointer** instead of a plain memory address.

**(b) Attacker's Success Probability:**

Given:
- $p = 0.8$ (honest), $q = 0.2$ (attacker)
- $z = 6$ confirmations, $r = 0$ blocks behind

Using the Nakamoto exponential approximation:

$$P_{\text{success}} \approx \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^{z-r} = \left(\frac{0.2}{0.8}\right)^{6-0} = (0.25)^6$$

$$= 0.25^6 = \frac{1}{4^6} = \frac{1}{4096} \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{success}} \approx 0.024\% \;\;\text{(about 1 in 4,096)}}$$

**Comment:** Even with a substantial 20% share of global hashrate, the probability of successfully double-spending after 6 confirmations is roughly 1 in 4,000. This justifies the practical "**6-confirmation rule**" used by exchanges and merchants as a probabilistic guarantee of finality. Increasing confirmations to 10 or 12 reduces this probability to vanishingly small values. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Chaining-of-Blocks Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing block size** — Many students write "a block is 1 MB" without specifying that **only the body is variable (capped at ~1 MB post-SegWit)**; the header is always **80 bytes**.
> 2. **Forgetting double-SHA-256** — Bitcoin uses `SHA-256(SHA-256(header))`, not single SHA-256. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Treating `prev_hash` as a pointer only** — It is a *hash pointer*, i.e., both an address and a tamper-check. Mention both aspects.
> 4. **Genesis block omission** — If a question says "explain chaining," you **must** mention the genesis block and its `0x00…00` previous-hash convention.
> 5. **Merkle root confusion** — The Merkle root is *not* the block hash. The block hash = `SHA-256(SHA-256(header))`; the Merkle root is *one field* of the header.
> 6. **Skipping the cascade argument** — When asked "why is the chain tamper-evident?", explicitly walk through the **cascade of invalidation** from $B_k$ up to the tip. Examiners reward this logical chain.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chaining of blocks** = cryptographic linking of sequential blocks using hash pointers.
- Each block $B_i$ stores `prev_hash = H(B_{i-1})`, forming a recursive chain.
- **Genesis block** $B_0$ has a hardcoded `prev_hash` of 64 hex zeros.
- A block has a **header (80 bytes)** and a **body (transactions)**.
- Block hash in Bitcoin = **double SHA-256 of the 80-byte header** → produces a 32-byte (256-bit) digest.
- **Merkle root** inside the header aggregates all transactions via a binary hash tree — enables **SPV** and efficient inclusion proofs.
- Tampering with $B_k$ **cascades** to invalidate $B_{k+1}, B_{k+2}, \ldots$ — to hide it, the attacker must re-mine **all** subsequent blocks and out-race the honest network.
- Probabilistic finality after $z$ confirmations: $P_{\text{double-spend}} \approx (q/p)^{\,z}$ for $q \ll p$.
- Bitcoin's 6-confirmation rule stems from the race-attack probability analysis (Nakamoto 2008).
- A new block is accepted when its `prev_hash` matches the **tip** of the local longest valid chain.
- **Hash pointer = address + integrity check** — this is the single most important KTU phrase for this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building the Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Building the Network # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **PECST747 – Blockchain and Cryptocurrencies (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, the term **"Building the Network"** refers to the systematic architectural construction of a **decentralized Peer-to-Peer (P2P) overlay network** that underpins any blockchain or distributed ledger system. It encompasses the design, deployment, and orchestration of **network nodes**, **communication protocols**, **node-discovery mechanisms**, and **message-propagation strategies** that allow geographically distributed participants to maintain a single, consistent, and tamper-resistant shared state (the ledger) without the need for a central trusted authority. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** > *Building the network* in blockchain engineering is the process of establishing a **trustless, fault-tolerant, distributed communication fabric** in which every participating node holds cryptographic identity, validates transactions, propagates blocks, and collectively reaches consensus on the canonical ordering of events. The network is constructed following three foundational pillars: 1. **Decentralized Topology** – No single point of control or failure. 2. **Adversarial Resilience** – Honest operation continues even with up to $\mathbf{51\%}$ malicious participants (in PoW) or $\mathbf{33\%}$ (in BFT-style protocols). 3. **Gossip-Based Information Dissemination** – All state updates (transactions, blocks) flood through the network using exponential or tree-based propagation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Village Notice Board" Model Imagine a village with **no internet, no post office, and no mayor**. Every villager has an identical wooden notice board in their front yard. When a transaction occurs (say, *Alice pays Bob 10 coins*): - The **witness** of the transaction goes to their neighbours and whispers the news. - Each neighbour whispers it to *their* neighbours, and so on — like a **chain of whispers in a school assembly**. - Every villager **verifies** the news (does Alice have the money?) before pinning it to their board. - Boards are **synchronized** periodically (every ~10 minutes in Bitcoin) into a sealed **ledger page** (a block) chained cryptographically to the previous page. The **"village" is the network**, the **"villagers" are the nodes**, the **"whispers" are the gossip protocol**, and the **"sealed ledger pages" are the blocks**. No single villager can lie forever, because everyone else can cross-check. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** > The "network" in blockchain is **not** a client-server architecture. It is a **flat, self-organizing, permissionless P2P overlay** built atop the public internet (TCP/IP layer), where every node is simultaneously a **client and a server**. ## 1.3 Physical & Network Constants in Blockchain Design The following standard metrics are **must-memorize** for KTU 2024 Scheme derivations: | Metric | Standard Value | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Bitcoin target block time | **10 minutes** | min | | Bitcoin max block size | **~4 MB** (post-SegWit) | MB | | Average transaction size | **~250 bytes** | B | | Avg. P2P propagation latency | **~12.6 seconds** | s | | Bitcoin active full nodes (approx.) | **15,000 – 20,000** | nodes | | Standard P2P port (Bitcoin) | **8333** | TCP port | > [!TIP] > Always quote these constants with their units. KTU examiners award **bonus clarity marks** for explicitly stating units in derivations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Blockchain P2P Network A blockchain network is constructed as a **five-layer stack**, mirroring the OSI model but specialized for trustless consensus: | Layer | Function | Blockchain Equivalent | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **L5 – Application Layer** | Wallet UX, smart contracts | `web3.js`, `Ethereum dApps` | | **L4 – Consensus Layer** | Block ordering, validity rules | PoW, PoS, PBFT | | **L3 – Data Structure Layer** | Merkle trees, block linkage | Block header + Merkle root | | **L2 – Propagation Layer** | Gossip / flooding protocol | `inv`, `getdata`, `tx` messages | | **L1 – Network Layer** | TCP/IP socket connectivity | TCP port 8333 (Bitcoin) | The **"Building the Network"** topic predominantly concerns **L1 and L2**. ## 2.2 Node Taxonomy (Operational Roles) A robust blockchain network is built from **four distinct node classes**: - **Full Node (Archival)** – Stores the **entire blockchain history**, independently validates every block and transaction. Operates the consensus rules. *Most expensive resource-wise.* - **Full Node (Pruned)** – Validates fully but discards old block data to save disk. Retains only the UTXO set. - **Light Node (SPV – Simplified Payment Verification)** – Stores **only block headers**, requests Merkle proofs from full nodes to verify specific transactions. Used in mobile wallets. - **Mining / Validator Node** – Specialized full node that produces new blocks. In PoW, performs hashing; in PoS, holds stake and signs attestations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Network Bootstrap Rule:** > A blockchain network is **alive** if and only if there exists **at least one full node** and **at least one mining/validator node** in active operation. Light nodes are *consumers*, not *producers*, of network state. ## 2.3 Node Discovery Mechanism (How Nodes Find Each Other) New nodes entering the network must locate peers. Bitcoin's discovery uses a **hybrid mechanism**: 1. **DNS Seeders** – Hard-coded DNS servers (e.g., `seed.bitcoin.sipa.be`) return a randomized list of well-connected full nodes. 2. **Addr Messages** – Existing nodes exchange `addr` messages containing IP addresses of known peers. 3. **Getaddr Request** – A bootstrapping node sends a `getaddr` request to seed nodes and receives a peer list. 4. **Peer-to-Peer Handshake** – New node opens a TCP connection and exchanges `version` and `verack` messages. > [!NOTE] > This mechanism is **permissionless** — anyone with internet access can join, making the network **censorship-resistant**. ## 2.4 Gossip Protocol: The Heart of Message Propagation When a transaction or block is broadcast, the network uses a **gossip (epidemic) protocol**: - The originating node announces the message hash via an `inv` (inventory) message to its **$\mathbf{k}$** peers (typically $k = 8$). - Interested peers respond with `getdata` requests, then receive the full message. - Each newly-informed node re-broadcasts to its own $k$ peers (excluding the source). - This creates an **exponential fan-out** reaching the entire network in $\mathcal{O}(\log_k N)$ rounds, where $N$ is the number of nodes. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Memorize the following table — it covers 80% of numerical questions in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for this topic. | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Gossip Rounds to Cover Network** | $r = \lceil \log_k N \rceil$ | $k$ = fan-out, $N$ = total nodes | | 2 | **Total Bandwidth per Node** | $B_{node} = k \cdot s \cdot f$ | $s$ = msg size (bits), $f$ = freq (Hz) | | 3 | **Block Propagation Time** | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{D \cdot \log_k N}{v}$ | $D$ = block size, $v$ = effective bandwidth | | 4 | **Network Throughput (TPS)** | $\theta = \dfrac{\text{tx per block}}{\text{block interval}}$ | Transactions per second | | 5 | **Diameter of Random Graph** | $\bar{d} \approx \dfrac{\ln N}{\ln \bar{k}}$ | $\bar{k}$ = mean node degree | | 6 | **Merkle Path Size** | $m = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | $n$ = number of transactions | | 7 | **Hash Rate to Block Time** | $T_{block} = \dfrac{d \cdot 2^{32}}{H}$ | $d$ = difficulty, $H$ = hash rate (H/s) | | 8 | **Network Resilience Bound** | $f < \lfloor (N-1)/3 \rfloor$ | Byzantine fault tolerance (PBFT) | | 9 | **Fork Probability (PoW)** | $P_{fork} \propto e^{-\lambda \cdot T_{prop}}$ | $\lambda$ = block arrival rate | | 10 | **Gossip Message Redundancy** | $R = 1 - \left(1 - \tfrac{k}{N}\right)^r$ | Fraction of nodes reached | ### Units & Boundary Conditions - $k$ is dimensionless (typically $\mathbf{8}$ in Bitcoin). - $v$ is in **bits per second (bps)**, $D$ in **bits**. - $T_{prop}$ is in **seconds (s)**. - All logarithms are **natural log $\ln$** unless otherwise specified (use $\log_2$ for Merkle paths). ## 2.6 Real-World Utility The "Building the Network" architecture powers: - **Bitcoin (BTC)** – Public, permissionless, PoW-based P2P network. - **Ethereum (ETH)** – Hybrid PoW/PoS network with `devp2p` protocol. - **Hyperledger Fabric** – Permissioned P2P network for enterprise consortia. - **IPFS / libp2p** – Generalized P2P networking stack used by Web3. - **Supply Chain Consortia** – Private blockchains for traceability (e.g., Maersk TradeLens). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Block Propagation Time in a Gossip Network **Problem Statement:** A blockchain network has $N = 10{,}000$ full nodes. Each node forwards a newly mined block to $k = 8$ randomly chosen peers. The block size is $D = 4 \text{ MB} = 32 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}$. Each node has effective upload bandwidth $v = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bits/s}$. Compute the time for the block to reach $99\%$ of the network. **Step 1 — Transmission Time per Hop** The time for one node to upload the block to a single peer is: $$ T_{tx} = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{32 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 32 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2 — Number of Gossip Rounds to Cover the Network** Using the coverage formula $R = 1 - (1 - k/N)^r$, we solve for $r$ such that $R \geq 0.99$: $$ (1 - \tfrac{k}{N})^r \leq 0.01 $$ Taking the natural logarithm of both sides: $$ r \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{k}{N}\right) \leq \ln(0.01) $$ Substituting $k = 8$, $N = 10{,}000$: $$ r \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{8}{10000}\right) \leq \ln(0.01) = -4.6052 $$ Using the approximation $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for small $x$: $$ r \cdot \left(-\frac{8}{10000}\right) \leq -4.6052 $$ $$ r \geq \frac{4.6052 \times 10000}{8} = 5756.5 $$ > [!NOTE] > This huge $r$ is misleading because it assumes a **linear chain** (each node has only 1 outbound link). In practice, gossip is **tree-based**, and all $k$ forwards happen **in parallel**. **Step 3 — Re-Derivation Using Parallel Tree Gossip** In a parallel tree, in each round a node's message reaches $k$ new nodes, then each of those reaches $k$ more, etc. After $r$ rounds, the number of informed nodes is $k^r$. We solve: $$ k^r = N \implies 8^r = 10{,}000 $$ $$ r = \frac{\ln(10000)}{\ln(8)} = \frac{9.2103}{2.0794} \approx 4.43 $$ Rounding up, $r = 5$ rounds. **Step 4 — Total Propagation Time** $$ T_{prop} = r \cdot T_{tx} = 5 \times 32 \text{ s} = 160 \text{ s} $$ **Step 5 — Verification via the Master Formula** $$ T_{prop} = \frac{D \cdot \log_k N}{v} = \frac{32 \times 10^6 \times 4.43}{10^6} \approx 141.8 \text{ s} $$ (The difference accounts for the fractional round.) **Answer: $\mathbf{T_{prop} \approx 142 \text{ s}}$** to reach the entire network. ## 3.2 Derivation II: Network Throughput (Transactions Per Second) **Problem Statement:** Bitcoin produces a $4 \text{ MB}$ block every $600$ seconds. Average transaction size is $s_{tx} = 250 \text{ B} = 2000 \text{ bits}$. Find the maximum TPS. **Step 1 — Transactions per Block** $$ n_{tx} = \frac{D}{s_{tx}} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \text{ B}}{250 \text{ B}} = 16{,}000 \text{ tx/block} $$ **Step 2 — TPS Calculation** $$ \theta = \frac{n_{tx}}{T_{block}} = \frac{16{,}000}{600} \approx 26.67 \text{ TPS} $$ **Step 3 — Comparison with Centralized Systems** - **Visa:** $\sim 1{,}700$ TPS (peak $\sim 65{,}000$ TPS). - **Bitcoin:** $\sim 7$ TPS (with smaller blocks) up to $\sim 27$ TPS. - **Ethereum:** $\sim 15 - 30$ TPS. This shows the **scalability trilemma** at the network layer. ## 3.3 Derivation III: Byzantine Fault Tolerance Bound In a PBFT-style blockchain with $N$ validator nodes, the maximum number of malicious (Byzantine) nodes the network can tolerate is: **Step 1 — Start with the Safety + Liveness Condition** PBFT requires $N \geq 3f + 1$, where $f$ = number of faulty nodes. **Step 2 — Solve for $f$** $$ f = \left\lfloor \frac{N - 1}{3} \right\rfloor $$ **Step 3 — Numerical Example** For $N = 10$ validators: $$ f = \left\lfloor \frac{9}{3} \right\rfloor = 3 $$ So the network can tolerate up to **3 malicious nodes out of 10** and still reach consensus. ## 3.4 Derivation IV: Block Time from Network Hash Rate **Step 1 — Bitcoin's Difficulty Target Formula** The expected time to find a valid nonce is: $$ T_{block} = \frac{d \cdot 2^{32}}{H} $$ where $d$ = current difficulty, $H$ = aggregate hash rate (hashes/second). **Step 2 — Example** If $d = 1.2 \times 10^{13}$ and $H = 1.5 \times 10^{20} \text{ H/s}$: $$ T_{block} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{13} \times 4.295 \times 10^{9}}{1.5 \times 10^{20}} $$ $$ = \frac{5.154 \times 10^{22}}{1.5 \times 10^{20}} = 343.6 \text{ s} \approx 5.73 \text{ min} $$ Bitcoin's target is $\mathbf{600 \text{ s}}$, so difficulty auto-adjusts every $2016$ blocks. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Network Design Choices | Design Choice | Bitcoin | Ethereum (Legacy) | Hyperledger Fabric | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Network Type** | Permissionless P2P | Permissionless P2P | Permissioned P2P | | **Discovery** | DNS seeds + addr msgs | Discv5 (Kademlia DHT) | Static config / MSP | | **Gossip Fan-out $k$** | ~8 | ~13 | Configurable | | **Block Propagation** | Compact blocks (Xthin) | GossipSub | Gossip (custom) | | **Consensus over Network** | PoW (Nakamoto) | PoW (Ethash) | Raft/PBFT | | **BFT Tolerance** | 51% assumption | 51% assumption | $f < N/3$ (PBFT) | | **Block Interval** | ~10 min | ~13 s | Configurable (~0.5 s) | | **Throughput** | ~7 TPS | ~15 TPS | ~3000 TPS | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram I: Blockchain P2P Network Topology The following diagram illustrates the **mesh-topology P2P network** underlying a blockchain, showing how full nodes, light nodes, and mining nodes interconnect. ```mermaid graph TD A1[Full Node A1
Archival Copy of Chain] A2[Full Node A2
Pruned Mode] A3[Full Node A3
Archival Copy] M1[Miner Node M1
PoW Solver] M2[Validator Node M2
PoS Attester] L1[Light Node L1
SPV Mobile Wallet] L2[Light Node L2
SPV Browser Wallet] L3[Light Node L3
SPV Hardware Wallet] S1[DNS Seeder S1
Bootstrap Entry] S2[DNS Seeder S2
Bootstrap Entry] S1 -.Initial Peer List.-> A1 S1 -.Initial Peer List.-> A3 S2 -.Initial Peer List.-> A2 S2 -.Initial Peer List.-> M1 A1 --- A2 A1 --- A3 A1 --- M1 A2 --- M2 A2 --- A3 A3 --- M1 M1 --- M2 L1 -.Queries Block Headers.-> A1 L2 -.Queries Merkle Proof.-> A2 L3 -.Verifies Payment.-> A3 M1 -->|Broadcasts New Block| A1 M1 -->|Broadcasts New Block| A2 A1 -->|Inv Message gossips| A3 A2 -->|Inv Message gossips| M2 M2 -->|Attestation| A1 M2 -->|Attestation| A3 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram II: Gossip Protocol Block Propagation Tree This diagram shows **how a new block fans out exponentially** through the network in 4 gossip rounds, reaching $8^4 = 4096$ nodes. ```mermaid graph TD R0R[Origin Miner Node
Mined New Block B] R1A[Peer 1A
Round 1] R1B[Peer 1B
Round 1] R1C[Peer 1C
Round 1] R1D[Peer 1D
Round 1] R1E[Peer 1E
Round 1] R1F[Peer 1F
Round 1] R1G[Peer 1G
Round 1] R1H[Peer 1H
Round 1] R2A[Peer 2A
Round 2] R2B[Peer 2B
Round 2] R2C[Peer 2C
Round 2] R2D[Peer 2D
Round 2] R3A[Peer 3A
Round 3] R3B[Peer 3B
Round 3] R4A[Peer 4A
Round 3 Leaves] R0R --> R1A R0R --> R1B R0R --> R1C R0R --> R1D R0R --> R1E R0R --> R1F R0R --> R1G R0R --> R1H R1A --> R2A R1A --> R2B R1B --> R2C R1B --> R2D R2A --> R3A R2B --> R3B R3A --> R4A ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram III: Node Discovery & Connection Lifecycle This flowchart captures the **complete bootstrap process** when a new node joins the blockchain network. ```mermaid graph TD S0[New Node Bootstraps] S1[Query Hard-coded DNS Seeds] S2[Receive IP List of Active Peers] S3[Open TCP Connections to Peers] S4[Send Version Message] S5[Receive Verack from Peer] S6[Handshake Complete - Add to Peer List] S7[Subscribe to Inv Messages] S8[Begin Gossip Listener Loop] S9[Sync Block Headers from Tip] S10[Validate and Store New Blocks] S0 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10 S10 --> S8 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram IV: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Network Stack This block diagram shows the **five functional layers** of a blockchain network as data flows from a user transaction down to TCP/IP and back up to consensus validation. ```mermaid graph TD L5A[Layer 5 Application
Wallet UI, dApp Frontend] L4C[Layer 4 Consensus
PoW PoS PBFT Validation] L3D[Layer 3 Data Structure
Merkle Tree Block Header] L2P[Layer 2 Propagation
Gossip Protocol inv getdata tx] L1N[Layer 1 Network
TCP IP Socket Layer] L5A -->|Sign and Submit TX| L4C L4C -->|Validate TX and Bundle| L3D L3D -->|Serialize Block| L2P L2P -->|Multicast via Gossip| L1N L1N -->|Reassemble at Peer| L2P L2P -->|Deliver Block| L3D L3D -->|Verify Merkle Root| L4C L4C -->|Append to Local Chain| L5A ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Network Architecture in Blockchain Context. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network** in blockchain is a **decentralized, flat-topology communication architecture** where every participant (node) acts simultaneously as a **client and a server**. Unlike the traditional client-server model, no central authority exists; instead, all nodes are equal peers that: 1. **Maintain** a copy of the distributed ledger (full nodes) or block headers (light nodes). 2. **Validate** incoming transactions and blocks using consensus rules. 3. **Propagate** new transactions and blocks to other peers using a **gossip protocol**. This architecture provides **fault tolerance**, **censorship resistance**, and **permissionless participation** — the three foundational requirements of a blockchain network. ### Question 2: List and Briefly Explain Any Three Types of Nodes in a Blockchain Network. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Full Node (Archival)** – Stores the complete blockchain history from the genesis block to the latest tip. Independently validates every transaction and block. *(1 mark)* 2. **Light Node (SPV)** – Stores only block headers and uses Merkle proofs to verify specific transactions, suitable for resource-constrained devices like mobile wallets. *(1 mark)* 3. **Mining / Validator Node** – A specialized full node that produces new blocks by solving cryptographic puzzles (PoW) or signing attestations (PoS). It participates in consensus. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern) ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Peer-to-Peer network architecture** of a blockchain system. Discuss the **gossip protocol** used for transaction and block propagation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A blockchain P2P network is a **distributed mesh of nodes** connected via TCP/IP. Each node maintains a **peer table** containing up to ~125 active connections. The architecture is **flat** (no hierarchy) and **self-organizing**. **Gossip Protocol Steps:** 1. A node receives a new transaction from its wallet/user. 2. It validates the transaction (signatures, UTXO, format). 3. It announces the transaction hash to its peers via an `inv` (inventory) message. 4. Peers not already aware respond with `getdata` requests. 5. The node transmits the full transaction data. 6. Each newly-informed peer repeats steps 3–5 with its own peers. This creates an **exponential fan-out** — in $\lceil \log_k N \rceil$ rounds, the message reaches the entire network. For $k = 8$ and $N = 10{,}000$ nodes, this requires **5 rounds** of parallel propagation. **Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid topology in Section 4.1. **(b)** A blockchain network has $\mathbf{N = 5000}$ full nodes. Each node forwards new blocks to $\mathbf{k = 10}$ randomly selected peers. Block size is $\mathbf{D = 2 \text{ MB}}$, and each node's effective bandwidth is $\mathbf{v = 2 \text{ Mbps}}$. Calculate the **time to propagate** the block to the entire network. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert Units** $$ D = 2 \text{ MB} = 2 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ bits} $$ $$ v = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} $$ **Step 2 — Transmission Time per Hop** $$ T_{tx} = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^6} = 8 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — Number of Gossip Rounds** $$ r = \lceil \log_k N \rceil = \lceil \log_{10} 5000 \rceil $$ $$ r = \frac{\ln(5000)}{\ln(10)} = \frac{8.5172}{2.3026} \approx 3.70 $$ Rounding up, $r = 4$ rounds. **Step 4 — Total Propagation Time** $$ T_{prop} = r \times T_{tx} = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ s} $$ **Final Answer:** $\mathbf{T_{prop} = 32 \text{ seconds}}$ to reach all $5000$ nodes. --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the **node discovery mechanism** used when a new node joins a blockchain network. Describe the **role of DNS seeders** and the **addr message exchange protocol**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** When a new node joins the blockchain network, it has **no prior knowledge of active peers**. The discovery process proceeds as follows: 1. **Hard-coded DNS Seeds** – The Bitcoin Core client (and most blockchain implementations) embeds a list of well-known DNS hostnames (e.g., `seed.bitcoin.sipa.be`, `dnsseed.bluematt.me`). The new node queries these and receives **IP addresses of long-running, well-connected full nodes**. *(2 marks)* 2. **Connection & Handshake** – The new node opens a TCP connection to several (typically 8) of the returned peers. It sends a `version` message containing protocol version, services flag, timestamp, and a random nonce. The peer responds with its own `version` and a `verack` (version acknowledge) message. *(2 marks)* 3. **Addr Message Exchange** – Once handshaken, the new node requests the peer's address book via a `getaddr` message. The peer responds with one or more `addr` messages containing up to 1000 IP addresses each (timestamps and services flags included). The new node adds these to its **peers.dat** database. *(2 marks)* 4. **Ongoing Maintenance** – Periodically, the node pings known peers, evicts non-responsive ones, and seeks new addresses. This **self-healing** property ensures the network remains robust. *(1 mark)* **(b)** Differentiate between **Full Nodes, Light (SPV) Nodes, and Mining/Validator Nodes** in a blockchain network. Discuss their **storage, trust, and computational requirements** with a comparative table. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Property | Full Node (Archival) | Light (SPV) Node | Mining/Validator Node | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Storage** | Entire chain (~500 GB Bitcoin) | Only block headers (~50 MB) | Entire chain + UTXO set | | **Validation** | Independent & complete | Delegates to full nodes | Independent + produces blocks | | **Trust Assumption** | Trustless (self-verifies) | Trusts a full node for Merkle proofs | Trustless but stake/power exposed | | **Compute** | Moderate (signature checks) | Low (Merkle path checks) | Very high (hashing or attestations) | | **Network Role** | Producer & propagator | Consumer (read-only) | Block producer | | **Bandwidth** | High (~200 GB/month) | Low (~1 GB/month) | Very high (block broadcasting) | | **Example Use Case** | Exchange backend, explorer | Mobile wallet, hardware wallet | Mining pool, staking service | *(2 marks for each row explanation and 1 mark for the concluding insight.)* **Concluding Insight:** A healthy blockchain network requires **all three node types** in balance — full nodes for security, light nodes for adoption, and mining/validator nodes for liveness. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing P2P with Client-Server** — A P2P network has *no* central server. Writing "the server validates transactions" will cost you 2-3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting Units in Derivations** — Always write `bits/s`, `seconds`, `nodes`. Numerical answers without units receive partial credit only. > 3. **Skipping the Logarithm in Gossip Rounds** — A common error is writing $r = N/k$. The correct formula is $r = \lceil \log_k N \rceil$. This is a **5-mark killer mistake**. > 4. **Mixing Consensus and Network Layers** — Building the network is about *communication*, not consensus. Don't discuss PoW hashing in a node-discovery question. > 5. **Drawing Topology Without Labels** — A diagram without node-type labels (full/light/miner) will lose 1-2 marks for incomplete labeling. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Wall):** > - Blockchain network = **flat, permissionless, P2P mesh** built on TCP/IP. > - **Node types:** Full (archival/pruned), Light (SPV), Mining/Validator. > - **Discovery mechanism:** DNS seeds → `getaddr` → `addr` exchange. > - **Gossip protocol:** Exponential fan-out with fan-out $k \approx 8$. > - **Propagation rounds:** $r = \lceil \log_k N \rceil$ (NOT $N/k$). > - **Propagation time:** $T_{prop} = (D/v) \cdot \lceil \log_k N \rceil$. > - **Throughput:** $\theta = (\text{tx/block}) / T_{block}$. > - **BFT bound:** $f < (N-1)/3$ (PBFT); PoW tolerates up to $49\%$ honest-participation-dominant. > - **Bitcoin constants:** Block time $10$ min, block size $4$ MB, port $8333$, fan-out $8$. > - **Ethereum constants:** Block time $12$ s, fan-out $\sim 13$, GossipSub protocol. > - **Network resilience:** Decentralization $\propto$ number of independent full nodes. > - **Scalability trilemma:** Decentralization ↔ Security ↔ Scalability — network design must balance all three. > - **Always quote units** in derivations to earn full marks. > - **Draw labeled diagrams** for every topology-related question — visual evidence earns easy marks. > - **Differentiate layers** clearly: L1 (Network), L2 (Propagation), L3 (Data), L4 (Consensus), L5 (Application).

topics.title: Accessing the Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accessing the Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
