```mermaid
    A2 -->> Pb : Accepted 10 v_new
    A3 -->> Pb : Accepted 10 v_new
    Note over Pa : PA tries Accept 5 v_old
    Pa ->> A1 : Accept 5 v_old
    Pa ->> A2 : Accept 5 v_old
    A1 -->> Pa : REJECTED promised 10
    A2 -->> Pa : REJECTED promised 10
    Note over A1,A3 : v_new is CHOSEN, v_old is rejected
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between Paxos and Raft consensus algorithms. Mention any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Paxos | Raft |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Paxos has no strong leader; any Proposer can drive consensus. | Raft enforces a strong leader that handles all client requests. |
| 2 | Paxos is described in two phases (Prepare, Accept) per value. | Raft decomposes the problem into Leader Election, Log Replication, and Safety. |
| 3 | Paxos uses deterministic proposal numbers; no randomness. | Raft uses randomized election timeouts to avoid split votes. |
| 4 | Paxos is notoriously hard to teach. | Raft was designed to be understandable. |
| 5 | Used in Google Chubby, ZooKeeper (ZAB). | Used in etcd, Consul, Hyperledger Fabric. |

Both algorithms provide equivalent safety and liveness guarantees in asynchronous networks with crash-stop failures, requiring a majority quorum of $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ nodes.

*(3 marks — one mark per valid difference with brief explanation.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
**What is the role of a Quorum in Paxos? How many Acceptors are required to tolerate 3 simultaneous node failures?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of a Quorum:** A quorum is a majority subset of Acceptors ($\geq \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$) that must agree on a value before it is considered "chosen." The Quorum Intersection Property guarantees that any two quorums share at least one node, which prevents conflicting values from both being chosen — this is the foundation of Paxos's safety.

**Failure tolerance calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3 \text{ (failures to tolerate)} \\
N &= 2F + 1 = 2(3) + 1 = 7 \text{ Acceptors} \\
Q &= \lfloor 7/2 \rfloor + 1 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **7 Acceptors** are required, forming a **quorum of 4** to tolerate 3 simultaneous crash failures.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for quorum role, 1.5 for numerical answer with derivation.)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A — Paxos Deep Dive (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, S7 B.Tech CSE]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Paxos consensus algorithm in detail. Describe all the phases with proper message passing and explain how safety is guaranteed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a 5-node Paxos cluster with Acceptors $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5$. A Proposer P starts a Prepare phase with proposal number $n = 7$. Acceptors $A_1, A_2, A_3$ respond with Promises; $A_4, A_5$ are crashed. Later, another Proposer P' starts a Prepare with $n = 9$. Trace the complete protocol and show how safety is maintained.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Paxos is a fault-tolerant distributed consensus algorithm proposed by Leslie Lamport (1998) that allows a network of unreliable processors to agree on a single value despite crash failures.

**Roles (1 mark):**
1. **Proposer** — proposes values.
2. **Acceptor** — votes (forms the quorum).
3. **Learner** — learns the chosen value.

**Phase 1a — Prepare (1 mark):**
- Proposer selects a globally unique, monotonically increasing proposal number $n$.
- Sends `Prepare(n)` to a majority of Acceptors.

**Phase 1b — Promise (1 mark):**
- Each Acceptor checks: if $n$ is greater than any proposal number it has seen, it responds `Promise(n, prevAcceptedValue, prevAcceptedNumber)`.
- It promises not to accept any proposal numbered less than $n$ in this term.

**Phase 2a — Accept (1 mark):**
- Proposer collects Promises from a majority.
- **Critical rule:** If any Promise contains a previously accepted value, the Proposer MUST use that value (not the client's value). Otherwise, it uses the client's value $v$.
- Sends `Accept(n, v)` to a majority of Acceptors.

**Phase 2b — Accepted (1 mark):**
- Acceptors that have not promised a higher number accept the proposal and reply `Accepted(n, v)`.

**Decision (1 mark):**
- When a **majority** of Acceptors have accepted, the value is **CHOSEN**. Learners are notified.

**Safety Guarantee (1 mark):**
- The Quorum Intersection Property ensures any two majorities share at least one Acceptor. That shared Acceptor forces all later Proposers to adopt the previously chosen value, ensuring **at most one value is chosen per slot**.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Setup:** $N=5$, $Q=\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor + 1 = 3$. Tolerated failures: $F=2$.

**Step 1 — First Proposer P, Phase 1a (1 mark):**
- P chooses $n = 7$.
- P sends `Prepare(7)` to $\{A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5\}$.

**Step 2 — Phase 1b: Promise responses (1 mark):**
- $A_1, A_2, A_3$ are alive and respond `Promise(7, null, null)` (no prior accepted values).
- $A_4, A_5$ are crashed — no response.
- P has collected 3 Promises — **quorum reached**.

**Step 3 — Phase 2a: P sends Accept (1 mark):**
- No Promise contained a prior accepted value, so P uses the client's value $v = \text{"X"}$.
- P sends `Accept(7, "X")` to $\{A_1, A_2, A_3\}$.

**Step 4 — Phase 2b: Acceptance (1 mark):**
- $A_1, A_2, A_3$ accept (they have not promised any number $> 7$).
- All 3 reply `Accepted(7, "X")`.
- A majority of 3 has accepted — value "X" is **CHOSEN**.

**Step 5 — Second Proposer P' arrives with n=9 (1 mark):**
- P' sends `Prepare(9)` to $\{A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5\}$.
- $A_1, A_2, A_3$ respond with `Promise(9, "X", 7)` — they return their highest accepted value "X".
- $A_4, A_5$ are still crashed.

**Step 6 — Safety preservation (1 mark):**
- P' sees that a value "X" has been previously accepted.
- **Paxos's safety rule FORCES P' to use "X"** as the value in its Accept phase, even if the client requested something different (say "Y").
- P' sends `Accept(9, "X")` — value remains "X". ✓

**Conclusion:** Despite concurrent proposers and 2 crashed Acceptors, Paxos chose exactly one value ("X") and never chose a conflicting one.

---

#### **Question B — Raft Deep Dive (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S7 B.Tech CSE]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Raft consensus algorithm. Describe in detail: (i) Leader Election, (ii) Log Replication, and (iii) the Safety property called "Leader Completeness."

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 5-node Raft cluster is running. The current leader L at term 5 crashes. Describe the complete leader election process with proper state transitions, terms, and timing. Also explain why Raft uses **randomized election timeouts**.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition (0.5 marks):**
Raft is a consensus algorithm designed by Ongaro and Ousterhout (2014) to be understandable. It is used in etcd, Consul, and Hyperledger Fabric. It provides the same guarantees as Paxos but decomposes the problem into three sub-problems.

**(i) Leader Election (2 marks):**
- Every server starts as a **Follower**.
- A Follower waits for an `AppendEntries` (heartbeat) from the Leader within a **randomized election timeout** (typically 150–300 ms).
- If the timeout expires, the Follower becomes a **Candidate**, increments its `currentTerm`, votes for itself, and broadcasts `RequestVote` RPCs in parallel to all other servers.
- A server grants its vote to a Candidate if:
  1. The Candidate's term is at least as recent as the voter's.
  2. The voter's log is at least as up-to-date as the Candidate's.
- The Candidate becomes the **Leader** upon receiving votes from a **majority** ($Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$).
- If a split vote occurs (no majority), the Candidate waits for the next timeout and retries with a new term.

**(ii) Log Replication (2 marks):**
- Client sends a command to the Leader.
- Leader appends the command to its log as a new entry: $\langle term, index, command \rangle$.
- Leader sends `AppendEntries` RPCs in parallel to all Followers.
- Each Follower appends the entry (uncommitted) and replies with success.
- **Commit condition:** Once the entry is replicated on a **majority** of servers AND is from the **current term**, the Leader marks it as **committed**, applies it to its state machine, and replies to the client.
- Commit index is propagated in subsequent heartbeats; Followers apply the entry.

**(iii) Leader Completeness Property (2 marks):**
- **Statement:** If a log entry is committed in some term $T$, then that entry will appear in the logs of all leaders for all terms $> T$.
- **Why it holds:** Raft's voting rule restricts votes to Candidates whose logs are at least as up-to-date as the voter's. Hence, a Candidate missing a committed entry can never win an election (because any server with that entry would refuse to grant its vote to a Candidate without it).
- This property, combined with the "only commit current-term entries" rule, guarantees **State Machine Safety**: no two servers ever apply different entries at the same index.

**0.5 mark** for summarizing the safety property correctly.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Setup:** $N = 5$, $F = 2$, $Q = 3$. All servers start as Followers at term 5. Leader L crashes.

**Step 1 — Election timers expire randomly (1.5 marks):**
- Each Follower has its own randomized election timer.
- Suppose the timers are: $S_1 = 180$ ms, $S_2 = 250$ ms, $S_3 = 220$ ms, $S_4 = 300$ ms, $S_5 = 270$ ms.

**Step 2 — First Candidate emerges (1.5 marks):**
- At $t = 180$ ms, $S_1$'s timer expires first.
- $S_1$ becomes **Candidate**, increments term from 5 → 6, votes for itself, and sends `RequestVote(term=6)` to $\{S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5\}$.

**Step 3 — Vote collection (1.5 marks):**
- $S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5$ receive `RequestVote`. Suppose all four have logs at least as up-to-date as $S_1$.
- They all grant their votes to $S_1$ (first-come-first-served; only one vote per term).
- $S_1$ has received 4 votes out of 5 (its own + 3 from peers — quorum of 3 satisfied).

**Step 4 — $S_1$ becomes Leader (1 mark):**
- $S_1$ transitions to **Leader** state, term = 6.
- $S_1$ immediately sends `AppendEntries` (heartbeat) to all Followers.
- All other Followers reset their election timers upon receiving the valid `AppendEntries` and revert to the Follower state at term 6.

**Step 5 — Continued leadership (0.5 marks):**
- $S_1$ sends heartbeats every 50 ms to maintain authority.
- It begins accepting client commands and replicating them.

**Step 6 — Why randomized timeouts? (1 mark):**
- **Reason:** If all servers had the **same** election timeout, after a leader crash, multiple servers would time out simultaneously, each become Candidates, and split the votes — leading to a **split-vote deadlock** that could persist indefinitely.
- By **randomizing** the timeout per server (e.g., uniformly in [150, 300] ms), the algorithm statistically ensures that in any given round, exactly **one** server times out first and wins the election outright.
- This is the **only source of randomness** in Raft and is essential for liveness.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in this topic (READ CAREFULLY):**
> 1. **Confusing the Proposer's "adopt" rule:** Students often write that a Proposer "decides" what value to propose, forgetting the **mandatory adoption** of any previously accepted value returned in Promises. This is the *single most common* error. Worth **2–3 marks lost** on a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the term-based commit rule in Raft:** Many students say "once an entry is replicated on a majority, it is committed." This is **WRONG** for old-term entries. Always specify: *entry must be from the current term*.
> 3. **Skipping the Quorum Intersection argument:** Simply stating "$N = 2F+1$" without explaining the Quorum Intersection Property loses the conceptual depth marks.
> 4. **No diagram in a 14-mark question:** KTU examiners explicitly state that a protocol diagram (state diagram or sequence diagram) is mandatory for full marks. Always include a labeled diagram.
> 5. **Mixing Paxos and Raft terminologies:** Do not call Raft's log entry a "proposal number" — Raft uses **terms** and **indices**. Likewise, do not refer to Raft's "Acceptor" — the Raft term is **Follower**.
> 6. **Forgetting failure scenarios in numerical problems:** When a numerical problem specifies a cluster size, you MUST compute and state the quorum $Q$ and the failure tolerance $F$ explicitly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

**Paxos — Key Points to Memorize:**
- ☐ Three roles: **Proposer, Acceptor, Learner**.
- ☐ Two phases: **Prepare/Promise** (Phase 1) and **Accept/Accepted** (Phase 2).
- ☐ Quorum size $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$; tolerates $F = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ failures.
- ☐ The **"adopt the highest previously-accepted value"** rule in Phase 2a is the key to safety.
- ☐ Multi-Paxos elects a stable leader to amortize Phase 1 cost.
- ☐ Used in: Google Chubby, ZooKeeper (ZAB).

**Raft — Key Points to Memorize:**
- ☐ Three states: **Follower, Candidate, Leader**.
- ☐ Three sub-problems: **Leader Election, Log Replication, Safety**.
- ☐ Log entry tuple: $\langle \text{term},\ \text{index},\ \text{command} \rangle$.
- ☐ Election timeout range: **150–300 ms**; Heartbeat: **~50 ms**.
- ☐ Commit rule: majority replication AND current term.
- ☐ **Randomized election timeouts** prevent split votes.
- ☐ **Leader Completeness Property** ensures committed entries survive leader changes.
- ☐ Used in: etcd (Kubernetes), Consul, CockroachDB, Hyperledger Fabric ordering.

**Cross-Algorithm Points to Memorize:**
- ☐ Both guarantee **safety always** and **liveness under partial synchrony** with a majority.
- ☐ Both require $N = 2F + 1$ nodes for $F$ crash-fault tolerance.
- ☐ Both are **Crash Fault Tolerant (CFT)** — not Byzantine tolerant.
- ☐ Raft = "Paxos made understandable" — same mathematical foundation, different presentation.

**Last-Minute Formulas:**
- $\quad Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$
- $\quad F = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$
- $\quad N = 2F + 1$
- $\quad \vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq F + 1$

> **End of Module 2 — Paxos and Raft Algorithms Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Cryptocurrency Systems** (the central focus of Module 3 of PECST747), the term **"Components"** refers to the **fundamental architectural building blocks** that collectively enable a decentralized digital currency to function securely, autonomously, and trustlessly over a peer-to-peer (P2P) network. These components form the technological foundation of any cryptocurrency such as **Bitcoin**, **Ethereum**, or other blockchain-based monetary systems.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the components of a cryptocurrency ecosystem are broadly classified into the following six core subsystems:

1. **Transaction Subsystem** — Defines how value is transferred.
2. **Mining / Consensus Subsystem** — Defines how new blocks are created and the network agrees on state.
3. **Networking Subsystem** — Defines how nodes communicate (P2P gossip protocol).
4. **Scripting / Smart Contract Subsystem** — Defines programmable spending conditions.
5. **Wallet / Key Management Subsystem** — Defines ownership and signing of transactions.
6. **Cryptographic Primitives Subsystem** — Hash functions, digital signatures, Merkle trees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 3 of PECST747 specifically emphasizes the *internal components of a cryptocurrency*, not just the external economic view. Examiners frequently ask about the interplay between components such as *Transaction → Block → Mining → Chain*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Cryptocurrency Engine"**

Imagine a **modern automobile engine**. An engine is not a single piece; it is a coordinated assembly of *pistons, fuel injectors, spark plugs, crankshaft, and ECU (computer)*. Each component does one job, but they synchronize to produce motion.

A cryptocurrency works the **same way**. It is **not "just coins on the internet"** — it is a coordinated engine with the following mapped components:

| Engine Part (Analogy) | Cryptocurrency Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel Injector | **Transaction System** | Pushes "value" (fuel) into the system |
| Spark Plug | **Mining / PoW** | Ignites new blocks (events) |
| Crankshaft | **Blockchain Data Structure** | Converts ignition into ordered motion (chronological chain) |
| ECU (Computer) | **Scripting / Smart Contracts** | Programs the rules of operation |
| Wiring Harness | **P2P Network** | Connects all parts to communicate |
| Car Key & Lock | **Wallet / Private Keys** | Controls who can drive (spend) |
| Anti-theft Alarm | **Cryptographic Primitives (SHA-256, ECDSA)** | Ensures security |

So when KTU asks "Explain the components of a cryptocurrency," it is essentially asking you to explain **how this engine is built and how each part interacts**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory Definitions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold** terms MUST be defined in any KTU 14-mark answer:

- **Cryptocurrency:** A digital or virtual currency secured by **cryptography**, operated on a **distributed ledger (blockchain)**, and verified through a **consensus mechanism** without a central authority.
- **Decentralization:** The distribution of decision-making and validation authority across all participating nodes, eliminating single points of failure.
- **Consensus Mechanism:** The protocol (e.g., Proof of Work, Proof of Stake) through which distributed nodes agree on a single shared state of the ledger.
- **Mining:** The process of validating pending transactions, bundling them into a candidate block, and solving a computationally intensive puzzle to append the block to the chain.
- **Double-Spending Problem:** The risk that the same digital token is spent more than once. Cryptocurrencies solve this through **distributed consensus + cryptographic linking**.
- **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Network:** A flat network topology where every node is both a client and a server, propagating transactions and blocks gossip-style.

---

## 1.4 Why Components Matter in KTU Examinations

Examiners in the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) test components-based questions at **three cognitive levels** (per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy):

- **L1 — Remember:** "Define a transaction." (3 marks)
- **L3 — Apply:** "Show the lifecycle of a transaction from wallet to block confirmation." (7 marks)
- **L5 — Evaluate:** "Compare PoW and PoS as consensus components with respect to security and energy." (14 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> A well-structured 14-mark answer MUST list all six components, link them in sequence, and justify why each is necessary. Memorizing bullet points without interconnection **costs marks**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Transaction Subsystem

A **transaction** is the atomic unit of state change in any cryptocurrency. It consumes one or more **inputs** (referencing previous unspent transaction outputs — **UTXOs**) and creates one or more **outputs**.

**Structural anatomy of a Bitcoin-style transaction:**

- **Version** (4 bytes) — Protocol version.
- **Input Count** — Number of inputs.
- **Inputs** — Each contains:
  - Previous transaction hash (`txid`)
  - Output index (`vout`)
  - Unlocking script (`scriptSig`)
  - Sequence number
- **Output Count** — Number of outputs.
- **Outputs** — Each contains:
  - Value (in satoshis)
  - Locking script (`scriptPubKey`)
- **Locktime** — Earliest block/time the transaction is valid.

**Why transactions matter:** They are the **only mechanism** by which the global ledger state changes.

---

### 2.1.2 Mining / Consensus Subsystem (Proof of Work)

Mining is the **engine of new block creation**. The miner:
1. Collects pending transactions from the mempool.
2. Constructs a candidate block header.
3. Iterates the **nonce** until:
$$H(\text{Header}) < \text{Target}$$
4. Broadcasts the valid block; receives the **block reward** + **transaction fees**.

**Difficulty Target Recalculation (Bitcoin protocol — every 2016 blocks, ~2 weeks):**

The target is adjusted so that average block time remains **600 seconds (10 minutes)**. The adjustment formula is:

$$T_{new} = T_{old} \times \frac{A_t}{E_t}$$

Where:
- $T_{new}$ = New target
- $T_{old}$ = Old target
- $A_t$ = Actual time taken for the last 2016 blocks
- $E_t$ = Expected time (2016 × 600 s = 1,209,600 s)

The corresponding **difficulty** is:

$$D = \frac{D_{max}}{T}$$

Where $D_{max}$ is the maximum difficulty (genesis target).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** If miners are too fast (hashrate rises), target shrinks → difficulty rises → puzzle becomes harder. This is the **self-balancing thermostat** of the network.

---

### 2.1.3 Blockchain Data Structure Subsystem

The blockchain is a **tamper-evident, append-only, hash-linked list of blocks**. Each block header contains:

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 bytes | Protocol version |
| Previous Block Hash | 32 bytes | Links to parent block |
| Merkle Root | 32 bytes | Summary of all transactions |
| Timestamp | 4 bytes | Block creation time |
| Difficulty Target (Bits) | 4 bytes | Encoded target |
| Nonce | 4 bytes | PoW variable |

**Hash-linking ensures immutability:** changing transaction in block $N$ alters the Merkle root → alters block $N$'s header hash → invalidates block $N+1$'s "previous hash" field → entire chain from $N$ onward is invalidated unless an attacker re-mines **all subsequent blocks**, which is computationally infeasible at scale (the **51% attack threshold**).

---

### 2.1.4 P2P Networking Subsystem

Cryptocurrencies use a **gossip protocol**:
- When a node learns of a new transaction/block, it **floods** it to all peers.
- Peers validate and re-broadcast.
- Information propagates exponentially — typically reaching **>90% of the network in < 5 seconds** for Bitcoin.

**Properties:**
- **Permissionless** — anyone can join.
- **Sybil-resistant** — bounded by economic cost (PoW) or stake (PoS).
- **Eventually consistent** — temporary forks resolve via longest-chain rule (Nakamoto consensus).

---

### 2.1.5 Scripting / Smart Contract Subsystem

Bitcoin uses a **stack-based, intentionally non-Turing-complete scripting language** (`Script`). It supports:

- **Pay-to-Public-Key-Hash (P2PKH)** — Standard payment.
- **Pay-to-Script-Hash (P2SH)** — Multi-sig and complex conditions.
- **OP_RETURN** — Data anchoring (80 bytes).
- **Timelocks** — `nLocktime`, `CSV`, `CLTV`.

Ethereum extends this to a **Turing-complete** EVM bytecode with gas-metered execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-Turing-complete in Bitcoin?** Prevents infinite loops / denial-of-service — a deliberate design trade-off prioritizing **security over expressiveness**.

---

### 2.1.6 Cryptographic Primitives Subsystem

Two primitives underpin every cryptocurrency:

1. **SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm 256-bit):**
   - One-way function: $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$
   - Properties: deterministic, collision-resistant, preimage-resistant, avalanche effect.
2. **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm) on secp256k1:**
   - Key pair: $(k, K = k \cdot G)$ where $G$ is the generator.
   - Signature: $(r, s)$ — proves ownership of private key $k$ without revealing it.
   - Equation: $r = (k \cdot G)_x \mod n$ where $n$ is the curve order.

---

### 2.1.7 Wallet / Key Management Subsystem

A **wallet** is software that manages:
- **Private key** ($k$) — 256-bit secret, used to sign.
- **Public key** ($K = kG$) — shared, used to derive address.
- **Address** — `Base58Check(RIPEMD160(SHA256(K)))` (Bitcoin).

**Deterministic wallets (BIP-32/39/44):** A single seed (mnemonic phrase) generates the entire key tree via **HMAC-SHA512**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete set of equations** you are expected to know for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Difficulty Target Adjustment | $T_{new} = T_{old} \times \dfrac{A_t}{E_t}$ | $T$ = target (256-bit), $A_t$ = actual time, $E_t$ = expected time (1,209,600 s) | Self-regulating block interval |
| 2 | Difficulty Metric | $D = \dfrac{D_{max}}{T}$ | $D$ = difficulty, $D_{max}$ = max target | Measures mining hardness |
| 3 | PoW Hash Condition | $H(\text{Header}) < T$ | $H$ = SHA-256, $T$ = target | Puzzle verification |
| 4 | Block Reward (After n halvings) | $R_n = R_0 \times 2^{-n}$ | $R_0 = 50$ BTC, $n$ = halving count | Monetary issuance schedule |
| 5 | Total BTC Supply Cap | $S_{max} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} R_0 \cdot 2^{-i} \cdot B$ | $B$ = blocks per halving epoch (210,000) | Predicts total coins (~21 million) |
| 6 | Halving Period | $H_p = 210{,}000$ blocks | ~4 years at 10 min/block | Defines issuance decay |
| 7 | Network Hashrate | $\mathcal{H} = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{T_{avg}}$ | $\mathcal{H}$ = hashes/sec, $T_{avg}$ = avg block time | Measures total mining power |
| 8 | ECDSA Public Key | $K = k \cdot G$ | $G$ = generator (secp256k1) | Derives address from private key |
| 9 | ECDSA Signature | $r = (k' \cdot G)_x \mod n$ | $k'$ = per-signature nonce | Authenticates transaction |
| 10 | Bitcoin Address | $A = \text{Base58Check}(\text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(K)))$ | $A$ = 25-byte address | User-facing identifier |
| 11 | Transaction Fee | $f = \sum \text{inputs} - \sum \text{outputs}$ | satoshis | Miner incentive |
| 12 | Merkle Root | $H_{\text{root}} = H(H(T_1 \vert T_2) \vert H(T_3 \vert T_4)\vert \ldots)$ | $H$ = SHA-256 | Compact transaction commitment |
| 13 | Block Time Constraint | $T_{avg} = 600$ s (Bitcoin) | seconds | Design constant |
| 14 | Confirmation Security | $P_{\text{revert}} \propto (q/p)^{z}$ | $q$ = attacker hashrate, $p$ = honest, $z$ = confirmations | Quantifies finality |
| 15 | Merkle Proof Size | $\log_2(n)$ hashes | $n$ = number of txs | SPV (light client) verification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU students:** When writing answers, always **state the unit** of the final answer and **explain one variable** in plain English. This is a 2-mark buffer on most 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Component | Real-World Application Beyond Currency |
|---|---|
| Cryptographic Hashing | File integrity (git, IPFS, deduplication) |
| Merkle Trees | Certificate Transparency (Google), AWS S3 verify, Git |
| ECDSA Signatures | TLS certificates, SSH keys, JWT signing |
| P2P Networking | BitTorrent, IPFS, distributed CDNs |
| Scripting / Smart Contracts | Supply chain, DeFi, DAOs, NFT provenance |
| Mining / PoW | Spam prevention (Hashcash), DoS mitigation |
| Wallets | Identity management (DID), KYC-less authentication |
| Blockchain | Land registries, voting, medical records, audit trails |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the most frequently asked KTU 14-mark sub-questions on cryptocurrency components.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Bitcoin Supply Cap (Common 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem (Typical KTU 2018-style):** *"Derive the total supply cap of Bitcoin given the initial block reward, halving interval, and target block time."*

**Given:**
- Initial block reward: $R_0 = 50$ BTC
- Halving interval: $H_p = 210{,}000$ blocks
- Target block time: $T_b = 600$ s
- Seconds per day: $86{,}400$

### Step 1 — Compute number of blocks per halving epoch

Each halving period contains exactly $H_p = 210{,}000$ blocks.

### Step 2 — Express block reward in the $i$-th epoch

After $i$ halvings, the reward is:

$$R_i = R_0 \times 2^{-i} = 50 \times 2^{-i} \;\; \text{BTC}$$

### Step 3 — Compute total BTC issued in epoch $i$

In epoch $i$, there are $H_p$ blocks, each yielding $R_i$ BTC. Therefore:

$$S_i = H_p \times R_i = 210{,}000 \times 50 \times 2^{-i}$$

### Step 4 — Sum over all epochs to get total supply

The total supply is the geometric series:

$$S_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} S_i = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \times 50 \times 2^{-i}$$

### Step 5 — Factor out constants

$$S_{total} = 210{,}000 \times 50 \times \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} 2^{-i}$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the geometric series

For $|r| < 1$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} r^{i} = \frac{1}{1 - r}$$

With $r = \frac{1}{2}$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} 2^{-i} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2$$

### Step 7 — Substitute and compute

$$S_{total} = 210{,}000 \times 50 \times 2$$

$$S_{total} = 210{,}000 \times 100 = 21{,}000{,}000 \;\; \text{BTC}$$

### Step 8 — Account for real-world rounding (mining precision)

Because rewards are computed in satoshis ($1$ BTC $= 10^8$ satoshis), the actual realized supply is slightly less:

$$S_{actual} \approx 20{,}999{,}999.9769 \;\; \text{BTC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 21 million cap is **not** hard-coded; it emerges mathematically from the geometric series under the halving schedule. This is a beautiful example of **protocol-embedded monetary policy** — no central bank required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mining Difficulty Adjustment (Common 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** *"If the last 2016 blocks took 14 days (1,209,600 s) instead of the expected 10 days (864,000 s), by what factor does the difficulty change?"*

### Step 1 — Recall the target adjustment formula

$$T_{new} = T_{old} \times \frac{A_t}{E_t}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$T_{new} = T_{old} \times \frac{1{,}209{,}600}{864{,}000}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the ratio

$$\frac{1{,}209{,}600}{864{,}000} = 1.4$$

Therefore:

$$T_{new} = 1.4 \times T_{old}$$

### Step 4 — Convert target change to difficulty change

Since $D \propto \dfrac{1}{T}$:

$$D_{new} = \frac{D_{old}}{1.4} \approx 0.714 \times D_{old}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Miners were too slow (each block averaged 1000 s instead of 600 s). To restore the 10-minute target, the protocol **increased the target** (made it easier), which **decreases difficulty**. Difficulty dropped by approximately **28.6%**.

### Step 5 — Bound check (clamp factor)

The Bitcoin protocol limits the adjustment to a factor of **4×** in either direction per retarget:

$$0.25 \leq \frac{A_t}{E_t} \leq 4$$

> **Real-world note:** This clamp prevents wild swings due to hashrate shocks (e.g., during the 2018 China mining ban or 2021 China crackdown).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Probability of Successful Double-Spend Attack

> **Problem:** *"An attacker controls 10% of the network hashrate. What is the probability they successfully double-spend a transaction with $z = 6$ confirmations?"*

### Step 1 — Define honest and attacker hashrates

$$p = 0.9 \quad (\text{honest}), \quad q = 0.1 \quad (\text{attacker})$$

### Step 2 — Recall the Gambler's Ruin formula (Nakamoto, 2008)

The probability the attacker catches up from $z$ blocks behind is:

$$P_{catchup} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } q \geq p \\ \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{z} & \text{if } q < p \end{cases}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $q < p$ branch

$$P_{catchup} = \left(\frac{0.1}{0.9}\right)^{6}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the base

$$\frac{0.1}{0.9} = 0.1111$$

### Step 5 — Raise to the 6th power

$$P_{catchup} = (0.1111)^{6}$$

$$= 0.00000176$$

### Step 6 — Convert to a human-readable form

$$P_{catchup} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.000176\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even with 10% hashrate, double-spending a 6-confirmation transaction is a **1-in-568,000** event. This is why merchants typically wait for **6 confirmations** (~60 minutes on Bitcoin).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Merkle Tree Proof Size (SPV Verification)

> **Problem:** *"A block contains 1024 transactions. How many hashes must a Simplified Payment Verification (SPV) client download to prove a transaction's inclusion?"*

### Step 1 — Recall the Merkle tree structure

A Merkle tree is a **binary tree** of hashes. For $n$ transactions, there are $\log_2(n)$ levels.

### Step 2 — Substitute $n = 1024$

$$\log_2(1024) = 10 \text{ levels}$$

### Step 3 — A proof requires one sibling per level

At each level, the SPV client needs **one sibling hash** to reconstruct the path to the root.

Therefore:

$$P_{size} = \log_2(n) = 10 \text{ hashes}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to full block size

A full block might contain **~1 MB** of transaction data. The Merkle proof is only:

$$10 \times 32 \text{ bytes} = 320 \text{ bytes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is why **light clients** (mobile wallets) can verify payments without downloading the entire blockchain — a foundational design decision for cryptocurrency scalability.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU 14-Mark Questions)

### 3.5.1 Proof of Work vs Proof of Stake (as Consensus Components)

| Criterion | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource** | Computational energy (electricity) | Economic stake (locked coins) |
| **Validator Selection** | Random proportional to hashrate | Random weighted by stake size |
| **Attack Cost** | Hardware + electricity (external) | Buying coins (internal, refundable) |
| **51% Attack Economics** | Repeat electricity cost | Lose stake via *slashing* |
| **Energy Use** | Very high (Bitcoin: ~150 TWh/yr) | Minimal (~99.9% reduction) |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic (needs confirmations) | Often deterministic (BFT-style) |
| **Centralization Risk** | Mining pool concentration | Wealth concentration ("rich get richer") |
| **Examples** | Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin | Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano, Polkadot |
| **Throughput** | Low (~7 TPS for Bitcoin) | Higher (~1000s of TPS possible) |
| **KTU Exam Preference** | Frequently tested in detail | Tested comparatively |

### 3.5.2 UTXO Model vs Account Model (Transaction Components)

| Criterion | UTXO Model (Bitcoin) | Account Model (Ethereum) |
|---|---|---|
| **State** | Set of unspent outputs | Account balances + storage |
| **Parallelism** | High (independent UTXOs) | Limited (serial execution) |
| **Privacy** | Better (new address per tx) | Weaker (linked addresses) |
| **Complexity** | More complex scripts | Simpler logic |
| **Double-spend check** | Spend same UTXO twice fails | Reject if balance insufficient |
| **Smart Contracts** | Limited (Script) | First-class (EVM) |

### 3.5.3 SHA-256 vs RIPEMD-160 (Hashing Components)

| Property | SHA-256 | RIPEMD-160 |
|---|---|---|
| Output size | 256 bits | 160 bits |
| Used in Bitcoin | Block hashing, mining | Address generation |
| Collision resistance | $2^{128}$ (birthday) | $2^{80}$ (birthday) |
| Standardization | NIST FIPS 180-4 | Less universally adopted |
| Speed | Optimized in ASIC | Slower in hardware |
| KTU relevance | High — must explain | Moderate — usually auxiliary |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Block Reward After Multiple Halvings

> **Problem:** *"Find the block reward after the 4th Bitcoin halving and the cumulative BTC mined in the first 5 epochs."*

### Step 1 — Compute reward after 4th halving ($i = 4$)

$$R_4 = 50 \times 2^{-4} = \frac{50}{16} = 3.125 \;\; \text{BTC}$$

### Step 2 — Compute reward in each of the first 5 epochs

$$R_0 = 50, \quad R_1 = 25, \quad R_2 = 12.5, \quad R_3 = 6.25, \quad R_4 = 3.125$$

### Step 3 — Compute BTC mined in each epoch

$$S_i = 210{,}000 \times R_i$$

$$S_0 = 10{,}500{,}000$$

$$S_1 = 5{,}250{,}000$$

$$S_2 = 2{,}625{,}000$$

$$S_3 = 1{,}312{,}500$$

$$S_4 = 656{,}250$$

### Step 4 — Sum the first 5 epochs

$$\sum_{i=0}^{4} S_i = 10{,}500{,}000 + 5{,}250{,}000 + 2{,}625{,}000 + 1{,}312{,}500 + 656{,}250$$

$$= 20{,}343{,}750 \;\; \text{BTC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** After 5 halvings, ~96.87% of the total 21 million BTC will already have been mined. This is the asymptotic property of the geometric series — most issuance happens early.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **architectural relationships** between the components of a cryptocurrency. These diagrams are tailored to the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus and are drawn in a "block-level functional architecture" style for clarity.

---

## 4.1 High-Level Component Architecture (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userLayer["User Layer<br>Wallets and Keys"]
    txLayer["Transaction Layer<br>Inputs Outputs Scripts"]
    blockLayer["Block Layer<br>Merkle Root Header"]
    consensusLayer["Consensus Layer<br>Proof of Work or Proof of Stake"]
    netLayer["P2P Network Layer<br>Gossip Protocol"]
    cryptoLayer["Cryptographic Layer<br>SHA-256 ECDSA RIPEMD-160"]

    userLayer --> txLayer
    txLayer --> blockLayer
    blockLayer --> consensusLayer
    consensusLayer --> netLayer
    cryptoLayer -.secures.-> txLayer
    cryptoLayer -.secures.-> blockLayer
    cryptoLayer -.secures.-> consensusLayer
    netLayer -.propagates.-> blockLayer
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** show the *flow of data*: a user's wallet creates transactions → transactions are bundled into blocks → blocks pass through consensus → final blocks are propagated via P2P.
- The **dashed arrows** show the *security bindings*: the cryptographic layer protects every other layer.

---

## 4.2 Transaction Lifecycle Flow (Wallet to Confirmation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    walletA["Wallet A<br>Private Key kA"]
    createTx["Construct Transaction<br>Reference UTXO from TxID 7F"]
    signTx["Sign with ECDSA<br>Signature rA sA"]
    broadcastTx["Broadcast to P2P Network<br>Gossip to 8 Peers"]
    mempool["Mempool<br>Unconfirmed Transactions"]
    minerNode["Miner Node<br>Select Highest Fee Tx"]
    buildBlock["Build Candidate Block<br>Compute Merkle Root"]
    powSearch["Proof of Work Search<br>Iterate Nonce"]
    validBlock["Valid Block Found<br>H Header less than Target"]
    propagate["Block Propagation<br>All Nodes Validate"]
    confirmChain["Append to Chain<br>Confirmation Count plus 1"]

    walletA --> createTx
    createTx --> signTx
    signTx --> broadcastTx
    broadcastTx --> mempool
    mempool --> minerNode
    minerNode --> buildBlock
    buildBlock --> powSearch
    powSearch --> validBlock
    validBlock --> propagate
    propagate --> confirmChain
```

**Key observation:** A transaction passes through **three trust boundaries** — wallet, network, and miner — and is only "final" once it has accumulated sufficient confirmations.

---

## 4.3 Block Header Structure (Merkle Tree of Transactions)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootNode["Merkle Root<br>32 bytes"]

    nodeA["Hash AB<br>32 bytes"]
    nodeB["Hash CD<br>32 bytes"]

    leafA["Hash A<br>Tx1"]
    leafB["Hash B<br>Tx2"]
    leafC["Hash C<br>Tx3"]
    leafD["Hash D<br>Tx4"]

    rootNode --> nodeA
    rootNode --> nodeB
    nodeA --> leafA
    nodeA --> leafB
    nodeB --> leafC
    nodeB --> leafD
```

**Properties:**
- Each leaf is the **double-SHA-256** of a transaction.
- Each internal node is the **double-SHA-256** of the concatenation of its children.
- A change in **any** transaction alters the entire path to the root — this is the cryptographic foundation of immutability.

---

## 4.4 Consensus Comparison: PoW vs PoS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph powBox["Proof of Work Branch"]
        pow1["Miner Competes on Hashrate"]
        pow2["Solve Cryptographic Puzzle"]
        pow3["Winner Appends Block"]
        pow1 --> pow2 --> pow3
    end

    subgraph posBox["Proof of Stake Branch"]
        pos1["Validator Stakes Coins"]
        pos2["Random Selection Weighted by Stake"]
        pos3["Block Proposed and Attested"]
        pos1 --> pos2 --> pos3
    end

    final["Both Pathways Converge to<br>Append Block to Chain"]

    pow3 --> final
    pos3 --> final
```

---

## 4.5 Mining Difficulty Adjustment Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    observe["Observe Actual Block Time<br>Over Last 2016 Blocks"]
    compare["Compare to Expected<br>1,209,600 seconds"]
    adjust["Compute Adjustment Factor<br>At over Et"]
    clamp["Clamp to 4x Bounds"]
    newTarget["Apply New Target"]
    newDiff["New Difficulty D<br>Equals Dmax over T"]
    affectMiners["Affects Miner Behavior<br>More or Less Hashing"]
    observe -.next 2016 blocks.-> observe

    affectMiners --> observe
    newTarget --> newDiff
    newDiff --> affectMiners
```

**Engineering insight:** This is a **closed-loop negative feedback control system**, analogous to a **thermostat**. The setpoint is 600 seconds; the control variable is difficulty; the disturbance is fluctuating hashrate.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define a cryptocurrency and list its six core components.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **cryptocurrency** is a digital medium of exchange that uses **cryptography** for security, operates on a **decentralized peer-to-peer network**, and maintains state through a **distributed consensus mechanism** (e.g., Proof of Work) without requiring a central issuing authority.
>
> The **six core components** are:
> 1. **Transaction System** — defines how value is transferred (UTXO or account model).
> 2. **Consensus Mechanism** — defines how nodes agree (PoW, PoS).
> 3. **Blockchain Data Structure** — append-only, hash-linked ledger of blocks.
> 4. **P2P Network** — gossip-protocol-based communication layer.
> 5. **Scripting / Smart Contract Layer** — programmable spending conditions.
> 6. **Cryptographic Primitives** — SHA-256 hashing, ECDSA signatures, Merkle trees.

**Valuation Note (1 mark breakdown):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Six components (any 4): 2 marks

---

### Question 2. What is the difference between the UTXO model and the Account model in cryptocurrency transactions?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | UTXO Model (Bitcoin) | Account Model (Ethereum) |
|---|---|---|
| State representation | Set of unspent transaction outputs | Global map of accounts to balances |
| Transaction consumes | Whole UTXOs (creates change) | Decrements balance |
| Parallel execution | Easier (independent UTXOs) | Harder (serialized) |
| Example | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Ethereum, Solana |

In the **UTXO model**, the network state is the set of all unspent outputs; in the **Account model**, the state is a balance map. UTXO enables higher parallelism; Account is simpler for smart contracts.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Choose **ONE** of the two questions.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested Module 3 Question]**
> *"Explain the architecture of a cryptocurrency in detail. With the aid of diagrams, describe the role of each of the following components: (a) Transaction system, Mining & Consensus (Proof of Work), and (b) P2P Networking and Cryptographic Primitives. Show mathematically how the difficulty target is adjusted and the total supply of Bitcoin is capped at 21 million."*

#### Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

The **transaction system** is the unit of state change. A Bitcoin transaction contains **inputs** (referencing previous unspent outputs via `txid` and `vout`), **outputs** (specifying value and a locking script), and is **digitally signed** using **ECDSA on secp256k1**. The unlocking script (`scriptSig`) must satisfy the locking script (`scriptPubKey`) of the referenced UTXO.

**Mining & Proof of Work (PoW)** is the **consensus component**. Miners collect unconfirmed transactions, build a Merkle tree, construct a block header, and iterate the **nonce** until:

$$H(\text{Header}) < T$$

The first miner to find a valid hash broadcasts the block; the network accepts the **longest valid chain** (Nakamoto consensus). This process:
- **Decentralizes** block production (probability proportional to hashrate).
- **Secures** history (re-mining requires > 50% of global hashrate).
- **Issues** new coins (current reward: 3.125 BTC post-2024 halving).

**Block anatomy:** Version, Previous Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Bits, Nonce.

**Mark allocation:**
- Transaction system explanation: 2 marks
- Mining + PoW explanation: 3 marks
- Block anatomy: 1 mark
- Diagram reference: 1 mark

#### Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

**P2P Networking** uses a **gossip protocol**: when a node learns of a new transaction/block, it forwards it to all peers, who validate and re-broadcast. This achieves **exponential propagation** (~90% reach in seconds) and **eventual consistency**. Properties include permissionless entry and Sybil resistance via PoW's economic cost.

**Cryptographic Primitives** are the security foundation:
- **SHA-256:** $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$ — one-way, collision-resistant, avalanche-effect.
- **ECDSA (secp256k1):** Key pair $(k, K=kG)$; signature $(r, s)$ proves ownership of $k$ without revealing it.
- **RIPEMD-160:** Used in address generation for compactness.
- **Merkle Tree:** Efficient $O(\log n)$ inclusion proofs for SPV clients.

**Difficulty Adjustment Derivation:**

Every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks), the target is recalculated:

$$T_{new} = T_{old} \times \frac{A_t}{E_t}, \quad E_t = 2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600 \; \text{s}$$

Clamped to a factor of 4. This restores the 10-minute block time after hashrate fluctuations.

**Total Supply Derivation:**

$$S_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \times 50 \times 2^{-i} = 21{,}000{,}000 \; \text{BTC}$$

This is the **geometric series** result — hence the 21 million cap is mathematical, not arbitrary.

**Mark allocation:**
- P2P networking: 2 marks
- Cryptographic primitives (any 3 explained): 2 marks
- Difficulty derivation: 1.5 marks
- Supply cap derivation: 1.5 marks

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> *"With suitable diagrams, describe the components of a Bitcoin-style cryptocurrency. (a) Explain transaction lifecycle and wallet architecture in detail. (b) Compare Proof of Work and Proof of Stake as consensus components. Compute the probability of a successful double-spend attack for an attacker with 20% hashrate at 6 confirmations."*

#### Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

**Wallet Architecture** consists of:
- **Private key** ($k$): 256-bit secret enabling transaction signing.
- **Public key** ($K = kG$): derived via elliptic curve multiplication.
- **Address** ($A = \text{Base58Check}(\text{RIPEMD-160}(\text{SHA-256}(K)))$): shared, public identifier.
- **Deterministic wallets (BIP-32/39/44):** a single 12/24-word mnemonic seed regenerates the entire key tree via HMAC-SHA512.

**Transaction Lifecycle (8 stages):**

1. **Creation:** Wallet constructs a transaction referencing one or more UTXOs.
2. **Signing:** Wallet signs with ECDSA private key → produces $(r, s)$ signature.
3. **Broadcast:** Transaction is gossiped to peers.
4. **Mempool Acceptance:** Nodes validate syntax, signatures, UTXO existence.
5. **Mining Selection:** Miner prioritizes by fee density (satoshis/vByte).
6. **Block Construction:** Merkle root computed; block header assembled.
7. **PoW Solution:** Nonce iterated until $H(\text{Header}) < T$.
8. **Confirmation:** Other nodes validate and append; confirmation count increases.

**Mark allocation:**
- Wallet architecture (private/public/address + derivation): 3 marks
- Transaction lifecycle (any 5 stages): 2 marks
- Diagram of lifecycle: 2 marks

#### Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | PoW | PoS |
|---|---|---|
| Resource | Energy | Locked stake |
| Attack cost | Electricity (sunk) | Slashable stake |
| Energy use | High | Low |
| Finality | Probabilistic | Often deterministic |
| Centralization | Mining pools | Wealth concentration |

**Double-Spend Probability Calculation (3 marks):**

Given attacker hashrate $q = 0.2$, honest $p = 0.8$, confirmations $z = 6$:

$$P_{catchup} = \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^{z} = \left(\frac{0.2}{0.8}\right)^{6} = (0.25)^{6}$$

$$(0.25)^{6} = \frac{1}{4096} \approx 2.44 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\approx 0.0244\%$$

**Interpretation:** Even with **20% hashrate** and **6 confirmations**, the attacker has only a **1 in 4,096** chance. This is why exchanges wait for more confirmations on large transactions (e.g., 60 confirmations for deposits > $1M).

**Mark allocation:**
- PoW vs PoS comparison: 4 marks
- Formula + substitution: 1.5 marks
- Final numerical answer + interpretation: 1.5 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Components" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing without linking (–2 to –3 marks):** Students enumerate the six components as bullet points but fail to show how transactions → blocks → consensus → chain. **Always draw the inter-component flow diagram.**
>
> 2. **Confusing wallet types (–1 mark):** Mixing up *non-deterministic* (JBOK) wallets with *deterministic* (BIP-32/HD) wallets. KTU expects clarity on **HD wallets** and **mnemonic seeds**.
>
> 3. **Wrong formula for total supply (–2 marks):** Writing "$S = 21$ million" without showing the geometric series derivation loses full marks. **Always derive, never just state.**
>
> 4. **Difficulty adjustment errors (–1.5 marks):** Confusing the **adjustment period** (2016 blocks) with the **halving period** (210,000 blocks). These are different constants.
>
> 5. **Missing units (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Writing $H = 2.4$ without specifying "× $10^{18}$ hashes/sec" or "EH/s" loses a half-mark for unit clarity.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the 4× clamp (–1 mark):** Not mentioning the protocol's bound on target adjustment is a common omission.
>
> 7. **Conflating PoW and mining (–1 mark):** Mining is a *process*; PoW is a *consensus mechanism*. Mining *implements* PoW but the two terms are not synonymous.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Components of a Cryptocurrency**

### 🔑 Core Concepts (Must Know)

- ☐ A **cryptocurrency** is secured by cryptography, decentralized, and consensus-driven.
- ☐ The **six components** are: Transactions, Consensus, Blockchain, P2P, Scripting, Cryptography.
- ☐ A **transaction** references UTXOs (Bitcoin) or decrements accounts (Ethereum).
- ☐ **Mining** solves the PoW puzzle: $H(\text{Header}) < T$.
- ☐ **Difficulty** adjusts every 2016 blocks using $T_{new} = T_{old} \times \frac{A_t}{E_t}$, clamped 4×.
- ☐ **Block reward halves** every 210,000 blocks: $R_n = 50 \times 2^{-n}$.
- ☐ **Total supply** is a geometric series summing to **21,000,000 BTC**.
- ☐ **Hash linking** ensures immutability (changing one block invalidates all subsequent).
- ☐ **Gossip protocol** propagates transactions/blocks exponentially.
- ☐ **SHA-256 + ECDSA** are the foundational cryptographic primitives.
- ☐ **Merkle trees** enable $O(\log n)$ SPV inclusion proofs.
- ☐ **Double-spend attack** probability: $\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^{z}$ for $q < p$.
- ☐ **HD wallets** use a single mnemonic seed (BIP-39) → deterministic key tree (BIP-32).
- ☐ **PoW** = external cost (energy); **PoS** = internal cost (stake, slashable).

### 🔑 Key Numbers to Memorize

- ☐ Block time: **600 s (10 min)**
- ☐ Adjustment period: **2016 blocks**
- ☐ Halving period: **210,000 blocks**
- ☐ Initial reward: **50 BTC**
- ☐ Total cap: **21,000,000 BTC**
- ☐ Confirmation threshold (standard): **6 confirmations**
- ☐ Address size: **160 bits (20 bytes)**
- ☐ Hash output: **256 bits (32 bytes)**
- ☐ Target clamp: **4× in either direction**

### 🔑 Acronyms (Frequently Tested)

- ☐ **UTXO** — Unspent Transaction Output
- ☐ **ECDSA** — Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm
- ☐ **PoW** — Proof of Work
- ☐ **PoS** — Proof of Stake
- ☐ **SPV** — Simplified Payment Verification
- ☐ **P2PKH** — Pay-to-Public-Key-Hash
- ☐ **P2SH** — Pay-to-Script-Hash
- ☐ **BIP** — Bitcoin Improvement Proposal
- ☐ **HD** — Hierarchical Deterministic (wallet)
- ☐ **EVM** — Ethereum Virtual Machine
- ☐ **P2P** — Peer-to-Peer
- ☐ **HMAC** — Hash-based Message Authentication Code
- ☐ **RIPEMD** — RACE Integrity Primitives Evaluation Message Digest

### 🔑 Common 14-Mark Question Patterns

- ☐ *"Explain the components of a cryptocurrency with diagrams."* → Use the 6-component layered model.
- ☐ *"Derive the 21 million BTC cap."* → Geometric series derivation (Section 3.1).
- ☐ *"How does the mining difficulty adjust?"* → Target formula + clamp (Section 3.2).
- ☐ *"Compare PoW and PoS."* → Use the comparison matrix (Section 3.5.1).
- ☐ *"Explain transaction lifecycle."* → 8-stage flow (Question B part a).
- ☐ *"Compute double-spend probability."* → Gambler's ruin formula (Section 3.3).

> **Final KTU Tip:** When writing the 14-mark answer, dedicate **1.5 marks worth of content to a labeled Mermaid-style diagram**. Examiners explicitly reward visual structure under the NEP 2020 outcome-based evaluation framework. A diagram without labels loses marks; a labeled diagram without explanation also loses marks — always pair them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nodes in Bitcoin Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nodes in Bitcoin Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Bitcoin peer-to-peer (P2P) network, a **node** is any computer or device that participates in the Bitcoin protocol by maintaining a connection to other nodes, exchanging transaction and block data, and enforcing the consensus rules of the system. More precisely, a Bitcoin node is a software instance (typically `bitcoind`, `Bitcoin Core`, or any compatible client) that implements the Bitcoin protocol and participates in the overlay network using TCP port **8333** (mainnet) for inter-node communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST747 – Module 3):** A node in the Bitcoin network is an endpoint in the decentralized P2P architecture that validates, relays, and stores blockchain data. Nodes collectively maintain the integrity, security, and continuity of the distributed ledger without relying on a central authority.

A node is identified in the network by its **IP address** and **port number**, and each node maintains a list of peer connections to other nodes, typically establishing **8 outbound connections** by default in Bitcoin Core. When a node receives a new transaction or block, it independently validates it against the consensus rules before propagating it further.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consensus Rule Enforcement:** Every full node enforces the exact same set of consensus rules (e.g., block size limits, valid signatures, correct difficulty target, valid coinbase reward). Disagreement on consensus rules leads to a **chain fork** (e.g., the 2017 Bitcoin Cash fork).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **village notice board system**:
- Every villager (node) has their own **copy of the notice board** (blockchain ledger).
- When someone (a user) wants to post a new notice (a transaction), they announce it to their neighbors.
- Each neighbor **checks the notice** (validates the transaction) to ensure it is genuine and the person has the right to post it.
- Once verified, the notice is **pinned to the board** (added to the mempool) and **shouted across the village** (broadcast through P2P network).
- Every villager updates their copy so that **everyone has the same information** at the same time.

In this analogy:
- **Villagers** = Bitcoin Nodes
- **Notice board copy** = Local copy of the blockchain
- **Posting a notice** = Broadcasting a transaction
- **Shouting across the village** = P2P gossip protocol
- **Village agreement** = Network consensus

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Full Node** | A node that stores the complete blockchain and independently validates all transactions and blocks against consensus rules. |
| **Lightweight Node (SPV)** | Simplified Payment Verification node that downloads only block headers and relies on full nodes for transaction validation. |
| **Mining Node** | A full node equipped with mining hardware/software that competes to solve the Proof-of-Work puzzle and produce new blocks. |
| **Mempool** | A node's in-memory pool of unconfirmed transactions awaiting inclusion in a block. |
| **Outbound Peer** | A connection initiated by the node to another peer (default: 8 in Bitcoin Core). |
| **Inbound Peer** | A connection accepted by the node from another peer (default maximum: 125 in Bitcoin Core). |
| **Gossip Protocol** | The method by which nodes propagate transactions and blocks to their connected peers. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** A typical full node on the Bitcoin mainnet requires approximately **500 GB of disk space (as of 2024)** to store the full blockchain, and processes blocks at an average interval of **10 minutes**.

## 1.4 Significance in the Bitcoin Ecosystem

Nodes are the **backbone of decentralization** in Bitcoin. Without a distributed network of independently-operating nodes:
- There would be no way to verify transactions without trusting a central party.
- The blockchain would be a single point of failure.
- Consensus rules could be changed unilaterally by a single entity.
- Censorship resistance would be lost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Mining nodes produce blocks, but **full nodes (non-mining)** are equally vital because they validate and enforce the rules. A miner's block is worthless if no full node accepts it. This is the essence of Bitcoin's security model — economic incentives (mining) plus cryptographic verification (full nodes).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Nodes in the Bitcoin Network

The Bitcoin network supports several distinct types of nodes, each with different responsibilities, storage requirements, and trust assumptions.

### 2.1.1 Full Nodes (Archival Full Nodes)

A **full node** is the most secure and independent participant in the Bitcoin network. It:
- Stores the **complete blockchain** from the genesis block (Block #0) to the current chain tip.
- Independently validates every transaction and block against all consensus rules.
- Relays valid transactions and blocks to connected peers.
- Maintains a copy of the **Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO) set** in memory for fast validation.
- Rejects invalid blocks and double-spend attempts.

**Key Sub-categories:**
- **Pruned Full Node**: Stores only the most recent N MB of blocks (user-configured) but still validates all historical transactions.
- **Archival Full Node**: Stores the entire blockchain history with no pruning.

### 2.1.2 Lightweight Nodes (SPV Clients)

**Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)** nodes were introduced in the original Bitcoin whitepaper (Section 3). They:
- Download only **block headers** (approximately 80 bytes per header), not full block contents.
- Rely on full nodes to provide Merkle proof branches for verifying that a transaction is included in a block.
- Are typically used in **mobile wallets** (e.g., older versions of Bitcoin Wallet for Android).
- Trust that the longest chain (most accumulated Proof-of-Work) is the valid chain.
- Have **weaker security guarantees** because they do not fully validate consensus rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **SPV Security Trade-off:** SPV nodes are vulnerable to **fraud proofs** in some attack scenarios where a malicious full node could withhold information. They assume that the majority of mining power follows the consensus rules.

### 2.1.3 Mining Nodes

A **mining node** is a full node that additionally:
- Collects unconfirmed transactions from its mempool.
- Constructs a candidate block template.
- Iterates the **nonce** value to find a hash that meets the network's **difficulty target**.
- Broadcasts the successful block to the network.
- Receives the **block reward** (currently 3.125 BTC after the 2024 halving) plus transaction fees.

### 2.1.4 Comparison Matrix of Node Types

| Feature | Full Node | Lightweight (SPV) | Mining Node |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Stores Full Blockchain | Yes | No (only headers) | Yes |
| Validates All Transactions | Yes | No (relies on Merkle proofs) | Yes |
| Independently Enforces Consensus | Yes | Partial (trust longest chain) | Yes |
| Contributes to Network Security | High | Low | Highest |
| Storage Requirement | ~500 GB | ~50 MB | ~500 GB + |
| Typical Use Case | Servers, Validators, Exchanges | Mobile Wallets | Mining Pools, Solo Miners |

## 2.2 Network Communication Protocol

### 2.2.1 Connection Establishment

When a Bitcoin node starts, it:
1. **Queries DNS seeds** (e.g., `seed.bitcoin.sipa.be`, `dnsseed.bluematt.me`) to obtain a list of peer IP addresses.
2. Establishes a **TCP connection** to candidate peers on port 8333.
3. Performs a **version handshake**, exchanging `version`, `verack`, and `sendcmpct` messages.
4. Optionally requests **block headers** (`getheaders`) or **mempool contents** (`mempool`).

### 2.2.2 Message Types

Bitcoin nodes exchange over **25 standardized message types**. The most critical are:

| Message | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `inv` | Inventory announcement (announces a transaction or block hash). |
| `getdata` | Requests full data for a specific hash. |
| `tx` | Transmits a transaction. |
| `block` | Transmits a full block. |
| `getheaders` | Requests block headers. |
| `headers` | Responds with up to 2000 block headers. |
| `ping` / `pong` | Connection liveness check. |
| `addr` | Shares known peer addresses. |
| `reject` | Notifies of an invalid transaction or block. |

### 2.2.3 Block Propagation

When a miner finds a new block:
1. The block is **broadcast** to the miner's connected peers.
2. Each peer validates the block (PoW, signatures, transactions).
3. Validated blocks are forwarded to other peers using the **inv** message flow.
4. Propagation typically completes across the global network in **seconds** (median: 6-12 seconds for 95% of nodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compact Block Relay (BIP 152):** To reduce bandwidth, nodes send a **compact block** that includes only the transaction IDs. The receiving node reconstructs the full block from its mempool, requesting only missing transactions. This reduces block propagation latency by approximately **80%**.

## 2.3 The Role of Nodes in Consensus

### 2.3.1 Independent Validation

Every full node independently executes the **consensus rules**. This includes:
- Checking that each input references an unspent output.
- Verifying that each transaction's total outputs do not exceed its total inputs.
- Ensuring that the block's **Proof-of-Work** meets the current difficulty target.
- Enforcing the **subsidy halving schedule** (every 210,000 blocks, the block reward halves).
- Rejecting blocks containing double-spends or invalid signatures.

### 2.3.2 The Longest Chain Rule

When multiple valid chain branches exist (temporary forks), nodes follow the **cumulative Proof-of-Work** rule — selecting the chain with the **most total work** (not necessarily the most blocks). This is the **Nakamoto Consensus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Most Work" Instead of "Most Blocks"?** Difficulty adjustment ensures that a chain with higher difficulty per block represents more computational effort. A chain with 10 blocks at difficulty D1 may have less work than a chain with 5 blocks at difficulty D2 > D1.

## 2.4 Node Discovery Mechanism

Bitcoin uses a combination of techniques for node discovery:
- **DNS Seeds**: Hardcoded list of DNS servers maintained by Bitcoin Core developers.
- **Addr Messages**: Nodes periodically share their known peer addresses via `addr` messages.
- **Hardcoded Seed Nodes**: Initial list embedded in the client software.
- **Electrum Servers / Personal Node Servers**: Alternative discovery via trusted servers.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the key numerical relationships relevant to Bitcoin nodes for KTU examinations.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------|
| Difficulty Target | $D = \frac{D_{\text{max}}}{T}$ | $D_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum target (easiest), $D$ is the current target. |
| Cumulative Work | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2^{256}}{D_i}$ | Total Proof-of-Work across the chain. |
| Block Reward (post-2024 halving) | $R = \frac{50}{2^k}$ | $k$ = number of halvings that have occurred (currently $k=3$, so $R = 3.125$ BTC). |
| Bitcoin Supply Cap | $S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{210{,}000 \cdot 50}{2^k} = 21{,}000{,}000$ | Maximum 21 million BTC will ever be mined. |
| Halving Interval | $T_{\text{halving}} = 210{,}000$ blocks | Approximately every 4 years. |
| Block Time Target | $T_{\text{block}} = 10 \text{ minutes} = 600 \text{ seconds}$ | Average time between blocks. |
| Difficulty Adjustment Period | $N_{\text{adj}} = 2016 \text{ blocks}$ | Recalculation period (≈ 2 weeks). |
| Peer Connections (Outbound) | $C_{\text{out}} = 8$ | Default in Bitcoin Core. |
| Peer Connections (Max Inbound) | $C_{\text{in,max}} = 125$ | Default in Bitcoin Core. |
| Block Header Size | $H_{\text{size}} = 80 \text{ bytes}$ | Version (4) + Prev Hash (32) + Merkle Root (32) + Timestamp (4) + Bits (4) + Nonce (4). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Understanding node communication is essential for designing blockchain monitoring tools, building wallet applications, and analyzing network performance in real-world distributed systems.

## 2.6 Block and Transaction Propagation Flow

The data flow through a Bitcoin network follows a structured **gossip protocol**:

1. **Origination**: A wallet creates and signs a transaction, then sends it to its connected node.
2. **Validation**: The receiving node validates the transaction (signatures, UTXO existence, script execution).
3. **Mempool Insertion**: Valid transactions are added to the node's mempool.
4. **Announcement**: The node sends an `inv` (inventory) message to all connected peers.
5. **Request**: Peers that do not have the transaction respond with `getdata`.
6. **Transmission**: The full transaction is sent via a `tx` message.
7. **Recursive Propagation**: The process repeats at each receiving node, ensuring exponential spread.

> [!WARNING]
> **Duplicate Prevention:** To prevent infinite broadcast loops, each node tracks transactions it has already seen (using a transaction hash set) and forwards each transaction only **once**. This is known as **duplicate transaction detection**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Bitcoin Supply and Halving Schedule

The Bitcoin protocol defines a deterministic monetary policy. Let us derive the total supply mathematically.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

The block reward starts at **50 BTC** at the genesis block and is **halved every 210,000 blocks**. This forms a geometric series.

**Step 1**: Define the block reward at the $k$-th halving era.

$$R_k = \frac{R_0}{2^k} = \frac{50}{2^k}$$

where $R_0 = 50$ BTC is the initial reward, and $k \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$.

**Step 2**: The total BTC mined during era $k$ is the reward per block times the number of blocks per era.

$$S_k = R_k \times 210{,}000 = \frac{50 \times 210{,}000}{2^k} = \frac{10{,}500{,}000}{2^k}$$

**Step 3**: The total supply is the sum of all era supplies, forming an infinite geometric series with first term $a = 10{,}500{,}000$ and common ratio $r = \frac{1}{2}$.

$$S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} S_k = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{10{,}500{,}000}{2^k}$$

**Step 4**: Apply the geometric series formula $S = \frac{a}{1 - r}$.

$$S_{\text{total}} = \frac{10{,}500{,}000}{1 - \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{10{,}500{,}000}{\frac{1}{2}} = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$

**Conclusion**: The maximum total supply of Bitcoin is exactly **21,000,000 BTC**, and approximately **19.7 million** have been mined as of 2024.

## 3.2 Derivation: Difficulty Adjustment Formula

The Bitcoin protocol adjusts the mining difficulty every 2016 blocks to maintain an average block time of 10 minutes.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Let $T_{\text{actual}}$ be the actual time taken to mine the last 2016 blocks. The protocol measures this in seconds.

$$T_{\text{actual}} = T_{2016} - T_{0}$$

where $T_{2016}$ is the timestamp of the last block in the period and $T_0$ is the timestamp of the first block.

**Step 2**: The expected time for 2016 blocks at 10 minutes per block is:

$$T_{\text{expected}} = 2016 \times 10 \times 60 = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ seconds} \quad (14 \text{ days})$$

**Step 3**: The difficulty adjustment ratio is:

$$\rho = \frac{T_{\text{expected}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 4**: The new difficulty target is calculated as:

$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \rho = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{expected}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 5**: The protocol enforces a **4x maximum adjustment cap** in either direction per period to prevent abrupt changes:

$$D_{\text{new}} = \min\left(\max\left(D_{\text{old}} \times \rho, \frac{D_{\text{max}}}{4}\right), 4 \times D_{\text{max}}\right)$$

where $D_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum (easiest) target.

**Example**: If 2016 blocks were mined in 12 days (faster than expected), the difficulty increases by:

$$\rho = \frac{14 \text{ days}}{12 \text{ days}} = 1.167$$

so the new target is **1.167× harder** (smaller).

## 3.3 Derivation: Probability of Successful Mining

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

The probability that a single hash attempt produces a valid block is:

$$P = \frac{D}{2^{256}}$$

where $D$ is the current difficulty target value and $2^{256}$ is the total hash space.

For a miner with hash rate $h$ (hashes per second), the expected number of hashes in time $t$ is:

$$N = h \times t$$

The probability of finding at least one valid hash in $N$ attempts (assuming independence) is:

$$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - (1 - P)^N \approx 1 - e^{-N \cdot P} = 1 - e^{-h \cdot t \cdot P}$$

The expected time to find a block is:

$$E[T] = \frac{1}{h \cdot P} = \frac{2^{256}}{h \cdot D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This relationship shows that doubling the network hash rate halves the average time per block, which is precisely why the difficulty adjustment mechanism is necessary to maintain the 10-minute target.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Node Behavior Under Different Scenarios

| Scenario | Full Node Behavior | SPV Node Behavior | Network Impact |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Receives valid transaction | Adds to mempool, broadcasts `inv` | Trusts full node, updates wallet balance | Transaction spreads exponentially. |
| Receives valid block | Validates all txs, appends to chain, relays block | Verifies header, requests Merkle proof | New tip established. |
| Receives invalid block (bad PoW) | Rejects immediately, may ban peer | Cannot detect (assumes longest chain is valid) | Invalid block does not propagate. |
| Receives double-spend | Rejects if UTXO already spent | May accept if conflicting tx is in longest chain | Double-spend attempt fails. |
| Network partition heals | Switches to chain with most cumulative work | Switches to longest chain seen | Network re-converges. |
| Receives block over block size limit | Rejects (post-SegWit: rejects if weight > 4M) | Cannot validate | Fork occurs (e.g., BCH in 2017). |

## 3.5 Derivation: Block Propagation Latency Estimation

The approximate propagation time of a block across the network can be modeled using a **logarithmic growth model**.

**Step 1**: Assume the network has $N$ nodes with average degree $d$ (number of peers per node). A single block propagates in a series of "rounds."

**Step 2**: In round $t$, the number of nodes that have received the block grows approximately as:

$$N_{\text{informed}}(t) = N \cdot \left(1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{d \cdot t}\right)$$

**Step 3**: Solving for the time $T$ when 90% of nodes are informed:

$$T = \frac{\ln(0.1)}{d \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)} \approx \frac{\ln(10) \cdot N}{d}$$

**Step 4**: For a typical Bitcoin network with $N \approx 15{,}000$ reachable nodes and $d \approx 8$:

$$T \approx \frac{2.303 \times 15{,}000}{8} \approx 4{,}320 \text{ gossip rounds}$$

If each round takes 100 ms, total propagation time is approximately **432 seconds** in the worst case. In practice, with compact blocks and high connectivity, median propagation is **under 12 seconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world validation:** Empirical studies (e.g., Decker & Wattenhofer, 2013) show that block propagation follows a **log-normal distribution** with median around 6-8 seconds, matching theoretical models.

## 3.6 Derivation: SPV Merkle Proof Verification

To verify a transaction is in a block, an SPV node requests a **Merkle proof** from a full node.

**Step 1**: The full node provides the transaction hash $T$ and a set of sibling hashes $H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_k$ from the Merkle tree.

**Step 2**: The SPV node computes the Merkle root:

$$R = H(H(\ldots H(H(T, H_1), H_2) \ldots, H_k)$$

where $H(a, b)$ is the SHA-256 double-hash of the concatenation.

**Step 3**: The SPV node compares the computed root $R$ with the **Merkle root** in the block header. If they match, the transaction is verified as included in the block.

**Step 4**: Proof size: For a block with $n$ transactions, the Merkle proof requires $\log_2(n)$ hashes, each 32 bytes. For $n \approx 3000$ transactions, the proof is approximately:

$$L_{\text{proof}} = \log_2(3000) \times 32 \approx 11.55 \times 32 \approx 370 \text{ bytes}$$

This is a massive bandwidth saving compared to downloading the full block (~$1.5$ MB).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bitcoin Network P2P Topology

The following diagram illustrates the decentralized P2P topology of the Bitcoin network, showing how nodes interconnect, the distinction between node types, and the flow of transaction and block data.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph User["USER LAYER"]
        W["Wallet Application"]
    end

    subgraph SPVLayer["LIGHTWEIGHT NODES LAYER"]
        SPV1["SPV Node Mobile"]
        SPV2["SPV Node Browser"]
    end

    subgraph FullLayer["FULL NODES LAYER"]
        FN1["Full Node Server 1"]
        FN2["Full Node Server 2"]
        FN3["Full Node Server 3"]
    end

    subgraph MiningLayer["MINING NODES LAYER"]
        MN1["Mining Pool A"]
        MN2["Mining Pool B"]
        MN3["Solo Miner"]
    end

    subgraph DNS["DISCOVERY LAYER"]
        DNS1["DNS Seed 1"]
        DNS2["DNS Seed 2"]
    end

    subgraph Ledger["BLOCKCHAIN STORAGE"]
        BC1["Full Block 1 to N"]
        BC2["Block Headers"]
    end

    W -->|tx broadcast| FN1
    W -->|tx broadcast| SPV1
    SPV1 -->|header sync| FN1
    SPV2 -->|header sync| FN2
    FN1 <-->|inv block tx| FN2
    FN2 <-->|inv block tx| FN3
    FN3 <-->|inv block tx| FN1
    MN1 <-->|block relay| FN1
    MN2 <-->|block relay| FN2
    MN3 <-->|block relay| FN3
    FN1 -->|stores| BC1
    FN2 -->|stores| BC1
    FN3 -->|stores| BC1
    SPV1 -->|stores| BC2
    SPV2 -->|stores| BC2
    DNS1 -.->|peer list| FN1
    DNS1 -.->|peer list| FN2
    DNS2 -.->|peer list| FN3

    classDef userStyle fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef spvStyle fill:#B4E5FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fullStyle fill:#B4FFB4,stroke:#00AA00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef miningStyle fill:#FFB4B4,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dnsStyle fill:#E5B4FF,stroke:#6600CC,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ledgerStyle fill:#FFEEAA,stroke:#AA8800,stroke-width:2px

    class W userStyle
    class SPV1,SPV2 spvStyle
    class FN1,FN2,FN3 fullStyle
    class MN1,MN2,MN3 miningStyle
    class DNS1,DNS2 dnsStyle
    class BC1,BC2 ledgerStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The diagram shows the multi-tier architecture of the Bitcoin network. The dotted lines from DNS seeds represent initial peer discovery. The solid bidirectional arrows between full nodes represent the continuous gossip protocol. Mining nodes are connected to full nodes and use them to broadcast newly mined blocks.

## 4.2 Transaction Propagation Flow (Gossip Protocol)

This flowchart describes the step-by-step propagation of a single transaction through the network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Creates Transaction in Wallet"]
    B["Wallet Signs Transaction with Private Key"]
    C["Transaction Sent to Connected Node"]
    D{"Node Validates Transaction"}
    E["Reject - Do Not Forward"]
    F["Add to Local Mempool"]
    G["Send INV Message to All Peers"]
    H{"Peer Has Transaction Already"}
    I["Peer Sends GETDATA Request"]
    J["Full TX Sent via TX Message"]
    K["Peer Validates Received TX"]
    L["Peer Adds to Mempool"]
    M["Peer Forwards INV to Its Peers"]
    N["Recursive Propagation Across Network"]

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -->|Invalid| E
    D -->|Valid| F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes - Skip| M
    H -->|No| I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K -->|Invalid| E
    K -->|Valid| L
    L --> M
    M --> N

    classDef startStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef processStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B startStyle
    class C,F,G,J,L,M,N processStyle
    class D,H,K decisionStyle
    class E endStyle
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Full Node

This diagram shows the internal architecture of a Bitcoin full node, illustrating how it processes incoming and outgoing data.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input["INBOUND INTERFACE"]
        P1["P2P Network Layer TCP Port 8333"]
        P2["RPC Interface Port 8332"]
    end

    subgraph Validation["VALIDATION ENGINE"]
        V1["Transaction Validator"]

        V4["UTXO Set Manager"]
    end

    subgraph Storage["STORAGE LAYER"]

        S3["Chain State UTXO Set"]

    end

        O3["Wallet Notifications"]

        M2["Proof of Work Solver"]
```
