$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{20160 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Clamping rule** prevents wild swings:

$$\frac{1}{4} \le \frac{T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \le 4$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.**
Suppose last 2016 blocks took 30,240 minutes (50% slower than ideal).

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{30240}{20160} = 1.5 \times T_{\text{old}}$$

Target grows (difficulty drops) because miners were slower → make puzzle easier.

**Step 4 — Halving Schedule.**
The block reward halves every 210,000 blocks:

$$R_k = 50 \cdot 2^{-k} \text{ BTC}$$

| Epoch | Block Range | Reward (BTC) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 – 209,999 | 50 |
| 1 | 210,000 – 419,999 | 25 |
| 2 | 420,000 – 629,999 | 12.5 |
| 3 | 630,000 – 839,999 | 6.25 |
| 4 | 840,000 – 1,049,999 | 3.125 |

**Step 5 — Derivation of Total Supply.**
Using the geometric series identity for the sum across all epochs:

$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-k} = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$

*(Insert Mermaid Figure 3 from Section 4 here for full marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.** *Discuss the economics and security of cryptocurrency mining.*
*(a) Explain the components of miner profitability including revenue, costs, and break-even hash rate* **(7 marks)**
*(b) Describe the 51% attack, its economic deterrent, and the role of mining pools* **(7 marks)**

##### Part (a) — Miner Profitability (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Revenue Function.**
A miner's revenue per block found is:

$$\pi = R_k + \sum_{j=1}^{n_{tx}} f_j$$

where $R_k$ is the current block reward and $f_j$ is the $j$-th transaction fee.

**Step 2 — Cost Function.**
Operational cost per unit time:

$$C = P_e \cdot E_{\text{machine}} + C_{\text{fix}}$$

where $P_e$ is the electricity price (USD/kWh), $E_{\text{machine}}$ is the machine's power draw (kW), and $C_{\text{fix}}$ includes cooling, rent, and depreciation.

**Step 3 — Hash Rate Earned by Miner.**
A miner with hash rate $h_i$ in a network of hash rate $H$ earns expected blocks at rate:

$$\lambda_i = \frac{h_i}{H} \cdot \frac{1}{\bar{t}}$$

where $\bar{t} = 600$ s is the target block interval.

**Step 4 — Expected Daily Revenue.**

$$E[\text{Revenue}_{\text{day}}] = \lambda_i \cdot 86400 \cdot \pi$$

**Step 5 — Break-Even Condition.**
Profit $\Pi = 0$ when:

$$P_e \cdot E_{\text{machine}} \cdot 86400 = \frac{h_i}{H} \cdot 86400 \cdot \frac{\pi}{\bar{t}}$$

Solving for minimum profitable hash rate $h_i^{\min}$:

$$h_i^{\min} = \frac{P_e \cdot E_{\text{machine}} \cdot H \cdot \bar{t}}{\pi}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example.**
Suppose $P_e = \$0.05$/kWh, $E_{\text{machine}} = 3$ kW, $H = 600$ EH/s, $\bar{t} = 600$ s, $\pi = 6.25$ BTC $\approx \$400{,}000$:

$$h_i^{\min} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 3 \cdot 600 \times 10^{18} \cdot 600}{400{,}000} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{14} \text{ H/s} = 135 \text{ TH/s}$$

A miner needs at least ~135 TH/s of dedicated hardware to break even — typically several modern ASIC units.

---

##### Part (b) — 51% Attack & Mining Pools (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of 51% Attack.**
A **51% attack** occurs when a single entity (or colluding cartel) controls more than 50% of the network's total hash rate $H$. The attacker can then:

- Execute **double-spend attacks** by mining a private fork longer than the public chain.
- **Censor** specific transactions by refusing to include them in blocks.
- **Reverse** recently confirmed transactions (up to the depth their private fork can outpace the honest chain).

**Step 2 — Economic Deterrent.**
To mount a sustained 51% attack, the attacker must acquire hardware whose hash rate $\geq H$. Capital expenditure on ASICs at ~\$20/TH means a 600 EH/s attack requires $\sim\$12$ billion. Plus ongoing electricity cost ~\$15 million/hour at full throttle. Thus:

$$C_{\text{attack}} \gg C_{\text{honest mining}}$$

Furthermore, a successful 51% attack **destroys confidence** in the cryptocurrency, crashing its price and rendering the attacker's hardware and stolen coins worthless — making honest mining more profitable by Nash equilibrium.

**Step 3 — Mining Pools — Centralization Concern.**
Individual miners face high variance (a small miner may wait years for a block). **Mining pools** aggregate hash rate and distribute rewards proportionally. Popular pool reward schemes:

| Method | Risk Borne By | Miner's Income |
|---|---|---|
| **PPS** (Pay-Per-Share) | Pool operator | Constant, low variance |
| **PPLNS** (Pay-Per-Last-N-Shares) | Miner | Variable, high variance, lower fee |
| **FPPS** (Full Pay-Per-Share) | Pool operator | Constant + tx fee share |

**Step 4 — Centralization Risk.**
If any single pool exceeds 50% of network hash rate, it can launch a 51% attack — undermining the decentralization premise. Historical near-misses (e.g., GHash.io in 2014) prompted voluntary pool caps.

**Step 5 — Mitigation Strategies.**
- **Pool hopping limits** — Smart pool-hopping detection.
- **Decentralized pools** — P2Pool, ckpool.
- **Stratum v2 protocol** — Miners construct their own block templates, removing pool's transaction selection power.

*(Insert Mermaid Figure 4 from Section 4 here for full marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse *target* and *difficulty*.** Higher difficulty = *smaller* target. Many students write the inverse.
> 2. **Don't write "Bitcoin is unlimited supply."** It is *capped at 21 million* via the geometric halving sum.
> 3. **Don't omit the clamp** $[1/4, 4]$ in the difficulty adjustment formula. Marks are deducted if you show only the raw ratio.
> 4. **Don't call SHA-256 "encryption."** It is a *hash function*, not encryption. Encryption is reversible; hashing is not.
> 5. **Don't confuse block *header* hash with block hash.** The header is 80 bytes; the full block (with transactions) is hashed *indirectly* via the Merkle root inside the header.
> 6. **In derivation questions, always state the geometric series identity** $\sum r^k = 1/(1-r)$ explicitly — examiners reward showing the identity used.

---

### 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 PoW Mining**

- ✅ **PoW = Brute-force hash search.** No shortcut exists; the only strategy is trial and error.
- ✅ **Block Header = 80 bytes** = Version + PrevHash + MerkleRoot + Timestamp + nBits + Nonce.
- ✅ **Hash Puzzle:** $\text{HASH256}(\text{Header}) < T$. Valid block found when output is below target.
- ✅ **Difficulty** $D$ is inversely proportional to target $T$. Higher $D$ ⇒ smaller $T$ ⇒ puzzle harder.
- ✅ **Adjustment Period:** Every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks).
- ✅ **Adjustment Formula:** $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times (t_{\text{actual}}/20160)$, clamped to $[1/4, 4]$.
- ✅ **Average Block Time = 10 minutes**, self-stabilized by the adjustment.
- ✅ **Block Reward Halves Every 210,000 blocks** (~4 years). $R_k = 50 \cdot 2^{-k}$ BTC.
- ✅ **Total Supply = 21,000,000 BTC** — derived from geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(1/2)^k = 2$.
- ✅ **Mining Hardware Evolution:** CPU → GPU → FPGA → ASIC (6+ orders of magnitude efficiency gain).
- ✅ **Mining Pools** reduce variance but introduce centralization risk; **51% attack** is the canonical threat model.
- ✅ **ASIC resistance** is a design goal of altcoins like Ethereum (pre-Merge), Monero (RandomX).
- ✅ **51% Attack Cost** scales with $H$ — economically infeasible on large networks like Bitcoin.
- ✅ **Selfish Mining** is a strategic deviation where rational miners withhold blocks to gain disproportionate revenue.
- ✅ **Verification is O(1)** (single hash check), but mining is O($2^{32}/D$) per solution on average.
- ✅ **Energy consumption** of PoW is ~150 TWh/year for Bitcoin — comparable to a mid-sized country.
- ✅ **Future-proofing:** Post-merge Ethereum uses **Proof of Stake** instead — studied in Module 5.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Voce Quick-Fire Facts:**
> - "What is extraNonce?" → Field in coinbase tx that miners modify to refresh the nonce space.
> - "Why double SHA-256?" → Defeats length-extension attacks.
> - "Who created the difficulty formula?" → Satoshi Nakamoto, embedded in Bitcoin Core v0.1.0.
> - "Can SHA-256 be replaced?" → Yes, via soft fork (e.g., to SHA-3), but coordination cost is enormous.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard and Soft Forks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hard and Soft Forks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fork in Blockchain?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **fork** in a blockchain is a divergence in the distributed ledger that occurs when the network's participants fail to reach consensus on a single, canonical history of transactions. Forks manifest when nodes operate under differing protocol rules, resulting in two (or more) potentially valid chains.

In simple terms, a **fork is a software upgrade (or disagreement)** applied to a blockchain protocol. Because a blockchain is a decentralized system with no central administrator, any change to the protocol rules must be voluntarily adopted by the network's nodes (miners, full nodes, validators). When some nodes adopt new rules and others do not, the chain "splits" — this is a fork.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** A fork is fundamentally about **rule changes** in a decentralized consensus system. The *type* of rule change determines whether the fork is "hard" or "soft."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Road Rules

Imagine a country where every driver (node) follows the same traffic rule book (protocol). 

- One day, the government passes a new law: **"All cars must now drive on the right side of the road instead of the left."** Some drivers update their rule book and start driving on the right. Others keep the old rule book. **Accidents (consensus failure) occur, and traffic literally splits into two incompatible systems.**
  - This is a **Hard Fork** — a *backward-incompatible* rule change.

- Now imagine a different law: **"All trucks must now have a rear reflector (a new optional feature)."** Regular cars don't need to change. Trucks with the new reflector can still drive safely among cars without one. **No split, no accidents, just an upgrade.**
  - This is a **Soft Fork** — a *backward-compatible* tightening of rules.

---

## 1.3 The Two Principal Categories of Forks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Hard Fork** — A **radical, backward-incompatible** change to the protocol. Nodes that do not upgrade are permanently separated from the upgraded chain. May result in **two distinct cryptocurrencies**.

> **2. Soft Fork** — A **backward-compatible** change to the protocol. Old nodes continue to accept new blocks (because the new rules are a *restriction* of the old rules). No permanent chain split occurs (under healthy majority conditions).

### 1.3.1 Quick Comparison Table (Concept Anchor)

| Property | Hard Fork | Soft Fork |
|---|---|---|
| Backward Compatibility | ❌ Not compatible | ✅ Compatible |
| Mandatory Upgrade | **All** nodes must upgrade | Only majority miners/validators |
| Old Nodes on New Chain | Cannot validate (see blocks as invalid) | Still see blocks as valid |
| Chain Split | Usually permanent (two coins) | Temporary or none |
| Typical Use | Major feature additions, philosophical splits | Tightening rules, optimizations (e.g., SegWit) |
| Consensus Risk | High (permanent split) | Low (soft transition) |

---

## 1.4 Why Do Forks Occur? (Motivations)

1. **Technical Upgrades:** Adding new features, improving scalability, fixing bugs.
2. **Philosophical Disagreements:** The community splits on the *vision* (e.g., block size debate).
3. **Response to Security Incidents:** The DAO hack (2016) → Ethereum/Ethereum Classic hard fork.
4. **Protocol Optimization:** New transaction formats, signature schemes (e.g., SegWit soft fork).
5. **Reversal of Transactions:** Controversial rollback of malicious activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Forks are the **mechanism by which decentralized systems evolve** without central authority. They are simultaneously a *feature* (governance flexibility) and a *risk* (community fragmentation).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hard Fork — Detailed Technical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Definition (Rigorous)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Fork:** A protocol change whereby blocks previously considered *invalid* under the old consensus rules become *valid* under the new rules (or vice-versa). It is a **non-backward-compatible** change requiring **all** participating nodes to upgrade to remain on the consensus chain.

### 2.1.2 Operational Mechanics (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Proposal:** Developers write a new client implementation with modified consensus rules.
- **Step 2 — Adoption:** A subset of nodes, miners, and users install the new software.
- **Step 3 — Activation Block:** At a pre-defined block height, the new rules activate. Miners on the *new* chain begin producing blocks valid only under the new rules.
- **Step 4 — Divergence:** Nodes still on the *old* chain reject these blocks (they violate old rules). The chain splits into two independent ledgers sharing history up to the fork block.
- **Step 5 — Independent Operation:** Both chains continue producing blocks, each with its own token economy (if the fork is contentious).

### 2.1.3 The "Why" Behind the Split

The split occurs because of the **asymmetry in validity**:

$$\text{Block}_{\text{new}} \in \text{Valid}_{\text{new}} \quad \text{but} \quad \text{Block}_{\text{new}} \notin \text{Valid}_{\text{old}}$$

A new-rule block is **invalid** to old-rule nodes. The old chain continues with old rules; the new chain enforces new rules. There is no way for old nodes to "catch up" without upgrading.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Planned Hard Forks:** Coordinated upgrades (e.g., Ethereum's *Byzantium*, *Constantinople*, *London* upgrades — technically "upgrades" that did not split the chain because of overwhelming community agreement).
- **Contentious Hard Forks:** Splits like **Bitcoin Cash (BCH)** from Bitcoin (2017) over block size; **Ethereum Classic (ETC)** from Ethereum (2016) over The DAO reversal.
- **Emergency Hard Forks:** Bug fixes (e.g., Bitcoin's overflow bug fix in 2010).

---

## 2.2 Soft Fork — Detailed Technical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Definition (Rigorous)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Fork:** A protocol change whereby blocks previously considered *valid* under the old consensus rules become *invalid* under the new rules. It is a **backward-compatible** tightening of the protocol. Old nodes still see new blocks as valid (because the new rules are a *subset* of the old rules).

### 2.2.2 The Crucial Mathematical Property

$$\text{Valid}_{\text{new}} \subset \text{Valid}_{\text{old}}$$

The set of valid blocks under new rules is a **strict subset** of the valid blocks under old rules. Therefore, **any block a new node accepts is automatically accepted by an old node.** This is the source of backward compatibility.

### 2.2.3 Operational Mechanics (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Proposal:** New rules are drafted (e.g., a new transaction structure).
- **Step 2 — Signaling:** Miners signal readiness by including specific data in blocks they mine (e.g., the `BIP-9` version bits).
- **Step 3 — Activation Threshold:** When a **super-majority (typically 95% or 151/201 blocks in a difficulty period)** of hashing power signals support, the new rules activate.
- **Step 4 — Enforcement:** Upgraded miners begin enforcing the new rules. Non-upgraded miners may still produce valid blocks *as long as* those blocks also satisfy the new (tighter) rules.
- **Step 5 — Gradual Obsolescence:** Non-upgraded miners eventually fall behind as they may produce blocks the network rejects.

### 2.2.4 Hash Power Requirements — The Critical Threshold

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** For a soft fork to be safe, the **upgraded miners must control a super-majority of hash power.** If the majority is *not* upgraded, they could produce blocks invalid under the new rules but valid to old nodes, causing a de-facto chain split.

The standard activation threshold:

$$H_{\text{required}} \geq 0.95 \times H_{\text{total}}$$

where $H_{\text{required}}$ is the hash power of upgraded miners and $H_{\text{total}}$ is the total network hash power.

### 2.2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Segregated Witness (SegWit, 2017):** Restructured transaction data to effectively increase Bitcoin's block capacity without changing the 1 MB block size limit.
- **Pay-to-Script-Hash (P2SH, BIP-16):** Required new-style addresses to be used in a specific way.
- **BIP-65 (CheckLockTimeVerify):** Added a new opcode by repurposing an existing, previously unused opcode (NOP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Soft forks are *preferred* by conservative blockchain communities because they preserve unity. Hard forks are *necessary* when the change is too fundamental to fit within the old rule set (e.g., increasing block size itself).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Block Validity (Hard Fork) | $\text{Block}_{\text{new}} \in V_{\text{new}} \setminus V_{\text{old}}$ | New-rule blocks invalid to old nodes |
| Block Validity (Soft Fork) | $V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$ | New rules are a subset of old rules |
| Soft Fork Hash Threshold | $H_{\text{upgrade}} \geq \theta \cdot H_{\text{total}}, \quad \theta \approx 0.95$ | Super-majority required |
| Signaling Threshold (BIP-9) | $S_{\text{blocks}} \geq \frac{1916}{2016}$ | 95% of blocks in difficulty period |
| Chain Reorganization Safety | $D_{\text{conf}} \geq 6$ blocks | Standard confirmation depth for finality |
| Fork Block Height | $h_{\text{fork}} \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Specific block number where rules change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Notation:** $H$ = hash power (hashes/second), $V$ = set of valid blocks, $\theta$ = threshold fraction, $D_{\text{conf}}$ = confirmation depth, $h$ = block height (integer).

---

## 2.4 Comparative Deep Dive: Why Two Coins Emerge from a Hard Fork

When a hard fork activates at block $h_f$:

1. Both chains share all blocks from genesis to $h_f - 1$.
2. At block $h_f$, miners on the *new* chain apply new rules; miners on the *old* chain apply old rules.
3. The UTXO set (or account state) is duplicated — every user holding the original coin now holds an equivalent balance on **both** chains.
4. Exchanges *list* the new coin; users can trade the split token independently.

This is why Bitcoin holders received an equivalent amount of **Bitcoin Cash (BCH)** at the August 2017 fork block.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Validity Set Relationship for Forks

Let us derive the formal conditions that distinguish a hard fork from a soft fork using set theory.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $\Sigma$ = the set of *all possible* blocks constructible under any rules.
- $V_{\text{old}} \subset \Sigma$ = the set of blocks considered valid by the **old** protocol.
- $V_{\text{new}} \subset \Sigma$ = the set of blocks considered valid by the **new** protocol.

A protocol upgrade defines a transition from $V_{\text{old}}$ to $V_{\text{new}}$.

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Soft Fork Condition

**Claim:** A soft fork occurs when $V_{\text{new}} \subseteq V_{\text{old}}$.

**Proof:**

We require that any block accepted by an upgraded node is *also* accepted by a non-upgraded node. A non-upgraded node validates using $V_{\text{old}}$. An upgraded node validates using $V_{\text{new}}$.

For backward compatibility:

$$\forall b \in \Sigma: \quad b \in V_{\text{new}} \implies b \in V_{\text{old}}$$

By the definition of subset, this is equivalent to:

$$V_{\text{new}} \subseteq V_{\text{old}}$$

In practice, the soft fork is *strict* ($V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$), meaning some blocks valid in the old system are no longer valid (the "tightening").

$$\therefore \text{Soft Fork} \iff V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$$

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Hard Fork Condition

**Claim:** A hard fork occurs when $V_{\text{new}} \not\subseteq V_{\text{old}}$.

**Proof:**

By contrapositive of the soft fork condition, if backward compatibility fails, then:

$$\exists b \in V_{\text{new}} : b \notin V_{\text{old}}$$

There exists at least one new-rule block that an old node would reject. The chains diverge because old nodes cannot follow the new chain.

$$\therefore \text{Hard Fork} \iff V_{\text{new}} \not\subseteq V_{\text{old}}$$

A special case is the *expansive* hard fork where:

$$V_{\text{old}} \subset V_{\text{new}}$$

The new rules *loosen* the old constraints, allowing blocks previously invalid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Soft Fork Activation Threshold

Let us derive why a super-majority is required for safe soft fork activation.

### 3.2.1 Model

- Total network hash power: $H_{\text{total}}$.
- Hash power of upgraded miners: $H_u$.
- Hash power of non-upgraded miners: $H_{nu} = H_{\text{total}} - H_u$.
- Fraction upgraded: $\alpha = H_u / H_{\text{total}}$.

### 3.2.2 Probability of an Old-Rule Block Winning the Race

In a Proof-of-Work system, a miner's probability of finding the next block is proportional to their hash power. If a non-upgraded miner finds a block that *violates* the new rules (but is valid under old rules), the upgraded network will reject it. The probability of this occurring in any single block race is:

$$P_{\text{old block}} = \frac{H_{nu}}{H_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \alpha$$

### 3.2.3 Probability of n Consecutive Old-Rule Blocks

For an old-rule miner to reverse $n$ new-rule blocks (creating a de-facto split), all $n$ blocks in a row must be old-rule. Assuming independence:

$$P_{\text{split}}(n) = (1 - \alpha)^n$$

### 3.2.4 Safety Condition

For the soft fork to be considered safe, the split probability over a long horizon must be negligible. For $n$ confirmation blocks:

$$P_{\text{split}}(n) = (1 - \alpha)^n \ll 1$$

For $\alpha = 0.95$ and $n = 6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{split}}(6) &= (1 - 0.95)^6 \\
&= (0.05)^6 \\
&= 1.5625 \times 10^{-8}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is approximately **1 in 64 million** — a practically negligible probability. This justifies the BIP-9 threshold of 95%.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Hard vs. Soft Fork

| Scenario | Recommended Fork Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Increase block size from 1 MB to 8 MB | **Hard Fork** | Loosens old rules ($V_{\text{old}} \subset V_{\text{new}}$) |
| Add a new opcode (repurpose NOP) | **Soft Fork** | Tightens rules; old nodes ignore unknown opcode |
| Add new signature scheme (e.g., Schnorr) | **Soft Fork** | Implemented via SegWit-style witness data |
| Reverse a major hack (e.g., The DAO) | **Hard Fork** | Requires rewriting history or state |
| Add new transaction format | **Soft Fork** | Backward-compatible with new witness data |
| Split due to philosophical dispute | **Hard Fork** | Communities cannot agree; permanent split is the resolution |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: The Hard Fork Chain Split

The following diagram illustrates how a hard fork creates two independent chains sharing history up to the fork block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Genesis Block"] --> A1["Block 1"]
    A1 --> A2["Block 2"]
    A2 --> A3["Block N-1"]
    A3 --> AF["FORK BLOCK H_f - Shared History Ends"]
    AF --> A4["OLD CHAIN - Block N (Old Rules)"]
    AF --> B4["NEW CHAIN - Block N (New Rules)"]
    A4 --> A5["OLD CHAIN - Block N+1"]
    A4 --> A6["OLD CHAIN - Block N+2"]
    B4 --> B5["NEW CHAIN - Block N+1"]
    B4 --> B6["NEW CHAIN - Block N+2"]
    B5 --> B7["NEW CHAIN - Block N+3"]
    B6 --> B8["NEW CHAIN - Block N+4"]
    A5 --> A7["OLD CHAIN - Block N+3"]
    A7 --> A8["OLD CHAIN - Block N+4"]

    classDef oldChain fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#D9534F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef newChain fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#0275D8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef forkBlock fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#F0AD4E,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class A0,A1,A2,A3 oldChain
    class AF forkBlock
    class A4,A5,A6,A7,A8 oldChain
    class B4,B5,B6,B7,B8 newChain
```

**Interpretation:** The red branch represents the *original* chain continuing under old protocol rules. The blue branch represents the *new* chain following the upgraded protocol. The yellow node is the **fork block** — the last shared block. After this point, the two chains are permanently independent.

---

## 4.2 Diagram: The Soft Fork — Backward-Compatible Upgrade

The following diagram shows a soft fork activation in a single chain with no permanent split.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Phase 1: Proposal & Signaling"] --> P2["Phase 2: Super-Majority Signaling (95% of hash power)"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase 3: LOCKED IN - Rules Will Activate"]
    P3 --> P4["Phase 4: ACTIVE - New Rules Enforced"]
    P4 --> P5["Phase 5: Old Nodes Still Accept All Blocks"]
    P5 --> P6["Phase 6: Gradual Migration of Remaining Nodes"]

    N1["Old Node (Legacy)"] -.Validates.-> P5
    N2["New Node (Upgraded)"] --> P4
    N3["Miner (Upgraded)"] --> P3
    N4["Miner (Non-upgraded)"] -.May produce blocks but must follow new rules.-> P4

    classDef phaseStyle fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#E2E3E5,stroke:#6C757D,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef minerStyle fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#FFC107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 phaseStyle
    class N1,N2 nodeStyle
    class N3,N4 minerStyle
```

**Interpretation:** The green flow shows the six lifecycle phases of a soft fork. Crucially, old nodes (grey) continue to validate the chain because new-rule blocks are still valid under old rules. Non-upgraded miners (yellow) must follow the new rules or produce blocks the network rejects.

---

## 4.3 Diagram: Decision Matrix — Hard vs. Soft Fork

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the change\nbackward-compatible\nwith old rules?"} -->|Yes| Q2{"Does the change\ntighten or\nloosen rules?"}
    Q1 -->|No| HARD["HARD FORK\nRequired\nPermanent chain split possible\nTwo coins may emerge"]

    Q2 -->|Tighten\nV_new subset V_old| SOFT["SOFT FORK\nBackward compatible\nRequires 95% hash power majority\nNo chain split if majority upgraded"]
    Q2 -->|Loosen\nV_new superset V_old| HARD

    SOFT --> EX1["Example: SegWit (Bitcoin 2017)\nP2SH (Bitcoin 2012)"]
    HARD --> EX2["Example: Bitcoin Cash (2017)\nEthereum Classic (2016)\nBlock size increase"]

    classDef decision fill:#F8F9FA,stroke:#343A40,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hardStyle fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#DC3545,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef softStyle fill:#D1ECF1,stroke:#17A2B8,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef exampleStyle fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#FFC107,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class Q1,Q2 decision
    class HARD,EX2 hardStyle
    class SOFT,EX1 softStyle
```

**Interpretation:** A simple decision tree. If the change makes *new things valid* (or removes existing restrictions), it is a hard fork. If the change *restricts* what is valid (but old validators don't notice), it is a soft fork.

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Replay Attack Risk in Hard Forks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Security Note:** When a hard fork occurs, transactions on one chain may be *valid* on the other chain because they share the same cryptographic format. An attacker can "replay" a transaction from chain A onto chain B, causing unintended fund movement.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant CA as Chain A (Original)
    participant CB as Chain B (Forked)

    Note over U,CB: After Hard Fork - No Replay Protection
    U->>CA: Send 10 BTC to merchant (TxA)
    Note over CA: TxA broadcast on Chain A
    CA-->>CB: Attacker copies TxA
    CB->>CB: TxA is also valid format on Chain B
    CB-->>U: TxA confirmed on Chain B
    Note over U: User unintentionally sent 10 BCH to merchant!

    Note over U,CB: After Hard Fork - WITH Replay Protection
    U->>CA: Send 10 BTC (TxA with chain-specific data)
    Note over CA: TxA accepted on Chain A
    Note over CB: TxA rejected on Chain B\n(different chain ID)
```

**Interpretation:** The top sequence shows a *vulnerable* hard fork where a transaction on one chain is replayable on the other. The bottom shows the solution: include **chain-specific data** (e.g., a chain ID in the signature hash) to make transactions non-replayable. Ethereum's EIP-155 implemented this protection.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a hard fork and a soft fork in a blockchain network. Provide one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Fork | Soft Fork |
|---|---|---|
| Backward Compatibility | Not backward-compatible | Backward-compatible |
| Rule Change | Loosens or replaces old rules | Tightens old rules |
| Chain Split | Usually permanent | Temporary or none |
| Node Upgrade | **All** nodes must upgrade | Only majority hash power required |
| Real-World Example | **Bitcoin Cash (BCH)** split from Bitcoin in 2017 (block size dispute) | **Segregated Witness (SegWit)** activated on Bitcoin in 2017 |

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**What is a soft fork? Why is a super-majority (95%) of mining hash power required for a soft fork to be safely activated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **soft fork** is a backward-compatible protocol change where the new rules are a strict subset of the old rules ($V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$), allowing old nodes to continue validating the chain.

A super-majority of **95% hash power** is required because:

1. If non-upgraded miners control more than ~5% of hash power, they can produce blocks violating the new rules but valid to old nodes.
2. This creates a de-facto chain split even though the protocol intended no split.
3. The probability of $n$ consecutive old-rule blocks (split) is $(1 - \alpha)^n$. For $\alpha = 0.95$ and $n = 6$, this is $(0.05)^6 \approx 1.56 \times 10^{-8}$ — negligible.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the threshold justification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a hard fork in blockchain with a suitable diagram. Discuss the technical reasons that necessitate a hard fork and explain the phenomenon of chain split.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the Bitcoin Cash (BCH) hard fork of 2017 in detail. What was the underlying technical dispute? Explain the replay attack risk and how it was mitigated.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition [1 mark]**
A **hard fork** is a non-backward-compatible change to a blockchain's protocol rules. Blocks valid under the new rules are invalid under the old rules, causing a permanent divergence in the chain.

**Step 2: Formal Validity Condition [1 mark]**
Mathematically: $V_{\text{new}} \not\subseteq V_{\text{old}}$, meaning there exists at least one block valid in the new protocol but invalid in the old.

**Step 3: Diagram [2 marks]**

```
[Genesis] → [B1] → [B2] → ... → [B_{H-1}] → [FORK BLOCK H_f]
                                            ├── Old Chain (Old Rules)
                                            │     → [H+1_old] → [H+2_old] → ...
                                            └── New Chain (New Rules)
                                                  → [H+1_new] → [H+2_new] → ...
```

**Step 4: Reasons Necessitating a Hard Fork [2 marks]**
1. **Loosening of rules:** When the change adds new block types or transaction formats that old rules cannot validate (e.g., increasing block size).
2. **Fundamental protocol redesign:** Major cryptographic changes (e.g., changing the signature algorithm).
3. **Dispute resolution:** When the community cannot reach consensus on the protocol's future direction.
4. **Emergency correction:** Fixing critical bugs that require modifying consensus-critical logic.

**Step 5: Chain Split Phenomenon [1 mark]**
At the activation block $H_f$, miners on the new chain apply new rules while miners on the old chain apply old rules. Both chains continue independently, sharing history up to $H_f - 1$. A new cryptocurrency emerges on the new chain; the old chain retains the original asset.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Background [1 mark]**
The Bitcoin scalability debate centered on how to increase transaction throughput. Bitcoin's 1 MB block size limit restricted transactions to ~7 per second.

**Step 2: The Dispute [2 marks]**
- **Big-block camp:** Argued for an immediate increase in block size (e.g., 8 MB) to scale on-chain.
- **Small-block camp:** Advocated for off-chain solutions like the Lightning Network and SegWit (a soft fork).

**Step 3: The Fork [1 mark]**
On **August 1, 2017**, at block height **478,559**, Bitcoin Cash (BCH) hard-forked from Bitcoin. BCH adopted 8 MB blocks (later increased to 32 MB) and removed SegWit.

**Step 4: Replay Attack Risk [2 marks]**
Initially, transactions on Bitcoin (BTC) were also valid on BCH because both shared the same format. An attacker could copy a BTC transaction and broadcast it on BCH, causing unintended spending of BCH.

**Step 5: Mitigation [1 mark]**
- **BCH** introduced a new sighash algorithm (`SIGHASH_FORKID`) via replay protection.
- **BTC**'s SegWit (activated later) also included chain-specific witness data, reducing cross-chain replayability.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a soft fork. Using the set-theoretic relationship $V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$, prove that a soft fork is backward-compatible. Illustrate the BIP-9 activation mechanism with its phases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Segregated Witness (SegWit) soft fork on Bitcoin with respect to: (i) the problem it solved, (ii) why it was implemented as a soft fork, and (iii) the mechanism by which backward compatibility was achieved.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition [1 mark]**
A **soft fork** is a backward-compatible protocol change where new rules are a strict subset of old rules, allowing non-upgraded nodes to continue validating the chain.

**Step 2: Backward-Compatibility Proof [2 marks]**

*Claim:* A soft fork is backward-compatible.
*Proof:* Let $V_{\text{old}}$ and $V_{\text{new}}$ be the validity sets. By definition, a soft fork satisfies:

$$V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$$

This means every block $b \in V_{\text{new}}$ is also in $V_{\text{old}}$. Therefore, an old node validating any block in $V_{\text{new}}$ will accept it (since it is in $V_{\text{old}}$). The protocol remains consistent for non-upgraded nodes. $\blacksquare$

**Step 3: BIP-9 Activation Phases [3 marks]**

BIP-9 (Version bits) defines a signaling-based activation:

| Phase | Description | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| **DEFINED** | Parameters defined in the spec | Until start |
| **STARTED** | Miners signal support via block version bits | ~1 difficulty period (2016 blocks) |
| **LOCKED_IN** | Threshold met (≥ 1916/2016 = 95%); activation confirmed | One period |
| **ACTIVE** | New rules enforced; non-upgraded miners must comply | Permanent |
| **FAILED** | Threshold not met; requires redeployment | If not met in 1 year |

**Step 4: Threshold Justification [1 mark]**
The 95% threshold ensures that the probability of a de-facto split is $(0.05)^6 \approx 1.56 \times 10^{-8}$ over 6 confirmation blocks — practically zero.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: The Problem — Transaction Malleability and Capacity [2 marks]**
- **Malleability:** Transaction IDs (`txid`) could be altered by modifying signature data without changing transaction semantics, breaking unconfirmed transaction chains.
- **Capacity:** The 1 MB block size limit constrained throughput.

**Step 2: Why SegWit Was a Soft Fork [2 marks]**
SegWit moves the *witness data* (signatures) out of the main block into a separate *witness structure*. Old nodes still see the transaction as valid because:
- The block is still ≤ 1 MB of "non-witness" data.
- The signatures (moved to the witness) are stripped or ignored by old nodes but enforced by new nodes.
- The transaction format change is a *tightening* of the rules: $V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$.

**Step 3: Backward Compatibility Mechanism [3 marks]**
1. **Witness commitment:** Miners commit to the witness data via a `Merkle root` in the coinbase transaction. Old nodes ignore this commitment.
2. **Block size accounting:** The block weight is calculated as:
$$W = B_{\text{base}} \cdot 3 + B_{\text{witness}} \cdot 1 \leq 4{,}000{,}000$$
where $B_{\text{base}}$ is the non-witness byte count and $B_{\text{witness}}$ is the witness byte count. Old nodes only count $B_{\text{base}}$ (≤ 1 MB), while new nodes enforce the weight limit.
3. **Script versioning:** SegWit introduced `witness programs` with version bytes. Old nodes see an "anyone-can-spend" output and accept it; new nodes enforce the witness conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Confusing validity sets:** Students often write $V_{\text{old}} \subset V_{\text{new}}$ for a soft fork. The correct direction is $V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$. This error costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 95% threshold:** When asked about soft fork safety, students often describe it as "minority-friendly" without quantifying the **95% hash power** requirement.
> 3. **Mixing up replay protection vs. hard fork:** Not all hard forks have replay attacks; SegWit was a soft fork and thus had different security considerations.
> 4. **Ignoring the activation mechanism:** Naming BIP-9 phases and thresholds (1916/2016) is essential for full marks in Part B.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Hard and Soft Forks**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [ ] A **fork** = divergence in blockchain protocol rules due to upgrade or disagreement.
- [ ] **Hard Fork:** $V_{\text{new}} \not\subseteq V_{\text{old}}$. **Not backward-compatible.** May cause permanent chain split and create a new cryptocurrency.
- [ ] **Soft Fork:** $V_{\text{new}} \subset V_{\text{old}}$. **Backward-compatible.** Old nodes still validate new blocks. No split (under 95% majority).

### 🔑 Critical Formulas & Thresholds
- [ ] Soft fork hash power threshold: $\alpha \geq 0.95$ (i.e., 95%).
- [ ] BIP-9 signaling: $\geq 1916$ out of $2016$ blocks per difficulty period.
- [ ] Split probability over $n$ blocks: $P_{\text{split}}(n) = (1-\alpha)^n$.
- [ ] Confirmation depth: $D_{\text{conf}} \geq 6$ blocks for practical finality.
- [ ] SegWit block weight: $W = 3 \cdot B_{\text{base}} + 1 \cdot B_{\text{witness}} \leq 4{,}000{,}000$.

### 🔑 Real-World Examples (Memorize These)
- [ ] **Hard Fork:** Bitcoin Cash (Aug 2017), Ethereum Classic (July 2016), Monero (multiple).
- [ ] **Soft Fork:** SegWit (Aug 2017), P2SH (2012), BIP-65 CLTV (2014).

### 🔑 Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] ❌ Writing $V_{\text{old}} \subset V_{\text{new}}$ for a soft fork — **wrong direction**.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting that soft forks still need **super-majority** hash power to be safe.
- [ ] ❌ Saying hard forks are always "bad" — planned hard forks are routine upgrades (e.g., Ethereum's Constantinople).
- [ ] ❌ Confusing "anyone-can-spend" outputs with security holes — they are secure *only because* new nodes enforce the witness.

### 🔑 KTU Board Frequently Asked Patterns
- [ ] "Differentiate hard and soft fork" → Use the **validity set inclusion** relationship.
- [ ] "Why 95% threshold?" → Derive the split probability using $(1 - 0.95)^6$.
- [ ] "Why was SegWit a soft fork?" → Witness data separation + script versioning + block weight redefinition.
- [ ] "Explain chain split" → Draw the divergent diagram with shared history up to $H_f$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracking Bitcoins - Unspent Transaction Outputs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tracking Bitcoins - Unspent Transaction Outputs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO)** is the fundamental data structure used by the Bitcoin protocol to track the ownership and transfer of bitcoin. A UTXO represents a discrete, indivisible chunk of bitcoin that has been **authorized** for spending by a specific owner (via digital signature) but has **not yet been consumed** as an input to a new transaction. The sum of all UTXOs across the entire network at any given moment constitutes the **UTXO Set** — a snapshot of *all spendable bitcoin* at that point in time.

In the Bitcoin ledger, there are **no accounts** and **no balances** in the traditional banking sense. The blockchain does not store "Alice has 5 BTC." Instead, it stores a global set of UTXOs, and a user's "balance" is calculated dynamically as the sum of all UTXOs that the user's private keys can unlock.

$$ \text{User Balance} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{UTXO}_i \quad \text{where each UTXO}_i \text{ is spendable by the user} $$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Cash Coins" Model

Imagine walking into a store with **physical cash** in your wallet. Your wallet does not contain a single "balance card." Instead, it contains a collection of **individual coins and notes** of various denominations — say, one ₹500 note, two ₹100 notes, and three ₹10 coins. To pay a bill of ₹230, you cannot "split" the ₹500 note. You must hand over the ₹500 note and receive ₹270 back as change.

Bitcoin's UTXO model works **identically**:

- **UTXOs = Physical coins/notes in your wallet**
- **Transactions = Handing over a combination of coins to receive change**
- **Change = A new UTXO returned to you (or to a fresh address you control)**

If Alice wants to send Bob **0.8 BTC** but her wallet only contains a single UTXO of **1.0 BTC**, she must:
1. Consume the entire **1.0 BTC** UTXO as her transaction input.
2. Create an output of **0.8 BTC** to Bob's address.
3. Create a *change* output of **(1.0 − 0.8 − fee)** BTC back to an address she controls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The UTXO model is the *single most important* concept in understanding how Bitcoin tracks value. Every full node on the Bitcoin network maintains the complete UTXO set in memory (RAM) for fast validation. As of 2024, the Bitcoin UTXO set contains **tens of millions of entries** and is critical for **full verification** of new transactions.

## 1.3 Why UTXO Matters — The Engineering Rationale

| Property | Benefit |
|----------|---------|
| **Indivisibility** | Each UTXO is spent *wholly or not at all* — no partial spending, which simplifies double-spend prevention. |
| **Parallel Validation** | Since UTXOs reference previous outputs by hash and index, multiple transactions can be validated concurrently. |
| **Privacy via Fresh Addresses** | Every transaction can create a new change address, making address-based tracking harder. |
| **Stateless Verification** | A node only needs the UTXO set to verify any transaction — no historical scanning required. |
| **Double-Spend Immunity** | Once a UTXO is consumed, it is cryptographically impossible to spend it again (rejected by every node). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Bitcoin Transaction

A Bitcoin transaction is a structured data object containing:

- **Version Number** — Protocol version (currently 1 or 2 for SegWit).
- **Inputs** (one or more) — References to previously unspent outputs.
- **Outputs** (one or more) — New UTXOs being created.
- **Locktime** — Earliest block height/time at which the transaction becomes valid.

### Transaction Input Structure

$$ \text{Input} = \{ \text{prev\_txid}, \text{output\_index}, \text{scriptSig}, \text{sequence} \} $$

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `prev_txid` | Hash (TXID) of the transaction that created the UTXO being spent. |
| `output_index` | The specific output's position (vout) within that previous transaction (starting at 0). |
| `scriptSig` | Unlocking script containing the owner's signature and public key. |
| `sequence` | Used for Replace-By-Fee (RBF) signaling; default = `0xFFFFFFFF`. |

### Transaction Output Structure

$$ \text{Output} = \{ \text{value}, \text{scriptPubKey} \} $$

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `value` | Amount in **satoshis** (1 BTC = 100,000,000 satoshis). |
| `scriptPubKey` | Locking script specifying the conditions to spend this output (typically: owner provides valid signature + public key). |

## 2.2 The UTXO Lifecycle (Operational Steps)

The life of a UTXO moves through a strict, deterministic pipeline:

1. **Creation (Minting):** A new UTXO is born as an output of a transaction. In coinbase transactions, this represents newly mined bitcoin. In regular transactions, this represents transferred value.
2. **Broadcast & Confirmation:** The transaction is propagated across the network, validated by nodes, and included in a block. Once confirmed, the UTXO enters the **UTXO Set** maintained by all full nodes.
3. **Spendability:** The UTXO remains in the UTXO set until it is referenced as an input in a subsequent transaction. Only the holder of the correct private key (matching the `scriptPubKey`) can create a valid `scriptSig` to unlock it.
4. **Consumption:** When the UTXO is referenced as an input, it is **removed from the UTXO set** and can never be used again. The consuming transaction creates *new* UTXOs in its place.
5. **Extinction:** The consumed UTXO no longer exists anywhere in the active UTXO set. It only persists in the historical blockchain for archival purposes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students often confuse *consumed* outputs with *destroyed* bitcoin. Bitcoin is **never destroyed** except as transaction fees. All consumed UTXOs are merely reallocated into new UTXOs (minus the miner's fee).

## 2.3 The Conservation Law of Bitcoin

The total supply of bitcoin is conserved across every transaction. This is expressed by the **UTXO Conservation Equation**:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{m} \text{Input}_i^{\text{value}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Output}_j^{\text{value}} + \text{TxFee} $$

Where:
- $m$ = number of inputs being consumed
- $n$ = number of outputs being created
- $\text{TxFee}$ = the difference, collected by the miner who includes the transaction in a block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Invariant:** If the left side of the equation is *less than* the right side, the transaction is **invalid** and will be rejected by every honest node on the network. This conservation law is the mathematical foundation that prevents inflation bugs in Bitcoin.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Bitcoin smallest unit | $1\ \text{BTC} = 10^8\ \text{satoshis}$ | Satoshis are the on-chain integer unit |
| 2 | User balance | $\text{Balance} = \sum \text{UTXO}_i^{\text{value}}$ | Sum of all spendable UTXOs |
| 3 | Transaction conservation | $\sum I_{\text{value}} = \sum O_{\text{value}} + \text{Fee}$ | Mandatory for validity |
| 4 | Transaction fee | $\text{Fee} = \sum I_{\text{value}} - \sum O_{\text{value}}$ | Goes to miner, not an output |
| 5 | Change output | $\text{Change} = \sum I_{\text{value}} - \text{Sent} - \text{Fee}$ | Returned to sender's new address |
| 6 | Max BTC supply | $21{,}000{,}000\ \text{BTC}$ | Enforced by coinbase + halving schedule |
| 7 | Coinbase reward (post-2024 halving) | $3.125\ \text{BTC}$ | As of 2024-2028 epoch |
| 8 | Block reward halving | Every $210{,}000$ blocks ($\approx 4$ years) | Geometric series converges to 21M |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The UTXO model is not merely an academic curiosity — it powers **multi-billion-dollar** financial infrastructure. Key engineering applications include:

- **Lightning Network (Layer-2):** Uses pre-signed, time-locked UTXOs to enable off-chain micropayments with on-chain settlement.
- **CoinJoin / Wasabi Wallet:** Combines multiple users' UTXOs into a single joint transaction to enhance privacy through **ambiguity** of input ownership.
- **Chain Analysis (e.g., Chainalysis):** Forensic tools trace UTXO lineage across the DAG-like transaction graph to de-anonymize users.
- **Ordinals / BRC-20 (2023+):** Embed arbitrary data (inscriptions) into UTXOs to create NFT-like assets on Bitcoin.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Tracking a UTXO Through Multiple Transactions

Let us walk through a complete **multi-hop UTXO tracking** scenario. We will track the journey of bitcoin from its coinbase creation to its final resting UTXO, applying the conservation law at every step.

### Scenario Setup

| Party | Action |
|-------|--------|
| **Miner (Alice)** | Mines Block #100 and receives a **coinbase reward of 6.25 BTC** as a fresh UTXO. |
| **Alice** | Sends **4.00 BTC** to Bob, keeps the rest as change. |
| **Bob** | Sends **2.50 BTC** to Charlie, keeps the rest as change. |
| **Charlie** | Sends **1.00 BTC** to David, keeps the rest as change. |

We assume a **simplified fee of 0.01 BTC** per transaction for clarity.

### Step 1 — Coinbase Transaction (Block #100)

Alice's mining reward creates the **first UTXO** in our trace.

**Transaction T1 (Coinbase):**
- **Inputs:** None (coinbase has no previous reference).
- **Outputs:**
  - Output 0: `value = 6.25 BTC` locked to Alice's public key hash.

$$
\text{UTXO Set after Block \#100} = \{ \text{UTXO}_{T1\_0} : 6.25\ \text{BTC} \rightarrow \text{Alice} \}
$$

### Step 2 — Alice Sends 4.00 BTC to Bob (Transaction T2)

Alice must consume her **entire** 6.25 BTC UTXO (since UTXOs are indivisible). She creates two outputs.

**Conservation check for T2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum I_{\text{value}} &= 6.25\ \text{BTC} \\[4pt]
\text{Sent to Bob} &= 4.00\ \text{BTC} \\
\text{TxFee} &= 0.01\ \text{BTC} \\
\text{Change to Alice} &= \sum I_{\text{value}} - \text{Sent} - \text{Fee} \\[4pt]
&= 6.25 - 4.00 - 0.01 \\[4pt]
&= 2.24\ \text{BTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transaction T2 structure:**
- **Input 0:** References `T1` output 0 (consumes UTXO of 6.25 BTC).
- **Output 0:** `value = 4.00 BTC` locked to Bob's public key hash.
- **Output 1:** `value = 2.24 BTC` locked to Alice's *new* change address.

**UTXO Set updates:**

$$
\text{Removed: } \text{UTXO}_{T1\_0} \quad\quad \text{Added: } \text{UTXO}_{T2\_0},\ \text{UTXO}_{T2\_1}
$$

$$
\text{Alice's balance} = 2.24\ \text{BTC}, \quad \text{Bob's balance} = 4.00\ \text{BTC}
$$

### Step 3 — Bob Sends 2.50 BTC to Charlie (Transaction T3)

Bob consumes his 4.00 BTC UTXO entirely.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Change to Bob} &= 4.00 - 2.50 - 0.01 \\[4pt]
&= 1.49\ \text{BTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transaction T3 structure:**
- **Input 0:** References `T2` output 0 (consumes 4.00 BTC UTXO).
- **Output 0:** `value = 2.50 BTC` to Charlie.
- **Output 1:** `value = 1.49 BTC` to Bob's change address.

**UTXO Set updates:**

$$
\text{Removed: } \text{UTXO}_{T2\_0} \quad\quad \text{Added: } \text{UTXO}_{T3\_0},\ \text{UTXO}_{T3\_1}
$$

$$
\text{Bob's balance} = 1.49\ \text{BTC}, \quad \text{Charlie has new UTXO of } 2.50\ \text{BTC}
$$

### Step 4 — Charlie Sends 1.00 BTC to David (Transaction T4)

Charlie consumes his 2.50 BTC UTXO entirely.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Change to Charlie} &= 2.50 - 1.00 - 0.01 \\[4pt]
&= 1.49\ \text{BTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transaction T4 structure:**
- **Input 0:** References `T3` output 0 (consumes 2.50 BTC UTXO).
- **Output 0:** `value = 1.00 BTC` to David.
- **Output 1:** `value = 1.49 BTC` to Charlie's change address.

### Step 5 — Final UTXO Set Summary

| Holder | UTXO Reference | Value |
|--------|----------------|-------|
| Alice | `T2` output 1 | 2.24 BTC |
| Bob | `T3` output 1 | 1.49 BTC |
| Charlie | `T4` output 1 | 1.49 BTC |
| David | `T4` output 0 | 1.00 BTC |
| **Total (David → Alice)** | — | **6.22 BTC** |
| **Cumulative Fees Paid** | 3 × 0.01 | **0.03 BTC** |
| **Total Conservation** | 6.25 = 6.22 + 0.03 | **✓ Valid** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verification:** The original 6.25 BTC has been perfectly conserved. The 0.03 BTC in fees went to miners (not in any UTXO), and the 6.22 BTC has been split into four new UTXOs now spendable by Alice, Bob, Charlie, and David respectively.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: UTXO vs Account-Based Models

This comparison matrix is a **high-yield topic** for KTU exam questions comparing Bitcoin with Ethereum.

| Dimension | UTXO Model (Bitcoin) | Account Model (Ethereum) |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **State representation** | Set of unspent outputs | Account balances + nonce + storage |
| **Transaction effect** | Consumes old UTXOs, creates new ones | Decrements sender balance, increments receiver balance |
| **Verification complexity** | $O(1)$ per input (just check UTXO set) | $O(1)$ per account (lookup balance) |
| **Parallel processing** | High (independent UTXOs) | Limited (sequential per account, nonce ordering) |
| **Double-spend prevention** | UTXO can be referenced at most once globally | Account nonce must increment sequentially |
| **Privacy** | Better (fresh change addresses per tx) | Weaker (persistent account identity) |
| **Smart contract support** | Limited (Script is intentionally non-Turing-complete) | Native, Turing-complete (EVM) |
| **State bloat** | UTXO set grows monotonically | Account state grows with usage |
| **Fungibility within wallet** | Each UTXO has its own history (taint) | Funds are pooled at the account level |

## 3.3 The UTXO Set — A Deep Dive

The **UTXO Set** is a critical data structure maintained by every Bitcoin full node. It is typically implemented using a **LevelDB or RocksDB** key-value store:

- **Key:** `(txid, vout)` — a 36-byte composite identifier.
- **Value:** `{value, scriptPubKey, blockheight, coinbase flag}` — the full output data.

Memory optimization: Modern nodes (Bitcoin Core v22+) use an in-memory cache of approximately **~50,000 UTXOs** (default `dbcache=300` MiB) for fast lookups, falling back to disk for the rest.

**Growth characteristics:**

$$
\text{UTXO Set Size} \propto \text{Network Activity} \quad \text{(not total supply)}
$$

A transaction with 5 inputs and 2 outputs **increases** the UTXO set size by 2 (net) — even though the total bitcoin amount remains unchanged. This is why "dust" UTXOs (those with value less than the cost to spend them) are a network concern: they bloat the set without economic utility.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UTXO Lifecycle Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mining Reward Coinbase TX] -->|Creates UTXO_1| B[UTXO Set: Spendable by Alice]
    B -->|Alice signs TX consuming UTXO_1| C{Transaction T2 Broadcast}
    C -->|Valid: Creates 2 new UTXOs| D[UTXO Set: 4.0 BTC to Bob]
    C -->|Valid: Creates 1 new UTXO| E[UTXO Set: 2.24 BTC Change to Alice]
    D -->|Bob signs TX consuming his UTXO| F{Transaction T3 Broadcast}
    F -->|Valid: Creates new UTXOs| G[UTXO Set: 2.5 BTC to Charlie]
    F -->|Valid: Creates new UTXO| H[UTXO Set: 1.49 BTC Change to Bob]
    G -->|Charlie signs TX consuming UTXO| I{Transaction T4 Broadcast}
    I -->|Valid: Creates new UTXOs| J[UTXO Set: 1.0 BTC to David]
    I -->|Valid: Creates new UTXO| K[UTXO Set: 1.49 BTC Change to Charlie]
    L[Full Node UTXO Set] -.->|Continuously Updated| B
    L -.->|Continuously Updated| D
    L -.->|Continuously Updated| E
    L -.->|Continuously Updated| G
    L -.->|Continuously Updated| H
    L -.->|Continuously Updated| J
    L -.->|Continuously Updated| K
```

## 4.2 Transaction Structure: Inputs and Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PREV["Previous Transaction T1"]
        P1[Output 0: 6.25 BTC\nLocked to Alice PKH]
    end
    subgraph CUR["Current Transaction T2"]
        I1[Input 0:\nprev_txid = T1\nvout = 0\nscriptSig = Alice Sig]
        O1[Output 0:\nvalue = 4.00 BTC\nscriptPubKey = Bob PKH]
        O2[Output 1:\nvalue = 2.24 BTC\nscriptPubKey = Alice Change PKH]
    end
    subgraph NEXT["Next Transaction T3 will reference"]
        N1[T2 Output 0: UTXO for Bob]
        N2[T2 Output 1: UTXO for Alice Change]
    end
    P1 -->|consumed by| I1
    I1 -->|unlocks value| O1
    I1 -->|unlocks value| O2
    O1 --> N1
    O2 --> N2
```

## 4.3 UTXO Set vs Account Model — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UTXO["UTXO Model - Bitcoin"]
        direction TB
        U1[UTXO A: 4.0 BTC\nLock: Bob PKH]
        U2[UTXO B: 2.24 BTC\nLock: Alice Change PKH]
        U3[UTXO C: 1.49 BTC\nLock: Charlie Change PKH]
        U4[UTXO D: 1.0 BTC\nLock: David PKH]
        U5[Bob Balance = 4.0 BTC\nsum of UTXOs he can unlock]
        U6[Alice Balance = 2.24 BTC\nsum of UTXOs she can unlock]
        U1 --> U5
        U2 --> U6
    end
    subgraph ACC["Account Model - Ethereum"]
        direction TB
        A1[Account 0xBob: Balance = 4.0 ETH\nNonce = 7]
        A2[Account 0xAlice: Balance = 2.24 ETH\nNonce = 12]
        A3[Account 0xDavid: Balance = 1.0 ETH\nNonce = 1]
    end
```

## 4.4 Double-Spend Prevention via UTXO Reference

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Sender Wallet
    participant Node1 as Full Node A
    participant Node2 as Full Node B
    participant Mempool as Mempool
    Sender->>Node1: TX spending UTXO_X to Bob (signed)
    Node1->>Node1: Check UTXO Set: Does UTXO_X exist unspent?
    Node1->>Node1: Verify scriptSig unlocks scriptPubKey
    Node1->>Mempool: Valid -> Add to Mempool
    Sender->>Node2: Attempt DOUBLE-SEND of UTXO_X to Mallory
    Node2->>Node2: Check UTXO Set: UTXO_X already in mempool/UTXO
    Node2-->>Sender: REJECT: Input already spent (double-spend)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO). How is a user's "balance" computed from UTXOs in Bitcoin?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output)** is a discrete chunk of bitcoin that has been created as an output of a previous transaction but has not yet been consumed as an input in a subsequent transaction. The Bitcoin blockchain does not store account balances directly; instead, every full node maintains a global set of all currently unspent outputs called the **UTXO Set**.

A user's "balance" is dynamically computed by summing the values of all UTXOs in the UTXO set whose locking script (`scriptPubKey`) can be unlocked by the user's private key:

$$ \text{User Balance} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{value}(\text{UTXO}_i) \quad \forall\ \text{UTXO}_i\ \text{spendable by user} $$

### Question 2
**Q: State and explain the UTXO conservation law. What happens to transaction fees in the UTXO model?** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **UTXO Conservation Law** states that for every valid Bitcoin transaction, the total value of inputs must equal the total value of outputs plus the transaction fee:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{m} \text{Input}_i^{\text{value}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Output}_j^{\text{value}} + \text{TxFee} $$

If this equation is violated (i.e., if outputs exceed inputs), the transaction is rejected by every full node. **Transaction fees** are the implicit difference between input value and output value. They are not represented as a UTXO output — they are collected by the miner who includes the transaction in a block, effectively removing that bitcoin from circulation as far as the UTXO set is concerned.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — UTXO Trace and Conservation Analysis

**Q: Suppose Alice has a single UTXO of 5.0 BTC. She wants to send 2.5 BTC to Bob and 1.0 BTC to Charlie in a single transaction. The transaction fee is 0.05 BTC.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Draw the transaction structure showing all inputs and outputs with their values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the UTXO conservation law to verify the transaction is valid. What is Alice's change, and to which address is it returned? Explain why this change is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transaction Structure:**

```
Transaction T_X
├── Input 0:
│   ├── prev_txid   = (Alice's funding TX)
│   ├── output_index = 0
│   ├── scriptSig    = Alice's digital signature
│   └── value_consumed = 5.0 BTC
├── Output 0:
│   ├── value       = 2.5 BTC
│   └── scriptPubKey = Bob's public key hash
├── Output 1:
│   ├── value       = 1.0 BTC
│   └── scriptPubKey = Charlie's public key hash
└── Output 2 (Change):
    ├── value       = 1.45 BTC
    └── scriptPubKey = Alice's NEW change address PKH
```

**(b) Conservation Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum I_{\text{value}} &= 5.0\ \text{BTC} \\[4pt]
\sum O_{\text{value}} &= 2.5 + 1.0 + \text{Change} \\[4pt]
\text{TxFee} &= 0.05\ \text{BTC} \\[4pt]
\text{Applying conservation:} \quad 5.0 &= 2.5 + 1.0 + \text{Change} + 0.05 \\[4pt]
\text{Change} &= 5.0 - 2.5 - 1.0 - 0.05 \\[4pt]
\text{Change} &= 1.45\ \text{BTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alice's change = 1.45 BTC**, returned to a **fresh change address** that she controls (best practice for privacy).

**Why change is necessary:** UTXOs are **indivisible** — they cannot be partially spent. The entire 5.0 BTC input must be consumed, and any unspent value must be explicitly assigned to a new UTXO. If Alice had not included a change output, the leftover 1.45 BTC would be silently added to the miner's fee — a permanent loss. The change output ensures value conservation and returns the surplus to Alice's control.

### Question B (14 Marks) — UTXO Model vs Account Model

**Q: Compare the UTXO model used by Bitcoin with the Account-based model used by Ethereum.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the fundamental difference in how each model represents state and processes transactions. Provide one advantage of each model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a multi-hop transaction chain: Alice → Bob → Charlie → David, where each user forwards only a fraction of their received amount. Using the UTXO model, demonstrate (with a numerical example) how the conservation law applies at every hop and explain why this enables **double-spend prevention**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) State Representation and Processing:**

| Aspect | UTXO Model (Bitcoin) | Account Model (Ethereum) |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **State** | Global set of unspent outputs | World state of accounts (balance, nonce, storage, code) |
| **Transaction effect** | Consumes old UTXOs, creates new ones | Decrements sender balance, increments receiver balance |
| **Computation** | Stateless w.r.t. accounts; stateful w.r.t. UTXO set | Stateful, globally coherent |

**Advantage of UTXO:** Enables **parallel transaction validation** — since each transaction references independent UTXOs, validators can process them concurrently without conflict, improving throughput.

**Advantage of Account Model:** Enables **efficient smart contracts** — contracts can maintain persistent state across calls (e.g., ERC-20 token balances), which is awkward in the stateless UTXO paradigm.

**(b) Multi-Hop Conservation Example:**

**Setup:** Alice mines 6.0 BTC (coinbase). She sends 3.0 BTC to Bob. Bob sends 1.5 BTC to Charlie. Charlie sends 0.5 BTC to David. Assume fee = 0.01 BTC per transaction.

**Hop 1 (Alice → Bob):**

$$
\text{Change}_\text{Alice} = 6.0 - 3.0 - 0.01 = 2.99\ \text{BTC}
$$

**Hop 2 (Bob → Charlie):**

$$
\text{Change}_\text{Bob} = 3.0 - 1.5 - 0.01 = 1.49\ \text{BTC}
$$

**Hop 3 (Charlie → David):**

$$
\text{Change}_\text{Charlie} = 1.5 - 0.5 - 0.01 = 0.99\ \text{BTC}
$$

**Cumulative conservation check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original UTXO} &= 6.0\ \text{BTC} \\
\text{Sum of final UTXOs} &= 2.99 + 1.49 + 0.99 + 0.5 = 5.97\ \text{BTC} \\
\text{Total fees} &= 3 \times 0.01 = 0.03\ \text{BTC} \\
\text{Check: } 5.97 + 0.03 &= 6.00\ \text{BTC} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Double-spend prevention mechanism:** Each UTXO is uniquely identified by `(prev_txid, vout)`. When a full node validates a new transaction, it checks whether the referenced UTXO already exists in the **UTXO set**. The moment a UTXO is consumed, it is **atomically removed** from the set. Any subsequent attempt to spend the same UTXO will fail the lookup check, and the transaction will be rejected by every honest node — making double-spending cryptographically and economically infeasible without majority hashing power (the infamous 51% attack vector).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing outputs with balances:** Many students write "Alice has 5 BTC" as a stored value. Wrong — only UTXOs are stored; "balance" is a derived sum.
> 2. **Forgetting the change output:** A transaction without a change output where one is required will cause the leftover to be lost as an inflated fee. Examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Mixing up input and output values:** A frequent error is writing `Outputs = Inputs + Fee` instead of `Inputs = Outputs + Fee`.
> 4. **Ignoring coinbase uniqueness:** Students sometimes claim coinbase transactions have an input that references a "null" UTXO. Correct understanding: coinbase has **no inputs**; it creates UTXOs from thin air (subject to block reward limits).
> 5. **Neglecting indeterminism in multi-input transactions:** When a transaction has multiple inputs, they may come from different previous transactions — students often incorrectly assume a 1:1 input-output pairing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **UTXO = Unspent Transaction Output** = a discrete, indivisible chunk of spendable bitcoin.
- The Bitcoin ledger is a **UTXO set**, not an account database. Balances are derived, not stored.
- **Conservation Law:** $\sum I = \sum O + \text{Fee}$ — enforced by every full node on every transaction.
- Each UTXO is consumed **wholly or not at all** — partial spending is impossible.
- **Change outputs** are mandatory when input value exceeds intended payment + fee; they return surplus to a fresh sender address.
- A UTXO is uniquely identified by `(txid, vout)`. Once spent, it is permanently removed from the UTXO set.
- **Coinbase transactions** are the only way new bitcoin enters circulation; they have **no inputs**.
- **Transaction fees** are implicit (input value − output value); they do not appear as UTXOs.
- The **UTXO set grows monotonically** with network activity, not with total supply. Dust UTXOs bloat the set.
- **Double-spend prevention** relies on the global uniqueness of UTXO references — every full node checks the UTXO set before accepting a transaction.
- **1 BTC = 10⁸ satoshis** (the on-chain integer unit).
- UTXO model enables **parallel validation**; Account model (Ethereum) supports **stateful smart contracts**.
- In multi-hop transactions, conservation must hold at **every hop independently** — cumulative fees accumulate linearly.
- The UTXO model is **stateless for users** but **stateful for nodes** (the global UTXO set).
- **Privacy enhancement:** Best practice is to send change to a **newly generated address**, never reusing the sending address.
- The **smallest UTXO** that is economically rational to create is bounded by the cost to spend it (≈ fee per byte × input size).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethereum: Transition from Bitcoin to Ethereum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ethereum: Transition from Bitcoin to Ethereum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethereum** is a decentralized, open-source blockchain platform featuring **Turing-complete** smart contract functionality. Proposed in late 2013 by **Vitalik Buterin** and launched in **July 2015**, it extends the foundational blockchain architecture of Bitcoin into a generalized **stateful transaction-based state machine**.

Formally, Ethereum can be defined as a **deterministic but practically unbounded state machine**, consisting of two main components:
- A **world state** $\sigma$ that maps addresses (160-bit identifiers) to account states.
- A **state transition function** $\Upsilon$ that, given an old state $\sigma$ and a new set of valid transactions $T$, produces a new state $\sigma'$.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$\Upsilon(\sigma, T) \rightarrow \sigma'$$

This stands in sharp contrast to Bitcoin, which is best modeled as a **distributed ledger of unspent transaction outputs (UTXOs)** rather than a stateful machine.

| Property | Bitcoin | Ethereum |
|---|---|---|
| Genesis Year | **2009** | **2015** |
| Founder | Satoshi Nakamoto | Vitalik Buterin et al. |
| Primary Purpose | Peer-to-peer digital cash | Decentralized application platform |
| Scripting | Intentionally limited (non-Turing-complete) | **Turing-complete** (Solidity, Vyper) |
| State Model | **UTXO-based** | **Account/Balance-based** |
| Block Time | ~10 min | ~12-15 s |

## 1.2 The Inadequacy of Bitcoin — Why a Transition Was Needed

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Motivation:** Bitcoin was designed as a specialized "special-purpose" system for value transfer. By 2013, developers realized that the underlying blockchain primitives (cryptography, consensus, P2P networking) could be generalized into a "general-purpose" programmable platform.

Bitcoin's scripting language (`Bitcoin Script`) suffers from several fundamental constraints that prevent it from serving as a robust application development substrate:

1. **Lack of Turing-Completeness:** Bitcoin Script deliberately omits loops to prevent denial-of-service attacks on the network. This makes it impossible to build arbitrary conditional logic or complex iterative algorithms.
2. **No Native State:** Bitcoin has no concept of a persistent application state beyond unspent outputs. Building multi-stage applications (e.g., a Decentralized Autonomous Organization — DAO) is extraordinarily cumbersome.
3. **UTXO Blindness:** UTXOs cannot easily track the running balance of a user, making smart contract logic verbose and difficult.
4. **Limited Expressiveness:** Features like derivatives, decentralized exchanges, on-chain governance, and identity systems are either impossible or highly inefficient on Bitcoin's base layer.
5. **No Internal Mechanism for Off-Chain Data:** Oracles, real-world event triggers, and side-chain coordination mechanisms are not natively supported.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Calculator vs. The Smartphone:**
> 
> - **Bitcoin** is like a **pocket calculator** — extraordinarily good at one specific job (arithmetic) and built with hardware constraints that prevent it from doing anything else.
> - **Ethereum** is like a **smartphone** — a general-purpose platform on which millions of apps (smart contracts) can be built, each with its own persistent state, logic, and user interface.
> 
> Just as a smartphone's value comes not from the hardware alone but from the **ecosystem of apps** running on it, Ethereum's value emerges from the **ecosystem of decentralized applications (dApps)** running on the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Introduced in the Transition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Concepts to Master:**
> 
> - **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine):** The quasi-Turing-complete stack-based virtual machine that executes smart contract bytecode on every node.
> - **Smart Contract:** A piece of code (typically written in Solidity) that lives on-chain and executes deterministically when called.
> - **Gas:** A unit measuring the computational effort required to execute EVM operations; protects the network from infinite loops.
> - **Account:** The fundamental state unit in Ethereum (replaces the UTXO concept).
> - **State Trie:** A modified Merkle Patricia Trie storing the global world state.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Differences: Bitcoin UTXO vs. Ethereum Account Model

### 2.1.1 Bitcoin's UTXO Model

In Bitcoin, there are **no persistent "balances"** at addresses. Instead, the state is the set of all unspent transaction outputs scattered across the ledger.

For any transaction, the following invariant **must hold**:

$$\sum \text{Inputs} = \sum \text{Outputs} + \text{Fee}$$

Each UTXO can be spent **exactly once**. Once spent, it is consumed and replaced with new UTXOs. This provides strong privacy (each transaction is a fresh set of outputs) but complicates smart contract design.

### 2.1.2 Ethereum's Account Model

Ethereum abandons UTXOs in favor of two account types:

1. **Externally Owned Accounts (EOA):** Controlled by private keys; can initiate transactions.
2. **Contract Accounts:** Controlled by their contract code; cannot initiate transactions; execute only in response to transactions from EOAs or other contracts.

Each account is a 4-tuple of state variables:

$$\text{Account} = (n, \, b, \, s, \, c)$$

Where:
- $n$ = **nonce** (number of transactions sent / contracts created)
- $b$ = **balance** (in Wei, where $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$)
- $s$ = **storage root** (256-bit hash of the account's storage trie — empty for EOAs)
- $c$ = **code hash** (hash of the EVM bytecode — empty for EOAs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The presence of a **storage root** is what makes Ethereum "stateful" — a smart contract can read and write to its own persistent storage, something fundamentally impossible in Bitcoin's UTXO model.

## 2.2 The Ethereum State Transition Function

The state transition function $\Upsilon(\sigma, T)$ operates as follows:

1. **Check** that the transaction is well-formed (valid signature, valid nonce).
2. **Compute** the transaction fee: $T_{\text{fee}} = T_{\text{gasLimit}} \times T_{\text{gasPrice}}$.
3. **Decrement** the sender's balance by $T_{\text{fee}}$ and increment the nonce.
4. **Determine** the receiving account. If it is a contract account, execute its code with the transaction data and current state.
5. **Refund** any unused gas and consume gas used.
6. **Produce** the new state root $R'$ and append the new block to the chain.

## 2.3 Gas Mechanism — The Anti-DoS Backbone

Because the EVM is (practically) Turing-complete, an unprotected system would allow malicious contracts to halt every node with infinite loops. Ethereum solves this via **gas**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas** is the unit of computational cost. Every EVM opcode has a fixed gas cost (e.g., `ADD = 3 gas`, `SSTORE = 20,000 gas` for a new slot). Transactions specify:
> - `gasLimit` ($g_l$): maximum gas the sender is willing to consume.
> - `gasPrice` ($p$): Wei per unit of gas the sender is willing to pay.

### 2.3.1 Gas Refund and Consumption Rules

Total transaction fee paid in Wei:

$$T_{\text{fee}} = g_{\text{used}} \times p$$

Where $g_{\text{used}} \leq g_l$. If execution runs out of gas mid-way, the transaction **fails** but the sender still pays for all gas consumed up to the point of failure (a critical safety property for validators).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference Table)

| Symbol / Concept | Formula or Definition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| State Transition | $\Upsilon(\sigma, T) \rightarrow \sigma'$ | Models any blockchain as a deterministic state machine |
| Account State | $(n, b, s, c)$ | Defines the persistent state of every Ethereum address |
| Transaction Fee | $T_{\text{fee}} = g_{\text{used}} \times p$ | Foundation of the fee market (EIP-1559 modified this) |
| Wei ↔ Ether | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$ | Avoid floating-point arithmetic in EVM |
| Block Reward (post-Merge) | $\approx 0$ ETH + priority fee | Validators earn transaction fees, not new ETH |
| Gwei | $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9 \text{ Wei}$ | Standard unit for `gasPrice` in wallets |
| Block Gas Limit | $\approx 30{,}000{,}000$ gas | Caps computational throughput of the EVM |
| EVM Stack Depth | $\leq 1024$ | Limits recursive calls and deep call stacks |
| Block Time (target) | **12 seconds** | Determines finality and reorg risk |
| Patricia Trie Branching | $16$-ary (hex-prefix encoded) | Efficient storage of sparse 256-bit key-value pairs |

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

The transition from Bitcoin's specialized design to Ethereum's generalized state machine enables the engineering of:
- **Decentralized Finance (DeFi):** Lending, AMMs, derivatives.
- **Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs):** Standardized via ERC-721 / ERC-1155.
- **Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs):** On-chain governance.
- **Layer-2 Rollups:** Optimistic and ZK rollups settle batches on Ethereum L1.
- **Token Standards:** ERC-20 (fungible), ERC-721 (non-fungible), ERC-4626 (vaults).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost of a Smart Contract Execution

The total cost paid by a user invoking a contract is the product of gas consumed and the effective gas price. We derive it in three logical stages.

### Step 1: Intrinsic Gas (Base Cost)

The transaction itself, even before execution, costs a fixed amount known as the **intrinsic gas** $g_{\text{intrinsic}}$. This protects the network from spam transactions.

$$g_{\text{intrinsic}} = 21{,}000 + 4 \times \text{non-zero calldata bytes} + 16 \times \text{zero calldata bytes} \quad \text{(pre-EIP-2028)}$$

After EIP-2028 (implemented November 2019), the constants became:

$$g_{\text{intrinsic}} = 21{,}000 + 16 \times \text{non-zero calldata bytes} + 4 \times \text{zero calldata bytes}$$

The **21,000 gas** is the base cost of any transaction, regardless of whether it interacts with a contract.

### Step 2: Execution Gas (EVM Operations)

Each opcode executed by the EVM contributes its predefined cost. The total execution gas is:

$$g_{\text{exec}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{\text{op}}(i)$$

Where $c_{\text{op}}(i)$ is the gas cost of the $i$-th opcode executed.

### Step 3: Total Transaction Fee

The total fee in Wei is:
