$$\text{Total Fee (Wei)} = \left( g_{\text{intrinsic}} + g_{\text{exec}} \right) \times p$$

If we want to express the fee in **Ether** (the more readable unit):

$$\text{Total Fee (ETH)} = \frac{(g_{\text{intrinsic}} + g_{\text{exec}}) \times p}{10^{18}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** A user sends a transaction to an ERC-20 token's `transfer()` function. The transaction contains 100 bytes of calldata, of which 30 are non-zero. The EVM execution consumes an additional 45,000 gas (opcode costs). The user specifies `gasPrice = 50 Gwei`. Compute the total fee in Wei and in ETH.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Intrinsic Gas (post-EIP-2028):**

$$g_{\text{intrinsic}} = 21{,}000 + 16 \times 30 + 4 \times (100 - 30)$$

Breaking it down:
- Base cost: $21{,}000$ gas
- Non-zero bytes: $16 \times 30 = 480$ gas
- Zero bytes: $4 \times 70 = 280$ gas

Adding:

$$g_{\text{intrinsic}} = 21{,}000 + 480 + 280 = 21{,}760 \text{ gas}$$

**Step 2 — Total Gas Used:**

$$g_{\text{total}} = g_{\text{intrinsic}} + g_{\text{exec}} = 21{,}760 + 45{,}000 = 66{,}760 \text{ gas}$$

**Step 3 — Total Fee in Wei:**

Converting gas price: $p = 50 \text{ Gwei} = 50 \times 10^9 \text{ Wei} = 5 \times 10^{10} \text{ Wei}$.

$$\text{Fee} = 66{,}760 \times 5 \times 10^{10} = 3.338 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ETH:**

$$\text{Fee (ETH)} = \frac{3.338 \times 10^{15}}{10^{18}} = 0.003338 \text{ ETH}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always show the unit conversion. Many KTU students lose marks by writing "Gwei" as a value without converting to Wei before multiplication. The fee is always denominated in **Wei** for the actual EVM calculation, then optionally converted to ETH for human readability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bitcoin vs. Ethereum State Transition

| Aspect | Bitcoin's UTXO Transition | Ethereum's Account-Based Transition |
|---|---|---|
| State Representation | Set of unspent outputs | Merkle Patricia Trie of accounts |
| Concurrency | Multiple inputs from same wallet = separate UTXOs | Single nonce prevents replay |
| Contract Capability | Limited to simple locking scripts | Arbitrary code execution via EVM |
| Storage Persistence | None (UTXOs are destroyed when spent) | Per-account persistent `storage` trie |
| Transaction Format | Inputs referencing UTXOs + Outputs | Sender, recipient, value, data, gas params |
| Failure Recovery | Unused UTXOs remain valid | Entire transaction reverts; nonce increments |
| State Update Granularity | Per-UTXO update | Per-storage-slot update |

## 3.4 Derivation: Why the EVM is "Quasi-" Turing-Complete

Turing completeness requires the ability to compute any function. The EVM can do this **in principle** because it supports:
- Arbitrary memory (up to $2^{256}$ bytes, practically limited by gas).
- Loops, conditionals, and recursion (subject to gas).
- Arbitrary function calls between contracts.

However, because every computational step costs gas and gas is finite per transaction, the EVM is **bounded Turing-complete** in any single execution. The full Ethereum system (across all transactions and blocks) is **unboundedly Turing-complete** because new transactions can be initiated indefinitely. This nuance is a classic KTU exam trap.

Mathematically, for a transaction with gas limit $g_l$:

$$\text{Computation Possible} = \{M : \text{Steps}(M) \cdot c_{\text{step}} \leq g_l\}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Transition from Bitcoin to Ethereum Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bitcoin Genesis 2009] --> B[Nakamoto Whitepaper]
    B --> C[UTXO Ledger Model]
    C --> D[Limitations Identified]
    D --> D1[Non Turing Complete Script]
    D --> D2[No Persistent Application State]
    D --> D3[Limited Smart Contract Logic]
    D1 --> E[Ethereum Whitepaper 2013]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Generalized State Machine Design]
    F --> G[Ethereum Virtual Machine EVM]
    F --> H[Account Based State Model]
    F --> I[Gas Mechanism for DoS Protection]
    G --> J[Ethereum Mainnet Launch July 2015]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Smart Contract Ecosystem]
    J --> L[DeFi and Token Standards]
    J --> M[Layer 2 Rollup Infrastructure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Ethereum State Transition Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Incoming Transaction T] --> CHK{Well Formed}
    CHK -->|No| REJ[Reject Transaction]
    CHK -->|Yes| FEE[Deduct Gas Fee From Sender]
    FEE --> BAL[Decrement Sender Balance]
    BAL --> NON[Increment Sender Nonce]
    NON --> REC{Receive Type}
    REC -->|EOA| EOA[Credit Recipient Balance]
    REC -->|Contract| EVM[Execute EVM Bytecode]
    EVM --> CONS[Consume Gas Per Opcode]
    CONS --> STK{Stack Depth OK}
    STK -->|No| FAIL[Transaction Fails State Unchanged]
    STK -->|Yes| REF[Refund Unused Gas]
    EOA --> REF
    REF --> NS[Compute New State Root]
    NS --> BLK[Append New Block To Chain]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Account Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ACCT[Ethereum Account] --> EOA[Externally Owned Account EOA]
    ACCT --> CA[Contract Account CA]
    EOA --> EOA1[Private Key Controlled]
    EOA --> EOA2[Can Initiate Transactions]
    EOA --> EOA3[No Associated Code]
    EOA --> EOA4[Empty Storage Trie]
    CA --> CA1[Code Controlled]
    CA --> CA2[Cannot Initiate Transactions]
    CA --> CA3[Contains EVM Bytecode]
    CA --> CA4[Persistent Storage Trie]
    CA2 --> CA5[Reactive Only]
    EOA1 --> A1[State: nonce balance]
    CA3 --> A2[State: nonce balance storageRoot codeHash]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Comparison

| Layer | Bitcoin Stack | Ethereum Stack |
|---|---|---|
| Application Layer | Wallet, simple scripts | Smart contracts (Solidity, Vyper) |
| Execution Layer | Stack-based Script interpreter | EVM (quasi-Turing-complete) |
| State Layer | UTXO set | World state (account trie) |
| Consensus Layer | PoW (now retired 2024) | PoS (since The Merge, Sept 2022) |
| Networking Layer | P2P gossip protocol | DevP2P (RLPx) |
| Data Layer | Merkle Tree of transactions | Merkle Patricia Trie + Verkle (future) |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Smart Contract
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **smart contract** is a self-executing program stored on a blockchain that runs deterministically when predefined conditions are met. As defined by Nick Szabo in 1994 and popularized by Ethereum, smart contracts:

1. Live as **bytecode** on the Ethereum blockchain.
2. Are executed by every node in the EVM, ensuring **trustless execution**.
3. Have a persistent **state** stored in a Merkle Patricia Trie.
4. Are **immutable** once deployed (though they can be made upgradable through proxy patterns).

**Example:** An ERC-20 token's `transfer()` function is a smart contract subroutine.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of gas in the Ethereum network?
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gas serves **three critical roles** in Ethereum:

1. **Resource Metering:** Every EVM opcode has a fixed gas cost. The total gas used by a transaction is the sum of all opcodes executed.
2. **DoS Protection:** Because gas is finite per transaction, malicious infinite loops are bounded. The EVM is **quasi-Turing-complete**.
3. **Market Pricing:** The `gasPrice` field creates a fee market. Miners/validators prioritize transactions offering higher gas prices.

**Formula:** $\text{Transaction Fee} = g_{\text{used}} \times p_{\text{gas}}$ in Wei.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S6 CSE Elective]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the transition from Bitcoin to Ethereum. Discuss the major limitations of Bitcoin that motivated the design of Ethereum.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the Ethereum account model in detail. Differentiate between EOA and Contract accounts with suitable examples.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution [7 marks]

**The Transition — Timeline and Motivation [3 marks]:**

- **2008:** Nakamoto publishes the Bitcoin whitepaper — a peer-to-peer electronic cash system.
- **2013:** Vitalik Buterin proposes Ethereum as a **generalized blockchain platform**, arguing that Bitcoin's design is too narrow.
- **2014:** Ethereum crowdsale raises ~$18M.
- **July 30, 2015:** **Frontier** (Ethereum genesis) goes live.
- **2016:** The DAO hack leads to a hard fork, splitting Ethereum and Ethereum Classic.

**Limitations of Bitcoin that Ethereum Overcomes [4 marks]:**

| # | Bitcoin Limitation | Ethereum's Solution |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Non-Turing-complete Script | Quasi-Turing-complete EVM |
| 2 | UTXO model makes stateful apps hard | Account/balance model with persistent storage |
| 3 | No internal state for contracts | Per-account `storage` trie |
| 4 | Long block time (~10 min) limits throughput | ~12 s block time, planned sharding |
| 5 | No on-chain governance or DApps | Native support for DApps via Solidity |
| 6 | Specialized "cash transfer" purpose | General-purpose "world computer" |

---

#### Part (b) Solution [7 marks]

**Ethereum Account Model — The Four-Field State [3 marks]:**

Every Ethereum account is a 4-tuple:

$$\text{Account} = (n, b, s, c)$$

Where:
- $n$ = nonce (transaction counter)
- $b$ = balance (in Wei)
- $s$ = storage root (256-bit hash of the Merkle Patricia Trie of contract storage)
- $c$ = code hash (hash of the EVM bytecode)

**EOA vs. Contract Account — Comparative [4 marks]:**

| Property | EOA | Contract Account |
|---|---|---|
| Control | Private key | Contract code |
| Initiate Tx? | Yes | No (only reactive) |
| Code Hash | Empty | Hash of EVM bytecode |
| Storage | None | Persistent 256-bit key-value store |
| Example | MetaMask wallet | Uniswap V3 Router |
| Field `(n, b, s, c)` | $(n, b, 0, 0)$ | $(n, b, s, c)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students write that contract accounts can initiate transactions. **This is incorrect.** In Ethereum, only EOAs can originate transactions. Contract accounts execute only when called upon. Misremembering this costs 2 marks easily.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, S6 CSE Elective]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the Ethereum state transition function $\Upsilon(\sigma, T) \rightarrow \sigma'$. What is the world state $\sigma$ and how is it stored?

**(b) [7 marks]** A user calls a smart contract with `gasLimit = 200,000`, `gasPrice = 80 Gwei`. The intrinsic gas is 21,760 and execution consumes 110,000 gas. Calculate the total transaction fee in Wei and in ETH. What happens if execution tries to use more than 200,000 gas?

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution [7 marks]

**The World State $\sigma$ [3 marks]:**

The world state is a **mapping from 160-bit addresses to account states**:

$$\sigma: \{0,1\}^{160} \rightarrow (n, b, s, c)$$

It is stored in a **Merkle Patricia Trie** (specifically, a hex-prefix encoded sparse Merkle tree) whose root hash is the **state root** of every Ethereum block. This makes verification of any account balance $O(\log n)$.

**The State Transition Function [4 marks]:**

$$\Upsilon(\sigma, T) \rightarrow \sigma'$$

Operational steps:
1. Validate transaction $T$ (signature, nonce, balance).
2. Deduct upfront gas cost: $g_l \times p$.
3. Increment sender's nonce.
4. Execute contract code if recipient is a contract account.
5. Refund unused gas.
6. Output new state $\sigma'$ with a new state root.

**Why it is deterministic:** Every node runs the same EVM bytecode with the same initial state, guaranteeing consensus.

---

#### Part (b) Solution [7 marks] — Numerical

**Step 1 — Total Gas Available:**
The user set `gasLimit = 200,000`. This is the maximum gas that may be consumed.

$$g_l = 200{,}000 \text{ gas}$$

**Step 2 — Total Gas Used:**

$$g_{\text{used}} = g_{\text{intrinsic}} + g_{\text{exec}} = 21{,}760 + 110{,}000 = 131{,}760 \text{ gas}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Gas Price to Wei:**

$$p = 80 \text{ Gwei} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ Wei} = 8 \times 10^{10} \text{ Wei}$$

**Step 4 — Total Fee in Wei:**

$$\text{Fee (Wei)} = g_{\text{used}} \times p = 131{,}760 \times 8 \times 10^{10}$$

Breaking the multiplication:

$$131{,}760 \times 8 = 1{,}054{,}080$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Fee (Wei)} = 1.05408 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to ETH:**

$$\text{Fee (ETH)} = \frac{1.05408 \times 10^{15}}{10^{18}} = 0.00105408 \text{ ETH}$$

**Step 6 — What if execution exceeds 200,000 gas? [2 marks]**

If execution attempts to consume more than $g_l = 200{,}000$ gas:
- The EVM halts execution immediately with an **out-of-gas exception**.
- The transaction **fails and reverts** — no state changes are committed.
- **However**, the sender **still pays** for all gas consumed up to the out-of-gas point, which in the worst case is the full 200,000 gas (since gas is consumed before the check in some failure modes).
- The nonce is **incremented** to prevent replay attacks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks when students write "the transaction fails and the user does not pay" or "the entire block is reverted." The correct interpretation is: **state changes are reverted, but gas is NOT refunded** — this is a critical security property to prevent DoS attacks on the network.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**
> 
> - **Bitcoin** is a *UTXO-based, special-purpose* ledger optimized for value transfer. It uses a non-Turing-complete scripting language.
> - **Ethereum** is an *account-based, general-purpose* state machine. It uses a quasi-Turing-complete EVM.
> - An Ethereum **account** is a 4-tuple $(n, b, s, c)$: nonce, balance, storage root, code hash.
> - **EOAs** are controlled by private keys and can originate transactions. **Contract accounts** cannot.
> - **Gas** is a unit of computational effort that bounds EVM execution. Formula: $\text{Fee} = g_{\text{used}} \times p$.
> - $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$. $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9 \text{ Wei}$.
> - Intrinsic gas = $21{,}000 + 16 \times (\text{non-zero calldata bytes}) + 4 \times (\text{zero calldata bytes})$ (post-EIP-2028).
> - The state transition is modeled as $\Upsilon(\sigma, T) \rightarrow \sigma'$, where $\sigma$ is stored in a **Merkle Patricia Trie**.
> - **Out-of-gas exceptions** cause the transaction to revert but the sender still pays for consumed gas.
> - Ethereum is **"quasi-Turing-complete"** per transaction but **unboundedly Turing-complete** across the system.
> - Ethereum transitioned from **PoW to PoS** during **The Merge** in September 2022.
> - The Ethereum state is a mapping: $\sigma : \{0,1\}^{160} \rightarrow (n, b, s, c)$.
> - Always show **unit conversions** in numerical questions (Wei ↔ Gwei ↔ ETH).
> - Block time ≈ **12 seconds**; block gas limit ≈ **30 million gas**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Ethereum World Computer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Ethereum World Computer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethereum World Computer** is formally defined as a *decentralized, deterministic, single-state (shared) computing machine* whose state is maintained by thousands of independent nodes running the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** in consensus. Every node executes the same sequence of instructions (bytecode) in response to transactions, and the *post-state root* is committed to a Merkle Patricia Trie that forms part of the global blockchain.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the Ethereum World Computer is the architectural abstraction that allows any participant on the network to deploy **Turing-complete smart contracts** that manipulate a global *world state* $\sigma$, transitioning it deterministically from $\sigma_t$ to $\sigma_{t+1}$ through a well-defined state transition function:

$$\sigma_{t+1} \equiv \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$$

where $T$ is a valid transaction or set of transactions bundled into a block, and $\Upsilon$ is the *Ethereum State Transition Function* governed by the EVM specification (Yellow Paper, Wood et al.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Global Excel Spreadsheet**
>
> Imagine a magical spreadsheet that exists simultaneously in the computers of 10 million people around the world. No single person owns it. If Alice changes a cell from `5` to `10`, then *instantly and identically* Bob's, Chen's, and Priya's copies of that spreadsheet also change to `10` — without ever calling a central server (like Google Sheets). Now imagine that anyone can upload a tiny program (a *smart contract*) that runs automatically on every copy whenever a condition is met. That, in essence, is the **Ethereum World Computer**.

The key promises of this abstraction are:
- **Decentralization** — no central operator (authority killed).
- **Trustless execution** — output is verifiable without trusting the executor.
- **Censorship resistance** — no one can prevent a valid transaction from being processed.
- **Composability** — contracts can call other contracts, building "money Legos."

## 1.3 Physical / Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Block Time (post-Merge)** | **12 seconds** |
| **EVM Stack Depth** | **1024** |
| **EVM Word Size** | **256 bits (32 bytes)** |
| **Gas Limit per Block (target)** | **30,000,000 gas** |
| **World State Storage** | Distributed **Merkle Patricia Trie** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A commonly misunderstood point: the Ethereum World Computer is **not** a single physical server. It is a *logical* machine whose *physical instantiation* is spread across thousands of nodes worldwide. The "one computer" is a **consensus illusion** — every honest node arrives at the identical post-state independently.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Five Logical Layers

The Ethereum World Computer is best understood as a stack of five logical layers. Each layer has a specific responsibility, and separating them is critical for KTU 14-mark answers.

**Layer 1 — Data Layer (World State)**
- The global *world state* $\sigma$ maps addresses to account states.
- An account state is a 4-tuple:

$$\sigma(\text{addr}) = \big( n, \; \mathbf{balance}, \; \mathbf{storage}, \; \mathbf{code} \big)$$

- $n$ → nonce (tx count for EOAs, contract creation count for contracts).
- $\mathbf{balance}$ → Wei balance (1 ETH = $10^{18}$ Wei).
- $\mathbf{storage}$ → 256-bit word key-value store (only for contracts).
- $\mathbf{code}$ → contract bytecode hash (only for contracts).

**Layer 2 — Consensus Layer (post-Merge)**
- Uses **Proof of Stake (PoS)** via the Beacon Chain.
- Validators are chosen per slot (12 s) via **RANDAO** + **LMD-GHOST** fork-choice rule.
- Finality is achieved after two epochs (~**12.8 minutes**) through **Casper FFG**.

**Layer 3 — Execution Layer (EVM)**
- A **quasi-Turing complete** (gas-bounded) 256-bit stack machine.
- Executes EVM bytecode derived from high-level languages (Solidity, Vyper, Yul).
- Each opcode has a pre-assigned **gas cost**.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (Smart Contracts & DApps)**
- User-deployed programs that encode business logic (DeFi, NFTs, DAOs, etc.).

**Layer 5 — Interface Layer (Wallets, Web3 dApps)**
- MetaMask, WalletConnect, Web3.js, Ethers.js provide user-facing entry points.

## 2.2 Two Account Types in Ethereum

| Property | Externally Owned Account (EOA) | Contract Account |
|---|---|---|
| Controlled by | Private key | Contract code |
| Has `code`? | No (empty bytecode) | Yes |
| Can initiate tx? | **Yes** | **Only when called by an EOA or another contract** |
| Has `storage`? | No | **Yes (persistent 256-bit key-value store)** |
| Example | Human user wallet | ERC-20 token contract |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU answers:** The EOA/Contract distinction is a guaranteed 2–3 mark sub-question. Memorize the table above.

## 2.3 The State Transition Function — Formal Definition

The Ethereum World Computer behaves as a deterministic state machine. For a block $B$ applied to state $\sigma$:

$$\sigma' = \Upsilon(\sigma, B)$$

The block-level transition is the *sequential application* of every transaction inside the block:

$$\Upsilon(\sigma, B) = \big( \Upsilon(\sigma, T_1), T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n \big)$$

Each *transaction-level* transition executes the EVM bytecode under a **gas budget**:

$$\text{gasUsed} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} G_{\text{op}_i} \cdot c_{\text{op}_i}$$

where $G_{\text{op}_i}$ is the gas cost of opcode $i$ and $c_{\text{op}_i}$ is the number of times it is executed.

## 2.4 Gas Economics — The Anti-DoS Pillar

Gas is the metering unit that makes the EVM **Turing-complete yet bounded**. Every computational step costs a fixed amount of gas. The total fee for a transaction is:

$$\text{TxFee} = \text{gasUsed} \times (\text{baseFee} + \text{priorityTip})$$

- **`baseFee`** — algorithmically determined by network congestion, **burned** (EIP-1559).
- **`priorityTip`** — paid to the validator as an incentive.

If `gasUsed > gasLimit`, the EVM **reverts** all state changes but the gas is **still consumed** (a key anti-DoS property).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| World State Transition | $\sigma_{t+1} = \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$ | Deterministic function |
| Transaction Fee | $\text{Fee} = \text{gasUsed} \times \text{gasPrice}$ | Wei |
| EIP-1559 Fee | $\text{Fee} = \text{gasUsed} \times (\text{baseFee} + \text{tip})$ | baseFee burned |
| Gas Refund (post-London) | Up to **20%** of unused gas | SELFDESTRUCT / SSTORE |
| Wei → Ether | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18}$ Wei | Standard metric |
| Gwei | $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9$ Wei | Common fee unit |
| Block Time | **12 s** | post-Merge (PoS) |
| EVM Word | **256 bits** | All stack/memory ops |
| Account Tuple | $(n, b, s, c)$ | Nonce, Balance, Storage, Code |
| World State Trie Root | Stored in **block header** | 32-byte Keccak-256 hash |

> [!NOTE]
> Always escape absolute value bars using `\vert` or `\mid` in tables. For example, write $\lvert \text{gasUsed} \rvert$ rather than $\vert$ inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Ethereum World Computer abstraction is used in:
- **Decentralized Finance (DeFi)** — Uniswap, Aave, Compound.
- **Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs)** — ERC-721, ERC-1155 standards.
- **Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs)** — on-chain governance.
- **Decentralized Identity (DID)** — self-sovereign identity.
- **Supply Chain Provenance** — tracking goods on a public ledger.
- **Tokenization of Real-World Assets (RWA)** — bonds, real estate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How a Single Transaction Mutates the World State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks for the *order of operations* when a transaction is processed. The full pipeline (6 steps) below is examinable for **up to 7 marks**.

**Step 1 — Transaction Submission & Propagation**
The user signs a transaction $T = (\text{nonce}, \text{to}, \text{value}, \text{data}, \text{gasLimit}, \text{gasPrice}, v, r, s)$ with their private key. ECDSA produces a 64-byte signature $(r, s)$ and recovery id $v$. The signed transaction is gossiped across the devp2p network.

**Step 2 — Intrinsic Gas Calculation**
Before EVM execution even begins, an *intrinsic gas* is deducted:

$$
\text{intrinsicGas} = 21000 + 4 \times \text{nonZeroBytes} + 16 \times \text{zeroBytes} + 20000 \text{ (if contract creation)}
$$

The 21,000 base cost accounts for transaction overhead.

**Step 3 — Nonce Verification & Balance Check**
The transaction's nonce must equal `account.nonce + 1` of the sender. Sender balance must cover `value + (gasLimit × gasPrice)`. If either check fails, the transaction is **invalid** and never enters the EVM.

**Step 4 — EVM Bytecode Execution**
The EVM loads the *callee's* `code`, sets the program counter (PC) to 0, and steps through opcodes. Each opcode pops arguments from the 256-bit stack, executes, and pushes results. A running `gasCounter` decrements after every opcode:

$$
\text{gasCounter} \leftarrow \text{gasCounter} - G_{\text{op}}
$$

If `gasCounter` would go negative, execution halts with `OUT_OF_GAS` and **all state changes are reverted** (atomic execution).

**Step 5 — State Mutation & Receipt Generation**
On successful execution (`STOP`, `RETURN`, or implicit success), the new world state $\sigma'$ is built. A transaction *receipt* is generated containing:
- `status` (1 = success, 0 = revert)
- `cumulativeGasUsed`
- `logsBloom` (256-byte Bloom filter of emitted events)
- `logs[]` (event payloads)

**Step 6 — Block Inclusion & State-Root Commitment**
The validator orders the receipts into a **Receipts Trie**, computes the world state trie root $R_{\sigma'}$, and commits these roots in the new block header. Other nodes verify the root and accept the new state.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Transaction Fee Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked 3-mark problem** in KTU Module 3.

**Problem:** A user sends a transaction that uses `21,000 + 50,000 = 71,000` gas. The `baseFee` is 30 Gwei and the user includes a `priorityTip` of 2 Gwei. Compute (a) the total fee in Wei, and (b) the portion that is burned.

**Solution:**

Given:
- `gasUsed` = $71{,}000$
- `baseFee` = $30 \text{ Gwei} = 30 \times 10^9 \text{ Wei}$
- `priorityTip` = $2 \text{ Gwei} = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ Wei}$

**(a) Total Fee:**

$$
\text{TotalFee} = \text{gasUsed} \times (\text{baseFee} + \text{tip})
$$

$$
\text{TotalFee} = 71{,}000 \times (30 \times 10^9 + 2 \times 10^9)
$$

$$
\text{TotalFee} = 71{,}000 \times 32 \times 10^9
$$

$$
\text{TotalFee} = 2{,}272{,}000 \times 10^9
$$

$$
\text{TotalFee} = 2.272 \times 10^{18} \text{ Wei} = 2.272 \text{ ETH}
$$

**(b) Burned Portion:**

$$
\text{Burned} = 71{,}000 \times 30 \times 10^9 = 2.13 \times 10^{18} \text{ Wei} = 2.13 \text{ ETH}
$$

**Validator Reward (Tip):**

$$
\text{Tip} = 71{,}000 \times 2 \times 10^9 = 0.142 \times 10^{18} \text{ Wei} = 0.142 \text{ ETH}
$$

**Verification:** `Burned + Tip = 2.13 + 0.142 = 2.272 ETH` ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Ethereum vs. Bitcoin World Computer Comparison

| Property | Bitcoin | Ethereum |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Decentralized currency | **Dec general-purpose world computer** |
| Script Language | **Bitcoin Script** (not Turing complete, stack-based) | **EVM Bytecode** (quasi-Turing complete) |
| Loops | Not supported | Supported (bounded by gas) |
| State Model | UTXO (unspent tx outputs) | **Account-based (stateful)** |
| Block Time | ~10 min | **12 s** |
| Consensus | PoW (now PoS) | **PoS (post-Merge)** |
| Block Reward | BTC issuance | ETH issuance + burned baseFee |
| Smart Contracts | Limited (BTC, RGB) | **Native, first-class** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Module 3, the **UTXO vs Account** distinction is a board-favorite 5-mark question. Account-based state is precisely *why* Ethereum can host the World Computer abstraction — UTXO is stateless and thus unsuitable for complex contract storage.

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Gas Makes EVM Bounded-Turing Complete

In computability theory, a **Turing machine** is unbounded in memory and time. The EVM is *quasi*-Turing because every opcode has a finite cost and the `gasLimit` is finite (≤ block gas limit). Formally, the EVM can compute *any* function $f$ that a Turing machine can, **provided** the input contract is funded with sufficient gas. We can state this rigorously:

For any Turing-computable function $f(x)$ that halts in $N$ steps, there exists a finite gas budget $G$ such that the EVM program $P_f$ computes $f(x)$ without `OUT_OF_GAS`, where $G = \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_{\text{op}_i} < \text{blockGasLimit}$.

If the budget is insufficient, the EVM halts early — this is the **halting-problem workaround**. Ethereum thus *side-steps* the undecidability of the halting problem by simply aborting when gas runs out.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Ethereum World Computer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Wallet MetaMask] -->|Signed Tx| B[Tx Pool Mempool]
    B --> C[Validator Node PoS]
    C --> D[Block Builder]
    D --> E[Execution Layer EVM]
    E --> F[State Transition Function upsilon]
    F --> G[New World State sigma prime]
    G --> H[Merkle Patricia Trie]
    H --> I[Block Header State Root]
    I --> J[Consensus Layer Beacon Chain]
    J --> K[Finalized Block]
    K --> L[All Honest Nodes]
    L --> M[Global World State Synchronized]

    subgraph EVM_Internals[Inside EVM]
        E1[Stack 256-bit] --> E2[Memory Volatile]
        E2 --> E3[Storage Persistent 2 exp 256]
        E3 --> E4[Gas Meter]
    end

    F -.uses.-> EVM_Internals
```

## 4.2 World State Trie Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[World State Root 32-byte hash in block header] --> T1[Branch Node]
    T1 --> T2[Extension Node]
    T1 --> T3[Branch Node]
    T2 --> A1[Leaf EOA Alice]
    T3 --> C1[Leaf Contract USDC]
    T3 --> C2[Leaf Contract Uniswap V3]

    A1 --> A1a[nonce 5]
    A1 --> A1b[balance 2.5 ETH]
    A1 --> A1c[code EMPTY]
    A1 --> A1d[storage EMPTY]

    C1 --> C1a[nonce 1]
    C1 --> C1b[balance 0]
    C1 --> C1c[code 0x6080...]
    C1 --> C1d[storage Mappings]
```

## 4.3 Transaction Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unknown
    Unknown --> Pending: Tx broadcast
    Pending --> Queued: Nonce gap exists
    Queued --> Pending: Gap filled
    Pending --> InBlock: Validator includes
    InBlock --> Finalized: Two epochs later
    InBlock --> Reverted: EVM revert
    Finalized --> [*]
    Reverted --> [*]
```

## 4.4 EVM Execution Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fetch Opcode at PC] --> B[Decode Opcode]
    B --> C[Pop Operands from Stack]
    C --> D[Execute Operation]
    D --> E[Push Results to Stack]
    E --> F{Gas Remaining?}
    F -->|Yes| G{STOP or RETURN?}
    F -->|No| H[Revert All State Changes]
    G -->|No| A
    G -->|Yes| I[Commit New State]
    H --> J[Consume All Gas]
    I --> K[Refund Unused Gas]
    J --> [*]
    K --> [*]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
> **Q1. Define the term "Ethereum World Computer" and list its three key properties.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The *Ethereum World Computer* is a decentralized, deterministic state machine running the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** across thousands of nodes. Its three key properties are:
> 1. **Decentralization** — no single authority controls execution.
> 2. **Determinism** — given the same pre-state and transaction, every node produces the same post-state.
> 3. **Turing-completeness** — smart contracts can express any computable function, bounded by gas.
>
> *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per property = 3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between an EOA and a Contract Account in Ethereum.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | EOA | Contract Account |
> |---|---|---|
> | Control | Private key | Contract code |
> | Code field | Empty | Holds bytecode |
> | Can start tx | Yes | No (only responds to calls) |
> | Storage | None | 256-bit persistent storage |
> | Example | User wallet | ERC-20 token contract |
>
> *(1 mark for EOA description, 1 mark for Contract description, 1 mark for distinguishing point)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For questions on the Ethereum World Computer, students **commonly lose 3–4 marks** by:
> 1. Confusing **gas limit** (max willing to pay) with **gas used** (actually consumed).
> 2. Writing "Ethereum is Turing-complete" without the qualifier **"quasi-Turing-complete (gas-bounded)"** — this is a direct deduction of 1 mark in 2024 scheme valuation.
> 3. Forgetting that **`baseFee` is burned**, not paid to the validator.
> 4. Not mentioning the **Merkle Patricia Trie** when describing the world state — a 2-mark omission.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of the Ethereum World Computer with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the EVM and the Merkle Patricia Trie in maintaining the global world state. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the Ethereum State Transition Function $\sigma_{t+1} = \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$. With a suitable example, explain how gas limits the execution of smart contracts and prevents denial-of-service attacks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of the Ethereum World Computer (7 marks)**

The Ethereum World Computer is structured as a layered stack. **(1 mark)**

- **Execution Layer (EVM):** The Ethereum Virtual Machine is a 256-bit stack-based quasi-Turing-complete runtime. Every full node runs the EVM to execute smart-contract bytecode deterministically. **(1.5 marks)**
- **World State (Merkle Patricia Trie):** The world state $\sigma$ is stored as a deterministic, cryptographically authenticated trie. Each path from the root to a leaf corresponds to one account's `(nonce, balance, storage, code)` tuple. The **state root** is committed in each block header. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Consensus Layer (PoS Beacon Chain):** Validators are selected per slot to propose and attest to blocks. Finality is reached in ~12.8 minutes via Casper FFG. **(1 mark)**
- **Application Layer:** Smart contracts (Solidity/Vyper) and DApps (DeFi, NFTs, DAOs) sit on top. **(1 mark)**
- **Interface Layer:** Wallets (MetaMask) and web3 libraries (Ethers.js) let users interact. **(0.5 marks)**
- *Block diagram should be drawn.* **(0.5 marks)**

**(b) State Transition Function and Gas (7 marks)**

The state transition function is formally:

$$\sigma_{t+1} = \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$$

For a block with $n$ transactions:

$$\Upsilon(\sigma, B) = \big( \Upsilon(\sigma, T_1), T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n \big)$$

**(1 mark)** for the formula and its interpretation.

**Atomicity:** Every transaction either succeeds in its entirety or reverts all side effects. The EVM halts on any uncaught exception and reverts. **(1 mark)**

**Gas Mechanism:**
- Each opcode has a fixed gas cost (e.g., `ADD = 3`, `SSTORE = 20,000` for new value).
- The sender specifies `gasLimit` — the maximum they are willing to pay. **(1 mark)**
- Execution decrements a running gas counter:

$$
\text{counter} \leftarrow \text{counter} - G_{\text{op}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

- If `counter < 0` mid-execution, the EVM raises `OUT_OF_GAS` and **reverts** state but **keeps** the consumed gas. This makes DoS attacks prohibitively expensive. **(1.5 marks)**

**Example:** A loop iterating 10 million times where each iteration costs 100 gas would require $10^9$ gas — far exceeding the 30M block gas limit. The attacker must pay full price even if the computation aborts. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Compare the Ethereum World Computer with the Bitcoin system. Why is Ethereum considered a "world computer" while Bitcoin is not? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the role of Accounts (EOA and Contract) in Ethereum. With a worked example, compute the total transaction fee when the `baseFee` is 25 Gwei, `priorityTip` is 3 Gwei, and `gasUsed` is 85,000. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ethereum vs Bitcoin (7 marks)**

| Property | Bitcoin | Ethereum |
|---|---|---|
| Primary goal | Peer-to-peer cash | General-purpose decentralized computation |
| State model | UTXO (stateless) | Account-based (stateful) |
| Scripting | Bitcoin Script (not Turing) | **EVM (quasi-Turing)** |
| Block time | ~10 min | 12 s |
| Consensus | PoW | **PoS (post-Merge)** |
| Smart contracts | Limited (colored coins, BitVM) | **Native, first-class** |

**(4 marks)** for the table.

**Why Ethereum is a "World Computer":**
- It maintains a **persistent global state** (impossible for stateless UTXO chains).
- It supports **arbitrary computation** via EVM bytecode.
- Anyone can deploy **Turing-complete programs** that manipulate the world state.
- It functions as a *single, global, synchronized computer* rather than just a payment network.
**(3 marks)**

**(b) Accounts & Fee Calculation (7 marks)**

**Accounts (4 marks):**
- **EOA** — controlled by a private key. Has nonce, balance. No code, no storage. Initiates transactions.
- **Contract Account** — controlled by its bytecode. Has nonce, balance, code, and persistent 256-bit storage. Cannot initiate transactions on its own; reacts to incoming calls.
- Both account types are stored uniformly in the world state trie.

**Fee Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: `baseFee` = 25 Gwei, `tip` = 3 Gwei, `gasUsed` = 85,000.

**Step 1 — Effective gas price:**

$$
\text{effectiveGasPrice} = 25 + 3 = 28 \text{ Gwei} = 28 \times 10^9 \text{ Wei}
$$

**Step 2 — Total fee:**

$$
\text{TotalFee} = 85{,}000 \times 28 \times 10^9
$$

$$
= 2{,}380{,}000 \times 10^9 \text{ Wei}
$$

$$
= 2.38 \times 10^{18} \text{ Wei} = 2.38 \text{ ETH}
$$

**Step 3 — Burned vs. Tipped:**

- Burned: $85{,}000 \times 25 \times 10^9 = 2.125 \text{ ETH}$
- Tipped to validator: $85{,}000 \times 3 \times 10^9 = 0.255 \text{ ETH}$
- **Verification:** $2.125 + 0.255 = 2.380$ ETH ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep**

- **Definition:** Ethereum World Computer = decentralized, deterministic state machine running the EVM.
- **State:** $\sigma$ maps 20-byte addresses to $(n, b, s, c)$ tuples.
- **Transition:** $\sigma_{t+1} = \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$ — deterministic and atomic.
- **Two Account Types:** EOA (key-controlled) vs. Contract (code-controlled).
- **EVM Properties:** 256-bit stack, 1024 stack depth, quasi-Turing-complete (gas-bounded).
- **Gas Formula:** $\text{Fee} = \text{gasUsed} \times (\text{baseFee} + \text{tip})$; `baseFee` is **burned**.
- **Block Time:** **12 seconds** (post-Merge PoS).
- **Finality:** ~**12.8 minutes** (two epochs, Casper FFG).
- **Data Structure:** World state is a **Merkle Patricia Trie**; root stored in block header.
- **Intrinsic Gas:** $21{,}000 + 4 \times \text{nonZeroBytes} + 16 \times \text{zeroBytes}$ (+ 20,000 for contract creation).
- **DoS Resistance:** Gas-bounded execution sidesteps the halting problem and makes spam prohibitively expensive.
- **Ethereum vs Bitcoin:** Account-based + Turing-complete → "World Computer"; UTXO + non-Turing → "Digital Cash."
- **Wei ↔ ETH:** $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$; $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9 \text{ Wei}$.
- **Reverts:** Atomic — either all state changes apply or none do, but **gas is always consumed**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethereum Virtual Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ethereum Virtual Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is a **quasi-Turing-complete**, **decentralized**, **sandboxed runtime environment** that executes smart contract bytecode on every node participating in the Ethereum network. It is the **single canonical state machine** of Ethereum — every node runs the EVM to transition Ethereum from one valid global state to the next as a result of a transaction or message call.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the EVM is the **decentralized computational engine of the Ethereum blockchain** that enables deterministic execution of **smart contracts** (programs written in high-level languages such as **Solidity** or **Vyper**) compiled down to **EVM bytecode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Properties of the EVM (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> 1. **Quasi-Turing-Complete** — It is Turing-complete in theory, but practically bounded by the **gas limit**, which prevents infinite loops.
> 2. **Deterministic** — Given the same input and state, every EVM produces the exact same output. This is mandatory for consensus.
> 3. **Isolated / Sandboxed** — EVM code has **no access** to the network, file system, or other processes. It can only manipulate its own internal state.
> 4. **Stack-Based Architecture** — It uses a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack** of **256-bit words** (maximum depth of **1024**).
> 5. **256-bit Word Size** — It operates on **256-bit (32-byte) words** to natively handle cryptographic hashes and addresses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Global Distributed Computer"

Imagine a **gigantic, worldwide vending machine** that anyone in the world can drop a coin into (paying a **gas fee**), type a small program on its keypad (the **smart contract**), and watch it execute step by step. The catch is:

- **Thousands of identical copies** of this vending machine exist, all over the world.
- **Every single copy must agree** on the result of the program.
- If the program runs too long, the machine simply **refuses to keep going** (gas runs out) and refunds nothing.
- The machine cannot see the outside world — it can only read inputs that were inserted with the coin.

That vending machine is the **EVM**, the coin is the **transaction + gas**, the program is the **smart contract**, and the worldwide consensus on results is what makes Ethereum a **trustless world computer**.

## 1.3 Why the EVM Matters

> [!NOTE]  
> The EVM is the *reason* Ethereum is more than just a cryptocurrency. Bitcoin is a **decentralized ledger**; Ethereum is a **decentralized computer** because of the EVM. Any dApp (Decentralized Application) you build on Ethereum — DeFi, NFTs, DAOs — runs on the EVM.

**Standard Metrics (Mandatory to Memorize for KTU):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Stack depth | **1024 items** |
| Word size | **256 bits (32 bytes)** |
| Bytecode length cap | **24,576 bytes (EIP-170)** |
| Block gas limit | **30,000,000 (variable)** |
| Gas per EVM byte in transaction | **4 gas** |
| Gas for contract creation (CREATE) | **32,000 gas** |
| Gas for transaction itself | **21,000 gas** |
| Number of opcodes | **~150 (instrinsic + system)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EVM Architecture — The Four Pillars

The EVM is a **state machine** that consumes inputs (transactions) and produces outputs (state transitions). Internally, every execution context has access to:

### 1. **Stack (LIFO, Volatile)**
- 256-bit word stack, max **1024** elements deep.
- All arithmetic, logical, and comparison opcodes operate on stack items.
- PUSH1–PUSH32 push bytes onto the stack; POP removes the top item.
- **Access cost: 0 gas** (already accounted for in opcode cost).

### 2. **Memory (Volatile Byte Array)**
- A **byte-addressed, expandable, non-persistent** memory.
- Volatile — cleared after every message call.
- Expansion is **quadratic-cost** to prevent abuse.
- **Access cost: 3 gas** for every byte touched (read/write).

### 3. **Storage (Persistent Key-Value Store)**
- A **persistent word (256-bit) array** indexed by 256-bit keys.
- Maps to **Account Storage Trie** on-chain.
- **Most expensive** area to use.
- **Cost:**
  - `SSTORE` from zero to non-zero: **20,000 gas**
  - `SSTORE` modifying existing value: **5,000 gas**
  - `SLOAD`: **2,100 gas** (post-Berlin; cold) / **100 gas** (warm)
  - Clearing storage (non-zero → zero): **refund of up to 19,500 gas** (capped at gasUsed/2 since EIP-3529).

### 4. **Call Data (Read-Only Input Buffer)**
- Holds the transaction data or call parameters.
- One-time read access via `CALLDATALOAD`, `CALLDATASIZE`, `CALLDATACOPY`.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The rule of thumb for KTU:**  
> **Stack < Memory < Storage** in terms of gas cost. Use storage sparingly; it is the most expensive resource.

## 2.2 Execution Flow of a Transaction

Every EVM transaction follows this canonical pipeline:

1. **Transaction Validation** — Check signature, nonce, balance ≥ gas_limit × gas_price.
2. **Subtract gas prepayment** — `gasLimit × gasPrice` wei is deducted upfront.
3. **Load EVM context** — `pc=0`, gas available, address of recipient, msg.sender, msg.value.
4. **Decode bytecode opcodes** one by one.
5. **For each opcode:** pop operands → execute → push result → consume gas.
6. **On STOP / RETURN / REVERT / out-of-gas / invalid opcode:** halt.
7. **Settlement:** refund unused gas, send fees to miner/validator, persist new state to the **Merkle Patricia Trie**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transaction Fee** = `gasUsed × gasPrice` | Amount paid by `tx.origin` to miner/validator. | Wei |
| 2 | **Max Fee Cap** = `gasLimit × gasPrice` | Maximum wei a sender can lose. | Wei |
| 3 | **Refund** = `gasLimit − gasUsed` | Unused gas returned to sender. | Gas |
| 4 | **SSTORE (zero → non-zero)** = **20,000** | First write into a storage slot. | Gas |
| 5 | **SSTORE (non-zero → non-zero)** = **5,000** | Update an existing slot. | Gas |
| 6 | **SSTORE refund cap** = **gasUsed / 2** (EIP-3529) | Limits storage-clearing refunds. | Gas |
| 7 | **Memory expansion cost** = `3·n + ⌊n²/512⌋` where `n` is words (32 bytes) | Quadratic cost of memory. | Gas |
| 8 | **Base transaction cost** = **21,000** | Flat cost of any transaction. | Gas |
| 9 | **Contract creation** = **32,000** | Flat cost on top of 21,000 for `CREATE`. | Gas |
| 10 | **Call value transfer** = **9,000** (pre-Berlin) / **2,300** (post-EIP-2929, warm) | Extra cost if value is sent. | Gas |
| 11 | **EVM Word size** | All stack/memory/storage ops. | 256 bits |
| 12 | **Stack max depth** | Causes `STACK_TOO_DEEP` error. | 1024 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EVM is not just academic — it powers:
- **DeFi (Decentralized Finance):** Aave, Uniswap, Compound.
- **NFTs:** ERC-721 and ERC-1155 token standards.
- **DAOs:** Governance contracts that vote on proposals.
- **Layer-2 scaling:** Optimistic Rollups (Arbitrum, Optimism) and ZK-Rollups (zkSync) are essentially EVM-compatible environments.
- **EVM compatibility** has spawned **Polygon, BNB Chain, Avalanche C-Chain, Fantom**, all running the EVM.

> [!NOTE]  
> **"EVM-compatible"** means a chain implements the same opcode set and state-transition function, allowing Solidity contracts to be deployed with little or no modification. This is the de-facto standard for smart-contract platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **complete gas-cost derivation** of a sample smart-contract function execution and the **deterministic EVM state-transition** logic — both are recurring KTU 14-mark question patterns.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: EVM Components Comparison

| Component | Type | Volatility | Read Cost | Write Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stack** | LIFO (max 1024) | Per-opcode | 0 (combined) | 0 (combined) | Arithmetic, local variables |
| **Memory** | Byte array | Per call | **3 gas/byte** | **3 gas/byte** | Temporary buffers, `string`/`bytes` |
| **Storage** | 256-bit KV map | Persistent (trie) | **2,100 (cold) / 100 (warm)** | **20,000 (new) / 5,000 (update)** | State variables, mappings |
| **Call Data** | Read-only bytes | Per call | **3–6 gas/byte** | N/A | Function arguments, `msg.data` |
| **Code** | Bytecode | Persistent (trie) | Special `CODESIZE`, `CODECOPY` | N/A | Contract logic (immutable) |
| **Logs** | Bloom-filtered | Persistent | 375 + 375/topic + 8/byte | N/A | Events (cheaper than storage) |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Gas Cost of a Sample Transaction

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark Part (a)):**  
A user deploys a contract that writes one new storage slot (`uint256 x = 42`) and emits one event with 1 topic and 32 bytes of data, during a cold transaction (EIP-2929 model). Compute:
1. Intrinsic gas.
2. Execution gas.
3. Total gas used.
4. Total fee in Gwei, given `gasPrice = 50 Gwei`.

### Step 1 — Identify every gas-consuming operation

| Operation | Cost (gas) |
|---|---|
| Base transaction | **21,000** |
| Contract creation (CREATE) | **+32,000** |
| Call data cost (1 non-zero byte ≈ 16 gas after EIP-2028; assume method selector + args = 0 here for clarity) | **0–68** |
| **SSTORE** zero → non-zero (one slot) | **20,000** |
| **LOG1** opcode (1 topic, 32 bytes data) | 375 (log) + 375 (1 topic) + 8 × 32 (data) = **1,006** |
| Cold account access surcharge (post-EIP-2929) | **+2,500** (for recipient) |

### Step 2 — Sum intrinsic gas

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{intrinsic}} &= G_{\text{base}} + G_{\text{create}} + G_{\text{coldAccess}} \\
&= 21{,}000 + 32{,}000 + 2{,}500 \\
&= 55{,}500 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum execution gas

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{execute}} &= G_{\text{SSTORE}} + G_{\text{LOG1}} \\
&= 20{,}000 + 1{,}006 \\
&= 21{,}006 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Total gas used

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{total}} &= G_{\text{intrinsic}} + G_{\text{execute}} \\
&= 55{,}500 + 21{,}006 \\
&= 76{,}506 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Total fee in Gwei

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fee}_{\text{wei}} &= G_{\text{total}} \times \text{gasPrice} \\
&= 76{,}506 \times 50 \times 10^9 \\
&= 3.8253 \times 10^{15} \text{ wei} \\
&= 3{,}825.3 \text{ Gwei} \\
&\approx 0.0000038253 \text{ ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conversion reminders** (always use these in KTU answers):  
> `1 ETH = 10^18 wei`  
> `1 Gwei = 10^9 wei`  
> `1 ETH = 10^9 Gwei`

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: EVM State-Transition Function

The EVM is formally defined as a deterministic transition function:

$$
\sigma_{t+1} \equiv \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)
$$

where  
- $\sigma_t$ = the **Ethereum world state** at block height $t$,  
- $T$ = a **valid transaction**,  
- $\Upsilon$ = the **EVM state-transition function**.

This produces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{t+1} &= (\, \text{NewStateRoot},\; \text{TxIndex} + 1,\; \text{TxHash},\; \text{Logs},\; \text{Status}\,)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Working of $\Upsilon$ — Step by Step:**

1. **Check transaction format & signature** using `ecrecover`.
2. **Compute intrinsic gas**: $G_0 = 21{,}000 + G_{\text{data}} + G_{\text{create}} + G_{\text{cold}}$.
3. **Deduct prepayment**: $\text{balance}[\text{tx.origin}] \mathrel{-}= G_{\text{limit}} \times \text{gasPrice}$.
4. **Increment nonce**: $\text{nonce}[\text{tx.origin}] \mathrel{+}= 1$.
5. **Subtract value**: $\text{balance}[\text{from}] \mathrel{-}= \text{value}$, $\text{balance}[\text{to}] \mathrel{+}= \text{value}$.
6. **Execute bytecode** if recipient is a contract: loop on opcodes until `STOP`, `RETURN`, `REVERT`, `SELFDESTRUCT`, or `OOG`.
7. **Handle failure modes:**
   - `REVERT` → refund unused gas, but **consume all gas used up to revert point**; state is rolled back.
   - `Out-of-Gas` → consume **all** gas; state rolled back; transaction fails.
   - Invalid opcode / stack underflow → consume **all** gas.
8. **Refund unused gas** (subject to EIP-3529 cap = gasUsed / 2).
9. **Pay fee to miner/validator**: $\text{balance}[\text{miner}] \mathrel{+}= G_{\text{used}} \times \text{gasPrice}$.
10. **Return new state root** $\sigma_{t+1}$.

## 3.4 Opcodes Classification (KTU-Favorite Question)

EVM opcodes are typically grouped as:

| Category | Examples | Typical Gas |
|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic** | ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, ADDMOD, MULMOD | 3–8 |
| **Comparison** | LT, GT, EQ, ISZERO | 3 |
| **Bitwise** | AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHL, SHR, SAR | 3 |
| **SHA3 / Keccak** | SHA3 | 30 + 6/word |
| **Environmental** | ADDRESS, BALANCE, ORIGIN, CALLER, CALLVALUE | 2–700 (BEGINSUB to RETURNSUB) |
| **Block info** | BLOCKHASH, COINBASE, TIMESTAMP, NUMBER, DIFFICULTY | 2–800 |
| **Stack/Memory/Storage** | MLOAD, MSTORE, SLOAD, SSTORE | 3–20,000 |
| **Push/Pop/Dup/Swap** | PUSH1–PUSH32, DUP1–DUP16, SWAP1–SWAP16, POP | 3 |
| **Control flow** | JUMP, JUMPI, JUMPDEST, PC, GAS | 1–8 |
| **Logging** | LOG0, LOG1, LOG2, LOG3, LOG4 | 375 + 375×topics + 8×bytes |
| **System** | CREATE, CALL, CALLCODE, DELEGATECALL, STATICCALL, SELFDESTRUCT, RETURN, REVERT | 100–40,000 |

---
