# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define the Ethereum Virtual Machine. List any four of its key properties.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is a decentralized, **quasi-Turing-complete**, **stack-based** runtime environment that executes smart-contract bytecode on every Ethereum node and acts as the single canonical state-transition function of the Ethereum network.

**Four key properties:**
1. **Deterministic** — Same input + same state = same output on every node.
2. **Sandboxed** — No access to network, file system, or external processes.
3. **Stack-based with 256-bit words** — Max stack depth of 1024; operations are 256-bit.
4. **Gas-metered** — Every opcode consumes a fixed gas amount, preventing infinite loops and DoS.

**Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for properties (0.5 each).

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between EVM Stack, Memory, and Storage.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Stack | Memory | Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | LIFO (1024 × 256-bit) | Volatile byte array | Persistent key-value map |
| **Persistence** | Lost every opcode | Lost every call | Permanent (on-chain) |
| **Read cost** | 0 (combined) | 3 gas/byte | 2,100 gas (cold) |
| **Write cost** | 0 (combined) | 3 gas/byte | 20,000 / 5,000 gas |
| **Purpose** | Local variables, arithmetic | Buffers, strings, ABI decoding | State variables, mappings |

**Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark per correct differentiation row (or 1 for tabular + 2 for explanation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting the word "quasi-Turing-complete"** — saying "Turing-complete" alone costs 1 mark. Always write "**quasi**-Turing-complete, bounded by gas".
> 2. **Confusing gas limit and gas price** — `gasLimit` is in **gas**, `gasPrice` is in **wei/gas**. Fees are products.
> 3. **Writing "Bitcoin is Turing-complete"** — it is **intentionally NOT** Turing-complete (scripting is non-Turing).
> 4. **Forgetting the 256-bit word size** — a specific KTU-favorite fact.
> 5. **Skipping the gas refund cap** (EIP-3529 = gasUsed/2). Examiners deduct 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the architecture of the EVM with a neat block diagram. Describe each of the four execution components in detail. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

The **EVM architecture** consists of four primary execution components that are accessible during smart-contract execution:

**1. Stack (Volatile LIFO):**
- A 256-bit-wide LIFO data structure with a **maximum depth of 1024** items.
- All arithmetic (`ADD`, `MUL`, `SUB`, `DIV`), bitwise (`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`), and comparison (`LT`, `GT`, `EQ`) opcodes operate by popping operands from the top and pushing results back.
- PUSH1–PUSH32 push 1 to 32 bytes; DUP1–DUP16 duplicate stack items; SWAP1–SWAP16 swap with the i-th item.
- **No explicit gas cost** for stack access — gas is bundled with the opcode.

**2. Memory (Volatile Byte Array):**
- A **byte-addressed, expandable, non-persistent** linear memory.
- Reset to **zero** at the start of every message call.
- Reads and writes are achieved via `MLOAD` (32 bytes) and `MSTORE` (32 bytes), and `MSIZE` returns its current size in bytes.
- Memory expansion is **quadratic-cost** to prevent quadratic-blow-up attacks.
- Access cost: **3 gas per byte touched**.

**3. Storage (Persistent Key-Value):**
- A **persistent word (256-bit) array** indexed by 256-bit keys, mapped to the **Account Storage Trie**.
- Read via `SLOAD`; written via `SSTORE`.
- **SSTORE from zero → non-zero: 20,000 gas**; non-zero → non-zero: 5,000 gas; SLOAD (cold): 2,100 gas (post EIP-2929).
- Clearing storage (`SSTORE` from non-zero to zero) gives a **refund of up to 19,500 gas**, capped at `gasUsed / 2` (EIP-3529).
- **This is the most expensive resource** — use it sparingly.

**4. Call Data (Read-Only Input Buffer):**
- Holds the **transaction payload** or **call parameters**.
- Accessed via `CALLDATALOAD`, `CALLDATASIZE`, `CALLDATACOPY`.
- Used by Solidity to decode function selectors (first 4 bytes) and arguments.
- Cost: ~**16 gas per non-zero byte** (post EIP-2028); ~4 gas per zero byte.

**5. (Bonus) Program Counter, Gas Counter, Logs:**  
- **PC** tracks the current opcode position; **gas counter** tracks remaining gas; **logs** are written via `LOG0`–`LOG4` for emitting events.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
            ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
            │     EVM EXECUTION ENGINE         │
            │                                  │
            │   [Program Counter]   [Gas]      │
            └──────────────────────────────────┘
                │       │        │       │
            ┌───▼─┐ ┌──▼──┐ ┌──▼──┐ ┌──▼──┐
            │STACK│ │ MEM │ │STORE│ │CALLC│
            │ 1024│ │byte │ │ KV  │ │DATA │
            │×256 │ │array│ │ trie│ │ RO  │
            └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘
```

**Mark split (7 marks):**  
- 2 marks: Block diagram  
- 5 marks: Description of all 4 components (≈ 1.25 each)

---

#### **(b) Compute the total gas consumed and total transaction fee for the following scenario. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A user calls a contract function that:
- Reads 2 cold storage slots (`SLOAD`).
- Writes to 1 storage slot (changing from non-zero to a different non-zero value).
- Sends 2 Ether (`msg.value`).
- Emits one `LOG1` event with 2 topics and 64 bytes of data.
- `gasPrice = 30 Gwei`.

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Operation | Gas |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base transaction | **21,000** |
| 2 | Cold account access (post EIP-2929) | **2,500** (recipient) + 2 × 2,500 (storage) = **7,500** |
| 3 | `SLOAD` × 2 (cold) | 2 × 2,100 = **4,200** |
| 4 | `SSTORE` (non-zero → non-zero) | **5,000** |
| 5 | `LOG1` (2 topics, 64 bytes data) | 375 + 2 × 375 + 64 × 8 = **1,640** |

**Total gas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{total}} &= 21{,}000 + 7{,}500 + 4{,}200 + 5{,}000 + 1{,}640 \\
&= 39{,}340 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total fee:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fee}_{\text{wei}} &= 39{,}340 \times 30 \times 10^9 \\
&= 1.1802 \times 10^{15} \text{ wei} \\
&= 1{,}180.2 \text{ Gwei} \\
&= 0.0000011802 \text{ ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split (7 marks):** 1 for identifying each operation (5 × 0.4 ≈ 2 marks), 2 for summation, 2 for fee conversion (showing the unit ladder is mandatory).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

#### **(a) What is the EVM state-transition function? Describe the complete step-by-step execution of an Ethereum transaction. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The **EVM state-transition function** is the deterministic function:

$$
\sigma_{t+1} \equiv \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)
$$

where $\sigma_t$ is the Ethereum world state at block $t$, $T$ is a valid transaction, and $\Upsilon$ is the EVM transition function.

**Step-by-step execution of a transaction:**

1. **Transaction Validation:** Check that the RLP-encoded transaction is well-formed, that the signature is valid (using `ecrecover`), and that the nonce matches the sender's current nonce.

2. **Gas Prepayment:** Compute the **intrinsic gas** $= 21{,}000 + G_{\text{data}} + G_{\text{create}}$. Subtract `gasLimit × gasPrice` from `tx.origin` balance. Transaction fails if balance is insufficient.

3. **Increment Nonce:** `nonce[tx.origin] += 1` to prevent replay.

4. **Value Transfer:** `balance[from] -= value`; `balance[to] += value`.

5. **Bytecode Execution:** If `to` is empty, run `CREATE` (deploy new contract); otherwise load recipient's bytecode. Initialize `pc=0`, set `gas` to `gasLimit − intrinsicGas`.

6. **Opcode Loop:** While `pc < code.length` and `gas > 0`:
   - Fetch opcode byte.
   - Subtract its gas cost.
   - Execute (pop/push stack, read/write memory/storage, etc.).
   - Increment `pc` (or jump via `JUMP`/`JUMPI`).

7. **Termination Conditions:**
   - `STOP` / `RETURN` → success.
   - `REVERT` → refund unused gas, but state is rolled back.
   - `Out-of-Gas` / invalid opcode → consume **all** gas, fail.
   - `SELFDESTRUCT` → destroy contract, send balance to target.

8. **Refund Calculation:** Apply storage-clearing refunds, capped at `gasUsed / 2` (EIP-3529).

9. **Miner Payment:** `balance[miner] += (gasUsed − refunds) × gasPrice`.

10. **State Commitment:** Update the **State Trie** and emit a **transaction receipt** containing `status`, `cumulativeGasUsed`, `logsBloom`, and `logs[]`.

**Mark split (7 marks):** 2 for the state-transition formula, 5 for the steps (≈ 0.5 each).

---

#### **(b) Compare EVM with Bitcoin's transaction scripting language. Why is the EVM preferred for dApp development? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Bitcoin Script | EVM |
|---|---|---|
| **Turing-completeness** | Intentionally **NOT** Turing-complete | **Quasi**-Turing-complete (gas-bounded) |
| **Loop support** | No (no `JUMP` for cycles) | Yes (via `JUMP`/`JUMPI`) |
| **State model** | UTXO (Unspent Transaction Outputs) | Account-based (nonce, balance, code, storage) |
| **Word size** | 4 bytes (32-bit integers) | 32 bytes (256-bit integers) |
| **Stack** | Yes (max 520-byte push) | Yes (max 1024 × 256-bit) |
| **Opcodes** | ~80 (mostly crypto + simple logic) | ~150 (arithmetic, memory, storage, system) |
| **Gas / fee** | No gas; fees by tx size | **Gas-metered** per opcode |
| **Contract capability** | Limited (Bitcoin Script, TapScript) | Full **smart contracts** (Solidity, Vyper) |
| **Storage model** | No persistent storage | **Persistent Storage Trie** |
| **Failure handling** | Whole tx fails | `REVERT` (saves gas up to failure) |
| **Libraries / Reuse** | None | Library calls, DELEGATECALL, libraries |
| **DApp ecosystem** | Limited (RGB, Stacks) | Massive (DeFi, NFT, DAO) |

**Why EVM is preferred for dApps:**

1. **Turing-completeness** enables arbitrarily complex business logic (lending, swaps, voting).
2. **Persistent state** allows multi-step applications to remember data between calls.
3. **Account model** is intuitive for dApp developers (similar to OO programming).
4. **Rich standard library** through **ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155** standards.
5. **Vibrant tooling:** Truffle, Hardhat, Remix, OpenZeppelin, Ethers.js, Web3.js.
6. **EVM compatibility** across chains (Polygon, BNB Chain, Avalanche) means **one codebase, multiple deployments**.
7. **Predictable failure** via `REVERT` and explicit error messages.
8. **Composability:** "Money legos" — every dApp can call every other dApp on-chain.

**Mark split (7 marks):** 4 for the table (each row ≈ 0.5), 2 for "why preferred" bullets, 1 for conclusion.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 3 (EVM)**

- ✅ **EVM = quasi-Turing-complete, stack-based, 256-bit, deterministic, sandboxed** state machine.
- ✅ Word size = **256 bits**; Stack depth = **1024**; Bytecode limit = **24,576 bytes** (EIP-170).
- ✅ Stack < Memory < Storage **in terms of gas cost** — always prefer stack and memory over storage.
- ✅ **Base transaction = 21,000 gas**; **Contract creation = +32,000**; **Cold account access = +2,500** (EIP-2929).
- ✅ `SSTORE` zero → non-zero = **20,000**; non-zero → non-zero = **5,000**; refund cap = **gasUsed/2** (EIP-3529).
- ✅ EVM has **NO access** to network, file system, clock, or any external resource — it is fully sandboxed.
- ✅ `REVERT` consumes gas used but rolls back state; `Out-of-Gas` consumes **all** gas.
- ✅ Smart contracts are written in **Solidity** / **Vyper** and compiled to **EVM bytecode** (via Yul/IR).
- ✅ **EVM-compatible chains** (Polygon, BNB, Avalanche) reuse Ethereum's smart-contract ecosystem.
- ✅ Determinism is the **foundational property** — without it, blockchain consensus is impossible.
- ✅ Every Ethereum node runs the **same EVM** to verify that the new state root matches.
- ✅ The EVM is **quasi**-Turing-complete, not Turing-complete — the **gas mechanism** makes it bounded.
- ✅ **Logs (events)** are cheaper than storage for data that does not need to be read on-chain by contracts.
- ✅ Ethereum's **Account Model** (EOA + Contract) is the trigger for the EVM; **Bitcoin's UTXO Model** is not.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, **always draw a block diagram** of the EVM architecture — it earns 2 easy marks.

> **Mnemonic to recall the four EVM execution areas: "S M S C" → Stack, Memory, Storage, Call Data.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethereum Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ethereum Network # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Ethereum** is an open-source, decentralized, **Turing-complete** blockchain platform introduced by **Vitalik Buterin** in late **2013** (whitepaper) and launched on **July 30, 2015**. It extends the core innovation of Bitcoin (a peer-to-peer digital currency) into a generalized programmable blockchain, enabling the deployment of **smart contracts** and **decentralized applications (DApps)** through its runtime environment called the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**. The native cryptocurrency of the Ethereum network is **Ether (ETH)**, which acts as: 1. **Digital Currency** – A tradable cryptographic token. 2. **Gas Payment** – Used to compensate network validators for executing transactions and smart contract operations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ethereum is NOT just a cryptocurrency** — it is a **decentralized computing platform** (often called "World Computer"). Bitcoin is a *decentralized ledger*; Ethereum is a *decentralized state machine*. ## 1.2 The "Why" Behind Ethereum: Conceptual Analogy **Real-World Analogy — The Smartphone Analogy:** Imagine the early days of mobile phones: - **Nokia Phones (Bitcoin):** Could only do ONE specific job — make calls. Hard-coded, single-purpose hardware. - **Smartphones with App Store (Ethereum):** A general-purpose device where anyone can build and install applications (DApps) on top of a shared global infrastructure (the EVM). Just as the iPhone transformed the phone from a single-purpose device into a programmable platform, **Ethereum transformed blockchain from a single-purpose payment system into a programmable global computer**. **Another Analogy — "The Decentralized Vending Machine":** A smart contract is like a vending machine. You insert money (ETH/gas), select an item (function call), and the machine automatically dispenses the product (output). There is no human cashier, no possibility of cheating, and the rules are baked into the code. Ethereum is the network of millions of these vending machines operating in consensus. ## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) | |------|-------------------------------| | **EVM** | Ethereum Virtual Machine – the runtime sandbox executing smart contract bytecode on every node | | **Smart Contract** | Self-executing program with terms written directly into code, deployed on the blockchain | | **Gas** | Unit measuring computational effort required to execute operations on Ethereum | | **Wei** | Smallest denomination of ETH. **1 ETH = 10¹⁸ Wei** | | **Gwei** | Commonly used gas price unit. **1 Gwei = 10⁹ Wei** | | **EOA** | Externally Owned Account – controlled by a private key (human user) | | **Contract Account** | Account holding smart contract code, controlled by code logic (not a private key) | | **DApp** | Decentralized Application – frontend + smart contracts on blockchain | | **Solidity** | Primary high-level, statically-typed language for writing Ethereum smart contracts | | **difficulty** | A scalar value controlling block production difficulty under PoW (legacy) | ## 1.4 Ethereum vs Bitcoin — At a Glance > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparison is a **favourite KTU question** and must be memorised. | Parameter | Bitcoin | Ethereum | |-----------|---------|----------| | **Primary Purpose** | Peer-to-peer digital cash | Decentralized application platform | | **Turing-Complete?** | ❌ No (intentionally limited scripting) | ✅ Yes (EVM is Turing-complete) | | **Consensus (Pre-Merge)** | Proof of Work (SHA-256) | Proof of Work (Ethash) | | **Consensus (Post-Merge)** | Proof of Work (still) | **Proof of Stake (Beacon Chain)** | | **Block Time** | ~10 minutes | ~12 seconds (post-Merge) | | **Native Token** | BTC (21M cap) | ETH (no hard cap; EIP-1559 burn mechanism) | | **Language** | Bitcoin Script (limited) | **Solidity, Vyper, Yul** | | **Smart Contracts** | Limited (Bitcoin Script) | First-class citizen | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ethereum Architecture — Layered View The Ethereum network can be decomposed into **four logical layers**: ### Layer 1: Data Layer (The Blockchain Itself) - A **Merkle Patricia Trie**-based data structure storing the global **state**. - Each block contains: parent hash, state root, transactions root, receipts root, and the list of transactions. ### Layer 2: Network Layer (P2P Protocol) - Uses the **devp2p** protocol stack. - Nodes communicate via a **gossip protocol** (similar to Bitcoin). - Discovery protocol: **Kademlia DHT**. ### Layer 3: Consensus Layer (Post-Merge — Beacon Chain) - **Proof of Stake (PoS)** since **The Merge (September 15, 2022)**. - Validators stake **32 ETH** to participate in block proposal and attestation. - Finality achieved through **Casper FFG** (Friendly Finality Gadget). ### Layer 4: Application Layer (EVM + Smart Contracts) - The **EVM** executes **bytecode** instructions. - Smart contracts are deployed with `CREATE` opcode and invoked via transactions or `CALL` opcode. ## 2.2 Ethereum Account Model (Critical Concept) Ethereum uses an **account-based model** (unlike Bitcoin's UTXO model). > [!IMPORTANT] > **There are exactly TWO types of accounts in Ethereum:** ### 2.2.1 Externally Owned Account (EOA) - **Controlled by**: a private key (held by a human user via wallet like MetaMask). - **Has**: An address, ETH balance, **nonce** (transaction counter). - **Cannot**: Contain executable code. - **Can**: Initiate transactions (sign with private key). ### 2.2.2 Contract Account - **Controlled by**: the code stored at the account (no private key). - **Has**: Address, ETH balance, nonce, **associated storage and code**. - **Cannot**: start a transaction on its own; only reacts to calls/transactions. - **Created by**: `CREATE` opcode from an EOA (or another contract). **Account State Representation:** $$ \text{Account} = (n, \ \sigma, \ c, \ \text{code}) $$ Where: - $n$ = **nonce** (number of transactions sent / contracts created) - $\sigma$ = **balance** (in Wei) - $c$ = **storage root** (root of the account's storage trie) - $\text{code}$ = **contract bytecode hash** (only for contract accounts) ## 2.3 Gas Mechanism — The Fee Market > [!IMPORTANT] > The **EIP-1559** upgrade (London Hard Fork, August 2021) reformed the Ethereum fee market. **Transaction Fee Formula (Post-EIP-1559):** $$ \text{Total Fee} = \text{Gas Units Used} \times (\text{Base Fee} + \text{Tip}) $$ Where: - **Gas Units Used** = Computational steps required (e.g., simple ETH transfer ≈ **21,000 gas**). - **Base Fee** = Algorithmically determined by network congestion; **burned** (destroyed) — reducing ETH supply. - **Tip (Priority Fee)** = Optional incentive paid to the validator. **Block Gas Limit:** $$ L_{\text{block}} = \text{Maximum total gas allowed in a single block (target: 30M gas)} $$ **Block fullness adjustment (EIP-1559):** $$ \text{Base Fee}_{n+1} = \text{Base Fee}_n \times \left( 1 + \frac{\text{Used Gas} - \text{Target Gas}}{\text{Target Gas}} \cdot \delta \right) $$ Where $\delta$ is a bounded constant (max 12.5% change per block). ## 2.4 Ether Denominations — Official Table > [!NOTE] > Memorize this — KTU loves asking unit conversions (especially **Wei ↔ Gwei ↔ ETH**). | Unit | Wei Equivalent | Scientific | |------|----------------|------------| | **Wei** | 1 Wei | $10^{0}$ | | **Kwei (Babbage)** | 1,000 Wei | $10^{3}$ | | **Mwei (Lovelace)** | 1,000,000 Wei | $10^{6}$ | | **Gwei (Shannon)** | 1,000,000,000 Wei | $10^{9}$ | | **Microether (Szabo)** | $10^{12}$ Wei | $10^{12}$ | | **Milliether (Finney)** | $10^{15}$ Wei | $10^{15}$ | | **Ether (ETH)** | $10^{18}$ Wei | $10^{18}$ | ## 2.5 The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) — Operational Theory The **EVM** is a **quasi-Turing-complete** stack-based 256-bit virtual machine. The "quasi" caveat is due to the **gas limit**, which practically bounds computation. **EVM Properties:** 1. **Deterministic** — Same input → Same output on every node. 2. **Isolated (Sandboxed)** — Code cannot access network, filesystem, or other contracts' storage without explicit calls. 3. **Stack-based** — Operates on a stack of **256-bit words**. 4. **Globally Distributed** — Same code executed by every full node. **EVM Execution Cycle:** $$ \text{Transaction} \rightarrow \text{Pre-execution State} \rightarrow \text{EVM Processing} \rightarrow \text{Post-execution State} $$ **Opcodes Examples:** - `STOP` (0x00) — halts execution - `ADD` (0x01) — top two stack items sum - `CALL` (0xF1) — message call to another contract - `CREATE` (0xF0) — deploy a new contract - `SSTORE` / `SLOAD` — persistent storage read/write (most expensive: 20,000 gas for new slot) ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference) | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes | |---------|-----------------|--------------| | ETH ↔ Wei | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$ | Smallest indivisible unit | | ETH ↔ Gwei | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{9} \text{ Gwei}$ | Gas pricing unit | | Simple Transfer Gas | $21{,}000 \text{ gas}$ | Fixed cost of ETH transfer | | Contract Deployment Gas | $\geq 53{,}000 \text{ gas}$ | + 200 gas per byte of code | | Total Tx Fee (EIP-1559) | $\text{GasUsed} \times (\text{BaseFee} + \text{Tip})$ | Result in Wei | | Validator Stake | $32 \text{ ETH}$ | Required to run validator node | | Block Time (post-Merge) | $\approx 12 \text{ seconds}$ | Slot duration | | Epoch (post-Merge) | $32 \text{ slots} = 6 \text{ min } 24 \text{ s}$ | One finality checkpoint cycle | | EVM Word Size | $256 \text{ bits}$ | Native integer / hash size | | Max Base Fee Change | $\pm 12.5\% \text{ per block}$ | EIP-1559 bound | | Beacon Chain Slot | $12 \text{ s}$ | Time for one block | | Finality Delay | $\approx 12.8 \text{ minutes}$ | Two epochs (Casper FFG) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The gas mechanism is essentially a **computational market** — analogous to cloud computing billing (AWS Lambda charges per 100ms execution). It prevents Denial-of-Service attacks by making every opcode have a *real economic cost*, thus ensuring Ethereum can run as a **trustless global computer** for DeFi, NFTs, DAOs, and decentralized identity systems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting Between Ether Denominations > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 frequently asks:** *"A user pays 0.002 Gwei as a tip. How much is that in Wei and ETH?"* ### Problem Setup Given: $\text{Tip} = 0.002 \text{ Gwei}$. Find equivalent in Wei and ETH. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Starting from the unit relationship: $$ 1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^{9} \text{ Wei} $$ To convert 0.002 Gwei to Wei, multiply by $10^{9}$: $$ \text{Tip in Wei} = 0.002 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} $$ $$ \text{Tip in Wei} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} $$ $$ \text{Tip in Wei} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Wei} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ Wei} $$ Now converting to ETH using $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$: $$ \text{Tip in ETH} = \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{10^{18}} \text{ ETH} $$ $$ \text{Tip in ETH} = 2 \times 10^{6-18} \text{ ETH} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Tip in ETH} = 2 \times 10^{-12} \text{ ETH} } $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Transaction Fee Calculation (EIP-1559) ### Problem Setup A user executes a smart contract interaction that uses **85,000 gas**. The current **base fee** is **35 Gwei** and the user adds a **priority tip of 3 Gwei**. Compute the total transaction fee in **ETH**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Identify variables. $$ \text{GasUsed} = 85{,}000 \text{ gas} $$ $$ \text{BaseFee} = 35 \text{ Gwei} = 35 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} $$ $$ \text{Tip} = 3 \text{ Gwei} = 3 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the EIP-1559 fee formula. $$ \text{TotalFee} = \text{GasUsed} \times (\text{BaseFee} + \text{Tip}) $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values. $$ \text{TotalFee} = 85{,}000 \times (35 \times 10^{9} + 3 \times 10^{9}) \text{ Wei} $$ **Step 4:** Add the gas price components. $$ \text{TotalFee} = 85{,}000 \times 38 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} $$ **Step 5:** Multiply. $$ \text{TotalFee} = 3{,}230{,}000 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} $$ $$ \text{TotalFee} = 3.23 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei} $$ **Step 6:** Convert to ETH using $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$. $$ \text{TotalFee in ETH} = \frac{3.23 \times 10^{15}}{10^{18}} \text{ ETH} $$ $$ \text{TotalFee in ETH} = 3.23 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{TotalFee} = 0.00323 \text{ ETH} } $$ **Note:** Of this fee, the **base fee portion** ($85{,}000 \times 35 \times 10^{9} = 2.975 \times 10^{15}$ Wei) is **burned**, removing ETH from supply. The **tip** ($85{,}000 \times 3 \times 10^{9} = 2.55 \times 10^{14}$ Wei) goes to the validator. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: EIP-1559 Base Fee Adjustment ### Problem Setup A block has a **target gas** of **15,000,000** and a **base fee** of **20 Gwei**. The actual **used gas** was **18,000,000** (congested). Compute the **new base fee** for the next block. (Assume $\delta = 12.5\%$ maximum — but for small differences, the linear formula applies.) ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Write the adjustment formula. $$ \text{BaseFee}_{n+1} = \text{BaseFee}_n \times \left( 1 + \frac{\text{Used Gas} - \text{Target Gas}}{\text{Target Gas}} \right) $$ **Step 2:** Compute the ratio (parentheses term). $$ r = 1 + \frac{18{,}000{,}000 - 15{,}000{,}000}{15{,}000{,}000} $$ $$ r = 1 + \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{15{,}000{,}000} $$ $$ r = 1 + 0.20 = 1.20 $$ **Step 3:** Check the 12.5% bound. $$ \text{Max new fee} = 20 \times 1.125 = 22.5 \text{ Gwei} $$ $$ 20 \times 1.20 = 24 \text{ Gwei} \rightarrow \text{Exceeds 12.5\% cap!} $$ Therefore, the bound applies: $$ \text{BaseFee}_{n+1} = 20 \times 1.125 = 22.5 \text{ Gwei} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{New Base Fee} = 22.5 \text{ Gwei} } $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: EVM Opcode Gas Costs (Most Tested) | Opcode | Description | Gas Cost | |--------|-------------|----------| | `ADD` | Addition | **3** gas | | `MUL` | Multiplication | **5** gas | | `SSTORE` (zero → non-zero) | Write to new storage slot | **20,000** gas | | `SSTORE` (modify) | Update existing slot | **5,000** gas | | `SLOAD` | Read from storage | **2,100** (post-Berlin) / 800 (warm) | | `CALL` (warm) | Call to pre-accessed contract | **100** gas | | `CALL` (cold) | Call to new contract | **2,600** gas | | `CREATE` | Deploy new contract | **32,000** + tx cost | | `BALANCE` | Check account balance | **700** gas (cold) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** This is why Solidity developers pack multiple variables into a single 256-bit storage slot (**struct packing**). It can save thousands of gas, directly translating to lower transaction fees for users. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Ethereum Network Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB User[User with EOA Wallet
MetaMask Ledger] TxPool[Ethereum Mempool
Pending Transactions] Validator[Validator Node
Staked 32 ETH] EVM[Ethereum Virtual Machine
EVM Bytecode Runtime] SC1[Smart Contract A
Deployed Bytecode] SC2[Smart Contract B
Deployed Bytecode] Beacon[Beacon Chain
Consensus Layer PoS] State[Global State Trie
Merkle Patricia Tree] Block[Block N
Header + Tx List] User -- Signs and broadcasts TX --> TxPool TxPool -- Selected by proposer --> Validator Validator -- Executes TX --> EVM EVM -- CALL opcode --> SC1 EVM -- CALL opcode --> SC2 SC1 -- State update --> State SC2 -- State update --> State Beacon -- Assigns proposer and attesters --> Validator Validator -- Proposes new block --> Block Block -- Contains stateRoot --> State Block -- Finalized via Casper FFG --> Beacon ``` **Caption:** *A transaction flows from a user's EOA, sits in the mempool, gets selected by a PoS validator, runs through the EVM, triggers smart contract execution, updates the global state, and is finally sealed into a new block on the beacon chain.* ## 4.2 EOA vs Contract Account — Functional Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A[EOA Account] A1[Address 20 bytes] A2[Balance in Wei] A3[Nonce Transaction Count] A4[No Code Hash empty] A5[Controlled by Private Key] end subgraph B[Contract Account] B1[Address 20 bytes] B2[Balance in Wei] B3[Nonce Transactions Received] B4[Code Hash keccak256 bytecode] B5[Controlled by Code Logic] B6[Storage Root 256 bit] end A5 -. initiates .-> T1[Transaction] T1 --> B B4 -. executes when .-> T1 ``` ## 4.3 EIP-1559 Transaction Fee Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[User Submits Transaction] Start --> StepA[Specify Max Fee Per Gas
and Max Priority Fee Per Gas] StepA --> StepB[Network Computes Current Base Fee] StepB --> StepC{Base Fee + Tip
less than Max Fee?} StepC -- Yes --> StepD[Include TX in Block] StepC -- No --> StepE[Transaction Stuck
Increase Max Fee] StepD --> StepF[Validator Executes TX] StepF --> StepG[Base Fee Portion BURNED
Removed from Supply] StepF --> StepH[Tip Portion Paid to Validator] StepG --> End[Updated Global State] StepH --> End ``` ## 4.4 The Merge — Pre and Post Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Before[Before Merge PoW] B1[Mainnet with Ethash] B2[Mining Nodes GPUs] B1 --> B2 end subgraph After[After Merge PoS] M1[Execution Layer Mainnet] M2[Consensus Layer Beacon Chain] M3[Validator Nodes 32 ETH Staked] M1 <-- Engine API --> M2 M2 --> M3 end Before ==> After ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Post-Merge, Ethereum reduced its energy consumption by **~99.95%**, replacing GPU mining with capital-based staking validation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📚 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"What is the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)? Explain its role in the Ethereum network."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is the runtime execution environment for smart contracts on the Ethereum network. It is a **256-bit, stack-based, quasi-Turing-complete** virtual machine that runs on every full node globally. Its roles are: 1. **Execution Engine (1 mark):** Executes the compiled bytecode of smart contracts deterministically across all nodes. 2. **Isolation/Sandboxing (1 mark):** Code runs in a sandboxed environment with no access to network/filesystem, ensuring security. 3. **Consistency (1 mark):** Every node executing the same transaction must reach the same state, which is what makes Ethereum a *decentralized single state machine*. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"Differentiate between an EOA and a Contract Account in Ethereum."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature (1 mark each) | EOA | Contract Account | |----------------------|-----|------------------| | **Control** | Private key held by user | Code logic, no private key | | **Code** | No associated code | Contains executable bytecode | | **Initiation** | Can start transactions | Cannot initiate; only reacts to calls | --- ## 📚 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) > **As per KTU 2024 norms, Part B questions carry internal choice. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions — one must be attempted.** --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q. (a)** Explain the **Ethereum account model** in detail. Compare and contrast it with Bitcoin's UTXO model. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Describe the **EIP-1559 fee market mechanism**. Show, with a numerical example, how the **base fee** is calculated and how the **total transaction fee** is determined. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **1. Ethereum Account Model (5 marks):** Ethereum uses an **account-based model**, where the global state is a mapping of addresses to account states. The two account types are: **Externally Owned Account (EOA):** - Has fields: `(nonce, balance, storageRoot, codeHash)`. - The `codeHash` is the hash of an empty string (i.e., no code). - Controlled by a **private key**; the user signs transactions cryptographically. **Contract Account:** - Same fields, but `codeHash` is the keccak256 hash of the account's bytecode. - Storage is a **Merkle Patricia Trie** rooted at `storageRoot`. - Cannot start a transaction — executes only when called. - Created via the `CREATE` opcode; runs its constructor first. **2. Comparison with Bitcoin UTXO Model (2 marks):** | Aspect | Ethereum Account | Bitcoin UTXO | |--------|------------------|--------------| | **State Representation** | Single global state of accounts | Set of unspent transaction outputs | | **Privacy** | Lower (addresses re-used with balance) | Higher (new address per tx) | | **Parallelization** | Sequential (due to state) | Highly parallelizable | | **Smart Contracts** | First-class citizen | Limited scripting | | **Double Spend Prevention** | Nonce | Consume all referenced UTXOs | **Conclusion:** Ethereum's account model is **stateful**, enabling complex smart contract logic, while Bitcoin's UTXO model is **stateless** and more privacy-preserving but limited in programmability. --- #### ✅ Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **1. Pre-EIP-1559 Issues (1 mark):** Before London Hard Fork (Aug 2021), Ethereum used a simple **first-price auction** gas market, leading to overpayment, poor UX, and unpredictable fees. **2. EIP-1559 Mechanism (3 marks):** EIP-1559 introduced: - **Base Fee** — algorithmically adjusted per block, **BURNED** (not paid to validators). - **Priority Fee (Tip)** — optional, paid to validator as incentive. - **Max Fee Per Gas** — user-defined ceiling. **3. Numerical Example (3 marks):** A user executes a transaction using **65,000 gas**. The current **base fee** is **40 Gwei**, and the user tips **2 Gwei**. Apply the formula: $$ \text{Total Fee} = \text{GasUsed} \times (\text{Base Fee} + \text{Tip}) $$ $$ \text{Total Fee} = 65{,}000 \times (40 + 2) \text{ Gwei} $$ $$ \text{Total Fee} = 65{,}000 \times 42 \text{ Gwei} $$ $$ \text{Total Fee} = 2{,}730{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 2.73 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei} = 0.00273 \text{ ETH} $$ Of this, the base fee portion ($65{,}000 \times 40 = 2{,}600{,}000$ Gwei) is **burned**, and the tip ($65{,}000 \times 2 = 130{,}000$ Gwei) goes to the validator. --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q. (a)** What is **Proof of Stake (PoS)**? Explain how Ethereum transitioned to PoS in **The Merge**. List any **four advantages** of PoS over PoW. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Explain the **structure of an Ethereum block** in detail. What is the role of the **Merkle Patricia Trie** in maintaining the global state? **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **1. Proof of Stake Definition (2 marks):** **Proof of Stake (PoS)** is a consensus mechanism where validators are selected to propose and attest to new blocks based on the amount of cryptocurrency they **stake** as collateral. Misbehavior (e.g., double-signing) results in **slashing** — loss of staked ETH. **2. The Merge (3 marks):** - On **September 15, 2022**, Ethereum merged the existing mainnet (execution layer) with the **Beacon Chain** (consensus layer running PoS since Dec 2020). - The original Ethash PoW was **completely replaced** by PoS. - Block production: every **12 seconds (slot)**, a **validator** is pseudorandomly chosen as proposer. - **32 ETH** is the minimum stake to become a validator. - Finality: achieved via **Casper FFG** after **2 epochs (~12.8 minutes)**. **3. Four Advantages of PoS over PoW (2 marks):** 1. **Energy Efficiency** — ~99.95% reduction in energy consumption. 2. **Economic Security** — Attackers risk losing staked capital via slashing. 3. **Lower Entry Barrier** — No specialized ASIC hardware required. 4. **Issuance Reduction** — ETH issuance dropped from ~4.7% to ~0.4% annually. --- #### ✅ Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **1. Ethereum Block Structure (4 marks):** An Ethereum block has two parts: **Header** and **Body**. **Block Header (15 fields — key ones below):** - `parentHash` — keccak256 of the parent block's header - `stateRoot` — root hash of the global state trie **AFTER** this block's transactions - `transactionsRoot` — root hash of the transactions trie - `receiptsRoot` — root hash of the transaction receipts trie - `number` — block number (count from genesis) - `timestamp` — Unix time of block creation - `gasLimit` — total gas allowed in this block - `gasUsed` — actual gas consumed - `baseFeePerGas` — EIP-1559 base fee for this block - `mixHash` & `nonce` — pre-Merge PoW fields (now mostly zero) **Block Body:** - **Transaction List** — list of transactions ordered by inclusion. - **Ommer Headers** — pre-Merge; obsolete post-Merge. - **Withdrawals** — post-Merge; tracks validator withdrawals. **2. Role of Merkle Patricia Trie (3 marks):** The **Merkle Patricia Trie** is a deterministic, cryptographically verifiable data structure combining: - **Merkle Tree** — for tamper-evidence via a single root hash. - **Patricia Trie** — for efficient key-value lookup with prefix compression. In Ethereum, three main tries are used: 1. **State Trie** — maps address → account state. 2. **Storage Trie** — per-account, maps storage slot → value. 3. **Transactions & Receipts Tries** — per-block. **Key role:** The `stateRoot` in the block header is a **256-bit cryptographic summary of the entire Ethereum state**. Any single state change produces a different root, enabling **light clients** to verify the global state with just the block header. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Ethereum Questions:** > 1. **Confusing Wei with Wei denominations** — Students often write "$1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{9}$ Wei" instead of $10^{18}$. **Memorize the powers of 10!** > 2. **Forgetting EIP-1559 burns the base fee** — Many students say "the entire fee goes to the miner." After the Merge, **miners don't exist**; validators earn only the tip. > 3. **Calling PoS "interest"** — Staking rewards are NOT interest. They are protocol-issued incentives for active validation. > 4. **Mixing up EOA and Contract Accounts** — EOA **cannot hold code**; Contract Account **cannot initiate** a transaction. > 5. **In numerical questions, NOT showing the unit conversion step** — Always show the Gwei → Wei → ETH chain for full marks. > 6. **Saying Ethereum uses SHA-256** — It uses **Keccak-256** (a different, pre-NIST variant of SHA-3) for hashing and **secp256k1** ECDSA for signatures (same curve as Bitcoin). --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > **Quick-fire revision list — read this the night before the exam!** - ✅ Ethereum = **Decentralized World Computer**, not just a cryptocurrency. - ✅ EVM = **Stack-based 256-bit quasi-Turing-complete** runtime. - ✅ Two account types: **EOA (private key)** and **Contract Account (code)**. - ✅ EIP-1559 formula: $\text{Total Fee} = \text{GasUsed} \times (\text{Base Fee} + \text{Tip})$. - ✅ **Base fee is BURNED**; **tip** goes to validator. - ✅ **1 ETH = $10^{18}$ Wei = $10^{9}$ Gwei**. - ✅ **21,000 gas** = standard ETH transfer cost. - ✅ **The Merge (Sep 15, 2022)** transitioned Ethereum from PoW (Ethash) to PoS (Casper FFG). - ✅ **32 ETH** = minimum validator stake. - ✅ Block time **~12 seconds**; finality **~12.8 minutes**. - ✅ Block header contains: `parentHash`, `stateRoot`, `transactionsRoot`, `receiptsRoot`, `baseFeePerGas`. - ✅ Global state stored in a **Merkle Patricia Trie**. - ✅ Smart contracts are written mainly in **Solidity** (statically-typed, curly-brace). - ✅ Turing-completeness is bounded by **gas limit** (quasi-Turing). - ✅ **ERC-20** = fungible token standard; **ERC-721** = NFT standard. - ✅ Smart contracts are **immutable** once deployed (no patches possible — only migrations). - ✅ Accounts are identified by a **20-byte (160-bit) address** derived from the public key (or contract creation hash). - ✅ Nonce prevents **replay attacks**. - ✅ Ethereum is the foundation for **DeFi, NFTs, DAOs, and Decentralized Identity**. - ✅ Post-Merge, **~99.95% energy reduction** vs. pre-Merge.

topics.title: Transition from PoW to PoS - Working of PoS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transition from PoW to PoS - Working of PoS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof of Work (PoW)** is a **Sybil-resistant consensus mechanism** in which network participants (called *miners*) compete to solve a computationally expensive cryptographic puzzle. The first miner to find a valid *nonce* such that the block hash falls below a dynamically adjusted *target* value earns the right to append the next block and claim the block reward plus transaction fees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof of Stake (PoS)** is a **Sybil-resistant consensus mechanism** in which network participants (called *validators*) are selected to propose and attest to new blocks in proportion to the quantity of cryptocurrency they have *locked up* (staked) as collateral. Misbehavior is punished via **slashing**, which confiscates part or all of the staked capital.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Transition from PoW to PoS** refers to the protocol-level migration of a blockchain from energy-intensive mining-based consensus to capital-efficient validator-based consensus. The most prominent real-world instance is **Ethereum's "The Merge"** (finalized on **September 15, 2022**), where the Execution Layer (previously PoW) was fused with the Beacon Chain (PoS coordination layer).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **cooperative apartment building** where decisions about renovations need to be made:

- **PoW (Proof of Work) Analogy — "The Loudest Hammer":** Every week, the residents who want to lead the renovation meeting must run a noisy, exhausting physical race around the building. Whoever finishes first gets to make the proposal. The race consumes enormous electricity (energy = security), and only one person can win regardless of how much skin they have in the building. A resident who owns 1% of the units has the *same winning probability* as the resident who owns 99%.

- **PoS (Proof of Stake) Analogy — "The Shareholder Vote":** Residents instead **deposit a refundable security bond** with the building committee. The committee uses a weighted lottery — the more shares (stake) you own, the more lottery tickets you receive. Your *probability of being selected* to propose the renovation is exactly proportional to your economic share. If you sabotage the building, your bond is **confiscated**. This aligns incentives: the people with the most to lose are the ones making the proposals.

- **The Transition Analogy — "Renovating an Aircraft Mid-Flight":** Moving a live, multi-billion-dollar blockchain from PoW to PoS is like swapping the engine of a Boeing 747 while it is cruising at 35,000 feet. Ethereum achieved this by running **two parallel chains for two years** — the PoW chain (still validating) and the Beacon Chain (silent PoS coordination) — and then at a specific terminal total difficulty, the PoW engine was switched off and the PoS engine took over instantly, without any downtime or transaction loss.

## 1.3 Core Physical & Economic Metrics

The following constants and metrics are central to understanding the PoW → PoS transition:

| Metric | PoW (Pre-Merge Ethereum) | PoS (Post-Merge Ethereum) |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Producer** | Miner | Validator |
| **Selection Criterion** | Hashing power (TH/s) | Stake weight (ETH locked) |
| **Energy per block** | ~**215 kWh** | ~**0.03 kWh** |
| **Annualized energy** | ~**78 TWh** | ~**0.01 TWh** |
| **Security budget** | Electricity + hardware | Locked capital (slashing risk) |
| **Minimum entry stake** | Hardware cost (~$5,000–$10,000) | **32 ETH** (~$100,000 at 2024 prices) |

> [!NOTE]
> Ethereum's PoS reduces energy consumption by approximately **99.95%**, with no loss of cryptographic security guarantees — a figure frequently cited by the Ethereum Foundation and Vitalik Buterin.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Transition Was Necessary — The Three Pillars

### Pillar A — Environmental Sustainability
- PoW's **energy footprint** scales linearly with security. As Bitcoin's price rose, its annualized electricity consumption (**~150 TWh** as of 2024) approached that of mid-sized European nations (e.g., Poland).
- PoS replaces physical energy expenditure with **cryptoeconomic security** — validators risk real capital.

### Pillar B — Economic Security Scaling
- PoW security is bounded by the cost to rent hashing power (the "51% attack cost"). For Bitcoin, this is ~**$20 billion** (2024 estimates).
- PoS security scales with the **total value staked**. For Ethereum, the cost to acquire 51% of staked ETH (~$50+ billion at 2024 prices) plus the additional **slashing risk** makes attacks economically irrational.

### Pillar C — Scalability Enablement
- PoS decouples block production from heavy computation, enabling **sharding** (parallel chain processing), which is incompatible with PoW's homogeneous hardware assumption.
- PoS supports **finality** (irreversible confirmation) via Casper FFG, whereas PoW offers only *probabilistic* finality.

## 2.2 Working of PoS — Operational Walkthrough

### Step 1 — Staking & Activation
- A user deposits **32 ETH** (or a multiple thereof via staking pools) into the official staking contract on the Execution Layer.
- This generates a **validator record** in the Beacon Chain state. The validator enters an *activation queue* (recently capped at ~**8 validators/epoch** to bound state growth).
- Activation takes approximately **6.4 minutes** per validator before the deposit becomes active.

### Step 2 — Random Validator Selection (RANDAO + VDF)
- Time is divided into **slots** (every **12 seconds**, a new block proposer is selected) and **epochs** (every **32 slots**, ~6.4 minutes).
- The selection uses **RANDAO**, a verifiable random function: every validator contributes a value, XOR-combined with previous values to produce pseudo-randomness.
- Future Ethereum upgrades will add a **Verifiable Delay Function (VDF)** to remove last-revealer manipulation.

### Step 3 — Block Proposal
- The selected **proposer** assembles a block from the local mempool, signs it with their BLS private key, and broadcasts it across the gossip network.

### Step 4 — Attestation & Consensus
- A **committee of 128 validators** (randomly chosen per slot) attests to the validity of the proposed block.
- Each attestation is a BLS signature carrying: (slot, block root, current epoch, source justification, target justification).
- Honest validators follow **LMD-GHOST** (Latest Message Driven, Greediest Heaviest Observed SubTree) to vote for the canonical chain head.

### Step 5 — Finality via Casper FFG
- When **two consecutive epochs** receive supermajority (≥ **2/3 of total staked ETH**) attestations, they become *justified*, then *finalized*.
- Finalized blocks **cannot be reverted** without burning at least **1/3 of total staked ETH** (the cost of an attacker slashing 2/3 of honest validators).

### Step 6 — Reward & Penalty Distribution
- Validators earn **rewards** (in newly issued ETH) for correct proposals and attestations.
- Validators are **slashed** (lose stake + ejected) for:
  - **Double proposal** (signing two competing blocks at the same slot): minimum **1 ETH** slashed.
  - **Surround votes** (violating Casper FFG's safety rule): entire effective balance slashed (~**32 ETH**).
- **Inactivity leak** penalizes offline validators progressively, ensuring liveness under mass outages.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with Ethereum specification v1.4 documentation.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_i = \dfrac{s_i}{S_{total}}$ | Probability of validator *i* being selected as block proposer (uniform weighted lottery) | $P_i$ = selection probability (dimensionless), $s_i$ = stake of validator *i* (ETH), $S_{total}$ = total active stake (ETH) |
| 2 | $E[R_i] = P_i \cdot B_p + \dfrac{T_s}{C_s} \cdot B_a$ | Expected per-epoch reward for validator *i* | $E[R_i]$ = expected reward (ETH/epoch), $B_p$ = block proposal base reward (ETH), $T_s$ = total stake, $C_s$ = committee size, $B_a$ = attestation base reward (ETH) |
| 3 | $C_{finality} \geq \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot S_{total}$ | Minimum capital an attacker must burn to revert a finalized block | $C_{finality}$ = attack cost (ETH), $S_{total}$ = total staked supply |
| 4 | $E_{PoS} = N_v \cdot P_{idle} \cdot 12\text{ s} \cdot 0.03\text{ kWh}$ | Energy model for PoS (where $N_v$ = active validators) | $E_{PoS}$ = energy/epoch (kWh), $N_v$ = number of validators, $P_{idle}$ = idle power per validator (~10 W typical) |
| 5 | $\eta_{reduction} = \dfrac{E_{PoW} - E_{PoS}}{E_{PoW}} \approx 0.9995$ | Energy reduction ratio from PoW to PoS | $\eta_{reduction}$ = dimensionless efficiency ratio |
| 6 | $\Delta_{slash} = \alpha \cdot s_i + \beta$ | Slashing penalty magnitude | $\Delta_{slash}$ = ETH burned, $\alpha$ = proportional factor, $\beta$ = base penalty |
| 7 | $T_{epoch} = 32 \cdot 12\text{ s} = 6.4\text{ min}$ | Time per epoch (Ethereum) | $T_{epoch}$ = epoch duration |
| 8 | $T_{finality} \approx 2 \cdot T_{epoch} = 12.8\text{ min}$ | Time to economic finality (two justified epochs) | $T_{finality}$ = finality time |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Domains

PoS underpins not only cryptocurrencies but also **enterprise blockchain platforms** (Hyperledger Besu with QBFT, Polygon PoS, Cardano Ouroboros). For KTU students targeting roles in:
- **FinTech / Banking**: PoS enables permissioned consensus with deterministic finality (T+0 settlement).
- **Supply Chain**: PoS supports IoT-integrated validator nodes with low operational cost.
- **Decentralized Identity (DID)**: PoS secures self-sovereign identity registries.
- **Smart Cities**: PoS-based oracles validate off-chain sensor data with cryptoeconomic guarantees.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Validator Selection Probability

**Goal:** Show that validator *i*'s probability of being chosen as the block proposer in a given slot is exactly proportional to its share of the total active stake.

### Premise
The RANDAO-based selection assigns each validator *i* a number of "lottery tickets" equal to its effective balance, capped at **32 ETH** per validator. The total lottery tickets distributed is the total active stake $S_{total}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= \sum_{j=1}^{N_v} s_j \\
s_i &\in [0,\, 32] \quad \text{(ETH per validator)} \\
N_v &= \text{number of active validators}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Logical Transition
In a uniformly random draw over all lottery tickets, the probability that ticket *i* wins is the ratio of its tickets to the total tickets.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= \frac{\text{tickets held by validator } i}{\text{total tickets in the lottery}} \\
    &= \frac{s_i}{S_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Verification of Probability Axioms
The probabilities must sum to **1** (total certainty that some validator is chosen):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N_v} P_i &= \sum_{i=1}^{N_v} \frac{s_i}{S_{total}} = \frac{1}{S_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_v} s_i = \frac{S_{total}}{S_{total}} = 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Special Case — Uniform Distribution
If every validator stakes exactly **32 ETH** (the maximum), all validators have equal probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_i &= 32 \;\forall i \\
S_{total} &= 32 \cdot N_v \\
P_i &= \frac{32}{32 \cdot N_v} = \frac{1}{N_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

This recovers the classical "equal lottery" model when stake is uniform.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Reduction Ratio ($\eta_{reduction}$)

**Goal:** Derive the **99.95%** energy reduction figure cited by Ethereum.

### Premise — PoW Annual Energy
Assume pre-Merge Ethereum consumed approximately **78 TWh/year** (Ethereum Foundation estimate, May 2022).

### Premise — PoS Per-Epoch Energy
Post-Merge, validators consume only baseline node-computing power. A typical staking node uses ~**10 W** idle. Assume ~**900,000 active validators** (mid-2024 figure).

### Step-by-Step Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Epochs per year} &= \frac{365.25 \cdot 24 \cdot 3600}{T_{epoch}} = \frac{31{,}557{,}600}{384} \approx 82{,}160 \text{ epochs/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy per epoch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{epoch} &= N_v \cdot P_{idle} \cdot T_{epoch} \\
          &= 900{,}000 \cdot 10\text{ W} \cdot 384\text{ s} \\
          &= 3.456 \cdot 10^9 \text{ J} = 960\text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Annual PoS energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{PoS} &= 82{,}160 \cdot 960\text{ kWh} \\
        &\approx 7.87 \cdot 10^7 \text{ kWh} = 0.0787\text{ TWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reduction ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{reduction} &= \frac{E_{PoW} - E_{PoS}}{E_{PoW}} \\
                 &= \frac{78 - 0.0787}{78} \\
                 &= \frac{77.9213}{78} \\
                 &\approx 0.99899 \approx \mathbf{99.90\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The widely cited figure of **99.95%** assumes a slightly lower validator count and more efficient client software. The order of magnitude — *three-nines reduction* — is the headline conclusion KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost of Reverting Finalized Blocks

**Goal:** Compute the minimum capital an attacker must sacrifice to reverse a finalized block under Casper FFG.

### Premise
A finalized block is one where two consecutive epochs have received ≥ **2/3 supermajority** attestations. To revert a finalized block, the attacker must convince honest validators to *switch* their finalized justification, requiring the attacker to either:
- Control ≥ **2/3** of stake directly, or
- Equivocate in such a way that honest validators are slashed en masse.

### Economic Penalty Model
Casper FFG's **accountable safety** theorem states: any two conflicting finalized checkpoints share at least **1/3 of the validators**, who must have double-signed and are therefore slashed.

### Cost Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{attack} &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot S_{total} \cdot (1 - r_{recover}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $r_{recover}$ is the partial refund rate (~**50%** for the first offense after a 36-day withdrawal delay).

### Numerical Example (Mid-2024 Figures)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &\approx 32{,}000{,}000 \text{ ETH} \quad (\text{total staked supply}) \\
1/3 \cdot S_{total} &\approx 10{,}666{,}667 \text{ ETH} \\
\text{Assuming } r_{recover} &= 0 \\
C_{attack} &\geq 10{,}666{,}667 \text{ ETH} \\
\text{At } \$3{,}500\text{/ETH} &: C_{attack} \geq \mathbf{\$37.3\text{ billion}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This figure represents the **minimum economic cost** to break finality. In practice, the social and reputational cost (loss of confidence, ETH price collapse) makes such attacks self-defeating.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PoW vs PoS

| Dimension | Proof of Work | Proof of Stake |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource consumed** | Electricity + ASIC hardware | Locked capital |
| **Sybil resistance mechanism** | Cost of computation | Cost of capital |
| **Block producer identity** | Anonymous miner | Public, accountable validator |
| **Attack cost model** | Rent 51% hash power | Acquire 51% stake + face slashing |
| **Finality type** | Probabilistic (6+ blocks) | Economic (12.8 min) |
| **Throughput bottleneck** | Block size + propagation | Validator hardware + bandwidth |
| **Issuance model** | Block subsidy + fees | Net-negative issuance (EIP-1559 burn) |
| **Energy per transaction** | ~**1,400 kWh** (Bitcoin, 2024) | ~**0.03 kWh** (Ethereum, 2024) |
| **Fork choice rule** | Longest chain (Nakamoto) | LMD-GHOST + Casper FFG |
