# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]
**Q: Compare Proof of Work and Proof of Stake consensus mechanisms with respect to energy consumption and attack cost. Why did Ethereum migrate from PoW to PoS?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Proof of Work | Proof of Stake |
|---|---|---|
| Energy consumption | ~**78 TWh/year** | ~**0.01 TWh/year** |
| Resource | Electricity + hardware | Locked capital (32 ETH) |
| Attack cost | Rent 51% hash power | Acquire 51% stake + slashing |

Ethereum migrated to PoS via **"The Merge"** on **September 15, 2022** to achieve a **~99.95% reduction in energy consumption** while maintaining cryptoeconomic security through validator slashing. The Beacon Chain, launched in December 2020, ran in parallel for two years as a coordination layer before being merged with the Execution Layer.

*(Examiner's Note: A simple definition without the 99.95% figure or The Merge date will lose 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — KTU S7 Past Question Pattern]
**Q: Define slashing in Proof of Stake. List any two slashable offenses.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Slashing** is the cryptoeconomic mechanism in PoS by which a validator that violates protocol rules has a portion (or entirety) of its staked ETH forcibly burned and is forcibly ejected from the active validator set.

**Two slashable offenses:**
1. **Double proposal** — signing two distinct blocks for the same slot (equivocation).
2. **Surround votes** — submitting Casper FFG attestations that violate the safety rule (e.g., voting for two conflicting epochs).

A third (valid) offense is **inactivity**, though this incurs a softer "inactivity leak" rather than full slashing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU ESE — Module 3 Weight 14 Marks]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the working of Proof of Stake consensus mechanism. Describe the role of validators, the RANDAO-based selection process, and the Casper FFG finality gadget. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Analyze the security implications of the transition from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake using the example of Ethereum's "The Merge". Quantitatively justify the energy reduction and discuss the cryptoeconomic attack costs. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Validator Registration (2 Marks)**
A user deposits exactly **32 ETH** into the official staking contract on the Ethereum Execution Layer. This deposit is recognized by the Beacon Chain, which is the PoS coordination layer. Each deposit creates one validator identity, identified by a BLS public key. The validator then enters an activation queue and becomes eligible for selection after approximately **6.4 minutes**. Up to **8 validators per epoch** can be activated to bound state growth.

**Step 2 — RANDAO-Based Selection (2 Marks)**
Ethereum divides time into **slots** (each **12 seconds**) and **epochs** (each containing **32 slots** = **6.4 minutes**). For each slot, one validator is pseudo-randomly selected as the **block proposer**. The randomness is generated using **RANDAO** — a commit-reveal scheme where each validator contributes a fresh random value, XOR-combined with all previous contributions. Selection probability is proportional to stake: $P_i = s_i / S_{total}$. A **committee of 128 validators** is also selected per slot to attest to the proposed block.

**Step 3 — Casper FFG Finality (2 Marks)**
Once a block is proposed and attested, validators issue **Casper FFG votes** pointing to source and target checkpoints. When a checkpoint receives attestations from validators representing ≥ **2/3 of total staked ETH**, it becomes *justified*. When two consecutive checkpoints are justified, the earlier one becomes *finalized*. Finality is achieved in approximately **12.8 minutes** (two epochs). The **accountable safety** property guarantees that reverting a finalized block requires slashing ≥ **1/3 of all staked ETH**, making such attacks economically catastrophic.

**Step 4 — Rewards & Penalties (1 Mark)**
- Honest proposers and attesters receive ETH rewards proportional to their stake weight.
- Dishonest validators are **slashed** (lose ETH and are ejected).
- Offline validators suffer the **inactivity leak**, losing stake gradually until they return or are fully ejected.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Pre-Merge Ethereum Architecture (2 Marks)**
Before The Merge (Sep 15, 2022), Ethereum's Execution Layer was secured by the **Ethash** PoW algorithm. Miners competed to find a nonce such that `Keccak256(header, nonce) < target`. The Beacon Chain, launched on **December 1, 2020**, ran in parallel as a silent PoS layer, validating itself but not yet securing transactions.

**Step 2 — The Merge Event (2 Marks)**
At a pre-determined **terminal total difficulty**, the PoW Execution Layer was switched off and began importing blocks from the PoS Beacon Chain at the consensus level. This was achieved without downtime because both chains had been running in parallel for nearly two years. After The Merge, all transaction execution is validated by PoS validators, not miners.

**Step 3 — Quantitative Energy Reduction (2 Marks)**
- Pre-Merge energy: **~78 TWh/year**
- Post-Merge energy: **~0.01 TWh/year**
- Reduction ratio: $\eta = (78 - 0.01)/78 \approx \mathbf{0.99987}$ or **99.987%**

Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{reduction} &= \frac{E_{PoW} - E_{PoS}}{E_{PoW}} \\
                 &= \frac{78 - 0.01}{78} \\
                 &= \frac{77.99}{78} \\
                 &\approx 0.99987
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cryptoeconomic Attack Cost Comparison (1 Mark)**

| Attack Vector | PoW Cost | PoS Cost |
|---|---|---|
| 51% attack | Rent ASIC hash power (~**$20 B/day** for Ethereum-class chain) | Acquire 51% of staked ETH (~**$37 B** one-time) + face full slashing |
| Finality reversion | Not possible (probabilistic finality only) | Burn ≥ **1/3** of all staked ETH |

The PoS model converts energy expenditure into permanent capital-at-risk, providing equivalent or superior security at negligible energy cost.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU ESE — Alternative Module 3 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Describe the staking mechanism in Proof of Stake. What is the minimum stake required in Ethereum and why? Explain the activation queue and its purpose. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of slashing and the inactivity leak in maintaining the liveness and safety properties of a PoS blockchain. Provide a worked example of a slashing calculation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Staking Concept (2 Marks)**
Staking is the act of locking cryptocurrency as collateral to gain the right to validate blocks. In Ethereum, the minimum stake per validator is **32 ETH**. This threshold was chosen to:
- Limit the maximum number of active validators (capping beacon-chain state growth).
- Ensure that slashing is a meaningful economic penalty.
- Balance the trade-off between decentralization (many small stakers via pools) and operational efficiency (fewer, larger validators).

**Step 2 — Activation Process (3 Marks)**
1. User generates validator keys: a **BLS signing key** (withdrawable) and a **BLS withdrawal key**.
2. User sends a deposit transaction to the staking contract: `value = 32 ETH`.
3. The deposit is observed by Beacon Chain nodes after the next ~**12-second slot**.
4. Validator enters the **activation queue**.
5. To bound the rate of new validators joining (and prevent state-bloat attacks), Ethereum caps activations at **8 validators per epoch**.
6. Validator becomes active after queue wait — typically a few hours under normal load.
7. Once active, the validator is eligible for proposal and attestation duties.

**Step 3 — Pooled Staking (2 Marks)**
Since 32 ETH is a high barrier, **staking pools** (Lido, Rocket Pool) allow users to stake any amount of ETH and receive a liquid staking token (e.g., stETH, rETH) representing their share. Pool operators run validator nodes on behalf of pooled depositors.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Safety vs Liveness (2 Marks)**
- **Safety**: All honest nodes agree on the same chain (no forks).
- **Liveness**: The chain keeps producing blocks.
- Slashing enforces **safety**; the inactivity leak enforces **liveness**.

**Step 2 — Slashing Conditions (2 Marks)**
1. **Proposer equivocation** — signing two competing blocks at the same slot.
2. **Attester equivocation** — signing two conflicting attestations.
3. **Surround votes** — voting in a way that violates Casper FFG safety.

The validator is immediately **slashed**, removed from the active set, and suffers a penalty proportional to the stake and the number of other validators slashed around the same time (correlation penalty).

**Step 3 — Inactivity Leak (1 Mark)**
If a validator fails to attest for **more than 4 epochs**, the inactivity leak begins deducting stake progressively. This is critical during mass-outage events: it ensures that if > 1/3 of validators go offline, their stake eventually drops below 1/3 so the chain can recover finality.

**Step 4 — Worked Slashing Calculation (2 Marks)**

Validator stakes 32 ETH. Slashing base penalty = **1 ETH** (initial). Correlation bonus = up to **3× base penalty** if many validators are slashed simultaneously.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{slash} &= \alpha \cdot s_i + \beta \\
\text{where } \alpha &= 0.0625 \text{ (proportional factor)}, \; \beta = 1\text{ ETH} \\
\Delta_{slash} &= 0.0625 \cdot 32 + 1 \\
              &= 2 + 1 \\
              &= \mathbf{3\text{ ETH}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the validator is the only one slashed (no correlation), the penalty is **3 ETH**. If many validators are slashed in the same 36-day window, the penalty scales up to the entire **32 ETH** effective balance.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PoW and PoS terminology** — Examiners explicitly deduct marks if you say "miners" when discussing PoS. Always say **validators** in PoS context.
> 2. **Forgetting The Merge date (Sep 15, 2022)** — A precise date is required for full marks on transition questions.
> 3. **Missing the quantitative figure (~99.95%)** — Without numbers, energy comparison answers score only 1 of the 2 marks allocated.
> 4. **Mixing Casper FFG with LMD-GHOST** — These are distinct: **LMD-GHOST** is the *fork-choice rule*; **Casper FFG** is the *finality gadget*. Examiners expect you to name both.
> 5. **Omitting slashing conditions** — Listing "double-signing" alone is insufficient; the official three slashable offenses must be enumerated.
> 6. **Using `32 ETH` without the staking rationale** — Just stating the number without explaining why (state-growth bound, meaningful penalty) loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Merge** = Ethereum's transition from PoW to PoS, finalized on **September 15, 2022**, after the Beacon Chain ran in parallel from **December 1, 2020**.
- **PoW's three flaws**: environmental damage, hardware centralization, weak finality.
- **PoS replaces energy with capital** — security is provided by *locked, slashable stake* rather than electricity.
- **Minimum validator stake in Ethereum = 32 ETH** (chosen for state-growth bound).
- **Validator selection** uses RANDAO: probability $P_i = s_i / S_{total}$.
- **Time units**: 12 s/slot, 32 slots/epoch = **6.4 min/epoch**.
- **Finality gadget = Casper FFG**: two consecutive epochs with ≥ 2/3 attestation supermajority achieve finality in ~12.8 min.
- **Fork-choice rule = LMD-GHOST**: latest-message-driven, greediest heaviest subtree.
- **Slashing offenses** (3 total): proposer equivocation, attester equivocation, surround votes.
- **Inactivity leak** enforces liveness; **slashing** enforces safety.
- **Energy reduction** post-Merge = approximately **99.95%** (PoW ~78 TWh/yr → PoS ~0.01 TWh/yr).
- **Cost to break finality** ≥ 1/3 of all staked ETH (~$37 B at 2024 prices).
- **Net-negative issuance**: post-Merge Ethereum often burns more ETH in base fees (EIP-1559) than it issues to validators — ETH can become deflationary.
- **Future enhancements**: Single Slot Finality (SSF), Proposer-Builder Separation (PBS), Verifiable Delay Functions (VDFs), and sharding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Contracts in Ethereum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Contracts in Ethereum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Contract** is a self-executing computer program deployed on a blockchain that automatically enforces, executes, and documents the terms of an agreement without the need for trusted intermediaries. Within the Ethereum ecosystem, smart contracts are **Turing-complete programs** written predominantly in high-level languages such as **Solidity** (statically typed, JavaScript-like syntax) or **Vyper** (Python-like, security-focused), which are compiled into low-level **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine) bytecode** and executed on every full node in the decentralized network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A smart contract in Ethereum is an immutable, deterministic program whose code and state reside at a specific 20-byte address on the Ethereum blockchain. Once deployed, neither the code nor its storage can be altered (immutability principle), and execution is guaranteed by the consensus protocol.

The term "smart contract" was originally coined by **Nick Szabo in 1994**, long before Bitcoin or Ethereum existed. Szabo analogized them to automated vending machines — devices that enforce contractual clauses (insert coin → receive product) without a human operator.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Vending Machine Analogy

Imagine a vending machine:

| Real-World Step | Smart Contract Equivalent |
|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Manufacturer fixes rules in hardware | Developer writes Solidity code |
| Drop a coin (no human needed) | Send a transaction with ETH |
| Machine dispenses product | Contract executes function logic |
| No refunds / no tampering | Immutable, deterministic code |
| State: item is now dispensed | Blockchain state updated |

Just as a vending machine **cannot lie, cheat, or forget**, an Ethereum smart contract will execute its pre-written logic **exactly the same way on every node in the world** — forever.

## 1.3 Why Ethereum? The Blockchain Limitation Bitcoin Could Not Solve

Bitcoin supports a limited scripting language (Bitcoin Script) used primarily for transaction validation. It is **not Turing-complete** (loops are restricted). **Ethereum (proposed by Vitalik Buterin in 2013, launched 2015)** generalized this idea:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Innovation of Ethereum:** Ethereum introduced a **Turing-complete** on-chain execution environment (the EVM) backed by a global state machine, enabling arbitrary decentralized applications (dApps) beyond simple value transfer.

## 1.4 Account Model in Ethereum (Foundation Concept)

Ethereum has **two account types**, and this distinction is the gateway to understanding smart contracts:

- **Externally Owned Account (EOA):** Controlled by a private key. Holds an ETH balance, can initiate transactions. **Has no associated code.**
- **Contract Account:** Controlled by its **bytecode + storage**. **Cannot initiate transactions on its own** — it only reacts when called by an EOA or another contract. Has its own balance and persistent storage.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Point:** A Contract Account is *created* by an EOA via a special transaction whose `to` field is empty (or to a special address). Once created, the contract account is assigned a unique 160-bit address and lives forever on-chain.

## 1.5 Anatomy of an Ethereum Transaction

A transaction is the **only mechanism** to trigger a state change. A standard transaction contains:

- `nonce`: Number of transactions sent by the sender (replay protection).
- `to`: Recipient address (20 bytes) — empty/null for contract creation.
- `value`: Amount of Wei (1 ETH = 10¹⁸ Wei) to transfer.
- `data`: Optional payload — function selector + arguments for contract calls.
- `gasLimit`: Maximum computational units the sender is willing to consume.
- `gasPrice`: Wei paid per unit of gas (priority fee + base fee post-EIP-1559).
- `v, r, s`: Signature components (ECDSA over secp256k1 curve).

## 1.6 Gas — The Metered Fuel of Computation

Every EVM opcode (ADD, SSTORE, CALL, etc.) has a fixed gas cost. Gas prevents infinite loops, allocates scarce blockchain resources, and pays validators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Transaction Fee Formula:**
> **Tx Fee = Gas Used × Effective Gas Price**
> (Measured in **Wei**, commonly quoted in **Gwei** where 1 Gwei = 10⁹ Wei)

If computation exceeds `gasLimit`, the transaction **reverts**, but the fee is **still consumed** (defensive payment to validators).

## 1.7 Solidity — The Dominant Smart Contract Language

Solidity is a **statically-typed, contract-oriented, high-level language** influenced by C++, Python, and JavaScript. Key features include:

- Inheritance (including multiple via `is` keyword).
- Built-in units: `wei`, `gwei`, `ether`, `seconds`, `days`.
- Custom value types: `address`, `address payable`.
- Global variables: `msg.sender`, `msg.value`, `block.timestamp`, `block.number`.
- Error handling: `require`, `assert`, `revert`.

A minimal Solidity smart contract:

```solidity
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

contract SimpleWallet {
    address public owner;
    mapping(address => uint256) public balances;

    constructor() {
        owner = msg.sender; // EOA that deployed the contract
    }

    function deposit() public payable {
        balances[msg.sender] += msg.value;
    }

    function withdraw(uint256 _amount) public {
        require(balances[msg.sender] >= _amount, "Insufficient balance");
        balances[msg.sender] -= _amount;
        payable(msg.sender).transfer(_amount);
    }
}
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) — Architectural Backbone

The **EVM** is a simple, stack-based, quasi-Turing-complete execution environment. "Quasi" because it is Turing-complete in theory but bounded in practice by **gas**.

**Operational Pipeline of a Smart Contract Execution:**

1. **User Action:** An EOA signs a transaction with a `to` field set to a contract address and `data` containing the function selector + ABI-encoded arguments.
2. **Propagation:** Transaction is broadcast to the Ethereum peer-to-peer network.
3. **Block Inclusion:** A validator/sequencer (post-Merge: beacon chain validator) selects the transaction and includes it in a block.
4. **EVM Execution:** Every full node executes the transaction locally, running the EVM bytecode deterministically.
5. **State Transition:** The Ethereum world state (a Merkle Patricia Trie) is updated.
6. **Consensus:** Validators reach consensus on the new state root.

> [!NOTE]
> **Determinism Guarantee:** Given the same transaction and pre-state, every EVM on Earth produces the **same post-state**. This is what makes distributed consensus possible.

## 2.2 EVM Stack Architecture

The EVM is a **256-bit word machine** designed for cryptographic operations (SHA-3 hashes, elliptic curve computations naturally fit into 32-byte words).

- **Stack:** 32-byte items, max depth 1024. All arithmetic/comparison happens here.
- **Memory:** Volatile, byte-addressable, expands in 32-byte words. Cleared after each message call.
- **Storage:** Persistent, 256-bit key-value store, lives forever on-chain. **Most expensive** resource (20,000 gas for a fresh SSTORE, 5,000 for update).
- **Calldata:** Read-only buffer holding transaction input data.
- **Code:** ROM holding the deployed bytecode.
- **Logs:** Indexable event data (Bloom-filter searchable).

## 2.3 Solidity Function Visibility — Critical KTU Concept

| Visibility | Callable By | Use Case |
|------------|------------|----------|
| `public` | Anyone (EOA + contracts) | Public interface methods |
| `external` | Only from outside the contract | Gas-efficient public functions |
| `internal` | This contract + derived contracts | Helper functions, inheritance |
| `private` | Only this contract | Internal implementation hiding |

## 2.4 Storage vs Memory vs Calldata — Data Location Semantics

| Location | Persistence | Gas Cost | Modifiable |
|----------|-------------|----------|------------|
| `storage` | Permanent on-chain | **Highest** (SSTORE opcode) | Yes |
| `memory` | Per-function, ephemeral | Medium (MSTORE) | Yes |
| `calldata` | Per-function, ephemeral | **Lowest** (read-only) | No |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Marking function parameters of reference types as `calldata` instead of `memory` saves significant gas — frequently tested in KTU numerical/gas-optimization problems.

## 2.5 Contract Deployment Lifecycle

1. **Write** Solidity source `.sol` file.
2. **Compile** using `solc` (Solidity compiler) → produces **bytecode** and **ABI (Application Binary Interface)**.
3. **Deploy** via transaction with empty `to` field. The contract creation transaction's `data` field contains the bytecode.
4. **Address Derivation:** Contract address = `keccak256(RLP(sender, nonce))[12:]` (post-merge EIP still uses this for EOA-created contracts).
5. **Constructor Execution:** The EVM executes the constructor (a special function `constructor()`) only once.
6. **Immutability Locks In:** From this moment, **the deployed bytecode cannot be changed.**

## 2.6 Events and Logs — The Off-Chain Communication Bridge

Events allow contracts to emit structured data that is **cheap to store on-chain** (375 gas base + log data) but is **not accessible to other contracts**. DApps (front-ends) use event logs via `eth_getLogs` JSON-RPC.

```solidity
event Transfer(address indexed from, address indexed to, uint256 value);
```

- `indexed` parameters (max 3 per event) are stored in a Bloom filter for efficient search.
- Non-indexed parameters are stored as ABI-encoded data.

## 2.7 Token Standards — The ERC Ecosystem

| Standard | Purpose | Key Functions |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| **ERC-20** | Fungible tokens | `totalSupply`, `balanceOf`, `transfer`, `approve`, `transferFrom`, `allowance` |
| **ERC-721** | Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs) | `ownerOf`, `safeTransferFrom`, `approve`, `setApprovalForAll` |
| **ERC-1155** | Multi-token (both fungible & NFT) | `safeBatchTransferFrom` |
| **ERC-777** | Fungible with hooks | `send`, `tokensReceived` callback |

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Wei ↔ Ether | 1 ETH = 10¹⁸ Wei | Smallest unit of ETH |
| 2 | Wei ↔ Gwei | 1 Gwei = 10⁹ Wei | Common gas price unit |
| 3 | Tx Fee (Legacy) | `fee = gasUsed × gasPrice` | Wei |
| 4 | Tx Fee (EIP-1559) | `fee = gasUsed × (baseFee + priorityFee)` | Wei |
| 5 | Max Cost Cap | `maxCost = gasLimit × maxFeePerGas` | Wei — total upper bound |
| 6 | SSTORE (zero → non-zero) | 20,000 gas | Expensive storage write |
| 7 | SSTORE (non-zero → non-zero) | 2,900 gas | Cheaper update |
| 8 | SSTORE (non-zero → zero, refund) | -4,800 gas refund | Max 50% of total gas |
| 9 | Contract Creation Cost | 32,000 + 200 × code_bytes | Base deployment |
| 10 | Block Gas Limit | ~30,000,000 (post-merge) | All tx in block ≤ this |
| 11 | Contract Address (EOA deployer) | `keccak256(RLP(sender, nonce))[12:32]` | Last 20 bytes |
| 12 | EVM Word Size | 256 bits (32 bytes) | Aligned to cryptographic ops |
| 13 | EVM Stack Depth | 1024 items | Hard limit |
| 14 | msg.value range | uint256 max ≈ 1.16 × 10⁷⁷ Wei | Practically unlimited ETH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Never use the vertical pipe" rule:** All delimiter `|` inside math content are replaced with `\vert` or `\mid`. Example: absolute value of x is rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$, never as `|x|`.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart contracts power:
- **DeFi (Decentralized Finance):** Uniswap (AMMs), Aave (lending), Compound (interest).
- **NFTs & Digital Art:** OpenSea, Art Blocks, Bored Ape Yacht Club.
- **DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations):** Governance via on-chain voting.
- **Supply Chain:** Provenance tracking (e.g., IBM Food Trust).
- **Identity:** Self-sovereign identity (DID standards).
- **Insurance:** Parametric crop/flight insurance with oracle triggers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How a Contract Address Is Computed

When an EOA (with address `S`) deploys a contract at nonce `N`, the resulting contract address is deterministically derived as follows. This is **high-yield** for KTU derivations.

**Step 1 — Concatenate sender and nonce via RLP encoding:**

RLP (Recursive Length Prefix) is Ethereum's canonical serialization format. For a list of two items, the encoding is:

$$
RLP([S, N]) = \text{prefix} + \text{encode}(S) + \text{encode}(N)
$$

For a 20-byte address and a nonce `N` (small enough to fit in a single byte for low values):

$$
RLP([S, N]) = \text{prefix} + S \parallel N
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Keccak-256 hash function:**

$$
H = \text{Keccak256}(RLP([S, N]))
$$

**Step 3 — Extract the rightmost 160 bits (20 bytes):**

$$
\text{ContractAddress} = H[12 : 32]
$$

**Final compact form:**

$$
A_{\text{contract}} = \text{Keccak256}\big(\,RLP([S_{\text{addr}},\ n_{\text{sender}}])\,\big)\big[12 : 32\big]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **why** contract addresses are predictable before deployment — a feature exploited by `CREATE2` (EIP-1014) to enable counterfactual instantiation and Layer-2 channel factories.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example — Transaction Fee Calculation

This is a typical KTU numerical problem worth ~7 marks.

**Problem:** A user deploys a smart contract. The deployment transaction has:
- `gasLimit` = 3,000,000
- `gasPrice` = 25 Gwei
- Actual `gasUsed` = 1,250,000
- `value` sent with deployment = 0.5 ETH

Compute: **(a)** Total transaction fee in Wei and ETH. **(b)** Amount deducted from sender's balance. **(c)** Effective cost per byte of deployed code (assume 12,000 bytes of bytecode).

---

### Solution

**Part (a) — Transaction Fee in Wei:**

Using the legacy fee formula:

$$
\text{Fee}_{\text{Wei}} = \text{gasUsed} \times \text{gasPrice}
$$

Substituting the values. First convert gas price to Wei:

$$
25\ \text{Gwei} = 25 \times 10^9\ \text{Wei}
$$

Now compute the product:

$$
\text{Fee}_{\text{Wei}} = 1{,}250{,}000 \times (25 \times 10^9)
$$

Multiply coefficients:

$$
1{,}250{,}000 \times 25 = 31{,}250{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Fee}_{\text{Wei}} = 31{,}250{,}000 \times 10^9 = 3.125 \times 10^{16}\ \text{Wei}
$$

Convert to ETH (1 ETH = 10¹⁸ Wei):

$$
\text{Fee}_{\text{ETH}} = \frac{3.125 \times 10^{16}}{10^{18}} = 0.03125\ \text{ETH}
$$

**Part (b) — Total Deducted from Sender's Balance:**

The sender pays **both** the value transferred **and** the gas fee:

$$
\text{TotalDeduct}_{\text{Wei}} = \text{Fee}_{\text{Wei}} + \text{Value}_{\text{Wei}}
$$

Convert value to Wei:

$$
0.5\ \text{ETH} = 0.5 \times 10^{18} = 5 \times 10^{17}\ \text{Wei}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{TotalDeduct}_{\text{Wei}} = 3.125 \times 10^{16} + 5 \times 10^{17}
$$

Align powers of ten for clean addition:

$$
3.125 \times 10^{16} = 0.3125 \times 10^{17}
$$

$$
\text{TotalDeduct}_{\text{Wei}} = 0.3125 \times 10^{17} + 5 \times 10^{17} = 5.3125 \times 10^{17}\ \text{Wei}
$$

In ETH:

$$
\text{TotalDeduct}_{\text{ETH}} = 0.53125\ \text{ETH}
$$

**Part (c) — Effective Cost per Byte of Deployed Bytecode:**

$$
\text{CostPerByte} = \frac{\text{Fee}_{\text{Wei}}}{\text{Bytes}} = \frac{3.125 \times 10^{16}}{12{,}000}
$$

Perform the division:

$$
\frac{3.125}{12{,}000} = 0.0002604166...
$$

Multiply back by 10¹⁶:

$$
\text{CostPerByte} \approx 2.604 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Wei per byte}
$$

In Gwei (divide by 10⁹):

$$
\text{CostPerByte} \approx 2{,}604.17\ \text{Gwei per byte}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer Summary Box:**
> - **(a)** 3.125 × 10¹⁶ Wei = **0.03125 ETH**
> - **(b)** 5.3125 × 10¹⁷ Wei = **0.53125 ETH**
> - **(c)** ≈ **2604.17 Gwei/byte**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solidity Error Handling

| Construct | Gas Refund on Fail? | Use When |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| `require(cond, "msg")` | **Yes** (unused gas returned) | Validating inputs, user errors |
| `assert(cond)` | **No** (all gas consumed) | Checking internal invariants, overflow (pre-0.8) |
| `revert("msg")` | **Yes** (unused gas returned) | Conditional rollback with message |

> [!NOTE]
> **Version 0.8.0+ Insight:** Solidity 0.8.0 introduced **default checked arithmetic** — integer overflow/underflow now automatically triggers a `Panic` (revert). This is why `SafeMath` libraries became optional.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Smart Contract Vulnerabilities

| # | Vulnerability | Root Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | **Reentrancy** | External call before state update | Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern, ReentrancyGuard |
| 2 | **Integer Overflow** (pre-0.8) | Unchecked arithmetic | Solidity ≥ 0.8, SafeMath |
| 3 | **tx.origin Phishing** | Using `tx.origin` for auth | Use `msg.sender` only |
| 4 | **Front-Running** | Public mempool visibility | Commit-reveal schemes, slippage limits |
| 5 | **Delegatecall Injection** | Untrusted library call | Avoid user-supplied addresses in delegatecall |
| 6 | **Unchecked Low-Level Calls** | Ignoring return values | Check `.call()` return or use high-level calls |
| 7 | **Denial of Service** | Revert in loop, unbounded loops | Pull-payment pattern, pagination |

> [!WARNING]
> The **DAO Hack (June 2016)** exploited reentrancy, draining 3.6 million ETH and leading to the Ethereum / Ethereum Classic hard fork — a landmark case in smart contract history.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ethereum Account & Transaction Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User with Private Key] --> B[Signs Transaction with ECDSA]
    B --> C[Transaction Broadcast via P2P Network]
    C --> D[Validator in Beacon Chain]
    D --> E{EVM Execution Engine}
    E -->|Contract Call target=| F[Look up Contract Bytecode at to address]
    E -->|to field empty| G[Execute Init Code to Create Contract]
    F --> H[Decode Function Selector from calldata]
    H --> I[Match to Public/External Function]
    I --> J[Run Function Body on EVM Stack]
    J --> K[Read/Write Storage Memory Calldata]
    K --> L[Compute Gas Consumed per Opcode]
    L --> M[Update World State Merkle Patricia Trie]
    M --> N[Emit Events to Transaction Receipt Log Bloom]
    N --> O[Block Finalization via Casper FFG Consensus]
    G --> P[Compute Contract Address keccak256 RLP sender nonce 12 32]
    P --> M
```

## 4.2 Smart Contract Execution Stack Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Call Data] --> B1[Read Only Input]
    A2[Memory] --> B2[Volatile Per Call]
    A3[Storage] --> B3[Persistent On Chain SSTORE 20000 gas]
    A4[Stack] --> B4[256 bit 1024 depth]
    A5[Bytecode] --> B5[Immutable Code ROM]
    A6[Logs] --> B6[Bloom Filter Indexed Events]
    B1 --> C1[EVM Dispatcher]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[State Transition Function]
    D1 --> E1[New World State Root]
```

## 4.3 Smart Contract Development & Deployment Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Solidity Source sol file] --> P2[solc Compiler]
    P2 --> P3[Bytecode bin]
    P2 --> P4[ABI Application Binary Interface JSON]
    P3 --> P5[Deploy Transaction to field empty]
    P4 --> P6[Frontend DApp Library ethers web3]
    P5 --> P7[Contract Account Created]
    P7 --> P8[Constructor Executes Once]
    P8 --> P9[Code Immutable State Initialized]
    P9 --> P10[Ready for Function Calls]
    P6 --> P11[User dApp Browser Wallet]
    P11 --> P10
    P10 --> P12[Events Logs emitted]
    P12 --> P11
```

## 4.4 Solidity Function Call Architecture (Internal vs External)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Caller EOA or Contract] --> Y{Type of Call}
    Y -->|msg.sender set| Z1[External Call]
    Y -->|Direct Jump| Z2[Internal Call]
    Z1 --> W1[Pass calldata copy]
    Z2 --> W2[Pass memory reference]
    W1 --> V1[Run function body in target contract]
    W2 --> V2[Run function body in same contract context]
    V1 --> U1[Return value via returndata]
    V2 --> U2[Return value via memory or return]
    U1 --> T1[All state changes commit atomically]
    U2 --> T1
    T1 --> S1[State Root Update]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Safeguard Note:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `B3`) and labels contain only clean uppercase/lowercase text — no `**bold**`, `*italic*`, or HTML tables — to ensure successful rendering in KTU PDF conversion pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Smart Contract. How does it differ from a traditional contract? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **smart contract** is a self-executing program stored on a blockchain that automatically enforces the terms of an agreement once predefined conditions are met, without requiring a trusted third party.

| Aspect | Traditional Contract | Smart Contract |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|
| **Enforcement** | Legal system, courts | Code, consensus protocol |
| **Interpretation** | Subjective, language-based | Deterministic, byte-exact |
| **Execution** | Manual, time-consuming | Automatic, near-instant |
| **Tamper Resistance** | Low (paper can be altered) | High (cryptographic immutability) |
| **Cost** | Intermediaries, lawyers | Gas fees only |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for valid differences in table form)*

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain the two types of accounts in Ethereum. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Externally Owned Account (EOA):** Controlled by a private key; can initiate transactions; has a balance and nonce; contains no code.
2. **Contract Account:** Controlled by its deployed bytecode and storage; cannot start transactions on its own; executes only when triggered by an EOA or another contract; holds its own balance and persistent storage.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for EOA + Contract Account, 1 mark for contrast/extra clarity)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM). Discuss the roles of Stack, Memory, and Storage in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A user deploys a smart contract. The deployment transaction consumes **2,100,000 gas** with a **gas price of 30 Gwei** and sends **0.8 ETH** along with the deployment. The deployed bytecode is **8,000 bytes** long. Compute:
1. Total transaction fee in Wei and ETH.
2. Total amount deducted from sender's account.
3. Cost per byte of deployed code in Gwei.
**(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) EVM Architecture (7 marks):**

The EVM is a **simple, stack-based, quasi-Turing-complete** 256-bit execution environment that runs identically on every Ethereum node. Its key components are:

1. **Stack (2 marks):** A LIFO data structure holding 32-byte items, with a maximum depth of 1024. All arithmetic, comparison, and bitwise operations (ADD, MUL, GT, AND, etc.) operate on the top stack items. Solidity local variables of value types are pushed here.

2. **Memory (2 marks):** A volatile, byte-addressable, linearly-expandable buffer that is **cleared** after each message call. It is used to hold function arguments, return values, and complex data structures (arrays, strings) during execution. Expansion costs grow quadratically to discourage large memory usage.

3. **Storage (3 marks):** A **persistent 256-bit key-value store** that is part of the global Ethereum world state (Merkle Patricia Trie). Once written, data persists forever (unless explicitly deleted). The `SSTORE` opcode is the **most expensive** EVM operation — 20,000 gas for a fresh write, 5,000 gas for an update, and a 4,800 gas refund on deletion. This is why storage is the "scarce resource" of Ethereum.

Supporting components: **Calldata** (read-only input buffer), **Code** (immutable bytecode ROM), and **Logs** (Bloom-filter-indexed event data).

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- `gasUsed` = 2,100,000
- `gasPrice` = 30 Gwei = 30 × 10⁹ Wei
- `value` = 0.8 ETH = 0.8 × 10¹⁸ Wei
- `bytecode size` = 8,000 bytes

**Step 1 — Transaction Fee:**

$$
\text{Fee}_{\text{Wei}} = \text{gasUsed} \times \text{gasPrice}
$$

$$
\text{Fee}_{\text{Wei}} = 2{,}100{,}000 \times 30 \times 10^9
$$

$$
= 63{,}000{,}000 \times 10^9 = 6.3 \times 10^{16}\ \text{Wei}
$$

In ETH:

$$
\text{Fee}_{\text{ETH}} = \frac{6.3 \times 10^{16}}{10^{18}} = 0.063\ \text{ETH}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Deduction:**

$$
\text{Total}_{\text{Wei}} = 6.3 \times 10^{16} + 0.8 \times 10^{18}
$$

$$
= 0.063 \times 10^{18} + 0.8 \times 10^{18} = 0.863 \times 10^{18}\ \text{Wei}
$$

$$
= 8.63 \times 10^{17}\ \text{Wei} = 0.863\ \text{ETH}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost per Byte:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{perByte}} = \frac{6.3 \times 10^{16}}{8{,}000} = 7.875 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Wei/byte}
$$

In Gwei:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{perByte}} = \frac{7.875 \times 10^{12}}{10^9} = 7{,}875\ \text{Gwei/byte}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- **(i)** Fee = **6.3 × 10¹⁶ Wei = 0.063 ETH**
- **(ii)** Total Deduction = **8.63 × 10¹⁷ Wei = 0.863 ETH**
- **(iii)** Cost/byte = **7,875 Gwei/byte**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Gas** in Ethereum. Why is it necessary? Describe the EIP-1559 fee market mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Solidity smart contract named `TokenBank` that allows users to deposit and withdraw Ether. The contract must:
1. Track each user's balance using a mapping.
2. Emit a `Deposit` event with sender's address and amount.
3. Reject withdrawals exceeding the user's balance.
4. Allow only the contract deployer to perform an emergency sweep of all funds.
**(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Gas & EIP-1559 (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Gas is a unit that measures the **computational effort** required to execute specific operations on the EVM. Every opcode has a fixed gas cost (e.g., ADD = 3, SSTORE = 20,000). Transactions specify a `gasLimit` and `gasPrice`.

**Why Gas is Necessary (2 marks):**
1. Prevents infinite loops and DoS attacks (bounded execution).
2. Compensates validators for resource consumption (CPU, storage, bandwidth).
3. Allocates scarce block space via a market mechanism.
4. Discourages storage bloat (SSTORE is expensive).

**EIP-1559 Mechanism (3 marks):** Implemented in the London hard fork (August 2021), it replaced simple first-price auctions with a hybrid model:
- `baseFee` — algorithmically adjusted per block (target 50% utilization), **burned** (removed from supply).
- `priorityFee` (tip) — paid to the validator, incentivizes inclusion.
- `maxFeePerGas` — sender's upper bound; actual fee = `min(maxFeePerGas, baseFee + priorityFee)`.
- `maxPriorityFeePerGas` — cap on tip.

Formula:

$$
\text{EffectiveGasPrice} = \text{baseFee} + \text{priorityFee}
$$

$$
\text{TxFee} = \text{gasUsed} \times \text{EffectiveGasPrice}
$$

---

**(b) Solidity Code (7 marks):**

```solidity
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

contract TokenBank {
    address public owner;
    mapping(address => uint256) public balances;

    event Deposit(address indexed sender, uint256 amount);
    event Withdrawal(address indexed receiver, uint256 amount);
    event EmergencySweep(address indexed to, uint256 amount);

    constructor() {
        owner = msg.sender; // Set deployer as owner
    }

    // Deposit Ether into the bank
    function deposit() public payable {
        require(msg.value > 0, "Must send some ETH");
        balances[msg.sender] += msg.value;
        emit Deposit(msg.sender, msg.value);
    }

    // Withdraw a specified amount
    function withdraw(uint256 _amount) public {
        require(balances[msg.sender] >= _amount, "Insufficient balance");
        balances[msg.sender] -= _amount;
        (bool success, ) = payable(msg.sender).call{value: _amount}("");
        require(success, "Transfer failed");
        emit Withdrawal(msg.sender, _amount);
    }

    // Emergency sweep: only owner can call
    function emergencySweep() public {
        require(msg.sender == owner, "Only owner can sweep");
        uint256 contractBalance = address(this).balance;
        require(contractBalance > 0, "No funds to sweep");
        (bool success, ) = payable(owner).call{value: contractBalance}("");
        require(success, "Sweep failed");
        emit EmergencySweep(owner, contractBalance);
    }
}
```

**Marking Scheme:**
- SPDX & pragma (0.5)
- Owner + mapping + event declaration (1.5)
- Constructor (0.5)
- Deposit function with event emission (1.5)
- Withdraw with require + state update (1.5)
- Emergency sweep with access control (1.5)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Smart Contract Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `tx.origin` with `msg.sender`** — `tx.origin` is always the *original* EOA, while `msg.sender` is the *immediate* caller. Using `tx.origin` for authorization is a critical security flaw (loses 2-3 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting that gas is consumed even on revert** (in legacy gas mode) — students often write "user gets refund" which is wrong unless using `require` (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Not converting Gwei to Wei correctly** — common slip: leaving Gwei value in the final answer. Always convert to Wei (or explicitly state units).
> 4. **Wrong address derivation formula** — confusing `CREATE` (uses RLP of sender+nonce) with `CREATE2` (uses salt + init code hash).
> 5. **Ignoring the `payable` modifier** — forgetting `payable(msg.sender).transfer()` causes compile errors. Mention it explicitly.
> 6. **State changes after external calls** — reentrancy pattern. Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions in withdrawal logic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Smart contract** = immutable, deterministic program on the EVM at a 20-byte address.
- **Two account types:** EOA (key-controlled) vs **Contract Account** (code-controlled).
- **EVM** = stack-based, 256-bit, quasi-Turing-complete, deterministic.
- **Three data locations:** `storage` (persistent, expensive), `memory` (volatile), `calldata` (read-only, cheapest).
- **Gas** = metering unit; `TxFee = gasUsed × effectiveGasPrice`. EIP-1559 burns baseFee, pays priorityFee to validators.
- **EVM components:** Stack (1024 deep), Memory, Storage, Calldata, Code, Logs.
- **Solidity visibility:** `public`, `external`, `internal`, `private`.
- **Error handling:** `require` (refunds gas, user error), `assert` (no refund, internal bug), `revert` (manual rollback).
- **Contract address formula (CREATE):** `keccak256(RLP([sender, nonce]))[12:32]`.
- **Unit conversions:** 1 ETH = 10¹⁸ Wei = 10⁹ Gwei.
- **Token standards:** ERC-20 (fungible), ERC-721 (NFT), ERC-1155 (multi).
- **Vulnerabilities to remember:** Reentrancy, integer overflow, tx.origin, front-running.
- **Best practice:** Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern, use `msg.sender` not `tx.origin`, use Solidity ≥ 0.8.
- **Global variables to know:** `msg.sender`, `msg.value`, `block.timestamp`, `block.number`, `tx.origin`, `gasleft()`.
- **Immutability:** Once deployed, contract code **cannot be modified** — use proxy patterns for upgradeability.
- **Constructor:** Special function executed **only once** during deployment; used to initialize state and set the owner.
- **EIP-1559 components:** `baseFee` (burned), `priorityFee` (tip to validator), `maxFeePerGas` (sender cap).
- **EVM opcodes cost:** SSTORE fresh = 20,000 gas; MLOAD/MSTORE = 3 gas; ADD = 3 gas; SHA3 = 30 + 6 per word.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decentralised Applications in Ethereum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decentralised Applications in Ethereum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Decentralized Application (DApp)** in the Ethereum ecosystem is a distributed application whose backend logic runs on a decentralized peer-to-peer network (the Ethereum blockchain or its layer-2 rollups) through one or more **Smart Contracts**, while the frontend is rendered using conventional web technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript/React). The application's state and business rules are cryptographically enforced, trust-minimized, and replicated across every full node in the network, eliminating the need for a central authority, server, or intermediary.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine on a Global Ledger"

Imagine a vending machine, but instead of being owned by a corporation, **its entire operating code is pasted onto a giant public notice board that millions of strangers around the world hold an identical copy of**.

- You drop a coin (Ether / **ETH**) into the slot.
- The machine's rules ("if you insert ₹10 and press B3, dispense one chocolate") are written in code, locked, and visible to everyone.
- No single person can tamper with the machine, change the price, or steal the inventory.
- Every successful purchase is recorded as a permanent entry in a global, shared notebook that nobody can erase or edit.

> **That notebook is the Ethereum blockchain, and the vending machine is a DApp.** The code running it is a Smart Contract, and the entire setup operates without a Coca-Cola headquarters telling it what to do.

### 1.2 Why DApps? — Core Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — The Four Pillars of a DApp:**
> 1. **Decentralized** — Backed by a peer-to-peer blockchain; no single point of failure or control.
> 2. **Deterministic** — Same input always produces the same output across every node.
> 3. **Turing Complete** — Smart contracts (Solidity, Vyper) can implement arbitrary logic, given enough gas.
> 4. **Isolated** — Execution happens in the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**, a sandboxed runtime, keeping the host node secure.

| Property | Centralized App (Web 2.0) | Decentralized App (Web 3.0) |
|---|---|---|
| **Backend Hosting** | AWS, Azure, Google Cloud | Ethereum Mainnet / L2 (Arbitrum, Optimism) |
| **Database** | MySQL, MongoDB (admin-controlled) | Blockchain ledger (immutable) |
| **Logic Execution** | Node.js / Django on a server | EVM executing Smart Contracts |
| **Trust Model** | Trust the company | Trust the code & consensus |
| **Censorship Resistance** | Weak (can be shut down) | Strong (runs as long as 1 node exists) |
| **Payment Layer** | Stripe, PayPal | Native **ETH** or **ERC-20** tokens |

### 1.3 Anatomy of an Ethereum DApp

A typical DApp is a **three-tier hybrid architecture** — only the backend and trust layer are decentralized; the frontend is ordinary web code.

$$
\text{DApp}_{\text{Ethereum}} = \underbrace{\text{HTML/JS/React Frontend}}_{\text{User Interface}} + \underbrace{\text{Web3.js / Ethers.js}}_{\text{Bridge Layer}} + \underbrace{\text{Smart Contracts (Solidity)}}_{\text{On-Chain Backend}} + \underbrace{\text{Ethereum Blockchain}}_{\text{State Storage}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "F-W-C-B" (Front, Web3, Contract, Blockchain):** Read top-to-bottom. User sees the **Front**end, clicks trigger a **Web3** library call, the wallet signs a transaction, the **Contract** executes, and the new state is committed to the **Blockchain**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DApp Architectural Stack (Layer-by-Layer Breakdown)

The Ethereum DApp stack is a **5-layer pyramid**. Each layer encapsulates the one beneath it, isolating complexity and providing well-defined interfaces.

**Layer 1 — Smart Contract Layer (The "Brain")**
- Written primarily in **Solidity** (`.sol` files).
- Compiled to **EVM bytecode** — a stack-based, 256-bit word instruction set.
- Deployed to the chain via a special `CREATE` or `CREATE2` opcode; the resulting contract has a permanent **20-byte address** (e.g., `0xAb5801a7d398351b8bE11C439e05C5B3259aeC9B`).
- Holds state in **storage slots** (each a 256-bit word), consumes **gas** for every opcode.

**Layer 2 — Blockchain / Consensus Layer (The "Notary")**
- Ethereum's proof-of-stake consensus (since the **Merge**, Sept 2022) finalizes blocks in ~12–15 seconds.
- Stores every transaction and state root in a **Merkle Patricia Trie**, allowing cryptographic proofs of inclusion.

**Layer 3 — Data Propagation Layer (The "Postal Service")**
- **Gossip protocol** (libp2p / devp2p) — every node forwards new transactions and blocks to a random subset of peers.
- Ensures eventual global visibility; an event emitted in Tokyo reaches a node in Berlin in under a second.

**Layer 4 — Client API Layer (The "Reception Desk")**
- **JSON-RPC endpoints** expose methods like `eth_call`, `eth_sendRawTransaction`, `eth_getLogs`.
- Nodes like **Geth**, **Nethermind**, **Besu**, and **Reth** speak this protocol.
- Service providers like **Infura**, **Alchemy**, and **QuickNode** offer hosted RPC as a service.

**Layer 5 — Application Frontend (The "Storefront")**
- Browser dApps connect to user wallets (e.g., **MetaMask**, **Rabby**, **WalletConnect**) via the **EIP-1193** provider standard.
- JavaScript libraries — **Web3.js**, **Ethers.js** (v6), **viem** — abstract the JSON-RPC layer.
- Frameworks like **React** + **wagmi** + **RainbowKit** accelerate UI development.

### 2.2 Core Operational Lifecycle of a DApp Transaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** Examiners love asking the *exact sequence* of events from a user clicking "Swap" to the state being updated. Memorize these 6 steps:

1. **Trigger** — User interacts with the frontend (e.g., clicks "Mint NFT").
2. **Wallet Signing** — Frontend calls `provider.request({ method: 'eth_sendTransaction', params: [...] })`. MetaMask prompts the user to sign.
3. **Broadcast** — The signed RLP-encoded transaction is propagated via the **devp2p** gossip network to the **txpool (mempool)** of every node.
4. **Block Inclusion** — A validator (builder) selects the transaction, orders it in a block, and proposes it. The transaction pays a **priority fee** (tip) and a **base fee** that is burned.
5. **EVM Execution** — Every full node executes the transaction deterministically against the **current state root**, consuming gas per opcode.
6. **State Commitment** — The resulting state root is included in the block, and after ~12.8 minutes (2 epochs) the block is **justified**; after ~6.4 minutes more, **finalized** (cannot be reverted without burning ~$1B+ in ETH).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Gas — Economic Security

Ethereum's gas mechanism is a brilliant **DoS-prevention + resource-pricing** model. Every opcode has a fixed gas cost. The user must pre-pay `gasLimit × (baseFee + maxPriorityFeePerGas)`.

$$
\text{Tx Fee (in Wei)} = \underbrace{\text{gasUsed}}_{\text{actual EVM work}} \times \underbrace{(\text{baseFee} + \text{priorityFee})}_{\text{dynamic price per unit}}
$$

- **baseFee** — algorithmically adjusted up or down each block to target **50% full blocks**. Burned (deflationary).
- **priorityFee** — tip to the validator; economic incentive for inclusion.
- **maxFeePerGas** — user's absolute ceiling; the user is refunded the difference.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** This design lets a 10-gwei "quiet Sunday" transaction cost a few cents, while the same transaction during an NFT mint on a hot Saturday night may cost \$50+. The market self-regulates without an admin.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Standards Reference

| Symbol / Token | Definition | Typical Value / Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas** | Unit measuring computational effort of EVM operations | 1 gas = 1 opcode step | Core economic primitive |
| **Wei** | Smallest unit of ETH | $1 \text{ Wei} = 10^{-18} \text{ ETH}$ | Used in all smart-contract math |
| **Gwei** | Common fee denomination | $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9 \text{ Wei}$ | What users actually see in MetaMask |
| **Block Time** | Average time between blocks | **12 seconds** | Affects UX latency |
| **Block Gas Limit** | Total gas allowed per block | **30,000,000** (post-2024) | Caps network throughput |
| **Finality** | Time to absolute immutability | ~**12.8 min** (2 epochs) | Important for high-value dApps |
| **Contract Address** | Unique 20-byte identifier | Hex: `0x` + 40 chars | How users reference a DApp |
| **ERC-20** | Fungible token standard | — | Used by 99% of stablecoins |
| **ERC-721** | Non-fungible token (NFT) standard | — | Powers digital art, deeds |
| **ERC-1155** | Multi-token standard (game items) | — | Gas-efficient batched transfers |
| **EIP-1193** | Wallet provider JS API | — | Standard for dApp ↔ wallet |
| **EIP-4337** | Account Abstraction (smart wallets) | — | Next-gen UX (gasless txs) |

### 2.5 Token Standards — The "Plugins" of the DApp Ecosystem

A DApp is rarely just one contract. It typically composes multiple **ERC (Ethereum Request for Comments)** standard contracts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Tested Standards in KTU Exams:**

- **ERC-20** — Fungible tokens (USDC, DAI). Functions: `totalSupply`, `balanceOf`, `transfer`, `transferFrom`, `approve`, `allowance`.
- **ERC-721** — NFTs. Each tokenId is unique. Adds `ownerOf`, `tokenURI` (often pointing to IPFS metadata).
- **ERC-1155** — Hybrid. A single contract can mint both fungible (id 0 = 1000 gold coins) and non-fungible (id 1 = legendary sword) tokens in a single transaction.
- **ERC-4626** — Tokenized vaults (DeFi yield-bearing wrappers).

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Relevance:** A KTU graduate understanding DApp architecture can contribute to:
> - **DeFi** (Uniswap, Aave) — algorithmic trading without brokers.
> - **Supply Chain** (IBM Food Trust, Maersk TradeLens) — provenance tracking.
> - **DAOs** (MakerDAO, Aragon) — corporate governance as code.
> - **Digital Identity** (Ethereum Name Service, Spruce ID) — self-sovereign logins.
> - **Gaming & Metaverse** (Decentraland, Axie Infinity) — true player ownership of in-game assets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (COs)

| KTU Bloom Level | Cognitive Action | DApp Sub-Topic Mapped | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Remember** | Recall definitions | DApp, EVM, Smart Contract, Gas | 2–3 marks (Part A) |
| **L2 — Understand** | Explain architecture | Frontend ↔ Web3 ↔ Contract flow | 3–5 marks |
| **L3 — Apply** | Trace a transaction | User click → state change lifecycle | 7 marks (Part B) |
| **L4 — Analyze** | Compare ERC standards | ERC-20 vs 721 vs 1155 trade-offs | 7 marks (Part B) |
| **L5 — Evaluate** | Critique design choices | Why gas? Why EIP-1559? Why PoS? | 14 marks (full) |
| **L6 — Create** | Design a DApp | Architecture for supply-chain DApp | Open-ended |

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Gas Cost of a Token Transfer

A common KTU numerical: "Calculate the gas cost of an ERC-20 `transfer` from Alice to Bob, given the current base fee and priority fee."

**Given (hypothetical board values):**
- Base fee: **25 Gwei**
- Priority fee: **2 Gwei**
- ERC-20 `transfer` gas usage: **~65,000 gas** (typical: 51,500 base + storage writes for two `balanceOf` updates)

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$
\text{Effective gas price} = \text{baseFee} + \text{priorityFee}
$$

$$
= 25 \text{ Gwei} + 2 \text{ Gwei} = 27 \text{ Gwei per gas}
$$

$$
\text{Total fee in Gwei} = \text{gasUsed} \times \text{effective price}
$$

$$
= 65{,}000 \times 27 = 1{,}755{,}000 \text{ Gwei}
$$

$$
\text{Convert to ETH} = 1{,}755{,}000 \times 10^{-9} \text{ ETH} = 0.001755 \text{ ETH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Text Explanation of Each Step:**
> 1. We sum the **base fee** (burned) and **priority fee** (tip) to get the validator's effective price per unit of gas.
> 2. We multiply by the **gasUsed** (actual computational work the EVM performed) — note this is *not* the gas limit, but the actual consumption reported after execution.
> 3. The result is in **Gwei**; dividing by $10^9$ converts to **ETH** (since $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9 \text{ Wei} = 10^{-9} \text{ ETH}$).
> 4. If ETH = ₹2,00,000 at the time, the user paid ₹351 for the transfer.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Block Gas Limit vs Throughput

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why can't Ethereum process 100,000 transactions per second?** This is a classic 14-mark derivation question.

**Step 1 — Identify block parameters:**
$$
\text{Block Time} = 12 \text{ s}, \quad \text{Block Gas Limit} = 30{,}000{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical max transactions per block:**
$$
\text{TPS}_{\text{max}} = \frac{\text{Block Gas Limit}}{\text{Block Time} \times \text{Gas per Tx}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for a simple ETH transfer (21,000 gas):**
$$
\text{TPS}_{\text{max, ETH}} = \frac{30{,}000{,}000}{12 \times 21{,}000} = \frac{30{,}000{,}000}{252{,}000} \approx 119 \text{ TPS}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute for a complex Uniswap swap (~150,000 gas):**
$$
\text{TPS}_{\text{max, Uniswap}} = \frac{30{,}000{,}000}{12 \times 150{,}000} \approx 16.7 \text{ TPS}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Visa processes ~**65,000 TPS** on average. Ethereum L1's 15–20 TPS for complex dApps is a *design choice* — decentralization and security over raw speed. The throughput is recovered via **Layer 2 rollups** (Optimism, Arbitrum, zkSync) which batch thousands of L2 transactions into a single L1 transaction, effectively pushing practical TPS into the thousands.

### 3.4 Comprehensive DApp Transaction Lifecycle — 8-Phase Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Explain how a DApp transaction is processed," structure your answer in these 8 phases. Most students write 3–4 and lose marks.

**Phase 1 — User Authorization**
- Wallet (MetaMask) holds the user's **private key** in a vault. It never exposes the key to the dApp frontend.

**Phase 2 — Transaction Construction**
- Frontend calls `eth_sendTransaction` with `{ to, value, data, gasLimit, maxFeePerGas, maxPriorityFeePerGas, nonce }`.

**Phase 3 — Local Signing**
- MetaMask uses **ECDSA (secp256k1)** to sign the transaction. The output is a tuple `(v, r, s)` plus the raw RLP-encoded bytes.

**Phase 4 — Network Propagation**
- The signed tx enters the **mempool** of the user's RPC node, which gossips it to ~8–12 peers; full mesh visibility in ~300 ms.

**Phase 5 — Block Construction**
- Validators (proposers + builders via **MEV-Boost**) construct a block. Transactions are typically ordered by **effective tip** (priority fee) per gas.

**Phase 6 — Consensus & Block Finalization**
- Block is attested by **≥ 2/3 of staked ETH**; after 2 epochs, it is *finalized* (cannot revert without burning massive stake).

**Phase 7 — EVM Execution (at every full node)**
- Each node re-executes the transaction. This redundancy is why DApps are trustless — every participant verifies.

**Phase 8 — State & Receipt Storage**
- Updated `stateRoot` is committed. Logs are emitted via **EVENTS** (e.g., `Transfer(address,address,uint256)`). Frontends listen via `eth_subscribe("logs")` or use **The Graph** for historical indexing.

### 3.5 Comparison Table — DApp vs Traditional App (Detailed)

| Dimension | Traditional App (e.g., Uber) | Ethereum DApp (e.g., Uniswap) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Anchor** | Company + servers + legal contracts | Cryptography + open-source code + consensus |
| **Server Cost** | \$100k+/month (cloud) | Gas fees paid per action by user |
| **Censorship** | Govt can seize servers | No single entity to coerce |
| **Downtime** | Possible (server crash) | 99.99%+ (runs as long as Ethereum lives) |
| **Data Ownership** | Company owns it | User owns it via their private key |
| **Update Cycle** | Dev pushes update anytime | Contract is **immutable** (or uses proxy patterns) |
| **Monetization** | Ads, subscriptions, fees | Protocol fees, governance token, MEV |
| **Performance** | Sub-100 ms response | 12 s block, 12 min finality |
| **Privacy** | Pseudo-anonymous at best | Wallet address is pseudonymous; tx is fully public |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DApp Architecture — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef userLayer fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef frontendLayer fill:#E0F4FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000
    classDef bridgeLayer fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef contractLayer fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef chainLayer fill:#C1FFC1,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef walletLayer fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#C71585,color:#000

    userA[End User]:::userLayer
    browserA[Browser - React Frontend]:::frontendLayer
    wagmiClient[Wagmi - React Hooks Layer]:::bridgeLayer
    ethersLib[Ethers.js v6 - Core Web3 Lib]:::bridgeLayer
    walletProv[MetaMask - EIP-1193 Provider]:::walletLayer
    rpcNode[Hosted RPC Node - Alchemy or Infura]:::frontendLayer
    mempoolA[Ethereum Mempool - devp2p gossip]:::chainLayer
    validatorA[Validator Node - PoS Consensus]:::chainLayer
    evmA[EVM - Sandboxed Execution]:::chainLayer
    contractA[Smart Contract - Solidity Bytecode]:::contractLayer
    stateA[State Trie - Updated State Root]:::chainLayer
    ipfsA[IPFS - Off-chain Metadata Storage]:::frontendLayer

    userA -->|clicks Mint| browserA
    browserA -->|reads state| wagmiClient
    wagmiClient -->|JSON-RPC calls| ethersLib
    ethersLib -->|eth_sendTransaction| walletProv
    walletProv -->|ECDSA sign| walletProv
    walletProv -->|broadcast signed tx| rpcNode
    rpcNode -->|gossip| mempoolA
    mempoolA -->|selected by builder| validatorA
    validatorA -->|execute| evmA
    evmA -->|call| contractA
    contractA -->|read metadata| ipfsA
    contractA -->|emit event| evmA
    evmA -->|new state root| stateA
    stateA -->|block attested| validatorA
    validatorA -->|finalized in 2 epochs| rpcNode
    rpcNode -->|log subscription| wagmiClient
    wagmiClient -->|re-render UI| browserA
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — The Lifecycle of a Uniswap-Style DApp Swap

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor User as Trader
    participant FE as React Frontend
    participant WAL as MetaMask Wallet
    participant RPC as RPC Node (Alchemy)
    participant MEM as Mempool
    participant VAL as Validator (PoS)
    participant EVM as EVM Runtime
    participant CP as Smart Contract Pair
    participant ST as State Trie

    User->>FE: 1. Enters swap amount
    FE->>WAL: 2. eth_sendTransaction(...)
    WAL->>User: 3. Popup: Confirm + gas estimate
    User->>WAL: 4. Approves + ECDSA sign
    WAL->>RPC: 5. Broadcast signed raw tx
    RPC->>MEM: 6. Gossip to peers
    MEM->>VAL: 7. Block builder includes tx
    VAL->>EVM: 8. Execute in sandbox
    EVM->>CP: 9. Call swap() function
    CP->>ST: 10. Update reserves (x*y=k)
    CP-->>EVM: 11. Emit Swap event
    EVM-->>VAL: 12. Gas consumed = 138,402
    VAL-->>RPC: 13. Block proposed + attested
    RPC-->>FE: 14. eth_getTransactionReceipt
    FE->>User: 15. UI shows "Swap complete"
    Note over VAL,ST: Block finalized in ~12.8 min
```

```mermaid
    L1 --> L2

    L3 --> L4

    L2A[ERC-20 - Fungible Token]:::layer2

    L2C[ERC-1155 - Multi-Token]:::layer2
    L2D[ERC-4626 - Tokenized Vault]:::layer2

    L2 --> L2A
    L2 --> L2B
    L2 --> L2C
    L2 --> L2D
```
