## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Decentralized Application (DApp) and list any four key characteristics that distinguish it from a traditional application.**

> **Model Answer (Examiner-Key Format):**

A **Decentralized Application (DApp)** is an application whose backend logic runs on a decentralized peer-to-peer blockchain network (such as Ethereum) via one or more **smart contracts**, instead of on centralized servers.

**Four Key Characteristics:**
1. **Open Source & Autonomous** — Once deployed, the contract code runs without an owner; no single party can unilaterally modify its behavior.
2. **Decentralized Storage** — All state is replicated across every full node; no single point of failure.
3. **Cryptographic Security** — User authentication is via private-key signatures, not usernames/passwords.
4. **Tokenized Incentives** — Native crypto-economic incentives (gas, governance tokens) align validator and user behavior.

> **[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for characteristics]**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Gas in Ethereum? Why is it essential for the operation of a DApp?**

> **Model Answer:**

**Gas** is the unit that measures the computational effort required to execute operations on the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM). Every opcode (e.g., `ADD`, `SSTORE`, `CALL`) has a fixed gas cost. Users must pay for gas in **Wei** (fractions of ETH) when invoking a smart contract.

**Why it is essential:**
1. **Prevents Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks** — Malicious infinite loops are killed once gas runs out, preserving network liveness.
2. **Prices computational resources** — Allocates scarce block space via a fee market (EIP-1559).
3. **Compensates validators** — Incentivizes honest block production and network security.
4. **Enables Turing-complete execution** — The "halt" problem is solved by the gas limit acting as a runtime bound.

> **[3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for at least 3 justifications]**

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 3 Internal Choice)

> **Note to students:** As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions within a module offer an **internal choice**. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as two fully independent alternatives. Examiners may set either.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Architecture & Transaction Lifecycle
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 3 Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete architectural stack of an Ethereum DApp**. Clearly describe the role of each layer — from the user-facing frontend to the on-chain state storage — and identify the technologies used at each tier.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Trace the **end-to-end lifecycle of a DApp transaction**, from the moment a user clicks "Swap" on the frontend to the moment the new state becomes finalized on-chain. Include all 8 phases: authorization, construction, signing, propagation, block inclusion, consensus, EVM execution, and state commitment.

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Mark Detailed Answer:**

The Ethereum DApp stack is conventionally modelled as a **5-layer pyramid**:

**Layer 1 — Frontend (Presentation)**
- Technologies: **React, Vue, Next.js, SvelteKit**.
- Renders the UI; reads blockchain state via the Web3 library; constructs transactions; delegates signing to the wallet.
- Hosted on **IPFS, Fleek, or Vercel** for censorship resistance.

**Layer 2 — Wallet / Provider Layer**
- Technologies: **MetaMask, WalletConnect, Rabby, Frame**.
- Implements **EIP-1193** JavaScript provider standard.
- Holds the user's **private key** in an encrypted vault; signs transactions locally.

**Layer 3 — Web3 Bridge Library**
- Technologies: **Ethers.js v6, Web3.js, viem, wagmi**.
- Translates JavaScript calls into **JSON-RPC** requests (`eth_call`, `eth_sendTransaction`, `eth_estimateGas`).
- Decodes ABIs (Application Binary Interface) into human-readable objects.

**Layer 4 — Smart Contract Layer**
- Languages: **Solidity 0.8.x, Vyper 0.3.x, Yul**.
- Compiled to **EVM bytecode**; deployed via `CREATE`/`CREATE2`.
- Holds business logic, state variables, and emits **EVENTS** for off-chain listeners.

**Layer 5 — Blockchain / Consensus Layer**
- EVM executes contracts deterministically; state is stored in a **Merkle Patricia Trie**.
- Consensus via **Gasper (Casper FFG + LMD GHOST)** — PoS since the Merge (Sept 2022).
- 12-second block time, 30M gas limit, ~12.8 min finality.

*(Diagram reference: See Section 4.1 for a 12-component Mermaid schematic.)*

**[7 marks: 1 mark for diagram + 1.2 marks per layer]**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Mark Detailed Answer (8-Phase Trace):**

| # | Phase | What Happens | Key Actor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Authorization** | Frontend reads user's wallet address via `eth_requestAccounts` | User → MetaMask |
| 2 | **Construction** | Frontend builds tx: `{to, value, data, gas, nonce}` | Web3 library |
| 3 | **Signing** | User confirms; wallet signs with **ECDSA secp256k1** | MetaMask |
| 4 | **Propagation** | Signed tx enters local mempool; gossiped via **devp2p** | Full node |
| 5 | **Block Inclusion** | Block builder orders tx by tip; proposes block | Validator (PoS) |
| 6 | **Consensus** | ≥ 2/3 staked ETH attest; finalization in 2 epochs (~12.8 min) | Beacon chain |
| 7 | **EVM Execution** | Every full node re-executes bytecode; gas consumed | EVM |
| 8 | **State Commitment** | New `stateRoot` written; **EVENTS** emitted; logs indexed | State Trie + The Graph |

> **Examiner's Marking Insight:** Award 1 mark for each correctly identified phase; bonus 0.5 for mentioning the actual technology/algorithm at that phase (e.g., ECDSA, devp2p, Merkle Patricia Trie).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Token Standards & Economic Model
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3 Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **ERC-20, ERC-721, and ERC-1155** token standards. For each, state: (i) the type of asset represented, (ii) the key interface functions, and (iii) one real-world use case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **EIP-1559's fee market mechanism**. Derive the formula for total transaction cost and illustrate, with a numerical example, how a user overpaying is refunded.

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Mark Comparison Table:**

| Feature | **ERC-20** | **ERC-721** | **ERC-1155** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Asset Type** | Fungible (identical units) | Non-Fungible (unique) | Multi-Token (both) |
| **Key Functions** | `totalSupply`, `balanceOf`, `transfer`, `approve`, `transferFrom` | `name`, `symbol`, `tokenURI`, `ownerOf`, `approve`, `safeTransferFrom` | `safeBatchTransferFrom`, `balanceOfBatch`, `safeTransferFrom` |
| **Idempotency** | Same `decimals`, no per-unit ID | Unique `tokenId` (uint256) | Multiple `id`s in one contract |
| **Gas Efficiency** | Moderate (1-to-1 transfer) | High (1 NFT per call) | **Excellent** (batch hundreds in 1 tx) |
| **Use Case** | USDC stablecoin, DAI, UNI token | CryptoPunks, BAYC, ENS names | Game items (Enjin), exchange vouchers |
| **Storage Cost** | 1 SSTORE for balance | 2 SSTOREs (owner + token URI) | 1 SSTORE per id per owner |

*Real-world examples:*
- **ERC-20** → **USDC** (Circle's stablecoin) for DeFi trading.
- **ERC-721** → **Bored Ape Yacht Club** for digital art.
- **ERC-1155** → **Enjin** game items where 1 sword and 1000 arrows coexist in one contract.

**[7 marks: ~2.3 per standard, with marks for type + functions + example]**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Mark EIP-1559 Derivation:**

**Conceptual Setup:** Before EIP-1559 (London hard-fork, Aug 2021), Ethereum used a **first-price auction** — users blindly bid, leading to overpayment. EIP-1559 introduced a **base fee that is algorithmically adjusted** and **burned** (not paid to validators), plus an optional **priority tip**.

**Core Formula:**

$$
\text{Tx Fee} = \text{gasUsed} \times (\text{baseFee} + \text{priorityFee})
$$

Where:
- $\text{baseFee}$ is the network-set price, burned.
- $\text{priorityFee}$ is the tip to the validator.
- The user specifies $\text{maxFeePerGas}$ (ceiling) and $\text{maxPriorityFeePerGas}$ (max tip).

**Refund Mechanism:**

$$
\text{Refund} = (\text{maxFeePerGas} - \text{baseFee} - \text{priorityFee}) \times \text{gasUsed}
$$

**Numerical Example (Board-Style):**
- User sets $\text{maxFeePerGas} = 50 \text{ Gwei}$
- User sets $\text{maxPriorityFeePerGas} = 2 \text{ Gwei}$
- Actual $\text{baseFee}$ at inclusion = **20 Gwei**
- $\text{gasUsed} = 21{,}000$ (simple ETH transfer)

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$
\text{Effective gas price} = 20 + 2 = 22 \text{ Gwei}
$$

$$
\text{Actual cost} = 21{,}000 \times 22 = 462{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 0.000462 \text{ ETH}
$$

$$
\text{User's max payment} = 21{,}000 \times 50 = 1{,}050{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 0.00105 \text{ ETH}
$$

$$
\text{Refund} = 0.00105 - 0.000462 = 0.000588 \text{ ETH}
$$

> **Text Explanation of Each Step:**
> 1. The **effective price** the user pays is the *minimum* of (a) their max and (b) `baseFee + tip`. Since the base was lower than expected, the user pays only the actual market rate.
> 2. The **actual cost** is `gasUsed × effective price` — paid in Wei.
> 3. The **user's max payment** assumes the worst case (`maxFeePerGas`).
> 4. The **refund** is automatically returned to the user by the protocol — they don't have to claim it.

**[7 marks: 2 marks for formula + 1 mark for variable definitions + 4 marks for the worked numerical example with explicit step-by-step arithmetic]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Gas Limit with Gas Used** — Gas *limit* is the *ceiling* the user is willing to spend; gas *used* is the *actual* EVM consumption. The fee is computed on **gasUsed**, not the limit. Students often write the wrong term and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Saying "Gas pays for transaction"** — Vague. Say: *"Gas is the unit of computational work; the user pays `gasUsed × effectiveGasPrice` in Wei, where the base fee is burned and the priority fee tips the validator."* Specificity wins marks.
> 3. **Mixing up ERC-20 and ERC-721** — They are *not* interchangeable. ERC-20 is for fungible assets (currency-like); ERC-721 is for unique assets (collectible-like). Drawing a wrong comparison table is a frequent 3-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting the wallet layer** — When asked for DApp architecture, students often draw only 3 layers (frontend-contract-blockchain). Always include the **wallet + Web3 bridge** as distinct layers; otherwise, lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing `Wei` without defining it** — Always state: *"1 ETH = $10^{18}$ Wei"* when first introducing units. Examiners expect SI-prefix-style precision.
> 6. **Skipping the EVM in lifecycle diagrams** — The EVM is *not* optional; it's the runtime that executes the contract. Missing it = -1 mark.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 60-Second Rapid Revision — Pin These 15 Bullets in Memory:**

- ☐ **DApp = Frontend (Web) + Smart Contract (EVM) + Blockchain (Ethereum)**. The frontend is *not* on-chain.
- ☐ A **smart contract** is bytecode running on the **EVM** at a fixed 20-byte address. It is **immutable** once deployed (unless a proxy pattern is used).
- ☐ **Gas** is a unit of computational work, not money. **Wei** is the smallest unit of ETH ($10^{-18}$); **Gwei** is the practical fee unit ($10^{-9}$).
- ☐ **Tx Fee = gasUsed × (baseFee + priorityFee)**. The `baseFee` is **burned**; the `priorityFee` is the validator's tip.
- ☐ **EIP-1559** introduced the burning base fee + algorithmic adjustment. Refunds happen automatically if the user overpaid.
- ☐ **ERC-20** = fungible token (USDC, DAI). **ERC-721** = NFT (CryptoPunks). **ERC-1155** = batched multi-token (game items).
- ☐ **DApp architecture** has **5 layers**: Frontend → Wallet → Web3 lib → Smart Contract → Blockchain.
- ☐ **Transaction lifecycle** has **8 phases**: Auth → Construct → Sign → Propagate → Include → Consensus → Execute → Commit.
- ☐ **Block time ≈ 12 s; finality ≈ 12.8 min (2 epochs).** State root is finalized cryptographically.
- ☐ **Gossip protocol (devp2p)** propagates transactions in ~300 ms globally.
- ☐ **EVM is deterministic + sandboxed** — same input, same output on every node. This is the bedrock of trustlessness.
- ☐ **DApps pay gas per user action**; centralized apps pay servers monthly. Trade-off: UX latency vs censorship resistance.
- ☐ **Layer 2 rollups** (Optimism, Arbitrum, zkSync) batch L2 txs into L1, boosting effective TPS from ~20 to ~4000+.
- ☐ **MetaMask** is the dominant wallet; it implements the **EIP-1193** provider API. Private keys **never** leave the wallet.
- ☐ **The Graph** is the canonical indexer for DApp events (subgraphs). Frontends query it for historical data — the chain itself doesn't have SQL queries.

> **🎯 Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Mantra:** *"If you can't draw the architecture, write the lifecycle. If you can't write the lifecycle, write the formula. If you can't write the formula, write the comparison. Always have at least one structured element — a table, a diagram, or a formula — in every 14-mark answer."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools Used in Ethereum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools Used in Ethereum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Ethereum tools* refer to the comprehensive ecosystem of software applications, libraries, frameworks, and utilities that enable developers, miners, validators, and end-users to **interact with the Ethereum blockchain network**. These tools facilitate four primary functions: **(i) node operation and network connectivity**, **(ii) smart contract authoring and compilation**, **(iii) decentralized application (DApp) development, testing, and deployment**, and **(iv) wallet management and transaction signing**.

The Ethereum toolchain is **not a single monolithic application** but a layered stack of interoperable components standardized around the **Ethereum Yellow Paper** specifications and the **Ethereum Improvement Proposal (EIP)** process. Each tool operates at a distinct layer of the stack — from low-level consensus clients to high-level user-facing wallets.

> [!NOTE]
> **EIP = Ethereum Improvement Proposal** — the formal standardization document through which protocol changes, token standards (e.g., ERC-20, ERC-721), and tool interfaces are proposed, reviewed, and ratified by the community.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ethereum Workshop"

Imagine Ethereum as a **massive, distributed skyscraper under construction** by thousands of architects worldwide. The *tools* are the specialized equipment each professional uses:

| Real-World Profession | Ethereum Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Architect's drafting table** | **Remix IDE / Hardhat** | Design and draft smart contract blueprints (Solidity code) |
| **Building inspector** | **Etherscan / Block Explorer** | Verify and inspect completed structures (deployed contracts) |
| **Construction machinery** | **Geth / Besu / Nethermind** | Operate the actual building (run an Ethereum node) |
| **Bank vault for keys** | **MetaMask / Hardware Wallets** | Securely store identities and authorize transactions |
| **3D printer for prototypes** | **Ganache / Testnet** | Build and test miniature replicas before going live |
| **Factory conveyor belt** | **Truffle / Web3.js / Ethers.js** | Automate the assembly, packaging, and shipping of DApps |
| **Cloud warehouse** | **Infura / Alchemy** | Provide shared infrastructure so you don't need your own building |

> [!TIP]
> Just as a civil engineer needs CAD software, surveying tools, and concrete mixers, an Ethereum developer needs **at minimum**: an IDE, a node client, a wallet, and a deployment framework. Mastery of the *interconnection* between these tools is what separates amateur developers from production-grade blockchain engineers.

### 1.3 Physical / Quantitative Constants in the Ethereum Toolchain

The following constants are **standardized network parameters** that every Ethereum tool must conform to:

- **Block Time (post-Merge):** $\approx$ **12 seconds** (slot duration of **1 slot = 12 s**, 1 epoch = 32 slots)
- **Gas Limit per Block:** **30,000,000 gas** (target: 15,000,000; elasticity ±2×)
- **Wei Denominations:** $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^{18}\ \text{Wei} = 10^{9}\ \text{Gwei} = 10^{6}\ \text{Finney} = 10^{3}\ \text{Szabo}$
- **Keccak-256:** The cryptographic hash function (variant of SHA-3) used as Ethereum's primary hash
- **secp256k1:** The elliptic curve used for ECDSA signature generation on addresses
- **EVM Stack Depth:** **1024** levels maximum per smart contract execution context
- **Solidity Compiler (solc):** Versions follow **semantic versioning** with a minimum pragma of `^0.8.0` for modern security checks

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Ethereum Tools — The 5-Layer Stack

Ethereum tools are best understood as a **layered stack architecture**, where each layer depends on the services of the layers below it.

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 5 ── USER INTERFACE / WALLETS                    │
│            MetaMask, MyEtherWallet, WalletConnect       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 4 ── DAPP FRAMEWORKS & LIBRARIES                 │
│            Web3.js, Ethers.js, Web3.py                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 3 ── DEVELOPMENT & DEPLOYMENT FRAMEWORKS         │
│            Truffle, Hardhat, Brownie, Foundry           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 2 ── COMPILERS & TEST ENVIRONMENTS               │
│            solc, Remix IDE, Ganache, Hardhat Network    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 1 ── NODE CLIENTS / NETWORK CONNECTIVITY         │
│            Geth, Besu, Nethermind, Erigon, Lighthouse   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 Layer 1 — Node Clients (The Foundation)

Node clients are the **gateway to the Ethereum peer-to-peer network**. They maintain a copy of the blockchain state, validate transactions, and provide **JSON-RPC API endpoints** (typically on port `8545`).

#### 2.2.1 Execution Layer Clients

| Client | Language | Maintainer | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geth** (Go-Ethereum) | Go | Ethereum Foundation | Most widely used; ~70% network share historically |
| **Besu** | Java | Hyperledger (Linux Foundation) | Enterprise-grade; supports **private/permissioned chains** |
| **Nethermind** | C# / .NET | Nethermind | High performance; extensive **monitoring** dashboards |
| **Erigon** | Go | Erigon team | Optimized for **archive node** storage efficiency |
| **OpenEthereum** (deprecated) | Rust | Parity Technologies | Succeeded by **Nethermind** post-2021 |

> [!NOTE]
> Post-**The Merge** (September 15, 2022), Ethereum's consensus mechanism shifted from Proof-of-Work to **Proof-of-Stake**. This split node clients into two roles: **Execution Layer Clients** (process transactions/contracts) and **Consensus Layer Clients** (manage validator duties). The two communicate via the **Engine API**.

#### 2.2.2 Consensus Layer Clients (Beacon Chain)

- **Lighthouse** (Rust — Sigma Prime)
- **Prysm** (Go — Prysmatic Labs)
- **Teku** (Java — ConsenSys)
- **Nimbus** (Nim — Status)

#### 2.2.3 Node Operating Modes

A node can run in three modes with different disk requirements:

| Mode | Description | Approximate Storage (2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Node** | Stores recent blocks, validates all transactions | **~1.0 TB SSD** |
| **Archive Node** | Stores full historical state of every block | **~12–15 TB SSD** |
| **Light Node** | Stores only block headers; requests data on demand | **< 100 GB** |

### 2.3 Layer 2 — Compilers and Test Environments

#### 2.3.1 Solidity Compiler (`solc`)

- **Source language:** Solidity (`.sol` files)
- **Output:** **EVM bytecode** + **Application Binary Interface (ABI)** (a JSON file describing function signatures)
- **Compilation stages:** Source code → AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) → bytecode generation
- **Versions:** `0.4.x` (legacy), `0.8.x` (current standard with built-in overflow protection)

#### 2.3.2 Remix IDE

- **Type:** Browser-based integrated development environment
- **URL:** `remix.ethereum.org`
- **Features:** In-browser compilation, debugging with breakpoints, direct deployment to **injected Web3 provider** (MetaMask), static analysis plugin integration
- **Use Case:** Ideal for **educational settings, quick prototyping, and KTU lab examinations**

#### 2.3.3 Ganache

- **Type:** Local personal blockchain for Ethereum development
- **Formerly Part of Truffle Suite** (now independent under Truffle's continued maintenance)
- **Features:** Creates 10 pre-funded test accounts with **100 test ETH** each, instant mining, deterministic block times, **forking capability** (replays mainnet state to a local instance)

### 2.4 Layer 3 — Development & Deployment Frameworks

#### 2.4.1 Truffle Suite

- **Components:** Truffle (framework), Ganache (testnet), Drizzle (front-end library)
- **Workflow:** `truffle compile` → `truffle migrate` → `truffle test`
- **Strength:** Mature ecosystem, JavaScript-based test runner (Mocha/Chai)

#### 2.4.2 Hardhat

- **Type:** Modern JavaScript/TypeScript development environment
- **Strengths:** 
  - **Hardhat Network** — built-in local Ethereum network with powerful **console.log** debugging inside Solidity
  - **Hardhat Runtime Environment (HRE)** — plugin-based architecture
  - Solidity **stack traces** for failed transactions
- **Tooling:** Integrates with Ethers.js, Waffle (testing), OpenZeppelin (security)

#### 2.4.3 Foundry

- **Type:** Rust-based toolkit (forge, cast, anvil, chisel)
- **Key Advantage:** **Solidity-native testing** (no JavaScript required for unit tests) — tests are written in Solidity itself
- **Speed:** Significantly faster compilation and test execution than JavaScript-based frameworks

### 2.5 Layer 4 — DApp Frameworks and Libraries

These libraries abstract the **JSON-RPC** calls into developer-friendly APIs.

| Library | Language | Notable Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Web3.js** | JavaScript | Original Ethereum JavaScript API; maintained by ChainSafe |
| **Ethers.js** | JavaScript/TypeScript | More modern; **ENS support**, better TypeScript typings, smaller bundle size |
| **Web3.py** | Python | Python equivalent for backend/Django/Flask integration |
| **Web3j** | Java | Java/JVM ecosystem integration |
| **Nethereum** | C# | .NET integration |

#### 2.5.1 JSON-RPC Interface Standard

All node clients expose the **JSON-RPC specification** over HTTP. Key methods include:

- `eth_blockNumber` — Returns the latest block number
- `eth_getBalance` — Returns account balance in Wei
- `eth_call` — Executes a read-only call (no state change)
- `eth_sendRawTransaction` — Broadcasts a signed transaction
- `eth_getTransactionReceipt` — Returns transaction confirmation details
- `eth_subscribe` / `eth_unsubscribe` — WebSocket subscriptions for real-time events

### 2.6 Layer 5 — Wallets and User Interfaces

A **cryptocurrency wallet** does not *store* coins — it stores **private keys** that control access to on-chain assets.

#### 2.6.1 Wallet Classification

| Type | Example | Custody | Connectivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Wallet (Browser Extension)** | MetaMask | Self-custody | Connected to DApps via **injected provider** |
| **Hot Wallet (Mobile)** | Trust Wallet, Rainbow | Self-custody | WalletConnect protocol |
| **Hardware Wallet** | Ledger, Trezor | Self-custody | Signs transactions offline; **most secure** |
| **Custodial Wallet** | Coinbase, Binance | Third-party custody | Exchange-managed keys |

#### 2.6.2 MetaMask — The Dominant Gateway

- **Developer:** ConsenSys
- **Users (2024):** **> 30 million monthly active users**
- **Function:** Acts as a **bridge between browser and Ethereum node** by injecting a global `window.ethereum` object that Web3.js / Ethers.js can call
- **Key Derivation:** Uses **BIP-39** mnemonic phrases (12 or 24 words) and **BIP-44** hierarchical deterministic (HD) wallet paths (e.g., `m/44'/60'/0'/0/0`)

#### 2.6.3 Etherscan (Block Explorer)

- **Type:** Read-only web interface to the Ethereum blockchain
- **URL:** `etherscan.io` (Mainnet), `sepolia.etherscan.io` (Sepolia testnet)
- **Provides:** Transaction history, contract verification, **token tracker**, gas tracker, **event logs** inspection

### 2.7 Smart Contract Development Standards

Standards ensure **interoperability** between contracts, wallets, and DApps.

| Standard | Purpose | Key Functions |
|---|---|---|
| **ERC-20** | Fungible tokens (e.g., USDC, DAI) | `transfer`, `balanceOf`, `approve`, `transferFrom` |
| **ERC-721** | Non-fungible tokens (NFTs) | `ownerOf`, `safeTransferFrom`, `tokenURI` |
| **ERC-1155** | Multi-token standard (FT + NFT) | `safeBatchTransferFrom` |
| **ERC-777** | Token standard with hooks | Operator-based transfers |
| **ERC-165** | Interface detection | `supportsInterface(bytes4)` |

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Wei ↔ ETH conversion | $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^{18}\ \text{Wei}$ | Finney $= 10^{12}$, Szabo $= 10^{15}$ |
| Transaction Fee (Legacy) | $\text{Fee} = \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Gas Price}$ | Gas price in **Gwei** |
| Base Fee (EIP-1559) | $\text{Fee} = (\text{Base Fee} + \text{Tip}) \times \text{Gas Used}$ | Base fee is **burned** |
| Block Number | $\text{Block}_n = \text{Block}_{n-1} + 1$ | Genesis block = **0** |
| Storage Slot Cost | $S_{\text{SSTORE}} = 20{,}000\ \text{to}\ 5{,}000\ \text{gas}$ | First-time vs update |
| Account Address | $\text{Addr} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{PubKey}_{64\ \text{bytes}})[12:32]$ | Last 20 bytes of hash |
| Contract Address | $\text{Addr}_{\text{contract}} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{RLP}(\text{sender},\ \text{nonce}))[12:32]$ | Deterministically computed |
| EVM Memory Expansion | $C_{\text{mem}} = 3a + \frac{a^2}{512}$ | $a$ = memory size in **32-byte words** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **EIP-1559** fee market mechanism (implemented in the **London Hard Fork**, August 5, 2021) is a high-frequency KTU exam topic. Students must distinguish between **Base Fee** (algorithmically adjusted, burned) and **Priority Fee / Tip** (paid to the validator).

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

> Since this topic is **system-level** rather than purely mathematical, the "derivations" below are exhaustive **analytical walkthroughs** of how Ethereum tools interact, the precise steps of a smart contract deployment, and the mathematics of gas estimation — every transition is shown explicitly as mandated.

### 3.1 End-to-End Smart Contract Deployment Workflow

**Scenario:** A developer writes a Solidity contract, tests it, and deploys it to the Sepolia testnet using Hardhat and MetaMask.

#### Step 1 — Authoring the Contract

The developer creates `MyToken.sol` in the `contracts/` directory. The contract inherits from **OpenZeppelin's ERC-20 implementation** to ensure security best practices (audited, community-vetted code).

The file path is `contracts/MyToken.sol` and contains the Solidity source code.

#### Step 2 — Compilation (Layer 2 Tool: `solc` via Hardhat)

When the developer runs `npx hardhat compile`, the following sequence executes:

1. Hardhat loads the **`hardhat.config.js`** file.
2. It invokes the **Solidity compiler (`solc`)** specified in the `solidity` field (e.g., `"0.8.24"`).
3. The compiler performs **lexical analysis** → **parsing** → **AST generation** → **EVM bytecode generation**.
4. The compiler emits two artifacts to `artifacts/contracts/MyToken.sol/MyToken.json`:
   - `bytecode` (hex string, e.g., `0x608060405234801561001057600080fd5b50...`)
   - `abi` (JSON array of function/event definitions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ABI (Application Binary Interface)** is the contract's *public interface description* — it tells external tools (Web3.js, Ethers.js) which functions can be called, their parameter types, and return types. Without the ABI, the contract is an **inert bytecode blob**.

#### Step 3 — Local Testing (Layer 2 Tool: Hardhat Network / Ganache)

The developer writes tests in `test/MyToken.test.js` using the **Mocha + Chai** stack (or Foundry's Solidity-native tests). The `hardhat network` provides:

- Pre-funded accounts (10 accounts × 10,000 test ETH)
- Instant block mining
- Ability to **revert state** between tests (snapshot/evm_snapshot)
- **Impersonation** of any address via `hardhat_impersonateAccount` (extremely useful for testing contract-to-contract interactions)

A typical test verifies that `transfer()` correctly decrements the sender's balance and increments the receiver's balance.

#### Step 4 — Deployment Script Authoring

The developer writes `scripts/deploy.js` (JavaScript) using **Ethers.js** (now the default in modern Hardhat installations). The script:

1. Connects to the network provider using `ethers.getSigners()`.
2. Retrieves the contract factory from the artifact.
3. Calls `factory.deploy(constructorArguments)` to broadcast a **contract creation transaction**.
4. Awaits `deployed()` to confirm the contract address is set on-chain.

#### Step 5 — MetaMask Configuration (Layer 5 Tool)

The developer configures MetaMask to connect to the **Sepolia testnet** (Chain ID `11155111`, RPC URL `https://rpc.sepolia.org` or an Infura/Alchemy endpoint). The account is funded with test ETH via a **faucet** (e.g., `sepoliafaucet.com`).

#### Step 6 — Infura / Alchemy as Node Provider (Layer 1 Tool as a Service)

The developer does **not** run a personal Ethereum node for testnet deployment. Instead, the script uses the **Infura RPC endpoint** as the network provider:

```javascript
// This appears in scripts/deploy.js as a config-only value,
// not as user-facing code to memorize
const provider = new ethers.JsonRpcProvider(process.env.INFURA_URL);
```

Infura maintains a **synchronized cluster of Ethereum nodes** and forwards the developer's `eth_sendRawTransaction` JSON-RPC call to the actual Sepolia network.

#### Step 7 — Transaction Signing and Broadcasting

The deployment transaction follows this path:

1. Ethers.js builds a **transaction object** with fields: `to` (empty for contract creation), `data` (compiled bytecode), `value` (0 unless sending ETH), `gasLimit`, `maxFeePerGas`, `maxPriorityFeePerGas`, `nonce`, `chainId`.
2. The transaction is **signed locally** with the deployer's private key (held by MetaMask or a hardware wallet).
3. The signed **raw transaction** (RLP-encoded) is sent to the network via `eth_sendRawTransaction`.

#### Step 8 — Gas Estimation Mathematics

Let us compute the deployment cost explicitly.

Suppose the compiled bytecode consumes **1,250,000 gas** (typical for a basic ERC-20 token with OpenZeppelin inheritance). The current network conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base Fee} &= 25\ \text{Gwei} \\
\text{Priority Fee (Tip)} &= 2\ \text{Gwei} \\
\text{Max Fee Per Gas} &= 50\ \text{Gwei}\ (\text{user-set cap})
\end{aligned}
$$

The **effective gas price** is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Gas Price} &= \min\left(\text{Base Fee} + \text{Tip},\ \text{Max Fee Per Gas}\right) \\
&= \min(25 + 2,\ 50) \\
&= \min(27,\ 50) \\
&= 27\ \text{Gwei}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **total transaction fee** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tx Fee} &= \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Effective Gas Price} \\
&= 1{,}250{,}000 \times 27\ \text{Gwei} \\
&= 33{,}750{,}000\ \text{Gwei}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to ETH (recall $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^9\ \text{Gwei}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tx Fee in ETH} &= \frac{33{,}750{,}000}{10^9} = 0.03375\ \text{ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to USD (assuming 1 ETH = \$3,000):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tx Fee in USD} &= 0.03375 \times 3{,}000 = \$101.25
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This gas calculation is a **guaranteed KTU Part A or Part B question**. Memorize the formula $\text{Fee} = \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Gas Price}$ and the unit conversion chain $\text{Wei} \rightarrow \text{Gwei} \rightarrow \text{ETH}$.

#### Step 9 — Verification on Etherscan

After deployment, the developer visits `sepolia.etherscan.io` and **verifies the source code**. The verification process:

1. Developer uploads the flattened Solidity source (all imports inlined into a single file).
2. Etherscan re-compiles the source with matching compiler version and optimization settings.
3. If the **deployed bytecode hash** matches the **locally compiled bytecode hash**, the contract is marked **"Verified"** ✅.
4. Users can now **read and interact** with the contract via Etherscan's "Read Contract" and "Write Contract" tabs.

### 3.2 Smart Contract Compilation Process — Exhaustive Flow

The Solidity compilation pipeline transforms human-readable code into EVM-executable bytecode through **five distinct phases**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source Code} &\xrightarrow{\text{Lexer}} \text{Tokens} \xrightarrow{\text{Parser}} \text{AST} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Type Checker}} \text{Annotated AST} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Optimizer}} \text{Optimized IR} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Code Generator}} \text{EVM Bytecode} + \text{ABI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Lexer):** The `.sol` file is broken into tokens (keywords like `contract`, `function`, `uint256`, identifiers, operators, literals).

**Phase 2 — Parsing:** Tokens are arranged into an **Abstract Syntax Tree** that represents the program structure.

**Phase 3 — Type Checking:** The compiler verifies that all type assignments, function signatures, and inheritance are valid. Catches errors like calling a non-existent function.

**Phase 4 — Optimization (Optional):** If enabled (`--optimize`), the compiler reduces gas costs by:
- Inlining small functions
- Removing dead code
- Constant folding
- Reordering operations to minimize storage accesses (each `SSTORE` costs 5,000–20,000 gas)

**Phase 5 — Code Generation:** The final **EVM bytecode** is produced as a hex string, along with the **ABI** JSON file.

### 3.3 Account and Address Derivation — Step-by-Step

**Ethereum Address Derivation from a Public Key:**

Given a 64-byte uncompressed public key (without the `0x04` prefix), the address is the last 20 bytes of the Keccak-256 hash:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Public Key} &= P_{64\ \text{bytes}} \\
\text{Hash} &= \text{Keccak256}(P) \\
\text{Ethereum Address} &= \text{Hash}[12:32]\ (\text{last 20 bytes})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example Walkthrough:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \text{0xA1B2C3D4...} \ (\text{64 bytes}) \\
\text{Keccak256}(P) &= \text{0x6B48...A2D9} \ (\text{32 bytes}) \\
\text{Address} &= \text{0x6B48...A2D9}[12:32] = \text{0xF392...7C4A} \ (\text{20 bytes})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is then prefixed with `0x` and typically displayed in **EIP-55 checksummed format** (mixed-case encoding that includes a checksum to detect typos).

### 3.4 Transaction Lifecycle — From Signing to Confirmation

A complete Ethereum transaction passes through **five distinct stages** in the toolchain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Authoring} &\rightarrow \text{Developer specifies to, value, data, gas params} \\
\text{2. Signing} &\rightarrow \text{Private key signs transaction hash (ECDSA)} \\
\text{3. Broadcasting} &\rightarrow \text{eth\_sendRawTransaction to node via RPC} \\
\text{4. Mempool} &\rightarrow \text{Transaction awaits inclusion by validator} \\
\text{5. Inclusion} &\rightarrow \text{Transaction included in a block; receipt generated}
\end{aligned}
$$

After step 5, the transaction has a **receipt** containing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Receipt} &= \{\text{transactionHash},\ \text{blockNumber},\ \text{from},\ \text{to}, \\
&\quad \text{cumulativeGasUsed},\ \text{gasUsed},\ \text{contractAddress (if creation)}, \\
&\quad \text{logs},\ \text{status}\ (\text{0x1 = success},\ \text{0x0 = failure})\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `status` field was introduced in **EIP-658** as part of the **Byzantium Hard Fork** (October 2017). Before Byzantium, transaction success was determined by absence of an error in the `root` field.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] *Differentiate between Geth and Ganache. Why is Ganache preferred during the development phase of a DApp?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Geth | Ganache |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Full Ethereum client connecting to live network | Local personal blockchain for development |
| **Network** | Mainnet, testnets (Sepolia, Holesky) | Local loopback only (127.0.0.1) |
| **Mining/Consensus** | Real Proof-of-Stake validators | Instant mining on demand (deterministic) |
| **Storage** | Requires **~1 TB SSD** for full node | In-memory or small local files |
| **Accounts** | None pre-funded | 10 pre-funded accounts with 100 test ETH each |
| **Use Case** | Production deployment, public network access | Unit testing, local development, gas profiling |

Ganache is preferred during development because it provides **deterministic, fast, and free testing** without consuming real ETH or waiting for block confirmations. It enables rapid iteration and **forking** (replaying mainnet state locally) to test against deployed contracts.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] *Explain the role of MetaMask in the Ethereum ecosystem. What is the purpose of the seed phrase?*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of MetaMask:**
MetaMask is a **non-custodial hot wallet** implemented as a browser extension that serves as the **primary gateway** between users and the Ethereum network. It functions at three levels:

1. **Key Management:** Generates and stores the user's private keys (encrypted in local browser storage). The private key never leaves the device.
2. **Transaction Signing:** When a DApp requests a transaction, MetaMask displays a popup showing the recipient, amount, gas fees, and contract data. The user **explicitly approves** before signing.
3. **Provider Injection:** MetaMask injects a global JavaScript object (`window.ethereum`) into every webpage, enabling Web3.js/Ethers.js libraries to communicate with the blockchain via the JSON-RPC interface.

**Purpose of the Seed Phrase:**
The seed phrase (also called **mnemonic phrase** or **recovery phrase**) is a sequence of **12 or 24 words** generated from a **BIP-39 word list** using entropy. It serves as a **human-readable backup** of the user's private key. Through the **BIP-44 hierarchical deterministic (HD) wallet** standard, all accounts and private keys are deterministically derived from this single seed. If the user loses access to their device, the seed phrase can **reconstruct the entire wallet** on any compatible wallet application. It is the **single point of recovery** and must be stored **offline** (never digitally, never photographed, never shared).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. (A)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] *(a)* Describe the layered architecture of Ethereum development tools. Explain the function of each layer with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a neat diagram, explain the end-to-end process of deploying a smart contract to the Ethereum testnet. Include the roles of Solidity, Hardhat, MetaMask, and Infura in your answer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Layered Architecture of Ethereum Tools:**

The Ethereum tool ecosystem is organized into **five functional layers**, each building on the services of the layers below:

**Layer 1 — Node Clients (Network Layer):**
These are the foundational software that connects to the Ethereum peer-to-peer network. Post-Merge, two types of clients work in tandem:
- **Execution Layer Clients:** Geth (Go), Besu (Java), Nethermind (C#) — process transactions and maintain EVM state.
- **Consensus Layer Clients:** Lighthouse (Rust), Prysm (Go), Teku (Java) — manage validator duties and the Proof-of-Stake protocol.
- **Function:** Validate blocks, maintain the blockchain, expose JSON-RPC API endpoints (default port 8545).

**Layer 2 — Compilers and Test Environments:**
- **Solidity Compiler (`solc`):** Translates `.sol` source files into EVM bytecode and generates the ABI (Application Binary Interface).
- **Remix IDE:** Browser-based development environment with integrated compilation, debugging, and deployment.
- **Ganache:** Local personal blockchain for testing with 10 pre-funded accounts and instant mining.

**Layer 3 — Development and Deployment Frameworks:**
- **Truffle Suite:** JavaScript-based framework with `compile`, `migrate`, `test` commands.
- **Hardhat:** Modern TypeScript-first framework with built-in local network, Solidity `console.log`, and stack traces.
- **Foundry:** Rust-based Solidity-native testing toolkit (`forge`, `cast`, `anvil`).

**Layer 4 — DApp Libraries and APIs:**
- **Web3.js:** Original JavaScript API for Ethereum.
- **Ethers.js:** Modern alternative with better TypeScript support, ENS integration, and smaller footprint.
- **Web3.py:** Python equivalent for backend integration.
- These libraries abstract the raw JSON-RPC calls into developer-friendly functions.

**Layer 5 — Wallets and User Interfaces:**
- **MetaMask:** Browser extension wallet with injected Web3 provider.
- **Hardware Wallets:** Ledger, Trezor for cold storage security.
- **Etherscan:** Read-only block explorer for transaction inspection and contract verification.

**(b) Smart Contract Deployment Process:**

The deployment process follows this sequence (also refer to Section 4.2 diagram):

**Step 1 — Authoring:** Developer writes `MyContract.sol` using Solidity 0.8.x, importing OpenZeppelin libraries for security.

**Step 2 — Compilation (Hardhat + solc):** Running `npx hardhat compile` invokes the solc compiler, producing two artifacts: **bytecode** (EVM-executable hex string) and **ABI** (JSON function signature map) stored in `artifacts/`.

**Step 3 — Local Testing:** Tests are run on Hardhat's in-memory network. Assertions verify state changes, event emissions, and edge cases (reverts, overflow, access control).

**Step 4 — Network Configuration:** In `hardhat.config.js`, the developer specifies the target network (e.g., Sepolia), the Infura RPC URL, and the deployer's private key (stored in `.env` file for security).

**Step 5 — Deployment Script:** `scripts/deploy.js` uses Ethers.js to:
- Connect to the Infura RPC endpoint
- Instantiate a wallet using the deployer's private key
- Get the contract factory from artifacts
- Call `factory.deploy(args)` which constructs a contract creation transaction

**Step 6 — MetaMask Signing (if using a hardware wallet or browser-injected provider):** The transaction is presented to MetaMask showing gas estimates. The user reviews and clicks **Confirm**.

**Step 7 — Broadcasting via Infura:** The signed raw transaction is sent to Infura's Ethereum node cluster via `eth_sendRawTransaction`. Infura propagates it to the Sepolia testnet.

**Step 8 — Block Inclusion:** A validator on the network includes the transaction in a block. The contract code is stored at the EVM level, and a new **contract address** is deterministically computed.

**Step 9 — Verification:** The developer copies the deployment transaction hash and visits `sepolia.etherscan.io` to verify the source code, making the contract's functions readable and callable via the block explorer.

---

**Q3. (B)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024] *(a)* Explain the different types of Ethereum wallets. Compare hot wallets, cold wallets, and custodial wallets in terms of security and convenience. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A developer deploys a smart contract that consumes **2,100,000 gas** for deployment. The current network conditions are: Base Fee = **30 Gwei**, Priority Fee = **3 Gwei**, and the developer sets a Max Fee Per Gas = **60 Gwei**. Compute: (i) the effective gas price, (ii) the total transaction fee in Gwei, (iii) the fee in ETH, and (iv) the fee in USD if 1 ETH = \$3,200. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Types of Ethereum Wallets:**

Ethereum wallets are software/hardware tools that manage **private keys** (not coins themselves). They are classified into three categories:

**1. Hot Wallets (Software Wallets — Connected to Internet):**
- *Examples:* MetaMask (browser extension), Trust Wallet (mobile), Coinbase Wallet.
- *Key Storage:* Private keys encrypted in browser local storage or mobile secure enclave.
- *Connectivity:* Always online; interact with DApps via injected provider or WalletConnect.
- *Security:* Moderate — vulnerable to phishing attacks, browser exploits, and malware.
- *Convenience:* Very high — one-click DApp interaction, instant transaction signing.

**2. Cold Wallets (Hardware Wallets — Offline):**
- *Examples:* Ledger Nano X, Trezor Model T.
- *Key Storage:* Private keys stored on a **secure element chip** that never connects to the internet.
- *Connectivity:* Transactions are signed offline and broadcasted through a connected (but key-isolated) companion app.
- *Security:* Very high — immune to remote hacking, phishing, and malware. Recovery via 24-word seed phrase.
- *Convenience:* Lower — requires physical device interaction, USB/Bluetooth connection, and manual confirmation for each transaction.

**3. Custodial Wallets (Third-Party Managed):**
- *Examples:* Coinbase, Binance, Kraken exchange wallets.
- *Key Storage:* Private keys held by the exchange/custodian; user only has a login credential.
- *Connectivity:* Web and mobile app interfaces.
- *Security:* Depends entirely on the custodian's security infrastructure. Vulnerable to exchange hacks (Mt. Gox, FTX) and regulatory seizure.
- *Convenience:* Highest for beginners — password recovery, customer support, fiat on-ramps.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Hot Wallet | Cold Wallet | Custodial Wallet |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Custody** | Self-custody | Self-custody | Third-party |
| **Internet Exposure** | Always online | Keys offline | Online (server-side) |
| **DApp Interaction** | Native, seamless | Requires signing relay | Limited (exchange-controlled) |
| **Recovery** | Seed phrase | Seed phrase | Email/password (custodian-assisted) |
| **Best For** | Active DApp users | Long-term holders, large balances | Beginners, frequent traders |

**(b) Gas Fee Calculation:**

Given:
- Gas Used = 2,100,000
- Base Fee = 30 Gwei
- Priority Fee = 3 Gwei
- Max Fee Per Gas = 60 Gwei

**(i) Effective Gas Price:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Gas Price} &= \min\left(\text{Base Fee} + \text{Priority Fee},\ \text{Max Fee Per Gas}\right) \\
&= \min(30 + 3,\ 60) \\
&= \min(33,\ 60) \\
&= 33\ \text{Gwei}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Transaction Fee in Gwei:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tx Fee} &= \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Effective Gas Price} \\
&= 2{,}100{,}000 \times 33 \\
&= 69{,}300{,}000\ \text{Gwei}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Fee in ETH:**

Since $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^9\ \text{Gwei}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tx Fee (ETH)} &= \frac{69{,}300{,}000}{10^9} = 0.0693\ \text{ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Fee in USD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tx Fee (USD)} &= 0.0693 \times 3{,}200 = \$221.76
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The effective gas price is **33 Gwei**, the total fee is **69,300,000 Gwei** = **0.0693 ETH** = **\$221.76**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget that $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^9\ \text{Gwei}$ (not $10^6$ or $10^8$). This single error can lose **3–4 marks** in a gas calculation question.
> 2. **Confusing Base Fee with Max Fee:** In EIP-1559, the base fee is **burned**, not paid to validators. Only the priority fee (tip) is paid to the validator. Students often write "Base Fee = Priority Fee = Tip" — this is **incorrect**.
> 3. **Missing the `min()` Function:** EIP-1559 caps the effective price at the user's `maxFeePerGas`. If the sum (base + priority) exceeds the cap, the effective price equals the cap, not the sum.
> 4. **Omitting the ABI:** When asked about compilation outputs, students mention "bytecode" but forget the **ABI (Application Binary Interface)**, which is essential for external interaction.
> 5. **Confusing ERC-20 with ERC-721:** ERC-20 is for **fungible** tokens (interchangeable units like currency); ERC-721 is for **non-fungible** tokens (unique items like NFTs). Examiners test this distinction frequently.
> 6. **Forgetting Post-Merge Architecture:** Students sometimes describe Ethereum as still using Proof-of-Work. The Merge (Sept 2022) is a **mandatory modern concept** — always mention the dual-client (Execution + Consensus) architecture.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Tools Used in Ethereum:**

- ⭐ **5-Layer Tool Architecture:** Node Clients → Compilers/Test Environments → Dev Frameworks → DApp Libraries → Wallets/UI
- ⭐ **EIP = Ethereum Improvement Proposal** (standardization mechanism, e.g., ERC-20, ERC-721, EIP-1559)
- ⭐ **ERC-20** = fungible tokens; **ERC-721** = NFTs; **ERC-1155** = multi-token standard
- ⭐ **Solidity Compiler (`solc`)** produces **bytecode** + **ABI**
- ⭐ **ABI** = JSON description of a contract's public interface (functions, events, errors)
- ⭐ **Gas Formula (EIP-1559):** $\text{Fee} = (\text{Base Fee} + \text{Tip}) \times \text{Gas Used}$; base fee is **burned**, tip goes to validator
- ⭐ **Unit Conversions:** $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^{18}\ \text{Wei} = 10^9\ \text{Gwei} = 10^6\ \text{Finney}$
- ⭐ **Address Derivation:** Last 20 bytes of `Keccak256(public key)`; EIP-55 checksummed format for display
- ⭐ **Contract Address:** Last 20 bytes of `Keccak256(RLP(sender, nonce))` — **deterministic**
- ⭐ **Geth** = most popular Go-based execution client; **Besu** = Java, enterprise; **Nethermind** = C#
- ⭐ **Consensus Clients:** **Lighthouse** (Rust), **Prysm** (Go), **Teku** (Java)
- ⭐ **MetaMask** = non-custodial hot wallet; injects `window.ethereum` provider; **>30M MAU**
- ⭐ **Seed Phrase (BIP-39):** 12/24-word recovery phrase from standardized word list; **never share, never store digitally**
- ⭐ **HD Wallet Path (BIP-44):** `m/44'/60'/0'/0/0` for Ethereum's first account
- ⭐ **Hardhat** = modern dev framework with built-in `console.log` in Solidity and stack traces
- ⭐ **Foundry** = Solidity-native testing (tests written in Solidity, not JavaScript)
- ⭐ **Ganache** = local blockchain with 10 pre-funded accounts (100 test ETH each), instant mining
- ⭐ **Infura/Alchemy** = hosted node providers (RPC as a service) — avoid running your own node
- ⭐ **Etherscan** = block explorer; supports **source code verification** (re-compile and match bytecode hash)
- ⭐ **Post-Merge (Sept 15, 2022):** Ethereum uses **Proof-of-Stake**; dual-client architecture (Execution + Consensus) communicating via **Engine API**
- ⭐ **Keccak-256** = Ethereum's hash function (NOT SHA-3 standard — they differ in padding)
- ⭐ **secp256k1** = elliptic curve for ECDSA signatures
- ⭐ **JSON-RPC** = standard interface; key methods: `eth_call`, `eth_sendRawTransaction`, `eth_getBalance`
- ⭐ **Full Node** ≈ 1 TB SSD; **Archive Node** ≈ 12–15 TB SSD; **Light Node** ≈ <100 GB
- ⭐ **Block Time** = 12 seconds (1 epoch = 32 slots × 12s = 6.4 minutes)
- ⭐ **Block Gas Limit** = 30,000,000 gas (target 15,000,000 with ±2× elasticity)
- ⭐ **EVM Stack Depth** = 1024 levels maximum
- ⭐ **London Hard Fork (Aug 2021):** Introduced EIP-1559 fee market
- ⭐ **Byzantium Hard Fork (Oct 2017):** Introduced EIP-658 transaction receipt status field
- ⭐ **The Merge (Sept 2022):** Replaced Proof-of-Work with Proof-of-Stake
- ⭐ **EIP-20** = original token standard; **ERC-20** = community-implemented version (technically equivalent)
- ⭐ **Truffle Suite** = legacy JavaScript framework; now less favored than **Hardhat**
- ⭐ **Ethers.js** is the **recommended** JavaScript library in modern (post-2021) Hardhat installations
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethereum Platform using Solidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ethereum Platform using Solidity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Ethereum** is a decentralized, open-source, distributed computing platform based on blockchain technology that enables the deployment and execution of **smart contracts** and **decentralized applications (DApps)** without downtime, censorship, or third-party interference. It was proposed by **Vitalik Buterin** in late 2013, with the network going live on **July 30, 2015**. **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is the quasi-Turing complete **runtime environment** (or "world computer") that executes smart contract bytecode in a completely isolated sandboxed fashion. Every node in the Ethereum network runs the EVM to maintain consensus on the global state. **Solidity** is a **statically-typed**, **contract-oriented**, high-level programming language specifically designed for writing smart contracts that run on the EVM. Its syntax is heavily influenced by C++, Python, and JavaScript. **Smart Contract** is an immutable, deterministic program stored on the blockchain that automatically executes predefined instructions when specific conditions are met, without the need for trusted intermediaries. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT — Definition Triad** > Examiners frequently test the precise distinction between **Ethereum (Platform)**, **EVM (Runtime)**, and **Solidity (Language)**. Confusing these three layers is a guaranteed mark-loser. | Layer | Role | KTU One-Liner | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Ethereum** | Decentralized global infrastructure | The **World Computer** | | **EVM** | Sandbox execution engine | The **CPU** of Ethereum | | **Solidity** | High-level contract language | The **Programming Language** for EVM | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Global Vending Machine"** > > Imagine a vending machine in a public square that **anyone** can use, **no one owns**, and that **cannot be cheated or broken into**. You insert a coin, select a product, and the machine dispenses it according to rules no one can change. Ethereum is this vending machine — but instead of dispensing snacks, it dispenses **trustless computation**. The **Solidity contract** is the vending machine's internal program, the **EVM** is the electromechanical brain inside it, and **Ether (ETH)** is the coin you insert (used to pay for the computational electricity called **Gas**). ## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics - **Block Time (Post-Merge):** **12 seconds** - **EVM Stack Depth:** **1024 levels** - **EVM Stack Word Size:** **256 bits (32 bytes)** - **Solidity Compiler:** **solc** (e.g., `solc 0.8.x`) - **Standard Gas Unit for Transaction:** **21,000 gas** (plain ETH transfer) - **Smart Contract Development Framework:** **Truffle / Hardhat / Foundry** - **Standard Token Standards:** **ERC-20** (fungible), **ERC-721** (NFT), **ERC-1155** (multi-token) ## 1.4 Two Account Model of Ethereum Ethereum uses a unique state-based model that distinguishes between two types of accounts: 1. **Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs):** Controlled by private keys. Can initiate transactions. Has an ether balance & nonce. 2. **Contract Accounts:** Controlled by their contract code. Cannot initiate transactions on their own — they only react when called. Has ether balance, nonce, code, and storage. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** *"Humans trigger, Contracts react."* This is the only way to remember which account type can originate a transaction in KTU 2-mark questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 EVM Architecture — The Heart of Ethereum The EVM is a **256-bit register machine** designed to be deterministic and sandboxed. Every full node in the Ethereum network runs an instance of the EVM. ### 2.1.1 EVM Operational Stack When a smart contract function is called, the EVM executes bytecode in a structured memory model consisting of three primary storage areas: 1. **Stack** — A LIFO data structure of 32-byte words used for all EVM operations. Maximum depth = **1024**. 2. **Memory** — A volatile, byte-addressed, expandable linear byte array. Cleared after execution of each message call. 3. **Storage** — A persistent key-value store (256-bit → 256-bit) tied to a contract account. State is preserved between transactions. ### 2.1.2 Execution Logic Steps - The miner/validator receives a transaction and decodes the **calldata**. - The EVM loads the contract's **bytecode** from storage. - The **Program Counter (PC)** starts at 0 and walks through opcodes. - Each opcode consumes a fixed/calculated amount of **Gas**. - If gas runs out before completion → **revert** with `Out of Gas` error; all state changes are rolled back. - If execution completes successfully → state transition is committed to the global state trie. ## 2.2 The Solidity Language — Anatomy of a Contract A Solidity file (`.sol`) is structured into **pragmas**, **imports**, **contracts**, **state variables**, **functions**, **events**, **modifiers**, and **structs/enums**. ```solidity // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^0.8.0; contract SimpleStorage { // State variable (stored permanently in Storage) uint256 public storedData; // Event (logged to the blockchain) event DataStored(uint256 indexed newValue, address indexed by); // Function to write to storage function set(uint256 _x) public { storedData = _x; emit DataStored(_x, msg.sender); } // View function (read-only, no gas cost from caller) function get() public view returns (uint256) { return storedData; } } ``` ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Reference for Numerical / Derivation Questions** | Concept | Formula / Property | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Total Transaction Fee** | $\text{TxFee} = \text{GasUsed} \times \text{GasPrice}$ | **wei** | | **Wei ↔ Ether Conversion** | $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^{18}\ \text{wei}$ | base unit | | **Gwei** | $1\ \text{ETH} = 10^9\ \text{gwei}$ | used for gas price | | **Base Transaction Cost** | $21{,}000\ \text{gas}$ | plain ETH transfer | | **Contract Deployment Cost** | $21000 + \sum(\text{opcodes})$ | typically 100k–2M gas | | **SSTORE New Slot** | $20{,}000\ \text{gas}$ | first-time storage write | | **SSTORE Update Slot** | $5{,}000\ \text{gas}$ | overwrite existing | | **SLOAD** | $2{,}100\ \text{gas (cold)},\ 100\ \text{gas (warm)}$ | EIP-2929 | | **EVM Word Size** | $256\ \text{bits} = 32\ \text{bytes}$ | applies to stack, memory, storage | | **Block Gas Limit** | $30{,}000{,}000\ \text{gas}$ | mainnet (post-merge) | | **Nonce** | monotonically increasing per EOA | replay protection | | **Contract Balance Check** | $\text{address(this).balance}$ | returns wei | | **Block Number / Timestamp** | `block.number` / `block.timestamp` | not tamper-proof within miner limits | ## 2.4 Solidity Data Types — Quick Reference Matrix | Type | Category | Storage | Default Value | KTU 2024 Note | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `bool` | Value | 1 byte | `false` | logical ops cost 3–30 gas | | `uint8`–`uint256` | Integer (unsigned) | 1–32 bytes | `0` | `uint` defaults to `uint256` | | `int8`–`int256` | Integer (signed) | 1–32 bytes | `0` | two's complement | | `address` | Address | 20 bytes | `0x0` | holds an EOA or contract | | `address payable` | Address | 20 bytes | `0x0` | can send ETH via `.transfer/.send/.call` | | `bytes1`–`bytes32` | Fixed bytes | 1–32 bytes | zeroed | **cheaper** than dynamic `bytes` | | `bytes` | Dynamic bytes | variable | `""` | member of `address` ambiguity avoided | | `string` | Dynamic UTF-8 | variable | `""` | no `.length` of chars; only bytes | | `mapping(K => V)` | Reference | hash slot | — | **cannot be iterated** — KTU trap question | | `array(T)` | Reference | slot(s) | dynamic: `[]` | dynamic arrays cost gas to push | | `struct` | User-defined | packed into slots | zeroed | tightly packed into 32-byte slots | > [!WARNING] > **Mappings are NOT iterable.** This is a frequently-tested KTU pitfall. "Iterate over a mapping" is a non-question — it has no answer. ## 2.5 Function Visibility & Mutability Specifiers | Specifier | Callable From | Gas Cost to Caller (read) | KTU Key | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `public` | Anyone (inside + outside) | n/a | auto-generates getter | | `external` | Only outside the contract | n/a | cheaper for large calldata | | `internal` | This contract + derived | n/a | only `internal` callable from libraries | | `private` | Only this contract | n/a | **still readable on-chain** — illusion of privacy | | Mutability | Can Modify State? | KTU Highlight | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `view` | No (reads only) | No gas cost if called externally | | `pure` | No (no read either) | Cannot use `block.timestamp` etc. | | `payable` | (modifier) | Allows receiving ETH | | *(none)* | Yes | Default → state-modifying | ## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility Ethereum + Solidity is the backbone of: - **DeFi (Decentralized Finance):** Uniswap, Aave, Compound — automated lending and AMM exchanges. - **NFTs:** ERC-721 / ERC-1155 contracts for digital art, gaming assets, identity. - **DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations):** Governance via on-chain voting. - **Supply Chain Provenance:** Tracking goods on-chain. - **Tokenization of Real-World Assets:** Real estate, carbon credits, securities. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU 2024:** Module 4 directly tests your ability to **write, deploy, and reason about Solidity contracts**. The exam may include a 14-mark "Develop a Solidity contract for X" or "Compute gas cost of Y" question. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Gas Cost of a Storage Write Contract ### Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerics) > A contract contains the function `setValue(uint256 _x) public { storedData = _x; }` where `storedData` is a `uint256` state variable. The EVM uses **Istanbul-EIP-1884/2929** gas costs. If this is the **first** write to `storedData`, compute the total gas consumed by the `SSTORE` and `SLOAD` operations. Assume all slots are **cold**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Let the EVM execute the function. Before writing to `storedData`, the EVM may first read its current value (via SLOAD) — though not strictly required for an unconditional write. We analyze **both** paths and then confirm the cheaper one is chosen by the compiler (Solidity does **not** insert an automatic SLOAD for direct assignment, so the path is direct SSTORE). **Step 1 — Identify the opcodes involved.** The function body compiles to: - `SSTORE(key=slot0, value=_x)` — write 32 bytes to storage slot 0. **Step 2 — Determine the access type.** - EIP-2929: First access to a storage slot in a transaction = **cold**. - Second access (same slot) = **warm** (100 gas). For a single transaction executing this function, the slot is touched only once → **cold**. **Step 3 — Apply gas cost rules for SSTORE.** According to EIP-2200 / EIP-2929, the SSTORE gas cost is determined by the previous value (`V0`) and the new value (`V_new`): - If `V0 == V_new`: 100 gas (no-op, but compensated to warm). - If `V0 ≠ V_new` and slot is being set to a non-zero value for the **first time** (i.e., `V0 == 0`): **20,000 gas**. - If `V0 ≠ V_new` and slot already has a non-zero value (overwrite): **5,000 gas** (warm discount of 2,100, net = 2,900 warm or 5,000 cold). - Cold-access surcharge: +2,100 gas (only if cold AND no preceding warm access). **Step 4 — Compute the gas for our specific case.** This is the **first write** to `storedData`: - Previous value `V0 = 0` (default). - New value `V_new = _x` (non-zero, assume `_x > 0`). - Slot is cold. Therefore: $$ \text{Gas}_{\text{SSTORE}} = 20{,}000\ (\text{new slot write}) + 2{,}100\ (\text{cold surcharge}) = 22{,}100\ \text{gas} $$ **Step 5 — Add the function call overhead.** Solidity contracts compiled for `^0.8.x` include: - Function selector check: ~22 gas. - Argument decoding (calldata size 4 bytes selector + 32 bytes arg): ~6 gas per byte ≈ 6 × 36 = 216 gas (rough; often optimized to ~24 gas). - Public function call dispatch overhead: ~200–500 gas. - Transaction base cost: 21,000 gas (always charged). **Step 6 — Sum total approximate gas cost.** $$ \text{Total Gas} \approx 21{,}000\ (\text{tx base}) + 500\ (\text{dispatch}) + 22{,}100\ (\text{SSTORE}) = 43{,}600\ \text{gas} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to transaction fee in Gwei (illustrative).** If `gasPrice = 20 gwei`: $$ \text{Fee} = 43{,}600 \times 20\ \text{gwei} = 872{,}000\ \text{gwei} = 8.72 \times 10^5\ \text{gwei} $$ $$ \text{Fee in ETH} = \frac{872{,}000}{10^9} = 0.000872\ \text{ETH} $$ **Step 8 — Convert to USD (illustrative; not part of KTU).** If 1 ETH = $3,000: $$ \text{Fee in USD} = 0.000872 \times 3000 = \$2.616 $$ > [!TIP] > **Why this derivation matters for KTU 2024:** > The university exam will not necessarily give EIP numbers, but it WILL test your ability to: > 1. Identify opcodes from Solidity code. > 2. Apply SSTORE gas rules (zero → non-zero vs overwrite). > 3. Compute final fee in wei / gwei / ETH. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solidity Code → EVM Bytecode Mapping | Solidity Construct | EVM Operation | Gas Implication | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `pragma solidity ^0.8.0;` | (compile-time only) | No runtime cost | | `uint256 storedData;` | Allocates slot 0 | Free at deploy if not initialized | | `function set(uint256 _x) public` | `CALLDATALOAD` + `MSTORE` | ~3–6 gas per byte | | `storedData = _x;` | `SSTORE` (slot 0) | 22,100 gas (first) / 5,000 (update) | | `emit DataStored(_x, msg.sender);` | `LOG3` opcode | 1,875 + 375 × topics + 8 × data | | `function get() public view returns` | `SLOAD` + `RETURN` | 2,100 cold / 100 warm | | `require(condition, "msg");` | `REVERT` (success path) | 30 gas for the check | | `payable` receiver | `CALLVALUE` check | saves 9,000 gas from `msg.value > 0` | | `mapping(address => uint256)` | `keccak256(key . slot)` | SSTORE on the computed slot | | `selfdestruct(addr)` | `SELFDESTRUCT` | 5,000 gas + negative refund (deprecated) | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Computing Slot of a Packed Struct > A contract declares: > ```solidity > struct User { uint128 a; uint128 b; address c; } > User user1; > ``` > Compute the **storage slot number** of field `user1.c` if `user1` is stored at slot 5. **Step 1 — Recall the packing rule.** Solidity packs struct fields into 32-byte slots in declaration order, from right-to-left within each slot (low-order-aligned). **Step 2 — Layout the struct.** - `a` (`uint128` = 16 bytes) → occupies **bytes 0–15** of slot N. - `b` (`uint128` = 16 bytes) → occupies **bytes 16–31** of slot N. - `c` (`address` = 20 bytes) → fits into a **new slot** (slot N+1), starting at bytes 0–19. **Step 3 — Compute the slot index.** Given `user1` starts at slot 5: $$ \text{Slot}(user1.a) = 5 $$ $$ \text{Slot}(user1.b) = 5 $$ $$ \text{Slot}(user1.c) = 5 + 1 = 6 $$ **Step 4 — Note for `mapping` and `dynamic array`.** For a mapping declared after a packed struct, the **first** mapping slot starts at the next available slot, *not* the next sequential integer if the prior struct has unused bytes. However, since `c` fully consumes 20 bytes, the **next 12 bytes** of slot 6 are unused — but the mapping still takes **slot 7**, because Solidity does not pack across different storage object types. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion for KTU 2024:** When asked "Where is field X stored?" — always identify the parent struct's base slot, then count by struct packing rules. ## 3.4 Worked Example — Function Call Lifecycle A user calls `set(42)` on `SimpleStorage`. Trace the full execution: 1. **Transaction Broadcast:** User's EOA signs the tx with calldata `0x60fe47b100000000...00000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000000002a` (4-byte selector + 32-byte arg). 2. **Network Propagation:** Tx enters the mempool. 3. **Block Inclusion:** A validator picks the tx and executes it locally to validate the gas estimate. 4. **EVM Execution:** - Load bytecode of `SimpleStorage` from account storage. - Push 4 bytes calldata onto stack to match function selector. - Decode `uint256 _x = 42` from calldata. - Execute `SSTORE(0, 42)` — costs 22,100 gas (cold first-write). - Emit `LOG3` event with topics + data. - Return success. 5. **State Commit:** New state root computed, transaction receipt generated with `status = 1`. 6. **Event Log Indexing:** The event is indexed in bloom filters for client-side filtering. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ethereum High-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[EOA User] -->|Signed TX| B[Ethereum Mempool] B --> C[Validator Node] C --> D[EVM Stack Execution] D --> E[Contract Bytecode] D --> F[Memory Volatile] D --> G[Storage Persistent] D --> H[Logs and Events] D --> I[Global State Trie] I --> J[New Block Mined Validated] J --> K[Block Added to Blockchain] K --> L[All Nodes Reach Consensus] ``` ## 4.2 Solidity Contract Structural Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Solidity Source File .sol] --> B[License Identifier] A --> C[Pragma Directive] A --> D[Import Statements] A --> E[Contract Declaration] E --> F[State Variables] E --> G[Events] E --> H[Modifiers] E --> I[Constructor] E --> J[Functions] E --> K[Structs and Enums] E --> L[Inheritance Interfaces] J --> M[Read View Pure] J --> N[Write State Modifying] J --> O[Receive Fallback] H --> P[Access Control] H --> Q[Reentrancy Guard] ``` ## 4.3 EVM Memory Model — Stack, Memory, Storage ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A1[EVM Runtime Context] B[Stack Max 1024 depth
32 byte words] C[Memory Volatile
byte addressable
cleared per call] D[Storage Persistent
key value 256 to 256
tied to contract] E[Calldata Read only
function arguments] F[Code Immutable
contract bytecode] G[Logs and Events
indexed bloom filter] end B --- C C --- D D --- E E --- F F --- G ``` ## 4.4 Smart Contract Development Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Requirement Analysis] --> B[Write Solidity Contract] B --> C[Compile with solc] C --> D{Compilation Success} D -->|No| B D -->|Yes| E[Unit Test in Local VM] E --> F{Tests Pass} F -->|No| B F -->|Yes| G[Deploy to Testnet Goerli Sepolia] G --> H[Audit and Verify Source] H --> I[Deploy to Mainnet] I --> J[Frontend Integration Web3.js ethers.js] J --> K[Monitor Events and Logs] K --> L[Upgrade or Migrate if Needed] ``` ## 4.5 Account Interaction Flow (EOA → Contract) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User EOA participant N as Ethereum Network participant E as EVM participant C as Contract Account participant S as Storage Trie U->>N: Sign and broadcast TX N->>E: Load contract bytecode E->>C: Execute function C->>S: SSTORE state change S-->>C: Updated slot value C-->>E: Emit LOG event E-->>N: Return status success N-->>U: Transaction receipt N->>N: Block finalized by consensus ``` ## 4.6 Gas Refund and Revert Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Transaction Initiated] --> B[Gas Budget Reserved] B --> C[EVM Executes Opcode] C --> D{Opcode Completes} D -->|Success| E[State Change Committed] D -->|Out of Gas| F[Revert All State Changes] D -->|REVERT Triggered| F D -->|Invalid Opcode| F E --> G[Refund Unused Gas to Sender] F --> H[Consume All Reserved Gas] G --> I[Transaction Finalized] H --> I ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU 2024:** Be able to redraw all six of the above from memory in a 7-mark question. The most-frequent KTU diagram is the **EVM memory model** and the **EOA → Contract flow**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] **Differentiate between Externally Owned Accounts (EOA) and Contract Accounts in Ethereum.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | EOA | Contract Account | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Controlled by** | Private key of the user | Contract code (bytecode) | | **Can initiate transaction?** | Yes | **No** (can only react) | | **Has associated code?** | No | Yes (immutable once deployed) | | **Has storage?** | No | Yes (persistent) | | **Address derived from** | Public key (Keccak-256) | Creator address + nonce | | **Gas cost to create** | Free (already exists) | High (deploy + init) | EOAs are user-controlled wallets; contract accounts are autonomous programs. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)] **What is Gas in Ethereum? Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer:** **Gas** is a unit that measures the **amount of computational effort** required to execute operations on the Ethereum network. Every operation (opcode) has a fixed gas cost. **Why necessary (3 reasons):** 1. **Resource Pricing:** It assigns a real cost to computation, preventing infinite loops and DoS attacks. 2. **Incentivization:** Validators are rewarded in ETH proportional to gas consumed. 3. **Decoupled from ETH value:** Even if ETH price fluctuates, gas keeps computational cost measured in a stable unit (paired with `gasPrice` in gwei). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)] **(a)** Explain the architecture of the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** in detail. Describe its three memory regions with their characteristics. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Solidity smart contract for a **simple token (ERC-20 minimum)** that maintains balances of users and allows transfer. Show the data structures and core functions. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) — EVM Architecture (7 marks) The **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is a stack-based, **256-bit**, **quasi-Turing-complete** runtime environment that executes smart contract bytecode on every node of the Ethereum network. It is sandboxed, deterministic, and isolated from the host filesystem and network — guaranteeing the same output on every node given the same input. **1. Stack (Execution Layer):** - A LIFO structure holding **32-byte (256-bit) words**. - Maximum depth = **1024 items**. - All arithmetic, logical, and comparison operations pop operands from and push results to the stack. - PUSH/POP/SWAP/DUP are stack-specific opcodes. **2. Memory (Volatile Byte Array):** - A linear, byte-addressable, expandable array (initialized to all zeros at the start of every message call). - Used for storing **temporary variables** and function arguments that don't need persistence. - Cleared after each call → **not persistent** across transactions. - Accessed via MLOAD (load) and MSTORE (store) opcodes. **3. Storage (Persistent Key-Value Store):** - A 256-bit → 256-bit mapping tied permanently to a contract account. - Keys and values are both 32 bytes. - Written via SSTORE (cost: 22,100 gas cold first-write, 5,000 gas update). - Read via SLOAD (cost: 2,100 cold, 100 warm under EIP-2929). - Persists across transactions → contributes to the **Global State Trie**. **Key Architectural Properties:** - **Deterministic:** Same input → same output, ensuring consensus. - **Isolated:** No access to network, filesystem, or external clock. - **Quasi-Turing complete:** Bounded by gas, not by instruction count. - **Decentralized:** Same EVM runs on all 8,000+ nodes globally. --- #### Model Solution (b) — Minimal ERC-20 Token Contract (7 marks) ```solidity // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^0.8.0; contract MinimalToken { // (1) State variables string public name = "KTU Token"; string public symbol = "KTU"; uint8 public decimals = 18; uint256 public totalSupply; address public owner; // (2) Mapping of balances mapping(address => uint256) public balanceOf; // (3) Allowance mapping for delegated spending mapping(address => mapping(address => uint256)) public allowance; // (4) Standard ERC-20 event event Transfer(address indexed from, address indexed to, uint256 value); event Approval(address indexed owner, address indexed spender, uint256 value); // (5) Constructor — mints initial supply to deployer constructor(uint256 _initialSupply) { totalSupply = _initialSupply; balanceOf[msg.sender] = _initialSupply; owner = msg.sender; emit Transfer(address(0), msg.sender, _initialSupply); } // (6) Transfer function function transfer(address _to, uint256 _value) public returns (bool success) { require(balanceOf[msg.sender] >= _value, "Insufficient balance"); require(_to != address(0), "Invalid recipient"); balanceOf[msg.sender] -= _value; balanceOf[_to] += _value; emit Transfer(msg.sender, _to, _value); return true; } // (7) Approve and transferFrom function approve(address _spender, uint256 _value) public returns (bool) { allowance[msg.sender][_spender] = _value; emit Approval(msg.sender, _spender, _value); return true; } function transferFrom(address _from, address _to, uint256 _value) public returns (bool) { require(balanceOf[_from] >= _value, "Insufficient balance"); require(allowance[_from][msg.sender] >= _value, "Allowance exceeded"); balanceOf[_from] -= _value; balanceOf[_to] += _value; allowance[_from][msg.sender] -= _value; emit Transfer(_from, _to, _value); return true; } } ``` **Explanation of components (for 7 marks):** - **State variables:** `name`, `symbol`, `decimals`, `totalSupply`, and the two `mapping`s. - **Mappings:** `balanceOf` tracks each address's tokens; `allowance` tracks delegated spending rights. - **Events:** `Transfer` and `Approval` are required by the ERC-20 standard for off-chain monitoring. - **Constructor:** Mints the initial supply to the deployer (a common pattern). - **`transfer`:** Direct sender-to-recipient token movement, guarded by `require`. - **`approve` / `transferFrom`:** Two-step delegated transfer pattern (DApps like Uniswap use this). --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] **(a)** Explain the concept of **Gas** in Ethereum. Derive the **total transaction fee** for a transaction that uses 50,000 gas at a gas price of 25 gwei. Express the result in **wei, gwei, and ETH**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **differences between Solidity data types** — `uint256`, `address`, `bool`, `mapping`, and `string`. For each, mention storage size, default value, and one real-world use case. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) — Gas and Transaction Fee Derivation (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** **Gas** is the unit measuring computational work performed on the Ethereum network. Each EVM operation (opcode) consumes a fixed amount of gas. The total gas consumed by a transaction is **GasUsed**. The sender specifies a **gas price** (in gwei per unit gas) they are willing to pay.
