# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define CBDC. Differentiate between wholesale and retail CBDC.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC)** is the digital liability of a central bank, denominated in the national unit of account, and serves as a digital equivalent of physical cash.

**Wholesale CBDC (wCBDC):**
- Used by **financial institutions** for interbank settlements.
- Restricted access (limited to banks and select institutions).
- Improves **monetary policy transmission** and reduces settlement risk.
- Example: RBI's wholesale e₹-W pilot (call money market).

**Retail CBDC (rCBDC):**
- Used by the **general public** and businesses for everyday transactions.
- Available to all citizens via digital wallets.
- Competes with bank deposits and physical currency.
- Example: RBI's retail e₹-R pilot launched on 01 Dec 2022.

| Parameter | wCBDC | rCBDC |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Users | Banks | Public |
| Purpose | Interbank settlement | Daily transactions |
| Reach | Limited | Universal |
| Settlement | Atomic, real-time | Near-instant |

---

### Question 2: List any three government use-cases of blockchain with one-line justification.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Land Registry** — Blockchain prevents fraudulent property transfers by providing a tamper-proof, time-stamped title history.
2. **Digital Identity** — Citizens can self-manage their identity using cryptographic key pairs, reducing dependence on centralized databases vulnerable to breaches.
3. **Public Procurement & Tendering** — Smart contracts release payments only when delivery conditions are cryptographically verified, reducing corruption and delays.
4. *(Optional fourth)* **E-Voting** — Provides end-to-end verifiability and reduces booth-capturing / vote-rigging risks.
5. *(Optional fifth)* **Direct Benefit Transfer** — Eliminates middlemen in welfare distribution, ensuring subsidies reach the intended beneficiary.

---

## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** A single Module 4 question is asked for 14 marks, with an internal choice between two options. Each option has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[Internal Choice Option 1]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC). Differentiate between the **direct (single-tier)** and **indirect (two-tier)** models. Discuss the **Indian e₹ pilot** in this context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **any four government use-cases of blockchain** other than CBDC. For each, explain the problem solved and one real-world pilot/project. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

A **Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC)** is a digital form of sovereign money issued and regulated by the central bank, representing a direct claim on the central bank. It is built on a **permissioned distributed ledger** to ensure controlled access, identity verification, and regulator oversight.

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Core Ledger** — A permissioned DLT (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric, Corda, Quorum) maintained by the central bank and authorized participants.
2. **Issuance Engine** — Mints/destroys CBDC tokens backed by reserves.
3. **Distribution Layer** — Channels for delivering CBDC to end-users (banks, wallets).
4. **Identity & KYC Module** — Verifies participants; supports anonymity for small-value cash-like transfers.
5. **Settlement Module** — Atomic, real-time settlement replacing T+1 / T+2 cycles.
6. **Monitoring & Compliance** — Anti-money-laundering, transaction limits, and audit logs.

**Direct (Single-Tier) Model:**

$$ \text{Central Bank} \longrightarrow \text{Citizen} $$

- Central bank directly handles **all customer accounts**.
- Maximum control for the regulator.
- **Disadvantages:** Huge operational burden; lacks commercial-bank intermediation.
- **Example:** **Bahamas' Sand Dollar** — the world's first CBDC (launched 2020).

**Indirect (Two-Tier) Model:**

$$ \text{Central Bank} \longrightarrow \text{Commercial Banks} \longrightarrow \text{Citizen} $$

- Central bank issues CBDC to commercial banks; banks distribute to the public.
- Leverages existing banking infrastructure and KYC.
- **Advantages:** Scalable, leverages KYC/AML compliance, preserves credit-creation function.
- **Example:** **India's e₹-W and e₹-R** (RBI pilot), **e-CNY** (China), most proposed models.

**India's e₹ Pilot:**

- **Launch Date:** 01 December 2022 — first wholesale pilot in the call money market.
- **01 November 2023 — Retail pilot e₹-R** launched for select users and merchants in 4 cities.
- **Architecture:** Indirect, two-tier model. RBI issues to 4 PSU banks initially (SBI, ICICI, Yes Bank, IDFC First Bank), later expanded.
- **Form:** Token-based (held in a digital wallet).
- **Use cases tested:** Person-to-person transfers, person-to-merchant payments, programmable CBDC for specific use-cases (e.g., GIFT City).
- **Distinct from cryptocurrency** because it is a **liability of the RBI**, not decentralized.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **CBDC with cryptocurrency**. Mark the difference clearly:
> - CBDC = **centralized** (RBI controls), **sovereign** (legal tender), **permissioned** (KYC-based).
> - Cryptocurrency = **decentralized**, **not legal tender**, **pseudonymous**.
> Loss of **2–3 marks** is common if this distinction is not stated explicitly.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Use-Case 1 — Land Registry:**

- **Problem:** Land fraud, forged titles, ownership disputes, slow manual verification.
- **Solution:** Each property record becomes a transaction on a permissioned blockchain, cryptographically linked to the owner's public key. Any transfer requires a signed multi-party agreement (buyer, seller, registrar, bank).
- **Real-World Pilot:**
  - **Telangana (India):** Integrated Land Records Management System (ILRMS) on blockchain.
  - **Sweden's Lantmäteriet:** Land registry on a permissioned blockchain (Chromaway / Hedera).
  - **Dubai:** All real-estate transactions on blockchain by 2030 (Smart Dubai initiative).

**Use-Case 2 — Digital Identity (Self-Sovereign Identity):**

- **Problem:** Centralized identity databases are vulnerable to breaches (e.g., Aadhaar-related leaks).
- **Solution:** Citizens hold a **Decentralized Identifier (DID)** and a private key. Verifiers receive only the minimum required credentials (e.g., "age > 18" without revealing DOB) using **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP)**.
- **Real-World Pilot:**
  - **Estonia e-Residency:** Programmable digital identity for global citizens to access Estonian e-services.
  - **Microsoft ION (Identity Overlay Network):** Bitcoin-anchored DID infrastructure.

**Use-Case 3 — Public Procurement & Smart Contracts:**

- **Problem:** Corruption, delays, and disputes in government tendering.
- **Solution:** Smart contracts encode tender terms, milestone-based payments, and penalty clauses. Payment is released only when delivery is cryptographically attested (e.g., IoT sensors confirming equipment installation).
- **Real-World Pilot:**
  - **Procurement tracking** by several state governments in India.
  - **Hewlett-Packard's Trade Finance** blockchain (used by some government-backed trade platforms).

**Use-Case 4 — Subsidy / Welfare Distribution:**

- **Problem:** Middlemen siphon off welfare funds; leakages in PDS, MNREGA, scholarships.
- **Solution:** Beneficiaries are issued a blockchain-based digital token. Smart contracts release funds only when conditions are met (e.g., school attendance verified by biometric + blockchain log).
- **Real-World Pilot:**
  - **World Food Programme's "Building Blocks"** in Jordan (Azraq refugee camp) — distributed food vouchers on blockchain. Saved an estimated **$1.5 million per month** in bank transaction fees.
  - **Andhra Pradesh** — pilot for DBT to fisherfolk using blockchain.

> [!NOTE]
> Each use-case answered with: **(1) Problem → (2) Blockchain solution → (3) Real pilot** = full 7 marks. Mentioning only one element per case leads to partial marking.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[Internal Choice Option 2]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, June 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **regulatory challenges** that governments face in dealing with cryptocurrencies. Explain the **Indian regulatory framework** (taxation, KYC, RBI's stance). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **architectural components of a permissioned government blockchain** (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric). Compare it with permissionless blockchains like Bitcoin/Ethereum and justify why permissioned ledgers are preferred for government use. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Key Regulatory Challenges:**

1. **Pseudonymity vs AML/CFT** — Crypto transactions are pseudonymous, making it hard to track illicit finance.
2. **Cross-Border Nature** — No single jurisdiction controls decentralized networks; a single transaction may touch nodes in 50+ countries.
3. **Consumer Protection** — Frequent exchange hacks, rug-pulls, and Ponzi schemes.
4. **Monetary Sovereignty** — A parallel currency outside central-bank control can undermine monetary policy (e.g., dollarization via USDT in hyperinflation economies).
5. **Tax Evasion** — Difficulty tracking capital gains across thousands of wallets.
6. **Systemic Risk** — Stablecoins and exchange failures (FTX collapse 2022) can cause financial instability.
7. **Energy & Environmental Concerns** — PoW-based mining consumes massive electricity (Bitcoin ≈ 200 TWh/year).

**Indian Regulatory Framework:**

| Aspect | Status / Rule |
|--------|---------------|
| **Legal Tender** | Crypto is **NOT legal tender** (RBI circular 2018, clarified post-2020). |
| **RBI Banking Ban** | Struck down by Supreme Court (March 2020) — Internet and Mobile Association of India v. RBI. |
| **Crypto Tax** | **30% flat tax** on virtual digital asset (VDA) gains — Section 115BBH, Finance Act 2022. |
| **TDS** | **1% TDS** on transfer of VDAs — Section 194S, effective 01 April 2022. |
| **Loss Set-off** | **Not allowed** to set off crypto losses against other income. No carry-forward. |
| **Gift Tax** | Crypto gifts are taxable (except from specified relatives). |
| **Exchanges** | Must register with FIU-IND (Financial Intelligence Unit) for PMLA compliance. |
| **CBDC** | RBI launched e₹ pilots (Dec 2022 onwards) — see Question A. |
| **Crypto Bill** | **Cryptocurrency and Regulation of Official Digital Currency Bill, 2021** is still pending in Parliament. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often say "Bitcoin is illegal in India" — this is **WRONG**. Crypto is **legal to hold and trade** but **not legal tender** and is **heavily taxed**. Conflating these states costs 1–2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Hyperledger Fabric Architecture (Permissioned):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Peer Nodes** | Maintain the ledger, execute chaincode, endorse transactions. |
| **Orderer Nodes** | Establish transaction order, package into blocks, broadcast to peers. |
| **Channel** | Private subnet of communication between specific organizations; data isolation. |
| **Chaincode (Smart Contract)** | Business logic executed on the ledger. |
| **Membership Service Provider (MSP)** | Issues X.509 digital identities to all participants. |
| **World State Database** | Current state of the ledger (e.g., CouchDB) — complements the immutable transaction log. |
| **Gossip Protocol** | Disseminates ledger data across peers for eventual consistency. |
| **Endorsement Policy** | Specifies which peers must sign a transaction before it is committed. |
| **CouchDB / LevelDB** | State database (key-value store). |

**Comparison: Permissioned (Fabric) vs Permissionless (Bitcoin/Ethereum)**

| Feature | Hyperledger Fabric | Bitcoin / Ethereum |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Identity | Permissioned, KYC-based | Pseudonymous |
| Consensus | **Raft, Kafka, PBFT (execute-order-validate)** | PoW / PoS |
| Throughput | **1000–100,000 TPS** | 7–100 TPS |
| Finality | Deterministic (instant) | Probabilistic (≈ 60 min for BTC) |
| Energy | Negligible | High (PoW) |
| Privacy | Channel-based, data isolation | Public ledger |
| Smart Contracts | Chaincode (Java, Go, Node.js) | Solidity / Vyper (Ethereum), Bitcoin Script |
| Currency Token | Optional (e.g., utility token) | Native (BTC, ETH) |
| Governance | Linux Foundation | Open community / DAO |

**Why Permissioned Ledgers are Preferred for Government:**

1. **Identity & KYC** — Every node is a known entity (RBI, banks, state govt) — essential for legal recourse.
2. **Regulator Access** — Read/write access for regulators without compromising integrity.
3. **Throughput** — Government applications (tax filing, land registry) demand thousands of TPS.
4. **Privacy** — Channels allow isolation of data between departments (defense vs welfare).
5. **Finality** — Deterministic finality is critical for legal records.
6. **Energy Efficiency** — PoW would be politically unacceptable in government deployments.
7. **Compliance** — Easier to integrate with existing GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance) frameworks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Avoid writing only the "general blockchain" benefits. For 7 marks, the examiner expects a **direct comparison table** and **at least 4 specific government use-cases** justifying the choice. Generic answers = 3–4 marks. Specific, structured answers = full 7.

---

## 📌 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons for losing marks on Module 4 Government questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CBDC with cryptocurrency** — clarify centralized vs decentralized.
> 2. **Confusing permissioned with permissionless** — remember Fabric vs Bitcoin/Ethereum.
> 3. **Writing generic answers** — the examiner expects **Indian context** (RBI, e₹, Section 115BBH, 194S).
> 4. **Missing the regulatory aspect** — every government use-case has a regulatory / privacy dimension.
> 5. **Skipping real-world pilots** — always cite **at least one real example** per use-case.
> 6. **Not mentioning BFT condition** ($n \geq 3f + 1$) — a favorite 2-mark sub-question.
> 7. **Ignoring the "why permissioned" angle** — this is the central KTU theme.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Box — KTU Module 4: Government**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [x] **CBDC = Central Bank Digital Currency** — issued by central bank, **NOT** decentralized.
- [x] **Two architectural models:** Direct (single-tier) vs **Indirect (two-tier)** — India uses **two-tier**.
- [x] **Two forms:** **Wholesale (wCBDC)** for banks, **Retail (rCBDC)** for public.
- [x] **Permissioned vs Permissionless** — Government = **Permissioned** (Fabric, Quorum, Corda).
- [x] **Government = User + Regulator + Issuer.**

### ✅ Indian Context (Highly Testable)
- [x] CBDC pilot: **01 Dec 2022 (wholesale), 01 Nov 2023 (retail)**.
- [x] CBDC name: **e₹-W (wholesale), e₹-R (retail)**.
- [x] Crypto tax: **30% flat (Sec 115BBH)**, **1% TDS (Sec 194S)**.
- [x] Crypto **NOT legal tender** but **legal to hold and trade**.
- [x] No loss set-off, no carry-forward of crypto losses.
- [x] Crypto bill 2021 — **still pending**.

### ✅ Key Use-Cases (memorize 4)
- [x] Land registry (Telangana, Sweden Lantmäteriet, Dubai).
- [x] Digital Identity / DID (Estonia e-Residency).
- [x] E-Voting (West Virginia, Sierra Leone).
- [x] Welfare distribution (WFP Building Blocks, India DBT).
- [x] Public procurement (smart contracts).
- [x] Healthcare (MedRec, MIT).

### ✅ Important Formulas
- [x] **BFT safety:** $n \geq 3f + 1$
- [x] **Block hash:** $H_n = \text{SHA-256}(H_{n-1} \mid T_n \mid n)$
- [x] **TPS:** $\text{TPS} = N_{\text{tx}} / T_{\text{block}}$
- [x] **Crypto tax (India):** $T = 0.30 \times (P_{\text{sell}} - P_{\text{buy}})$
- [x] **TDS:** $\text{TDS} = 0.01 \times V_{\text{tx}}$
- [x] **Merkle verification:** $O(\log n)$ proofs.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- [x] Bitcoin block time ≈ **10 min**, Ethereum ≈ **12 sec**.
- [x] Bitcoin TPS ≈ **7**, Hyperledger Fabric ≈ **3000**.
- [x] SHA-256 output = **256 bits**.
- [x] World Food Programme savings = **~$1.5 M / month**.
- [x] India's CBDC launched by **4 banks** initially (SBI, ICICI, Yes, IDFC First).
- [x] Estonia is the global benchmark for blockchain e-governance since **2008**.

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] CBDC ≠ Cryptocurrency.
- [x] Permissioned ≠ Permissionless.
- [x] Public blockchain ≠ Anonymous (pseudonymous).
- [x] "Legal tender" vs "legal to own" — distinct.
- [x] KYC ≠ AML (KYC = identity verification, AML = anti-money-laundering).

---

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 4: Government**
> *Best of luck for your KTU University Examinations! 🎓*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Healthcare and Supply Chain Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST747 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Healthcare and Supply Chain Management # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Blockchain in Healthcare — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Blockchain in Healthcare* refers to the deployment of distributed ledger technology (DLT) to securely store, share, and validate Electronic Health Records (EHR), pharmaceutical provenance data, clinical trial records, and patient consent logs across multiple untrusted stakeholders (hospitals, insurers, pharmacies, regulators) without relying on a centralized authority. The ledger is cryptographically tamper-evident, time-stamped, and auditable in real time. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Lock-Box" Imagine a hospital corridor where every doctor, nurse, lab technician, and insurance agent needs to write on the **same patient file**. In the old world (centralized servers), the file sits in one office — anyone with the master key can alter it, lose it, or sell it. Now picture that same file replaced by a **glass box with thousands of identical copies** distributed to every stakeholder. Every time someone writes a new line (a prescription, a lab report, a billing entry), a magic seal is pressed. The seal *cannot* be undone without breaking the glass in front of all 1,000 witnesses. That is blockchain in healthcare — a **shared, append-only, tamper-evident medical ledger**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Highlight — Why Healthcare Needs Blockchain:** > 1. **Interoperability** — Siloed hospital databases cannot currently exchange data safely. > 2. **Data Integrity** — Tampering with EHRs causes misdiagnosis and insurance fraud. > 3. **Patient Sovereignty** — Patients own their data and can grant/revoke access. > 4. **Drug Counterfeiting** — WHO estimates **>10%** of global medicines are counterfeit (≈ **$4.4 billion** annually). --- ## 1.2 Blockchain in Supply Chain Management — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** > *Blockchain-based Supply Chain Management (BSCM)* is the application of distributed ledger technology to record every transaction, transfer of custody, and quality-check event along the lifecycle of a physical good — from raw-material sourcing to last-mile delivery — in a chronologically ordered, cryptographically verifiable, and immutable manner. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Cargo Passport" Think of a shipping container crossing the Suez Canal carrying mangoes from Kerala to Dubai. In the legacy world, each port clerk handwrites a stamp on a paper manifest that *might* be lost, smudged, or forged. In a blockchain supply chain, every checkpoint (farm → packhouse → refrigerated truck → port scanner → customs → supermarket) **digitally signs a transaction** that is broadcast to all parties simultaneously. The mango's temperature, location, and chain-of-custody are visible to the farmer, exporter, importer, retailer, and consumer — in real time. The blockchain becomes the mango's **immutable digital passport**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Highlight — Two Foundational Pillars of BSCM:** > - **Provenance Verification** — *Where did this product come from?* > - **Chain-of-Custody Audit** — *Who handled it, when, and under what conditions?* --- ## 1.3 Why These Two Domains? (KTU Module 4 Justification) Both **healthcare** and **supply chains** share three chronic problems that blockchain uniquely solves: | Problem Domain | Healthcare Pain Point | Supply Chain Pain Point | |---|---|---| | **Data Fragmentation** | EHRs trapped in hospital silos | Shipment data fragmented across carriers | | **Trust Deficit** | Patients distrust hospitals with data | Consumers distrust "organic" / "fair-trade" labels | | **Counterfeiting & Fraud** | Fake drugs, fake insurance claims | Fake luxury goods, grey-market diversion | > [!TIP] > The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to draw **direct parallels** between healthcare and supply chain use-cases. Memorize the matrix above. --- ## 1.4 Core Metrics & Standard Units (Bolded Constants) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry-Standard Metrics Used in This Module:** > - **WHO Counterfeit Drug Estimate: ≈ 10%** of global pharmaceutical trade (≈ **$4.4 billion/year** loss in low/middle-income countries). > - **Drug Recall Latency (legacy): ≈ 7–14 days**; blockchain target: **< 1 hour**. > - **Foodborne-Illness Cost (US): ≈ $15.6 billion/year** (CDC estimate). > - **Cold-Chain Pharma Loss: ≈ $35 billion/year** due to temperature-excursion spoilage. > - **IBM Food Trust pilot (2018 Walmart)**: mango traceability reduced from **7 days → 2.2 seconds**. > - **Hyperledger Fabric transaction throughput: ≈ 3,000 TPS** (vs. Ethereum ~15 TPS). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Healthcare Architecture — Layered View The KTU 2024 syllabus frames blockchain healthcare as a **four-layer architecture**: ### Layer 1 — Data Layer (Off-Chain) - Stores **bulk medical data** (MRI scans, genomic sequences, prescriptions) **off-chain** because blockchain storage is expensive. - Typical storage: **IPFS (InterPlanetary File System)**, AWS S3, hospital on-premise DBs. ### Layer 2 — Blockchain Layer (On-Chain) - Stores only **cryptographic hashes, pointers, and access-control metadata** on-chain. - Hash functions used: **SHA-256** (Bitcoin/Ethereum) or **Keccak-256** (Ethereum-native). - Consensus: PoW (legacy), **PoS / PoA** (preferred for healthcare due to **HIPAA/GDPR** compliance needs). ### Layer 3 — Smart Contract Layer - **Solidity / Hyperledger Composer** smart contracts enforce access policy: - "Only Dr. X may read Patient Y's record between 9 AM and 5 PM." - Event-driven: every access emits an immutable log entry. ### Layer 4 — Application Layer - Patient-facing apps, doctor dashboards, insurer portals, regulator dashboards. --- ## 2.2 Supply Chain Architecture — Provenance Stack ### Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a BSCM Transaction 1. **Asset Registration** — Manufacturer registers SKU on-chain with a unique **GTIN (Global Trade Item Number)** or **GS1 EPC**. 2. **Hash Fingerprinting** — IoT sensors (temperature, GPS, humidity) publish **hashes** of telemetry data to the ledger every N seconds. 3. **Custody Transfer** — Each change of hands (factory → distributor → retailer) is a signed transaction with **multi-signature verification**. 4. **Smart Contract Trigger** — Condition-action clauses execute automatically (e.g., *if temp > 8°C for 15 min → auto-reject shipment and notify insurer*). 5. **Consumer Verification** — End consumer scans QR code → retrieves full provenance. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheat-Sheet Table — Memorize These for ESE:** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Bound | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hash function output length | $n = 256$ bits (SHA-256) | bits | On-chain fingerprint | | 2 | Storage cost (Ethereum ~2020) | $C_{tx} \approx 20{,}000$ gas per KB | gas | Block size budgeting | | 3 | Throughput (Bitcoin) | $\text{TPS}_{BTC} \approx 7$ | transactions/sec | Bottleneck analysis | | 4 | Throughput (Hyperledger Fabric) | $\text{TPS}_{Fabric} \approx 3{,}000$ | transactions/sec | Enterprise BSCM | | 5 | Block confirmation (Bitcoin ~6 blocks) | $t_{conf} \approx 60$ min | minutes | Latency estimate | | 6 | Block confirmation (PoA healthcare) | $t_{conf} \approx 2$–$5$ sec | seconds | Clinical record write | | 7 | Temperature excursion cost (pharma) | $L = Q \times P_{unit}$ | currency | Cold-chain penalty | | 8 | Merkle proof size | $\log_{2}(N)$ hashes | hashes per proof | EHR audit | | 9 | 51% attack cost (Bitcoin 2024) | $\approx \$20$ billion | USD | Security argument | | 10 | Energy per tx (PoW) | $\approx 1{,}173$ kWh | kWh | Sustainability critique | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Question 1 is the cryptographic anchor for every on-chain integrity proof. Question 8 (Merkle proof) is the answer to *"How does a hospital prove a single record exists without downloading the whole chain?"* --- ## 2.4 Mathematical Foundation — Merkle Tree Proof (Healthcare Audit) A hospital wants to prove that **Record R₁₀** is part of a block containing **N = 1,024** patient records without revealing the other 1,023. $$ \text{Proof Length} = \log_{2}(N) = \log_{2}(1024) = 10 \text{ hashes} $$ This is why blockchain is **O(log N)** for inclusion proofs — a critical KTU high-yield fact. --- ## 2.5 Why Off-Chain + On-Chain Hybrid (Healthcare-Specific) Pure on-chain storage of an MRI scan (≈ **150 MB**) is economically infeasible: $$ \text{Cost} = 150 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes} \times \frac{20{,}000 \text{ gas}}{1024 \text{ bytes}} \times 50 \text{ Gwei} \times 0.000001 \text{ ETH/gas} \approx \text{ prohibitive} $$ > [!TIP] > **Standard KTU Answer:** *"Store the bulk off-chain (IPFS); store the IPFS hash + access control list on-chain."* Examiners award full marks for this hybrid formulation. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility — Why Engineers Care - **For Healthcare Engineers:** Designing HIPAA-compliant DLT systems for hospitals (Apollo Hospitals' India deployment uses **Hyperledger Fabric**). - **For Supply Chain Engineers:** Designing traceability for the **Pharma Cold Chain** under **WHO PQS E001** standards and for the **Food Cold Chain** under **HACCP** regulations. - **For Insurance Engineers:** Auto-claim settlement for spoiled pharma shipments via smart-contract triggers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transaction Throughput Bottleneck **Problem:** Estimate the theoretical maximum throughput of a Bitcoin-like blockchain used for **supply-chain tracking** of 50 million shipments/day. ### Step 1 — Compute Daily Volume in TPS $$ \text{TPS}_{\text{required}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{6}}{86{,}400 \text{ sec/day}} $$ $$ \text{TPS}_{\text{required}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{7}}{8.64 \times 10^{4}} \approx 578.7 \text{ TPS} $$ ### Step 2 — Compare with Bitcoin Capacity $$ \text{TPS}_{BTC} \approx 7 \text{ TPS (avg)} \quad ; \quad \text{Peak} \approx 13 \text{ TPS} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Deficit $$ \text{Deficit} = \frac{578.7 - 7}{7} \times 100\% \approx 8{,}167\% $$ ### Step 4 — Engineering Conclusion Bitcoin's **~7 TPS** cannot handle even 1% of global supply-chain telemetry. Therefore, enterprises adopt **permissioned chains** (Hyperledger Fabric ≈ **3,000 TPS**) or **Layer-2 rollups** (Optimism, zkSync ≈ **2,000–4,000 TPS**). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cold-Chain Auto-Reject Threshold **Problem:** A pharma shipment must remain below **8°C**. IoT sensor samples every $t = 60$ s. Smart contract must auto-reject if excursion lasts more than **15 minutes**. ### Step 1 — Threshold in Sensor Readings $$ N_{\text{reads}} = \frac{T_{\text{tolerance}}}{t_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{15 \times 60}{60} = 15 \text{ consecutive readings} $$ ### Step 2 — Pseudocode Logic (Descriptive, Not Executable) 1. Initialize counter $c \gets 0$. 2. On each new sensor reading $T_i$: - If $T_i > 8°C$: $c \gets c + 1$. - Else: $c \gets 0$. 3. If $c \ge 15$: emit event $\texttt{RejectShipment}(\text{shipmentId})$ and call insurer payout. ### Step 3 — Smart Contract Event The event triggers an immutable ledger entry: $$ \texttt{Event}(S_{\text{id}},\ \text{timestamp},\ T_{\max},\ \text{duration} = 900 \text{ s}) $$ > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner accepts either pseudo-code narrative OR Solidity-style English description. Do **not** paste actual code — only the logic structure. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — EHR Inclusion Proof via Merkle Tree **Problem:** A hospital's daily EHR database has $N = 4{,}096$ records. Prove inclusion of record #R₁,₈₂₃ in **O(log N)** hashes. ### Step 1 — Depth of Merkle Tree $$ d = \log_{2}(N) = \log_{2}(4{,}096) = 12 \text{ levels} $$ ### Step 2 — Hashes Needed in Proof $$ \text{Proof Size} = d = 12 \text{ SHA-256 hashes} $$ ### Step 3 — Storage Cost per Proof $$ \text{Bytes per proof} = 12 \times 32 \text{ bytes} = 384 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 4 — Comparison to Linear Search $$ \text{Linear scan} = 4{,}096 \times 256 \text{ bytes (record size)} \approx 1 \text{ MB} $$ ### Step 5 — Efficiency Gain $$ \eta = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{384} \approx 2{,}730 \times $$ **Conclusion:** Merkle proofs enable **trustless, log-scale EHR auditability**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Counterfeit-Drug Detection Probability **Problem:** Suppose a counterfeit drug sneaks into a legacy supply chain. Probability of detection in legacy = 0.05 per checkpoint. There are **5 checkpoints** (manufacturer → wholesaler → distributor → pharmacy → patient). ### Step 1 — Probability of Escape (Legacy) $$ P_{\text{escape, legacy}} = (1 - 0.05)^{5} = 0.95^{5} \approx 0.7738 $$ ### Step 2 — Probability of Escape (Blockchain with provenance hash check) Assume blockchain + IoT verification increases per-checkpoint detection to 0.95: $$ P_{\text{escape, blockchain}} = (1 - 0.95)^{5} = 0.05^{5} \approx 3.125 \times 10^{-7} $$ ### Step 3 — Risk Reduction $$ \text{Reduction factor} = \frac{0.7738}{3.125 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.48 \times 10^{6} $$ > [!TIP] > This is the **classic KTU "killer stat"** for supply-chain blockchain essays. Write it in the exam — it earns you the full 14 marks. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Criteria for Healthcare BSCM | Criterion | Public Chain (Ethereum) | Permissioned (Hyperledger Fabric) | Centralized DB | |---|---|---|---| | Throughput | Low (15–30 TPS) | High (~3,000 TPS) | Very High | | Privacy | Pseudonymous only | Configurable channels | Full | | Regulatory (HIPAA) | Difficult | **Compatible** | Compatible | | Cost per tx | ~$0.10–$5 | ~$0.001 | ~$0.0001 | | Immutability | High | High | Low | | Trust model | Trustless | Known validators | Single trusted party | | **KTU Recommended** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ for audit-critical data | --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Counterfeit Detection Mechanics | Stage | Legacy Detection | Blockchain Detection | |---|---|---| | Manufacturer | Paper certificate | Signed digital hash on-chain | | Wholesaler | Manual inspection | Automated hash verification | | Distributor | Trust-based | Smart-contract enforced | | Pharmacy | Barcode scan | Provenance API call | | Patient | Visual inspection | QR-code → full ledger view | | Recall time | 7–14 days | < 1 hour | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Healthcare Blockchain — Multi-Stakeholder Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB patientA[Patient A
Mobile App] docB[Doctor B
Hospital Network] insurerC[Insurer C
Claims Portal] pharmaD[Pharma Company D
Drug Provenance] regulatorE[Regulator E

MRI Scans, Genomics)] patientA -->|Read EHR| chainA docB -->|Write Prescription| chainA insurerC -->|Verify Claim| chainC pharmaD -->|Register Batch| chainB regulatorE -->|Audit All| chainA regulatorE -->|Audit All| chainB regulatorE -->|Audit All| chainC chainA -->|Hash Pointer| ipfsNode chainB -->|Hash Pointer| ipfsNode orderer -.-> chainA orderer -.-> chainB orderer -.-> chainC ``` **Architectural Reading:** - The **central hexagon** is the consortium — only authorized validators (hospitals, regulators) run nodes. - **Channels** (A, B, C) are logically isolated ledgers for confidentiality (e.g., insurer cannot read raw clinical notes). - **IPFS off-chain** stores bulk data; only hashes are on-chain. --- ## 4.2 Supply Chain — End-to-End Provenance Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR farmA[Farm A

[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **List any three key advantages of using blockchain to manage Electronic Health Records (EHR) compared to a centralized hospital database.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Immutability** — Once a record is written, it cannot be silently altered by a rogue insider, preventing medical-record fraud. 2. **Interoperability** — A patient can grant cross-hospital access via a single cryptographic key without requiring point-to-point hospital integrations. 3. **Patient Sovereignty** — Smart contracts let patients encode consent ("Dr. X may read for 7 days only"), with all access attempts immutably logged for audit. *(Examiner's note: any 3 of — immutability, auditability, patient control, interoperability, tamper-evidence — score full marks.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Explain the role of IoT sensors and oracles in a blockchain-based pharmaceutical cold chain.** **Model Answer:** IoT sensors (temperature, humidity, GPS) continuously measure physical conditions of a pharma shipment. They publish readings to a **blockchain oracle**, which is a trusted bridge between the off-chain world and the on-chain smart contract. The oracle signs the sensor data and submits a transaction embedding the hash. The smart contract evaluates pre-defined rules (e.g., *"if $T > 8°C$ for 15 min, auto-reject"*). This eliminates human delay and forgery in the cold chain. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks)** ### **Q3. (a) [7 Marks]** **With a neat block diagram, explain the four-layer architecture of a blockchain-based healthcare data management system. Why is the hybrid on-chain/off-chain storage strategy preferred?** **Model Solution:** **Four Layers:** 1. **Data Layer (Off-Chain)** — Stores bulk medical artifacts (MRI, CT, genomic sequences) on IPFS or hospital servers. Reasoning: a single MRI ≈ 150 MB cannot fit economically on-chain. 2. **Blockchain Layer (On-Chain)** — Stores only cryptographic hashes (SHA-256) of off-chain files, plus access-control metadata. Hash pointer enables verification: *"Does my file match the on-chain hash?"* 3. **Smart Contract Layer** — Enforces policies: - Role-based access (Doctor, Nurse, Insurer). - Time-windowed consent. - Every access event → on-chain audit log. 4. **Application Layer** — User-facing dashboards (patient app, doctor EMR, regulator analytics). **Why Hybrid:** - On-chain storage costs ≈ $5,000 per MB on Ethereum (2022 gas prices); off-chain IPFS ≈ $0.0001 per MB. - HIPAA compliance is easier when raw PHI never touches a globally-readable ledger. - Privacy + cost efficiency = mandatory hybrid. ### **Q3. (b) [7 Marks]** **Describe how blockchain can solve the drug counterfeit problem in the pharmaceutical supply chain. Include the role of GTIN/serialization and the FDA Drug Supply Chain Security Act (DSCSA).** **Model Solution:** **The Counterfeit Problem:** WHO estimates **10%** of global drugs are counterfeit, causing **≈ 1 million deaths/year**. **Blockchain Solution:** 1. **Serialization at Manufacture** — Each drug pack gets a unique **GTIN + serial number + batch ID**. A signed transaction registers this triplet on-chain. 2. **Custody Tracking** — Every transfer (manufacturer → wholesaler → distributor → pharmacy) is a multi-sig transaction with timestamp and geolocation. 3. **Verification at Dispense** — Pharmacy scans QR code; smart contract queries the ledger: - If the serial exists in the legitimate registry → dispense. - If not → reject and quarantine. 4. **Recall Automation** — Manufacturer flags a contaminated batch on-chain; all downstream nodes are auto-notified in **< 1 hour**, replacing the legacy 7–14 day recall. 5. **DSCSA (USA, 2023 enforceable)** mandates **interoperable, electronic, package-level traceability** — blockchain natively satisfies this. **Quantitative Impact (from earlier derivation):** $$ P_{\text{escape, legacy}} \approx 0.77 \quad ; \quad P_{\text{escape, blockchain}} \approx 3.1 \times 10^{-7} $$ A **~2.5 million × risk reduction**. --- ### **OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)** ### **Q4. (a) [7 Marks]** **Explain the IBM Food Trust blockchain pilot conducted by Walmart in 2018. What problem did it solve, and what were the measurable outcomes?** **Model Solution:** **Context:** Walmart partnered with IBM (Hyperledger Fabric) in 2018 to trace the origin of sliced mangoes sold in US stores. Legacy traceability took **7 days** — calling each supplier manually to identify the farm of origin (critical during the 2018 US *E. coli* romaine lettuce outbreak). **Implementation:** - Mangoes tagged with GS1 EPC RFID tags at farm. - Each custody change written to the Food Trust ledger. - Walmart's internal system queries ledger via API. **Measurable Outcomes:** - Trace time: **7 days → 2.2 seconds**. - Cut time-to-market for sourcing investigations by ~99.99%. - Enabled rapid containment of outbreaks, saving lives and reducing waste. - Reduced food-fraud by enabling consumer-side provenance verification (QR scan on package). **Generalization:** This pilot is the **canonical KTU example** for any supply-chain traceability essay. Use it. ### **Q4. (b) [7 Marks]** **Compare blockchain-based supply chain management with traditional EDI (Electronic Data Interchange) systems on the dimensions of: (i) trust model, (ii) data integrity, (iii) latency, (iv) dispute resolution. State which approach is preferred for pharmaceutical cold chains.** **Model Solution:** | Dimension | Traditional EDI | Blockchain Supply Chain | |---|---|---| | **Trust Model** | Requires central clearinghouse; each party trusts the hub | **Trustless** — cryptographic verification by any participant | | **Data Integrity** | Mutable; can be retro-edited in the EDI vault | **Immutable** — append-only ledger, SHA-256 linkage | | **Latency** | Real-time for VAN-mediated msgs, but reconciliation is slow (hours-days) | Near-real-time (Fabric ~3,000 TPS, 2–5 sec finality) | | **Dispute Resolution** | Manual arbitration, paper trail | Smart-contract execution + on-chain evidence trail | | **Audit Cost** | High — third-party auditors needed | Low — self-verifying ledger | **Conclusion for Pharma Cold Chain:** Blockchain (preferably **permissioned Hyperledger Fabric** or **Quorum**) is preferred because: - Temperature-excursion events must be **auto-rejected** via smart contracts. - Regulatory auditability under **WHO PQS** and **DSCSA** requires tamper-evident logs. - Patient safety is at stake; **trustless verification** is non-negotiable. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Confusing "immutable" with "private"** — Blockchain is public *visibility* by default. Healthcare requires **permissioned chains + encryption**, not naïve public Ethereum. > 2. **Forgetting the cost argument** — On-chain storage of 1 GB of MRI scans is economically impossible. Always state the **off-chain + on-chain hash** hybrid. > 3. **Writing Solidity code in a theory paper** — KTU exams for this elective are *theory-marked*. Pseudocode/flowcharts are acceptable; full Solidity programs are not required and may lose marks for verbosity. > 4. **Missing quantitative anchors** — Always back claims with numbers (Walmart's 7-day → 2.2 sec; counterfeit reduction factor of 10⁶). > 5. **Ignoring regulatory frameworks** — HIPAA, GDPR, DSCSA, WHO PQS — naming these gives you +1 to +2 marks free. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Healthcare Blockchain** = shared, tamper-evident EHR + drug provenance + consent management. - [x] **Supply Chain Blockchain** = shared, tamper-evident custody + IoT-driven smart contracts. - [x] **MUST use hybrid storage**: off-chain bulk (IPFS) + on-chain hash + ACL. - [x] **MUST cite quantitative anchors**: Walmart 7-day → 2.2 sec; counterfeit reduction 2.5 × 10⁶×. - [x] **Preferred platform** for enterprise: **Hyperledger Fabric (~3,000 TPS)**, NOT public Ethereum. - [x] **Merkle proof size** = O(log N) — 12 hashes for 4,096 records. - [x] **Drug recall** latency: legacy 7–14 days vs. blockchain < 1 hour. - [x] **Cold-chain smart contract** triggers on temperature excursion (e.g., $T > 8°C$ for 15 min). - [x] **Regulatory frameworks**: HIPAA (healthcare privacy, USA), GDPR (EU privacy), DSCSA (US drug traceability, 2023), WHO PQS (pharma cold-chain standard). - [x] **51% attack cost** for Bitcoin ≈ **$20 billion** (2024) — use as the security-anchor argument. - [x] **GTIN + EPC** are the de-facto serialization standards for pharma & food traceability. - [x] **The killer diagram** = side-by-side legacy vs. blockchain drug recall workflow.
